topics.title: Energy as a Resource and Its Accounting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET526 - ENERGY ECONOMICS AND POLICY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Energy as a Resource and Its Accounting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Energy as a Resource?

**Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Standard):**
> **Energy as a Resource** refers to any natural substance, phenomenon, or process from which useful work, heat, or light can be extracted to serve human economic and social activities. It is treated as an *input factor of production*, comparable in classical economics to land, labor, and capital, but distinguished by the fact that it is **non-substitutable** at the thermodynamic level.

In energy economics, energy is the **primary enabler of every industrial, agricultural, transport, and domestic activity**. The total energy stock available to an economy is known as the **energy resource base**, while the portion economically recoverable at present prices is called the **energy reserve**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — Three Pillars of Energy Resource Definition:**
> 1. **Physical Availability** — Does the resource exist in measurable quantity?
> 2. **Technological Accessibility** — Can it be extracted/converted with current tech?
> 3. **Economic Viability** — Is the cost of extraction lower than the market price of the converted energy?

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: Energy = Currency of Civilization

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Energy Bank Account"**
> Think of Earth's energy resources as a **bank account**:
> - **Deposits** = Solar radiation, geothermal heat, fossil fuels formed over millions of years.
> - **Withdrawals** = Every kWh of electricity used, every litre of petrol burned, every tonne of coal combusted.
> - **Interest (Renewables)** = Solar, wind, hydro — these keep refilling naturally within a human lifetime.
> - **Principal (Non-Renewables)** = Coal, oil, gas — once spent, they're gone for *millions* of years.
>
> **Energy Accounting** is the science of *tracking these deposits, withdrawals, and balances* — exactly like a chartered accountant tracks financial ledgers — so that planners know whether we are living off interest (sustainable) or burning through the principal (unsustainable).

### 1.3 What is Energy Accounting?

**Energy Accounting** is the systematic process of **measuring, recording, reporting, and analyzing** all energy flows and stocks within a defined system boundary (a nation, an industry, a building, or a process plant).

| Term | Symbol/Unit | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Energy** | PJ (Petajoule) | Energy in its raw form before any human conversion (coal in ground, sunlight, wind). |
| **Secondary Energy** | PJ | Energy after conversion to a usable carrier (electricity, gasoline, LPG). |
| **Final Energy** | TJ | Energy delivered to end-user for actual service (electricity at the plug). |
| **Useful Energy** | TJ | Energy that actually performs the desired task (light from a bulb, motion of a motor). |
| **Energy Intensity** | toe / crore ₹ GDP | Energy consumed per unit of economic output. |
| **Conversion Efficiency** | $\eta$ (dimensionless, 0–1) | Ratio of useful energy output to energy input. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric for KTU:** **1 Tonne of Oil Equivalent (toe) = 41.868 GJ = 11.63 MWh**. All national energy statistics in India are reported in **Mtoe (Million Tonne of Oil Equivalent)**.

### 1.4 Fundamental Classification of Energy Resources

```
                              ENERGY RESOURCES
                                    │
              ┌─────────────────────┴─────────────────────┐
              │                                           │
        PRIMARY (Natural)                          SECONDARY (Derived)
              │                                           │
    ┌─────────┴──────────┐                    ┌───────────┴────────────┐
    │                    │                    │                        │
RENEWABLE          NON-RENEWABLE       CONVENTIONAL             NON-CONVENTIONAL
(Flux)             (Stock)              CARRIERS                 CARRIERS
    │                    │                    │                        │
• Solar            • Coal              • Electricity          • Hydrogen
• Wind             • Crude Oil         • LPG                  • Biodiesel
• Hydro            • Natural Gas       • Gasoline/Diesel      • Synthetic Fuels
• Biomass          • Nuclear (U-235)   • Kerosene
• Geothermal       • Shale Gas/Oil     • Charcoal
• Tidal/Wave
• Hydrogen (as fuel)
```

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Energy Resource Pyramid (Concentration Concept)

Energy resources are tiered based on their **energy density** (energy per unit mass or volume):

- **Tier 1 — High Density (Stock)**: Fossil fuels, Nuclear — millions of years of solar energy concentrated.
- **Tier 2 — Medium Density**: Biofuels, Stored Hydropower.
- **Tier 3 — Low Density (Flux)**: Direct Solar, Wind, Tidal — diffuse but continuously replenished.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in Energy Economics:** Low-density renewable resources require large land footprints and capital infrastructure but have near-zero marginal cost. High-density fossil fuels have small footprints but heavy emissions and import dependency costs.

### 2.2 Theoretical Framework of Energy Accounting

Energy accounting follows the **First Law of Thermodynamics (Conservation of Energy)**:

$$E_{in} = E_{out} + E_{stored} + E_{loss}$$

For a steady-state industrial process:

$$E_{input} = E_{output, useful} + E_{losses}$$

The **exergy-based accounting** uses the **Second Law** to account for *quality* of energy, not just quantity:

$$Exergy = E - T_0 \cdot S$$

where $T_0$ is the dead-state temperature (typically **298.15 K**) and $S$ is entropy.

### 2.3 The Four Levels of Energy Accounting (KTU-Standard)

| Level | Name | Description | Typical Reporting Body |
|---|---|---|---|
| **L1** | **Resource Accounting** | Total primary energy in nature | Ministry of Petroleum |
| **L2** | **Energy Balance** | Supply, transformation, end-use | IEA / CEA India |
| **L3** | **Sectoral Accounting** | Energy use by industry, transport, domestic | NITI Aayog |
| **L4** | **End-Use Accounting** | Useful energy for specific services (lighting, motive power) | BEE (Bureau of Energy Efficiency) |

### 2.4 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are essential for KTU PEMET526 Module 1 derivations and numerical problems.**

| # | Formula | Description | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{useful} = \eta \cdot E_{input}$ | Useful energy output | J, kWh, toe |
| 2 | $EI = \dfrac{E_{consumed}}{GDP}$ | Energy Intensity (economy-wide) | kgoe / ₹ or toe / crore ₹ |
| 3 | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_1 \cdot \eta_2 \cdot \eta_3$ | Cascaded efficiency (e.g., generation→transmission→motor) | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $E_{saved} = E_{baseline} - E_{actual}$ | Energy conservation saving | toe, kWh |
| 5 | $SEP = \dfrac{E_{output}}{E_{input}} \times 100$ | Specific Energy Performance (%) | % |
| 6 | $EROI = \dfrac{E_{obtained}}{E_{invested}}$ | Energy Return on Investment | Dimensionless (1–30 for fuels) |
| 7 | $C_{carbon} = EF \cdot E_{fuel}$ | Carbon emissions from fuel use | $tCO_2$ |
| 8 | $E_{primary} = \dfrac{E_{final}}{\eta_{conversion}}$ | Back-tracing final energy to primary | toe |
| 9 | $E_{net} = E_{gross} - E_{parasitic}$ | Net usable energy after auxiliary loads | kWh |
| 10 | $PBP = \dfrac{C_{investment}}{A_{savings}}$ | Pay-back for energy project (years) | years |

> **CRITICAL TABLE FORMAT WARNING:** All absolute-value bars and norms use $\vert \cdot \vert$ (rendered as `\vert`) to preserve the markdown table integrity.

### 2.5 The Energy Audit Chain (Operational 'Why' and 'How')

- **Step 1 — Walk-through Audit:** Visual survey to identify obvious losses (steam leaks, idle equipment).
- **Step 2 — Detailed Audit:** Instrumented measurement of every energy stream with flow meters, $\Delta T$ probes, gas analysers.
- **Step 3 — Investment-grade Audit:** Engineering-economic analysis with payback, EROI, NPV of conservation measures.
- **Step 4 — Verification Audit:** Post-implementation M&V (Measurement & Verification) to confirm claimed savings.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** Energy accounting is the backbone of **PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade)** scheme of BEE for Indian industries, **ESCert (Energy Savings Certificate)** trading, and **Carbon Credit** markets under **PAT-2 and CCTS** notified by the Ministry of Power, Government of India.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I: Primary Energy from Final Energy

**Problem Context:** A factory consumes **5,00,000 kWh** of electricity per month. The national grid has an average generation + transmission efficiency of **30%**. Determine the primary energy required.

**Step-by-step:**

The relationship between primary and final (delivered) energy is:

$$E_{primary} = \frac{E_{final}}{\eta_{conversion}}$$

Substituting the values:

$$E_{primary} = \frac{5{,}00{,}000 \text{ kWh}}{0.30}$$

$$E_{primary} = 1{,}666{,}666.67 \text{ kWh (thermal equivalent)}$$

Converting to **toe** (using $1 \text{ toe} = 11{,}630 \text{ kWh}$):

$$E_{primary, toe} = \frac{1{,}666{,}666.67}{11{,}630} = 143.31 \text{ toe/month}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** We are tracking upstream. The 5,00,000 kWh reaching the factory required **~3.33× more** energy at the primary stage because 70% is lost as heat in generation, transmission, and distribution.

### 3.2 Derivation II: Energy Intensity and Decomposition

**Energy Intensity** for a nation is defined as:

$$EI = \frac{TPES}{GDP}$$

where $TPES$ = Total Primary Energy Supply. The **log-mean Divisia index (LMDI)** decomposition breaks changes in $EI$ into activity, structure, and intensity effects.

Starting from a simple two-sector economy (Industry $i$ and Services $s$):

$$EI = \frac{E_i + E_s}{G_i + G_s} = \frac{E_i / G_i \cdot G_i + E_s / G_s \cdot G_s}{G_i + G_s}$$

Defining sectoral intensity $I_i = E_i / G_i$ and output share $S_i = G_i / GDP$:

$$EI = I_i \cdot S_i + I_s \cdot S_s$$

Differentiating with respect to time:

$$\frac{d(EI)}{EI} = \frac{I_i \cdot S_i}{EI} \cdot \frac{dI_i}{I_i} + \frac{I_s \cdot S_s}{EI} \cdot \frac{dI_s}{I_s} + \frac{I_i \cdot S_i}{EI} \cdot \frac{dS_i}{S_i} + \frac{I_s \cdot S_s}{EI} \cdot \frac{dS_s}{S_s}$$

**Three Effects Identified:**
- **Activity Effect** — change in overall GDP scale.
- **Structural Effect** — change in sectoral composition $S_i, S_s$.
- **Intensity Effect** — change in sectoral energy efficiency $I_i, I_s$.

> **Conversion Logic:** This decomposition is **the standard tool** used in IEA reports and India's *Energy Statistics* publication (MoSPI) to explain whether a fall in energy intensity is due to efficiency gains (good) or deindustrialization (bad).

### 3.3 Derivation III: EROI (Energy Return on Energy Invested)

The thermodynamic EROI is the ratio of energy delivered to society versus the energy expended to secure that energy:

$$EROI = \frac{E_{delivered}}{E_{invested}}$$

For fossil fuels, $E_{invested}$ includes extraction, refining, and transportation. The **minimum EROI for an industrial civilization** is debated; a commonly cited threshold is **EROI ≥ 15** for fossil fuels to sustain complex society.

**Example Calculation:** If 1 barrel of crude oil (≈ 6.1 GJ) requires 0.3 GJ to find, extract, and refine:

$$EROI = \frac{6.1}{0.3} = 20.3$$

For modern Canadian oil sands (heavy, deep):

$$EROI = \frac{6.1}{3.5} \approx 1.7$$

> **Conversion Logic:** A *declining EROI* signals **energy depletion economics** — every additional unit of energy becomes more expensive to obtain, eventually making alternative energy sources economically superior even before depletion is complete.

### 3.4 Derivation IV: Energy Savings through Efficiency

For replacing an old motor (efficiency $\eta_{old} = 0.85$) with a new IE4 motor ($\eta_{new} = 0.95$), where the motor operates at rated load $P = 50$ kW for $t = 6{,}000$ hours/year:

$$E_{input,old} = \frac{P \cdot t}{\eta_{old}} = \frac{50 \times 6000}{0.85} = 3{,}52{,}941.18 \text{ kWh/yr}$$

$$E_{input,new} = \frac{P \cdot t}{\eta_{new}} = \frac{50 \times 6000}{0.95} = 3{,}15{,}789.47 \text{ kWh/yr}$$

$$\Delta E = 3{,}52{,}941.18 - 3{,}15{,}789.47 = 37{,}151.71 \text{ kWh/yr}$$

In **toe** (using $1 \text{ kWh} = 8.6 \times 10^{-5} \text{ toe}$):

$$\Delta E_{toe} = 37{,}151.71 \times 8.6 \times 10^{-5} = 3.195 \text{ toe/yr}$$

In **₹** (at ₹ 8 / kWh):

$$Savings = 37{,}151.71 \times 8 = \text{₹ } 2{,}97{,}213.68 / \text{year}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** This is the **classic PAT scheme calculation** that determines the number of **ESCerts (Energy Savings Certificates)** an industry can claim, each ESCert = 1 toe of saved oil equivalent over a 3-year cycle.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

```mermaid

```
