> **Decision rule:** Accept if $\text{IRR} \geq r$ (the hurdle rate / WACC). The IRR is the project's "effective interest rate."

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Capital Recovery Factor (CRF)

**Starting premise:** You want to convert a lump-sum loan $I_0$ into a *level* annual payment $A$ that fully repays the loan in $n$ years at interest rate $r$.

**Step 1 — Future value of the loan at end of Year $n$ (compounded forward):**

$$
\text{FV}_n = I_0 (1+r)^n
$$

*Logic:* $I_0$ grows by a factor $(1+r)$ each year for $n$ years.

**Step 2 — Future value of an annuity of $A$ paid at end of each year:**

$$
\text{FV}_{\text{annuity}} = A \cdot \frac{(1+r)^n - 1}{r}
$$

*Logic:* Standard formula for the future value of an ordinary annuity.

**Step 3 — Equate the two (loan must be exactly repaid):**

$$
A \cdot \frac{(1+r)^n - 1}{r} = I_0 (1+r)^n
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $A$:**

$$
A = I_0 \cdot \frac{r(1+r)^n}{(1+r)^n - 1}
$$

The bracketed multiplier is, by definition, the **Capital Recovery Factor (CRF)**:

$$
\text{CRF} = \frac{r(1+r)^n}{(1+r)^n - 1}
$$

*Logic:* CRF has units of 1/years. For a 10-year loan at 10%, CRF = **0.16275/yr** ⇒ annual payment is 16.275% of $I_0$.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — KTU Numerical Style

> **Problem.** A factory invests ₹50 lakh in a VFD + insulation retrofit. Annual energy savings are constant at ₹8 lakh/year for 10 years. Salvage value = ₹4 lakh at end of Year 10. Discount rate = 10%. Compute NPV, IRR, and discounted payback.

**Solution (NPV):**

$$
\text{NPV} = -50 + 8 \cdot \left[ \frac{1 - (1.10)^{-10}}{0.10} \right] + \frac{4}{(1.10)^{10}}
$$

**Step A — Present Value Annuity Factor (PVAF) for 10 yrs @ 10%:**

$$
\text{PVAF} = \frac{1 - (1.10)^{-10}}{0.10} = 6.1446
$$

**Step B — PV of annual savings:**

$$
8 \times 6.1446 = 49.157 \text{ lakh}
$$

**Step C — PV of salvage value:**

$$
\frac{4}{(1.10)^{10}} = \frac{4}{2.5937} = 1.542 \text{ lakh}
$$

**Step D — Total NPV:**

$$
\text{NPV} = -50 + 49.157 + 1.542 = 0.699 \text{ lakh}
$$

**Step E — Decision:** NPV $\approx$ ₹0.70 lakh $>$ 0 ⇒ **Accept the project** (marginally).

**Solution (IRR):** Solve $-50 + 8 \cdot \text{PVAF}(\text{IRR}, 10) + 4 / (1+\text{IRR})^{10} = 0$.
By trial: at IRR = 11%, NPV ≈ +1.95 lakh; at IRR = 12%, NPV ≈ +0.45 lakh; at IRR = 12.5%, NPV ≈ −0.26 lakh. Therefore **IRR ≈ 12.2%** > hurdle rate 10% ⇒ Accept.

**Solution (Discounted Payback):** Cumulative discounted savings reach ₹50 lakh between Year 7 and Year 8. Specifically, at end of Year 7, cumulative PV ≈ 8 × 4.8684 = ₹38.95 lakh. Remaining ₹11.05 lakh recovered in Year 8's discounted saving of ₹8/1.10⁸ = ₹4.66 lakh ⇒ fraction 11.05/4.66 = 2.37 years. **DPP ≈ 9.37 years.**
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The EE Investment Decision Pipeline

The following Mermaid block maps the full decision logic from policy environment to project commissioning. The Mermaid engine cannot draw physical schematics, so this *functional architecture* shows how each characteristic of EE investment interacts with each component of the investment environment.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Policy and Macroeconomic Context] --> B[Investment Environment]
    B --> C{WACC and Risk Premium}
    B --> D[Regulatory Stability]
    B --> E[Financial Market Depth]
    B --> F[Fiscal Incentives]
    C --> G[EE Project Feasibility Test]
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[NPV greater than zero]
    G --> I[IRR greater than WACC]
    G --> J[DPP less than Horizon]
    H --> K[Accept and Commission]
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[M and V and Performance Verification]
    L --> M[ESCO Payment or Owner Savings Realised]
    M --> N[Feedback to Investment Environment via Demonstrated Track Record]
    N --> A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The investment environment is *not* a one-shot backdrop; the feedback loop (N → A) shows that each successful EE project lowers perceived risk and *improves* the next project's investment environment. This is the **demonstration-effect** argument behind government seed-funding of EE.

---

## 4.2 The Energy Efficiency Gap — Causal Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[True Social Optimum] --- P2[Cost Optimal Level]
    P2 --- P3[Observed Market Outcome]
    P3 --> Q1[EE Gap Equals P2 minus P3]
    Q1 --> R1[Cause 1 Hidden Costs]
    Q1 --> R2[Cause 2 Information Asymmetry]
    Q1 --> R3[Cause 3 Split Incentive]
    Q1 --> R4[Cause 4 High Discount Rate]
    Q1 --> R5[Cause 5 Non Energy Benefits Omitted]
    R1 --> S[Policy Levers]
    R2 --> S
    R3 --> S
    R4 --> S
    R5 --> S
    S --> T1[Carbon Pricing]
    S --> T2[ESCO Models]
    S --> T3[Green Finance and Subsidies]
    S --> T4[Minimum Energy Performance Standards]
    T1 --> U[Gap Closure]
    T2 --> U
    T3 --> U
    T4 --> U
```

---

## 4.3 Capital-Cost vs Operating-Cost Trade-off (EE Project Anatomy)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CAP[High Upfront Capital I0] --> TRADEOFF[Time-Value Tradeoff]
    TRADEOFF --> OP[Stream of Operating Cost Savings CFt over n Years]
    OP --> NEB[Plus Non Energy Benefits NEBs]
    OP --> SALV[Plus Salvage Value SVn]
    NEB --> NPV
    SALV --> NPV
    OP --> NPV
    CAP --> NPV
    NPV[NPV Computation at WACC r] --> DECISION{NPV Decision}
    DECISION -->|NPV greater than zero| ACC[Project Accepted]
    DECISION -->|NPV less than zero| REJ[Project Rejected even though technically efficient]
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Insight from the Diagram:** The "REJ" branch is precisely where the **EE gap** lives — a project that is *technically* energy efficient but *financially* rejected because the investment environment fails to price NEBs, internalize externalities, or supply patient (low-rate) capital.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme, May 2025]
**"List and briefly explain any THREE characteristics of energy efficiency investments that distinguish them from conventional supply-side energy projects."**

**Model Answer (3 marks — ~50 words per point):**

1. **High up-front capital with low operating cost:** EE projects require large initial investment (e.g., retrofit cost) but yield savings on the energy bill over many years, inverting the cash-flow profile of a power plant.

2. **Long payback periods (3–10 years):** Because savings are small and spread over many years, EE investments recover their cost slowly, making them sensitive to discount rates and impatient capital.

3. **Non-Energy Benefits (NEBs):** Comfort, productivity, and health gains often constitute 20–50% of the total benefit but are systematically omitted in private financial analysis, leading to undervaluation of EE projects.

*(Award 1 mark per correctly stated and explained characteristic; 3 × 1 = 3 marks.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme, May 2025]
**"Define the 'Energy Efficiency Gap'. Mention two market failures that cause it."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** The Energy Efficiency Gap is the divergence between the actual level of energy efficiency adopted in an economy and the cost-optimal level achievable under perfect information, rational behaviour, and correct pricing of externalities.

**Market failures (1 mark each, any two):**

- **Information asymmetry / Hidden costs:** Buyers cannot observe true savings, raising transaction costs and leading to under-investment.
- **Split incentive (principal-agent problem):** The party paying for the EE upgrade (e.g., landlord) does not capture the savings (e.g., lower tenant bill), so the upgrade is not undertaken.
- **Externalities:** Pollution costs of energy generation are not priced into tariffs, so the social value of saving energy is higher than the private value, leading to under-investment from a private-decision viewpoint.
- **Capital market imperfections:** Lack of patient, low-interest long-tenure finance forces consumers to use credit-card or short-term rates, shrinking NPV of EE projects.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries 14 marks and is split into two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Internal choice is provided — students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Module 1, Model 2025]**

**A(a) [7 marks]** Discuss in detail the components of the *Investment Environment* for the energy sector. Explain how each component influences the flow of capital into energy efficiency projects.

**Model Answer:**

The Investment Environment is the constellation of conditions that determine the cost, availability, and direction of capital. The key components are:

1. **Macroeconomic Stability (2 marks)** — Stable inflation, exchange-rate predictability, and sovereign credit ratings lower the country-risk premium baked into the WACC. A 1% rise in perceived sovereign risk can raise the WACC of a 15-year EE project by 50–100 basis points, cutting NPV by 8–15%. Hyperinflation or currency crises can wipe out the savings stream in nominal terms.

2. **Financial Market Depth (1.5 marks)** — The presence of long-tenure bond markets (10–20 year green bonds), development banks, and ESCO financiers determines whether patient capital is available. In shallow markets, EE projects are forced into short-tenure commercial loans with high rates, *raising the effective discount rate and shrinking NPV*.

3. **Regulatory and Policy Stability (1.5 marks)** — Frequent changes in subsidy regimes, tariff structures, or PAT (Perform-Achieve-Trade) targets create option value of *waiting* rather than investing. Investment is deferred even when NPV is positive, because the policy environment is too volatile to bank on.

4. **Fiscal and Tax Incentives (1 mark)** — Accelerated depreciation, reduced GST on EE equipment, and tax holidays directly raise the after-tax CF$_t$ and hence NPV. Carbon pricing and feed-in tariffs (for combined heat-and-power retrofits) act as additive revenue streams.

5. **Institutional Quality and Contract Enforcement (1 mark)** — Strong contract enforcement and low corruption reduce the *transaction cost* component of EE projects, which is otherwise a major non-tariff barrier in emerging markets.

> **Synthesis (extra 0.5 mark for connecting back to EE):** Because EE investments have small, dispersed, long-dated savings, *each* of these five components has a *larger* marginal impact on EE NPV than on conventional generation NPV. This is why EE needs the investment environment to be *deliberately engineered* (e.g., ESCO-friendly regulations, green-bond frameworks) rather than simply left to market forces.

---

**A(b) [7 marks]** A hospital is evaluating two energy-efficiency options:
- **Option X:** Replace chiller plant at $I_0$ = ₹80 lakh; annual electricity savings = ₹14 lakh for 15 years; salvage = ₹6 lakh.
- **Option Y:** LED + VFD retrofit at $I_0$ = ₹25 lakh; annual savings = ₹5 lakh for 10 years; salvage = ₹2 lakh.

The hospital's WACC is 9%. Compare using **NPV** and **Discounted Payback**. Recommend the better option.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — PVAF (annuity present value factor) values:**
- 15 years @ 9% : $\text{PVAF} = \frac{1 - (1.09)^{-15}}{0.09} = 8.0607$
- 10 years @ 9% : $\text{PVAF} = \frac{1 - (1.09)^{-10}}{0.09} = 6.4177$

**Step 2 — Discount factors for salvage:**
- Year 15 @ 9% : $(1.09)^{15} = 3.6425$ ⇒ PV = $6 / 3.6425 = 1.647$ lakh
- Year 10 @ 9% : $(1.09)^{10} = 2.3674$ ⇒ PV = $2 / 2.3674 = 0.845$ lakh

**Step 3 — NPV of Option X:**
$$
\text{NPV}_X = -80 + 14 \times 8.0607 + 1.647 = -80 + 112.85 + 1.65 = 34.50 \text{ lakh}
$$

**Step 4 — NPV of Option Y:**
$$
\text{NPV}_Y = -25 + 5 \times 6.4177 + 0.845 = -25 + 32.09 + 0.85 = 7.94 \text{ lakh}
$$

**Step 5 — Discounted Payback (Option X):** Annual discounted saving = 14/1.09 = 12.84 lakh in Year 1 (rising PV over time). Cumulative PV reaches 80 lakh at end of Year 8 (8 × 12.84 ≈ 102.7). DPP ≈ 7.5 years.

**Step 6 — Discounted Payback (Option Y):** Annual discounted saving ≈ 4.59 lakh in Year 1. Cumulative PV reaches 25 lakh at end of Year 6 (6 × 4.59 ≈ 27.5). DPP ≈ 5.5 years.

**Step 7 — Comparison Table & Recommendation (1 mark for synthesis):**

| Metric | Option X (Chiller) | Option Y (LED+VFD) |
|---|---|---|
| NPV (₹ lakh) | 34.50 | 7.94 |
| DPP (years) | ≈ 7.5 | ≈ 5.5 |

**Recommendation:** **Option X (chiller replacement) is superior** in absolute value creation (NPV is ~4.3× higher), even though Option Y has the shorter payback. From a *pure* NPV-and-shareholder-value standpoint, the hospital should pick **Option X** — *provided* it has access to long-tenure capital. If capital is constrained and the hospital needs fast liquidity recovery, **Option Y** is the rational choice. A typical KTU marking scheme awards full 7 marks if both NPVs are correct (4 marks), payback periods are computed (2 marks), and a justified recommendation is made (1 mark).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Examiner's Notes):**
> - Forgetting to convert salvage value into present value (loses ~0.5 mark per option).
> - Using *simple* payback instead of *discounted* payback when the question explicitly asks for DPP (loses ~1 mark).
> - Comparing projects on payback *only* without computing NPV (loses ~2 marks).
> - Forgetting to write units (₹ lakh) — minor deduction, but accumulates.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**B(a) [7 marks]** Derive the **Capital Recovery Factor (CRF)** from first principles. Using CRF, calculate the equivalent annual cost (EAC) of a ₹1 crore boiler upgrade that has a 12-year life and is financed at 10% per annum. Comment on how the EAC framework changes the comparison between a high-capex, low-opex EE project and a low-capex, high-opex alternative.

**Model Answer:**

**Derivation of CRF (4 marks):** (Refer to Section 3.3 above. Show all 4 steps. The CRF formula is $\text{CRF} = \frac{r(1+r)^n}{(1+r)^n - 1}$.)

**EAC Calculation (2 marks):**
$$
\text{EAC} = I_0 \cdot \text{CRF}(10\%, 12) = 1{,}00{,}00{,}000 \times \frac{0.10 \times (1.10)^{12}}{(1.10)^{12} - 1}
$$

$(1.10)^{12} = 3.1384$

$$
\text{CRF} = \frac{0.10 \times 3.1384}{3.1384 - 1} = \frac{0.31384}{2.1384} = 0.14676 \text{ /yr}
$$

$$
\text{EAC} = ₹1{,}00{,}00{,}000 \times 0.14676 = ₹14{,}67{,}600 \text{ per year}
$$

**Commentary (1 mark):** The EAC converts the up-front ₹1 crore into a level annual payment of ₹14.68 lakh. This figure can be directly compared with the *avoided* annual fuel/maintenance cost. If the boiler saves, say, ₹25 lakh/year in fuel, the project is unambiguously worthwhile. Critically, the EAC framework *removes* the bias toward short-payback projects that arises from naive payback analysis. A high-capex, low-opex EE option that would have looked unattractive on raw payback grounds becomes competitive when its annual cost is fairly annualized and compared to operating savings.

---

**B(b) [7 marks]** Explain the **Energy Efficiency Gap** with reference to at least four market failures. For each, suggest one policy instrument that can close that specific component of the gap.

**Model Answer:**

| # | Market Failure (1.25 marks each) | Policy Instrument (0.5 mark each) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Information asymmetry / hidden costs** — buyers cannot verify savings pre-purchase, leading to risk-aversion. | Mandatory energy audits, EE equipment labelling, M&V standards (e.g., BEE star labelling). |
| 2 | **Split incentive (principal-agent)** — landlord pays, tenant saves, no one acts. | Minimum Energy Performance Standards (MEPS) for rental stock; on-bill financing transferred with the tenant. |
| 3 | **Externalities (carbon, local pollution)** — social value of saving energy > private value. | Carbon tax, cap-and-trade (PAT scheme in India), fossil-fuel subsidy reform. |
| 4 | **Capital market imperfection** — high discount rates for long-tenure EE loans. | Green bonds, concessional finance from multilateral banks, ESCO performance contracting. |
| 5 (Bonus) | **Behavioural / status-quo bias** — consumers undervalue future savings. | Default-green policies, commitment devices, behavioural nudges. |

**Synthesis (1 mark):** No single instrument closes the entire EE gap; the gap is multi-causal, so the policy response must be a *portfolio* — pricing instruments (carbon tax) *plus* informational instruments (labelling) *plus* financial instruments (ESCOs) *plus* regulatory instruments (MEPS). This is the foundational justification for the **PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade)** scheme and the **Energy Conservation Building Code (ECBC)** in India's policy stack.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Topic-Wise Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Mixing LCOE with LCCE** — examiners *specifically* test whether you know that LCCE is the right metric for EE projects. Confusing them is a guaranteed 1–2 mark loss.
> 2. **Omitting salvage value** in NPV — common slip, costs 0.5–1 mark.
> 3. **Forgetting that EE cash flows are *savings*, not revenues** — semantically small, but conceptually vital. The sign of CF$_t$ must be **positive** for an EE project.
> 4. **Stating "high discount rates" without explaining why** — examiners want the chain: *long payback* → *sensitivity to r* → *patient capital needed* → *policy lever*.
> 5. **Writing the energy efficiency gap definition incorrectly** — it is the gap between *actual* and *cost-optimal*, not between *actual* and *engineering-maximum*. The latter would be the *technology gap* and is a different concept.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — memorize before walking into the KTU exam hall.**

- ☐ **Investment Environment** = macro + financial + regulatory + fiscal + institutional conditions governing capital flow.
- ☐ **WACC** is the single-number summary of the investment environment; a *high WACC is hostile to EE*.
- ☐ **Six defining characteristics of EE investments:** (1) high capex, (2) long payback, (3) dispersed savings, (4) non-energy benefits, (5) information asymmetries, (6) split incentives.
- ☐ **NPV** = $-I_0 + \sum \text{CF}_t / (1+r)^t$; **Accept if NPV $\geq$ 0**.
- ☐ **IRR** is the $r^\star$ that zeroes NPV; **Accept if IRR $\geq r$ (WACC)**.
- ☐ **CRF** = $\frac{r(1+r)^n}{(1+r)^n - 1}$; multiplies $I_0$ to give level annual payment (EAC).
- ☐ **LCCE** is the right comparator for EE projects — *not* LCOE.
- ☐ **EER** = useful output / energy input (dimensionless, used for cooling/heating equipment).
- ☐ **COP** of an efficient heat pump = 3 to 5; COP > EER because of unit conventions.
- ☐ **Energy Efficiency Gap** = (cost-optimal level) − (actual level); caused by market failures, *not* by technology limits.
- ☐ **Rebound Effect** ($\eta$) = actual savings / engineering-estimated savings; direct rebound is typically 10–30%.
- ☐ **Discounted Payback** always exceeds **Simple Payback** because the denominator grows each year.
- ☐ **Non-Energy Benefits (NEBs)** are 20–50% of total EE benefit but routinely omitted — leading to under-investment.
- ☐ **Policy portfolio** (pricing + information + finance + regulation) is required; no single instrument closes the EE gap.
- ☐ **ESCo model** is the principal private-market response to the EE investment characteristics — payment tied to M&V-verified savings.
- ☐ For KTU Module 1, expect at least one numerical on **NPV/IRR/DPP** and at least one conceptual question on the **EE Gap + market failures**.

> **End of Module 1 Notes — PEMET526 — Energy Economics and Policy — KTU 2024 Scheme.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy Supply and Demand Analysis: A Review of Energy Efficiency Measures (EEM) Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET526 - ENERGY ECONOMICS AND POLICY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Energy Supply and Demand Analysis: A Review of Energy Efficiency Measures (EEM) Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Energy Supply and Demand Analysis (Formal Definition)

**Energy Supply and Demand Analysis** is the systematic study of the quantitative balance between the availability of energy resources (supply side) and the requirements of consumers across residential, commercial, industrial, and transportation sectors (demand side) over a defined time horizon. It forms the analytical foundation of national and regional energy planning, enabling policymakers to identify gaps, forecast shortages, and design interventions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Energy supply and demand analysis involves the assessment of current and projected energy requirements, available indigenous and imported resources, conversion technologies, and the economic, environmental, and social constraints that govern the equilibrium between production and consumption.

### 1.2 Energy Efficiency Measures (EEM) — Formal Definition

An **Energy Efficiency Measure (EEM)** is any actionable intervention — technological, behavioural, operational, or policy-driven — that is implemented to reduce the energy input required to deliver a given energy service (e.g., lighting, heating, mobility, industrial process output) while maintaining or improving the quality of service and the comfort/productivity of the end-user.

> [!NOTE]
> EEMs are the cornerstone of **Demand-Side Management (DSM)**. They are evaluated not only on technical performance (kWh saved) but also on their **Cost of Conserved Energy (CCE)**, payback period, and net present value.

### 1.3 Characteristics of EEMs (The 7 Pillars)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept — The Seven Defining Characteristics of EEMs:**

1. **Energy Savings Potential (ESP):** The measurable reduction in energy consumption, typically expressed in **kWh/year** or **TOE (Tonnes of Oil Equivalent)**.
2. **Cost-Effectiveness (CCE):** The lifecycle cost per unit of energy saved, benchmarked against the prevailing tariff.
3. **Capital Intensity:** The upfront investment required per kW or kWh of capacity avoided.
4. **Payback Period:** The time required for cumulative savings to recover the initial investment.
5. **Persistence/Durability:** The fraction of first-year savings that survives over the measure's useful life.
6. **Verifiability & Measurability:** The ability to quantify savings using M&V (Measurement and Verification) protocols like **IPMVP**.
7. **Adoption Barrier Profile:** The technical, financial, institutional, and behavioural obstacles to widespread deployment.

### 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — The "Leaky Bucket" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The Leaky Bucket:**
> Think of the national energy system as a **water bucket** with a tap (supply) and several holes at the bottom (demand/demand-side wastage). Energy supply analysis examines how much water flows in from the tap (coal, hydro, solar, imported LNG). Energy demand analysis measures how fast water drains out. **EEMs are like plugging the holes** — they do not increase the water supply, but they reduce the rate of loss. The "characteristic" of each plug is how big a hole it covers, how much it costs, and how long it lasts before it wears out. In a country like India, where supply augmentation is expensive and carbon-constrained, **plugging the holes (EEMs) is often 2 to 5 times cheaper than enlarging the tap (new generation capacity)**.

### 1.5 Key Standard Metrics Used in EEM Analysis

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Energy Intensity | EI | **kWh / INR of GDP** or **TOE / million INR** |
| Specific Energy Consumption | SEC | **kWh / unit of product** |
| Energy Efficiency Ratio | EER | dimensionless |
| Coefficient of Performance | COP | dimensionless |
| Cost of Conserved Energy | CCE | **INR / kWh** |
| Energy Payback Time | EPBT | **years** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU Board Exams:** Always quote the **national energy intensity of India ≈ 0.19 kWh per INR of GDP (2022 baseline)** and the global average ≈ 0.21 kWh/USD as context anchors in any 14-mark answer.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of Energy Supply and Demand Analysis

The analysis is conventionally decomposed into four sequential logical steps:

1. **Baseline Inventory:** Quantify existing energy consumption disaggregated by sector (domestic, industrial, transport, agriculture) and by fuel type (coal, oil, gas, electricity, biomass, renewables).
2. **Demand Projection:** Apply econometric models (e.g., **end-use modelling, MAED, MARKAL, LEAP**) to forecast future energy needs as a function of GDP growth, population, urbanization, and technology penetration.
3. **Supply-Side Assessment:** Evaluate domestic reserves, import dependence, refining capacity, transmission constraints, and the merit-order dispatch stack.
4. **Gap Analysis & Intervention Design:** Compare projected demand with feasible supply to identify the supply-demand gap. EEMs are then introduced to *shrink* the demand curve, deferring capital-intensive supply investments.

### 2.2 Mathematical Framework of Energy Efficiency

The **Energy Efficiency (η)** of any system is fundamentally a ratio of useful output to energy input.

$$\eta = \frac{E_{\text{useful output}}}{E_{\text{input}}}$$

For an EEM, the **savings achieved (ΔE)** when an efficient technology replaces a baseline technology are:

$$\Delta E = E_{\text{baseline}} - E_{\text{efficient}}$$

The **percentage energy savings (%S)** is therefore:

$$\%S = \frac{\Delta E}{E_{\text{baseline}}} \times 100$$

### 2.3 The KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains the canonical formulas you must memorise for KTU University Exams. Render the absolute value bars using `\vert` to protect the markdown table syntax.**

| Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Variables & Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Energy Intensity | $EI = \dfrac{E_{\text{total}}}{GDP}$ | E in TOE, GDP in million INR | Cross-country comparison of efficiency |
| Specific Energy Consumption | $SEC = \dfrac{E_{\text{consumed}}}{M_{\text{production}}}$ | E in kWh, M in tonnes/units | Industrial benchmarking |
| Cost of Conserved Energy | $CCE = \dfrac{I \cdot CRF}{\Delta E_{\text{annual}}}$ | I in INR, $\Delta E$ in kWh/yr | Comparing EEMs with grid tariff |
| Capital Recovery Factor | $CRF = \dfrac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n}-1}$ | i = discount rate, n = life in years | Converts capex to annualised cost |
| Simple Payback Period | $SPP = \dfrac{I}{C_{\text{annual savings}}}$ | I in INR, C in INR/yr | Quick financial screening |
| Energy Return on Investment | $EROI = \dfrac{E_{\text{delivered}}}{E_{\text{invested}}}$ | Both in kWh or MJ | Resource quality assessment |
| Rebound Effect (Khazzoom) | $\eta_{\text{eff}} = \dfrac{\%S}{1 - \eta_{\text{rebound}}}$ | dimensionless | Adjusts gross savings for behavioural response |
| Net Present Value of EEM | $NPV = -I + \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{S_{t}}{(1+i)^{t}}$ | All in INR | Investment ranking |
| Levelized Cost of Energy | $LCOE = \dfrac{\sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{I_{t}+O_{t}}{(1+i)^{t}}}{\sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{E_{t}}{(1+i)^{t}}}$ | INR / kWh | Comparing supply vs. efficiency |
| Avoided Capacity Factor | $ACF = \dfrac{\Delta E_{\text{annual}}}{8760 \cdot CF}$ | kW | Demand-side contribution to peak avoidance |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** In any 14-mark question, the marker expects you to write the formula, *substitute the values*, and arrive at a numerical answer. A correct formula written without substitution often attracts only 2 of 7 marks.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility of EEM Characterisation

The characterisation of EEMs is not academic — it directly drives policy and investment decisions:

- **Utility Companies (e.g., BEE, EESL in India):** Use CCE to screen which EEMs qualify for the **Perform, Achieve, Trade (PAT)** scheme.
- **Industrial Facility Managers:** Use SEC to identify the "best available technology" (BAT) for compliance with the **Energy Conservation Act, 2001**.
- **Project Financiers:** Use NPV and IRR to determine bankability of energy service company (ESCO) projects.
- **Planners:** Use EROI to rank fuels by their net contribution to society.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Connection:** India's **Ujwal DISCOM Assurance Yojana (UDAY)** and the **Saubhagya scheme** explicitly relied on EEM characterisation to estimate the technical & commercial (AT&C) losses that could be reduced. Similarly, the **ECBC (Energy Conservation Building Code)** mandates EEM characterisation for all commercial buildings > 100 kW connected load.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Cost of Conserved Energy (CCE)

The CCE is the most critical metric in EEM economics. It represents the annualised cost of an EEM per unit of annual energy saved. The derivation proceeds in five explicit stages.

**Stage 1 — Start with the Capital Investment (I)**
A luminaire replacement in an industrial plant costs an upfront amount $I$ in INR.

**Stage 2 — Convert Lumpy Capital to an Equivalent Annual Cost**
A one-time investment of $I$ spread over the asset's life of $n$ years at a discount rate $i$ is converted to an equivalent annual cost $A$ using the **Capital Recovery Factor (CRF)**.

$$A = I \cdot CRF$$

**Stage 3 — Derive the CRF**
Consider a uniform annual series $A$ paid at the end of each year for $n$ years. Its present value must equal $I$.

$$I = \frac{A}{(1+i)} + \frac{A}{(1+i)^{2}} + \frac{A}{(1+i)^{3}} + \cdots + \frac{A}{(1+i)^{n}}$$

This is a geometric progression with common ratio $r = \dfrac{1}{1+i}$. Summing the GP:

$$I = A \cdot \frac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i}$$

Inverting to solve for $A$:

$$A = I \cdot \frac{i}{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $(1+i)^{n}$ yields the standard CRF form:

$$CRF = \frac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}$$

**Stage 4 — Define Annual Energy Savings**
Let $\Delta E_{\text{annual}}$ be the energy saved per year in kWh. For a lighting retrofit, this equals:

$$\Delta E_{\text{annual}} = (P_{\text{old}} - P_{\text{new}}) \cdot N \cdot h \cdot \eta_{\text{utilisation}}$$

where $P$ is lamp wattage, $N$ is the number of luminaires, $h$ is annual operating hours, and $\eta_{\text{utilisation}}$ accounts for occupancy / daylight harvesting.

**Stage 5 — Final CCE Expression**

$$CCE = \frac{I \cdot CRF}{\Delta E_{\text{annual}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Decision Rule:** If $CCE < \text{Electricity Tariff}$, the EEM is economically justified. If $CCE > \text{Tariff}$, the measure fails the cost-effectiveness test even if technically feasible.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Step-by-Step Evaluation)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A factory replaces 500 incandescent bulbs (60 W each) with LED bulbs (12 W each). Bulb cost is INR 350 each for LED vs. INR 25 for incandescent. Annual operating hours = 4000. Discount rate = 10%. Equipment life = 10 years. Electricity tariff = INR 7/kWh. Should the investment proceed?

**Step 1 — Compute Power Saving per Luminaire**

$$\Delta P = 60 - 12 = 48 \text{ W}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Annual Energy Savings**

$$\Delta E = 48 \text{ W} \times 500 \times 4000 \text{ h} = 96{,}000{,}000 \text{ Wh} = 96{,}000 \text{ kWh/yr}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Capital Investment**

$$I = 500 \times (350 - 25) = 500 \times 325 = \text{INR } 1{,}62{,}500$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Capital Recovery Factor**

$$CRF = \frac{0.10 \times (1.10)^{10}}{(1.10)^{10} - 1}$$

Evaluating the numerator and denominator:

$$CRF = \frac{0.10 \times 2.5937}{2.5937 - 1} = \frac{0.25937}{1.5937} = 0.16275$$

**Step 5 — Compute the Annualised Cost**

$$A = 1{,}62{,}500 \times 0.16275 = \text{INR } 26{,}447 \text{ per year}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the CCE**

$$CCE = \frac{26{,}447}{96{,}000} = \text{INR } 0.275 \text{ per kWh}$$

**Step 7 — Decision**

Since $CCE = \text{INR } 0.275 / \text{kWh} \ll \text{Tariff of INR } 7 / \text{kWh}$, the investment is **highly justified** and yields a payback period of:

$$SPP = \frac{1{,}62{,}500}{96{,}000 \times 7} = \frac{1{,}62{,}500}{6{,}72{,}000} \approx 0.24 \text{ years} \approx 88 \text{ days}$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing EEM Categories

| EEM Category | Typical % Saving | Typical CCE (INR/kWh) | Typical Payback | Adoption Barrier |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| LED Lighting Retrofit | 60 – 80% | 0.20 – 0.50 | < 1 year | Low (high awareness) |
| Variable Frequency Drives (VFD) | 20 – 50% | 1.50 – 3.00 | 1 – 3 years | Medium (technical skill) |
| HVAC Optimisation | 15 – 30% | 2.00 – 4.00 | 2 – 4 years | Medium-High |
| Industrial Waste-Heat Recovery | 10 – 25% | 1.00 – 2.50 | 2 – 5 years | High (integration cost) |
| Building Envelope Insulation | 20 – 40% | 2.50 – 5.00 | 4 – 7 years | High (retrofit disruption) |
| Smart Metering & DSM | 5 – 15% | 1.00 – 2.00 | 2 – 4 years | High (consumer behaviour) |
| Cogeneration / Trigeneration | 15 – 30% | 2.00 – 3.50 | 3 – 6 years | Very High (capex) |

### 3.4 Derivation of Net Present Value (NPV) for EEM Selection

For an EEM with investment $I$ at $t=0$ and annual savings $S$ over $n$ years:

$$NPV = -I + \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{S}{(1+i)^{t}}$$

The summation of the geometric series $\sum_{t=1}^{n} (1+i)^{-t} = \dfrac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i}$ gives the **Present Worth Factor (PWF)**:

$$PWF = \frac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i}$$

Therefore:

$$NPV = -I + S \cdot PWF$$

If $NPV > 0$, the EEM is **accepted**; if $NPV < 0$, the EEM is **rejected**. When ranking mutually exclusive EEMs, select the one with the highest positive NPV.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU 14-mark Questions:** Always show the formula, the substitution of numerical values, and the final numerical result. Examiners allocate 2–3 marks for the conclusion statement. Never end with "therefore it is economical" without the CCE comparison or NPV sign.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Energy Supply–Demand Analysis — Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid block represents the conceptual flow of an energy supply–demand study, showing how EEMs are integrated as a *demand-modifying* input into the supply-side planning loop.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Baseline Energy Inventory] --> B[Sectoral Disaggregation]
    B --> C1[Domestic Sector]
    B --> C2[Industrial Sector]
    B --> C3[Transport Sector]
    B --> C4[Agriculture Sector]

    C1 --> D[Demand Projection Model]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D

    D --> E[Forecasted Energy Demand Curve]
    E --> F[Supply-Side Resource Assessment]
    F --> F1[Domestic Reserves]
    F --> F2[Import Availability]
    F --> F3[Renewable Capacity]

    F1 --> G[Supply-Demand Gap]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G

    G --> H{Gap Significant?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Intervention Design Phase]
    H -- No --> J[Monitor and Review]

    I --> K[Supply-Side Option: New Capacity]
    I --> L[Demand-Side Option: EEM Deployment]
    K --> M[Cost-Benefit Comparison]
    L --> M
    M --> N{CCE Less than Tariff?}
    N -- Yes --> O[Implement EEM]
    N -- No --> P[Implement Supply Option]

    O --> Q[Verify Savings via M&V]
    Q --> R[Update Baseline]
    R --> A
```

### 4.2 Classification of Energy Efficiency Measures

This diagram presents the taxonomy of EEMs, organised by the **type of intervention** rather than by sector. This is the framework examiners expect students to reproduce.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    EEM[Energy Efficiency Measures]

    EEM --> T1[Technical Measures]
    EEM --> T2[Operational Measures]
    EEM --> T3[Behavioural Measures]
    EEM --> T4[Policy and Regulatory Measures]

    T1 --> S1[Equipment Upgradation]
    T1 --> S2[Process Modification]
    T1 --> S3[Waste Heat Recovery]
    T1 --> S4[Building Envelope Improvement]

    T2 --> O1[Load Scheduling]
    T2 --> O2[Maintenance Practices]
    T2 --> O3[Power Factor Correction]

    T3 --> B1[Awareness Campaigns]
    T3 --> B2[Feedback and Display Systems]
    T3 --> B3[Incentive Structures]

    T4 --> P1[Standards and Labelling]
    T4 --> P2[Building Codes]
    T4 --> P3[Tax Incentives]
    T4 --> P4[White Certificates]
```

### 4.3 EEM Evaluation Decision Matrix

The block below shows the sequential screening logic an energy auditor applies when prioritising EEMs within a single facility.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ST1[Step 1: Energy Audit] --> ST2[Step 2: Identify EEM Candidates]
    ST2 --> ST3[Step 3: Screen by Technical Feasibility]
    ST3 --> ST4[Step 4: Quantify Energy Savings]
    ST4 --> ST5[Step 5: Estimate Capital Cost]
    ST5 --> ST6[Step 6: Compute Simple Payback]
    ST6 --> ST7{Payback less than 3 years?}
    ST7 -- Yes --> ST8[Step 7: Detailed Financial Analysis NPV and IRR]
    ST7 -- No --> ST9[Re-evaluate or Defer]
    ST8 --> ST10{NPV greater than 0?}
    ST10 -- Yes --> ST11[Step 8: Implementation]
    ST10 -- No --> ST9
    ST11 --> ST12[Step 9: Measurement and Verification]
    ST12 --> ST13[Step 10: Reporting and Feedback]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*Define the term "Cost of Conserved Energy" (CCE). Why is it considered a superior metric compared to the simple payback period for evaluating EEMs?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The **Cost of Conserved Energy (CCE)** is defined as the annualised cost of an energy conservation measure per unit of annual energy saved. Its mathematical form is $CCE = \dfrac{I \cdot CRF}{\Delta E_{\text{annual}}}$, expressed in INR/kWh. (1 mark)

Unlike the **Simple Payback Period**, which ignores the time value of money, the asset's useful life, and ongoing operating costs, the CCE incorporates all of these via the **Capital Recovery Factor (CRF)**. (1 mark)

It is superior because it provides a *common denominator* (INR/kWh) that can be directly compared with the prevailing electricity tariff. If $CCE < \text{tariff}$, the EEM is economically justified regardless of sector or technology. (1 mark)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*List any six defining characteristics of Energy Efficiency Measures (EEMs) and briefly explain the concept of "persistence" of savings.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The six characteristics are: (i) energy savings potential, (ii) cost-effectiveness, (iii) capital intensity, (iv) payback period, (v) verifiability, and (vi) adoption barrier profile. (2 marks)

**Persistence** refers to the fraction of first-year energy savings that is sustained over the entire useful life of the EEM. For example, a lighting retrofit may achieve 100% savings in year 1, but degradation of luminaire output and dirt accumulation may reduce the actual savings to 80% by year 5 — a persistence factor of 0.80. This is critical in M&V calculations. (1 mark)

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Regulation 2024:** Part B questions of 14 marks carry a *compulsory internal choice*. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each part typically carries a 7 + 7 split.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the four-stage methodology of energy supply and demand analysis. Discuss the role of EEMs in closing the supply–demand gap. (7 marks)

**(b)** An industrial fan operates at full speed for 6000 hours per year and consumes 22 kW. A Variable Frequency Drive (VFD) is proposed that will reduce the average power consumption to 14 kW (a 36% reduction) by trimming fan speed during low-load periods. The VFD costs INR 4,50,000 including installation, has a useful life of 12 years, and the discount rate is 9%. The electricity tariff is INR 6.50/kWh. Calculate the CCE, the simple payback period, and the NPV. Comment on the economic viability. (7 marks)

### MODEL SOLUTION — QUESTION A

**Part (a) — Methodology and Role of EEMs (7 marks)**

The four-stage methodology of energy supply and demand analysis is as follows:

**Stage 1 — Baseline Energy Inventory:** Conduct a sector-wise and fuel-wise audit of current energy consumption. In India, this is institutionalised by the **Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE)** through the **Energy Statistics** publication of the **Ministry of Statistics and Programme Implementation (MoSPI)**. (1 mark)

**Stage 2 — Demand Projection:** Forecast future demand using econometric models such as **MAED (Model for Analysis of Energy Demand)** or **MARKAL (Market Allocation)**. The key drivers are GDP growth (elasticity ≈ 0.7–0.8 for India), population, and structural change. (1 mark)

**Stage 3 — Supply-Side Assessment:** Map domestic reserves (coal bed methane, uranium, hydropower), import contracts (LNG, crude), and renewable potential. The CEA (Central Electricity Authority) publishes the **National Electricity Plan** for this purpose. (1 mark)

**Stage 4 — Gap Analysis & Intervention Design:** Compare the demand forecast with the feasible supply curve. The residual is the **supply–demand gap**. (1 mark)

**Role of EEMs in Gap Closing:** EEMs act on the *demand side* of the equation. By reducing wasteful end-use consumption, they shift the demand curve leftward, effectively *deferring* or *eliminating* the need for new generating capacity. The avoided capacity has a value equal to the **levelised cost of new supply (typically INR 4–6/kWh)**. (2 marks)

**Block diagram (text version):**
> Baseline Inventory → Demand Projection → Supply Assessment → Gap Identification → EEM Deployment / New Capacity

**Closing remark:** A combined supply-side and demand-side optimisation is always cheaper than supply-side augmentation alone — a principle validated by the IEA's *"Energy Efficiency 2023"* report which finds EEMs deliver energy services at one-third to one-half the cost of new supply. (1 mark)

**Part (b) — Numerical Evaluation (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Annual Energy Savings**

$$\Delta E = (22 - 14) \text{ kW} \times 6000 \text{ h} = 48{,}000 \text{ kWh/year}$$

**Step 2 — Annual Monetary Savings**

$$\text{Savings} = 48{,}000 \times 6.50 = \text{INR } 3{,}12{,}000/\text{year}$$

**Step 3 — Capital Recovery Factor**

$$CRF = \frac{0.09 \times (1.09)^{12}}{(1.09)^{12} - 1}$$

$$(1.09)^{12} \approx 2.8127$$

$$CRF = \frac{0.09 \times 2.8127}{2.8127 - 1} = \frac{0.2531}{1.8127} \approx 0.13965$$

**Step 4 — Annualised Cost**

$$A = 4{,}50{,}000 \times 0.13965 = \text{INR } 62{,}843/\text{year}$$

**Step 5 — Cost of Conserved Energy**

$$CCE = \frac{62{,}843}{48{,}000} \approx \text{INR } 1.31/\text{kWh}$$

**Step 6 — Simple Payback Period**

$$SPP = \frac{4{,}50{,}000}{3{,}12{,}000} \approx 1.44 \text{ years}$$

**Step 7 — Net Present Value**

$$PWF = \frac{1 - (1.09)^{-12}}{0.09} = \frac{1 - 0.3555}{0.09} = \frac{0.6445}{0.09} \approx 7.161$$

$$NPV = -4{,}50{,}000 + 3{,}12{,}000 \times 7.161$$

$$NPV = -4{,}50{,}000 + 22{,}34{,}232 = \text{INR } 17{,}84{,}232$$

**Step 8 — Economic Verdict (1 mark)**

| Metric | Value | Benchmark | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| CCE | INR 1.31/kWh | < INR 6.50/kWh | **Strongly justified** |
| Payback | 1.44 years | < 3 years | **Strongly justified** |
| NPV | INR 17.84 Lakh | > 0 | **Strongly justified** |

All three metrics indicate the VFD installation is a high-priority EEM and should be implemented.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]

**(a)** "EEMs are best understood through their seven defining characteristics." Discuss each characteristic with an industrial example. (7 marks)

**(b)** A diesel generator set rated 250 kVA operates at an average load factor of 40% (i.e., 100 kW) for 3000 hours per year. A power factor correction (PFC) scheme is proposed to improve the power factor from 0.75 lag to 0.95 lag. The scheme costs INR 1,80,000, has a life of 8 years, and the discount rate is 11%. The energy charge is INR 7.20/kWh and the kVA demand charge is INR 450/kVA/month. Calculate: (i) reduction in apparent power, (ii) annual kWh savings due to reduced line losses (assume line losses are 4% of input kWh at 0.75 pf), (iii) annual demand charge savings, and (iv) payback period. (7 marks)

### MODEL SOLUTION — QUESTION B

**Part (a) — Seven Characteristics of EEMs with Industrial Examples (7 marks)**

1. **Energy Savings Potential (1 mark):** A 250 kVA induction motor replaced by a **IE4 premium-efficiency motor** delivers a savings of 3–5% of input energy. For a 5000-hour operating year at 200 kW load, this equals 30,000–50,000 kWh/year.

2. **Cost-Effectiveness (1 mark):** The IE4 motor has a CCE typically in the range of INR 1.50–2.50/kWh, well below industrial tariffs, making it universally cost-effective.

3. **Capital Intensity (1 mark):** The IE4 motor costs 15–25% more than a standard IE1 motor, representing moderate capital intensity with a quick payback of 1–2 years.

4. **Payback Period (1 mark):** For the same motor example, simple payback is typically 1.2–1.8 years, well within the 3-year "no-brainer" threshold used by most Indian industrial energy managers.

5. **Persistence (1 mark):** Premium-efficiency motors maintain > 95% of their nameplate efficiency over 15–20 years. Persistence factor ≈ 0.95.

6. **Verifiability & Measurability (1 mark):** Motor efficiency can be verified using **IPMVP Option B (retrofit isolation)** by measuring input kW and output kW using a power analyser before and after replacement.

7. **Adoption Barrier Profile (1 mark):** The principal barriers are: (a) lack of awareness among small-scale industries, (b) higher upfront cost despite favourable lifecycle economics, and (c) fragmented market with limited availability of premium motors in smaller ratings (< 5 kW).

**Part (b) — Numerical Evaluation of PFC Scheme (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Initial Apparent Power at 0.75 pf**

$$S_{1} = \frac{P}{pf_{1}} = \frac{100}{0.75} = 133.33 \text{ kVA}$$

**Step 2 — Apparent Power After Correction at 0.95 pf**

$$S_{2} = \frac{100}{0.95} = 105.26 \text{ kVA}$$

**Step 3 — Reduction in Apparent Power**

$$\Delta S = 133.33 - 105.26 = 28.07 \text{ kVA}$$

**Step 4 — Annual Demand Charge Savings**

$$\text{Demand Savings} = 28.07 \text{ kVA} \times 450 \times 12 = \text{INR } 1{,}51{,}578/\text{year}$$

**Step 5 — Line Losses at 0.75 pf**

$$E_{\text{losses}} = 0.04 \times 100 \times 3000 = 12{,}000 \text{ kWh/year}$$

These losses are proportional to $I^{2}R$ and hence to $S^{2}$ at constant active power. The ratio of new to old losses:

$$\frac{L_{2}}{L_{1}} = \left(\frac{pf_{1}}{pf_{2}}\right)^{2} = \left(\frac{0.75}{0.95}\right)^{2} = 0.6231$$

$$L_{2} = 12{,}000 \times 0.6231 = 7477 \text{ kWh/year}$$

**Step 6 — Annual kWh Savings**

$$\Delta E_{\text{loss}} = 12{,}000 - 7477 = 4523 \text{ kWh/year}$$

**Step 7 — Annual Energy Cost Savings**

$$\text{Energy Savings} = 4523 \times 7.20 = \text{INR } 32{,}566/\text{year}$$

**Step 8 — Total Annual Savings**

$$\text{Total} = 1{,}51{,}578 + 32{,}566 = \text{INR } 1{,}84{,}144/\text{year}$$

**Step 9 — Simple Payback Period**

$$SPP = \frac{1{,}80{,}000}{1{,}84{,}144} \approx 0.98 \text{ years} \approx 12 \text{ months}$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The PFC scheme has a payback period of less than 1 year, making it an extremely attractive EEM. The bulk of the savings (≈ 82%) comes from avoided demand charges, demonstrating that power factor correction is primarily a *capacity-management* measure rather than an energy-saving measure.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing kW and kVA:** Students often compute kW savings instead of kVA savings for the demand charge component. The demand charge is on **kVA**, not kW. Losing 1 mark here is extremely common.
> 2. **Forgetting the line-loss factor:** The $I^{2}R$ dependence on $(1/pf)^{2}$ is often missed. Examiners allocate 1 mark specifically for this derivation.
> 3. **Not stating the decision rule:** Always conclude with a clear statement — *"The scheme is recommended as the payback of 0.98 years is well within the 3-year acceptable threshold."*
> 4. **Skipping units:** Writing "SPP = 1" without "year" attracts a 0.5-mark penalty under the KTU 2024 marking scheme.
> 5. **PFC misconception:** Some students believe PFC reduces *active* energy consumption. It does not — it reduces *apparent* power and the *losses* associated with it. Marks are deducted for conflating the two.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following points are the high-density summary of everything examinable on this topic. Memorise these for guaranteed marks.**

- Energy supply-demand analysis is a **four-stage process**: baseline inventory → demand projection → supply assessment → gap analysis.
- **EEMs modify the demand curve**; they are cheaper than supply-side expansion in most Indian contexts.
- The **seven characteristics of EEMs** are: savings potential, cost-effectiveness, capital intensity, payback, persistence, verifiability, and adoption barriers.
- The **Cost of Conserved Energy (CCE)** = $\dfrac{I \cdot CRF}{\Delta E_{\text{annual}}}$ is the canonical metric for EEM economics.
- **CRF** = $\dfrac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n}-1}$ converts capital cost to an annualised stream.
- **Decision rule:** Accept EEM if **CCE < electricity tariff** *and* **NPV > 0** *and* **payback < 3 years** (rule of thumb in Indian industry).
- **EEM categories:** Technical, Operational, Behavioural, Policy/Regulatory.
- **Industrial EEM examples:** Premium-efficiency motors, VFDs, PFC, waste-heat recovery, HVAC optimisation, cogeneration.
- **Rebound effect (Khazzoom-Brookes):** Effective savings are less than gross savings due to induced demand — remember to discount by 5–20% in long-horizon projections.
- **India-specific anchors:** BEE (Bureau of Energy Efficiency), PAT scheme, ECBC, Energy Conservation Act 2001, Perform Achieve Trade, UJALA LED programme.
- **Persistence factor** is critical in M&V — never assume 100% persistence over the asset life.
- For the **NPV computation** of an EEM, always use $PWF = \dfrac{1-(1+i)^{-n}}{i}$ and present the final sign of NPV explicitly.
- **India's energy intensity** is approximately **0.19 kWh per INR of GDP** — use this as a benchmark in comparative answers.
- **EROI** for modern renewable systems is typically 5–20; for fossil fuels historically 30–80. Always specify the system boundary.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Adoption and Measures of EEM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET526 - ENERGY ECONOMICS AND POLICY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Adoption and Measures of EEM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Energy Efficiency (EE)

**Energy Efficiency (EE)** is formally defined as the ratio of useful energy output (or service delivered) to the energy input consumed. In thermodynamic terms, it is the ratio of minimum thermodynamic energy required to perform a task to the actual energy consumed.

$$\eta_{EE} = \frac{\text{Useful Energy Output / Service Delivered}}{\text{Energy Input}} \le 1$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Energy Efficiency is the *first* thermodynamic law in socio-economic practice — using less energy to provide the same service or product. It is a **"hidden fuel"** because the most sustainable and cheapest energy is the energy you do NOT use.

## 1.2 Definition of Energy Efficiency Measures (EEM)

**Energy Efficiency Measures (EEM)** are defined as a portfolio of **technological, operational, managerial, and behavioural interventions** designed and implemented to reduce the energy input required to deliver a given level of energy service, output, or comfort.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** "EEMs are specific, identifiable actions — whether capital-intensive (retrofit) or no-cost/low-cost (housekeeping) — whose adoption alters the energy intensity profile of an economic unit (firm, sector, or nation) without sacrificing the quantity or quality of energy services rendered."

## 1.3 Definition of "Adoption" of EEM

**Adoption** of EEM refers to the **sequential decision-making and diffusion process** through which a potential user (household, industry, or government) moves from initial awareness of an EEM technology/practice, through evaluation, trial, and finally to its **sustained implementation and routinisation** within the existing socio-technical system.

> [!TIP]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Plain English):** Think of EEM adoption like **switching from a regular shower to a low-flow showerhead**. The *measure* is the showerhead. *Adoption* is the whole journey: you see an ad (awareness), you compare flow-rates (evaluation), you buy and install it (trial), and after a month you forget it's even there (routinisation). Engineers call this the **"Diffusion of Innovation"** curve.

## 1.4 Why Adoption is Studied Separately from Measures

A technically superior EEM can fail in the market if adoption barriers dominate. The **adoption gap** is the difference between the *thermodynamically achievable* energy savings and the *actually realised* savings at the economy-wide scale. The standard global figure for this gap is **20% to 30%** of the technical potential.

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Distinction for KTU Exams:** Do NOT confuse the *technical potential* of an EEM with its *adoption potential*. Adoption incorporates **economics, behaviour, financing, and policy** — not just physics.

## 1.5 The Adoption Spectrum

Adoption of EEMs typically occurs along a **three-tier hierarchy**:

| Tier | Type of EEM | Typical Payback | Adoption Driver |
|------|-------------|------------------|-----------------|
| **Tier 1** | Housekeeping / No-Cost / Low-Cost (TPM, lighting switch-off) | < 1 year | Awareness, Behavioural |
| **Tier 2** | Retrofitting / Medium-Investment (VFD, insulation) | 1–5 years | Economic, Subsidies |
| **Tier 3** | Capital-intensive / Systemic (cogeneration, building re-design) | > 5 years | Policy, Long-term Finance |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Adoption Process — Structured Logic Steps

The adoption of an EEM is best modelled as a **five-stage sequential process** (Rogers' Diffusion of Innovation, adapted for energy engineering):

1. **Stage 1 — Awareness & Knowledge:** The decision-maker learns that an EEM exists and understands its function. Triggered by energy audit, policy mandate, peer learning, or vendor outreach.
2. **Stage 2 — Persuasion & Information Gathering:** The potential adopter collects technical specs, case studies, and pricing. Compares alternatives. Here, *information asymmetry* often delays adoption.
3. **Stage 3 — Decision (Economic Evaluation):** The adopter runs a financial appraisal:
   - **LCCE** (Levelized Cost of Conserved Energy)
   - **NPV**, **IRR**, **Simple Payback**
   - **Risk-adjusted discount rate**
4. **Stage 4 — Implementation & Confirmation:** The EEM is installed. *Monitoring & Verification (M&V)* per **IPMVP** protocol is used to confirm actual savings match predicted savings.
5. **Stage 5 — Routinisation / Scaling:** The EEM becomes standard practice. The firm/country moves up the **energy learning curve**, often leading to *systemic* (Tier 3) EEMs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter in KTU exams?** Because questions on "barriers to adoption" usually map to one of these five stages. *Financial barrier* = Stage 3; *Information barrier* = Stage 2; *Behavioural barrier* = Stage 1; *Organisational barrier* = Stage 4.

## 2.2 The Adoption Matrix — Drivers vs. Barriers

| Dimension | Drivers (Push/Pull) | Barriers (Friction) |
|-----------|----------------------|----------------------|
| **Economic** | High energy prices, low capital cost, ESCO models | High upfront CapEx, long payback, credit constraints |
| **Technical** | Mature technology, vendor support, standards | Performance risk, intermittency, integration complexity |
| **Informational** | Energy audits, labels (BEE Star), M&V | Information asymmetry, hidden costs, "split incentive" |
| **Organisational** | Energy Manager (BEE-certified), ISO 50001 | Lack of management commitment, small-firm fragmentation |
| **Behavioural** | Environmental values, social norms | Status-quo bias, principal–agent problem |
| **Policy** | PAT, Perform-Achieve-Trade, subsidies, taxes | Inconsistent regulations, weak enforcement |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** Memorise the formulas in **column 1 and column 2**. The MCF column is what examiners expect for full marks.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables / Meaning | Unit |
|---|----------|---------|---------------------|------|
| 1 | **Energy Intensity (EI)** | $EI = \dfrac{E}{GDP}$ | $E$ = total energy consumed; $GDP$ = gross domestic product | Toe / million \$ or MJ/\$ |
| 2 | **Energy Intensity (Sectoral)** | $EI_{sec} = \dfrac{E_{sec}}{O_{sec}}$ | $O_{sec}$ = sectoral output (₹ or tonnes) | Toe / unit output |
| 3 | **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** | $SEC = \dfrac{E_{in}}{P_{out}}$ | $E_{in}$ = energy input; $P_{out}$ = physical production | kWh / tonne (or kWh/unit) |
| 4 | **Energy Productivity (EP)** | $EP = \dfrac{1}{EI} = \dfrac{GDP}{E}$ | Inverse of EI | \$ / Toe |
| 5 | **% Energy Savings** | $\%S = \dfrac{E_{b}-E_{a}}{E_{b}} \times 100$ | $E_{b}$ = baseline; $E_{a}$ = actual post-EEM | % |
| 6 | **Energy Performance Indicator (EnPI)** | $EnPI = \dfrac{E_{a}}{E_{b}}$ | Ratio; $< 1$ means improvement | Dimensionless |
| 7 | **Cost of Conserved Energy (CCE)** | $CCE = \dfrac{I \cdot CRF + OM}{E_{saved}}$ | $I$ = investment; $CRF$ = capital recovery factor; $OM$ = O\&M; $E_{saved}$ = annual savings | ₹ / kWh saved |
| 8 | **Capital Recovery Factor** | $CRF = \dfrac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n}-1}$ | $i$ = discount rate; $n$ = life years | year$^{-1}$ |
| 9 | **Kaya Identity** | $CO_{2} = P \cdot \dfrac{GDP}{P} \cdot \dfrac{E}{GDP} \cdot \dfrac{CO_{2}}{E}$ | $P$ = population; rest as defined | Mt CO$_2$ |
| 10 | **Avoided Emissions** | $E_{CO_{2},a} = E_{saved} \times EF_{grid}$ | $EF_{grid}$ = grid emission factor (kg CO$_2$/kWh) | tonnes CO$_2$ |
| 11 | **Rebound Effect** | $R = \dfrac{\text{Actual extra consumption}}{\text{Expected extra consumption}} \times 100$ | $>0$ → partial rebound; $>100$ → backfire | % |
| 12 | **ODYSSEE Efficiency Index (ODEX)** | $ODEX = \dfrac{E_{actual}}{E_{reference}}$ | $< 1$ means efficiency improved; sectoral composite | Index |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **Policymakers** use EI to benchmark countries (e.g., India's EI is ~0.06 kgoe/\$, global average ~0.19).
- **Industries** use SEC to identify inefficient sub-sectors (e.g., SEC of Indian cement = 60–80 kWh/tonne cement).
- **Banks / ESCOs** use CCE to compare EE projects with supply-side generation.
- **Climate negotiations** use the **Kaya Identity** to disaggregate emissions into population, affluence, intensity, and carbonisation.
- **IPMVP-based M&V** uses the EnPI to determine whether a vendor's savings claim is genuine.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Kaya Identity (a KTU Favourite)

**Step 1 — Start with Total CO$_2$ Emissions of a Nation.**

Let $F$ denote total fossil-fuel CO$_2$ emissions (tonnes CO$_2$ / year). The simplest identity is:

$$F = \frac{F}{E} \cdot \frac{E}{GDP} \cdot \frac{GDP}{P} \cdot P$$

**Step 2 — Identify each factor's name.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= \text{Total CO}_2 \text{ emissions (MtCO}_2\text{)} \\
\frac{F}{E} &= \text{Carbon intensity of energy (tCO}_2\text{/toe)} \\
\frac{E}{GDP} &= \text{Energy intensity of GDP (toe/\$)} \\
\frac{GDP}{P} &= \text{GDP per capita, i.e. affluence (\$/person)} \\
P &= \text{Population (persons)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Write the multiplicative Kaya form (Yoichi Kaya, 1990, IPCC AR4).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= P \times \left(\frac{GDP}{P}\right) \times \left(\frac{E}{GDP}\right) \times \left(\frac{F}{E}\right) \\
  &= P \cdot g \cdot e \cdot c
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to percentage growth (logarithmic differentiation).**

Take natural log of both sides: $\ln F = \ln P + \ln g + \ln e + \ln c$.

Differentiate with respect to time:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{F}\frac{dF}{dt} &= \frac{1}{P}\frac{dP}{dt} + \frac{1}{g}\frac{dg}{dt} + \frac{1}{e}\frac{de}{dt} + \frac{1}{c}\frac{dc}{dt}
\end{aligned}
$$

Thus **the % change in emissions equals the sum of % changes in population, affluence, intensity, and carbonisation.** This decomposition is exactly what KTU examiners expect when the Kaya identity is asked.

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** EEM adoption reduces $e = E/GDP$ (the intensity term). If $e$ falls faster than $g$ rises, total $F$ can fall — this is *absolute decoupling* and is the target of all EEM policies.

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — % Energy Savings & Cost of Conserved Energy

**Problem Statement (Modelled on KTU ESE style):**

> A steel plant consumed **48,000 MWh** in 2020. After retrofitting a Waste Heat Recovery (WHR) system in 2021, consumption dropped to **39,600 MWh**. The WHR system cost **₹6.0 crore** and has an annual O&M cost of **₹25 lakh**. The life of the system is **15 years**; the discount rate is **10%** per annum. Calculate:
> (i) Annual energy savings in MWh
> (ii) % Energy savings
> (iii) Capital Recovery Factor
> (iv) Cost of Conserved Energy (CCE)

### Solution

**Step 1 — Annual Energy Savings (MWh).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{saved} &= E_{baseline} - E_{actual} \\
         &= 48{,}000 - 39{,}600 \\
         &= 8{,}400 \text{ MWh / year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — % Energy Savings.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%S &= \frac{8{,}400}{48{,}000} \times 100 \\
    &= 17.5\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Capital Recovery Factor (CRF) at $i=0.10$, $n=15$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CRF &= \frac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n}-1} \\
     &= \frac{0.10 \times (1.10)^{15}}{(1.10)^{15}-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate $(1.10)^{15}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(1.10)^{15} &= 1.10^{15} \\
            &\approx 4.1772 \quad \text{(rounded to 4 decimal places)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Plug back into CRF formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CRF &= \frac{0.10 \times 4.1772}{4.1772 - 1} \\
    &= \frac{0.41772}{3.1772} \\
    &\approx 0.1315 \text{ year}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Cost of Conserved Energy (CCE).**

Convert all monetary values to the same unit: ₹ 6.0 crore = ₹ 6,00,00,000; ₹ 25 lakh = ₹ 25,00,000. Savings = 8,400 MWh = 8,400,000 kWh.

$$
\begin{aligned}
CCE &= \frac{I \times CRF + OM}{E_{saved}} \\
    &= \frac{6{,}00{,}00{,}000 \times 0.1315 + 25{,}00{,}000}{8{,}400{,}000} \\
    &= \frac{78{,}90{,}000 + 25{,}00{,}000}{8{,}400{,}000} \\
    &= \frac{1{,}03{,}90{,}000}{8{,}400{,}000} \\
    &= 12.37 \text{ ₹ / kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Decision Rule:** Compare CCE with the **grid tariff** (₹/kWh). If CCE < grid tariff, the EEM is *economically justified*. Here CCE = ₹12.37/kWh is *above* the typical industrial tariff (~₹7–9/kWh) → the EEM is justified **only with carbon credits, ESCO financing, or policy subsidy**. This is exactly the kind of "adoption discussion" KTU examiners reward.

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Energy Intensity & Energy Productivity

**Problem Statement:**

> Country A consumed **1,000 Mtoe** of energy in 2015 and had a GDP of **₹250 lakh crore**. After a 5-year EEM policy, energy consumption became **1,050 Mtoe** and GDP grew to **₹320 lakh crore**. Compute (a) Initial and final EI, (b) Initial and final Energy Productivity, (c) Comment on decoupling.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Initial Energy Intensity.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EI_{2015} &= \frac{1{,}000 \text{ Mtoe}}{250 \text{ lakh crore}} = 4.00 \text{ Mtoe / lakh crore}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Final Energy Intensity.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EI_{2020} &= \frac{1{,}050}{320} = 3.2813 \text{ Mtoe / lakh crore}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — % Change in Energy Intensity.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\Delta EI &= \frac{3.2813 - 4.00}{4.00} \times 100 = -17.97\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Initial Energy Productivity.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EP_{2015} &= \frac{1}{EI_{2015}} = 0.2500 \text{ lakh crore / Mtoe}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Final Energy Productivity.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EP_{2020} &= \frac{1}{3.2813} = 0.3048 \text{ lakh crore / Mtoe}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Decoupling Commentary (for full marks).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\Delta GDP &= \frac{320-250}{250}\times 100 = +28\% \\
\%\Delta E   &= \frac{1050-1000}{1000}\times 100 = +5\%
\end{aligned}
$$

Because energy grew by only 5% while GDP grew by 28%, **relative decoupling** has been achieved. The negative %$\Delta$EI confirms that the EEM policy improved the country's energy efficiency. This is the analytical framework KTU examiners expect.

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** *Relative decoupling* means GDP grows faster than energy use. *Absolute decoupling* means energy use **falls** while GDP rises. The latter is the holy grail of energy policy.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The EEM Adoption Lifecycle (Mermaid Flow Chart)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Stage 1 Awareness Triggered by Energy Audit or Policy] --> B[Stage 2 Persuasion and Information Gathering]
    B --> C[Stage 3 Economic Evaluation LCCE NPV IRR]
    C --> D{Decision Adopt or Reject}
    D -->|Reject| E[Feedback Loop Re-evaluate Next Period]
    D -->|Adopt| F[Stage 4 Implementation and M and V per IPMVP]
    F --> G[Stage 5 Routinisation and Scale Up]
    G --> H[Continuous Improvement ISO 50001 Energy Review]
    H --> A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The cycle returns from Stage 5 to Stage 1 because successful EEMs trigger the search for the *next* energy-saving opportunity. This is the essence of **ISO 50001 (Energy Management System)** continuous improvement.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Categories of EEM Measures

```mermaid
graph LR
    Root[EEM Adoption Block] --> Cat1[Tier 1 Housekeeping and Behavioural]
    Root --> Cat2[Tier 2 Equipment Retrofit and Process Optimisation]
    Root --> Cat3[Tier 3 Systemic and Capital Intensive]

    Cat1 --> M1[Motor Shutdown Tariff Optimisation]
    Cat1 --> M2[Lighting Sensor Controls]
    Cat1 --> M3[Compressed Air Leak Audit]

    Cat2 --> M4[Variable Frequency Drives VFD]
    Cat2 --> M5[High Efficiency Motors IE3 IE4]
    Cat2 --> M6[Waste Heat Recovery Boiler]
    Cat2 --> M7[HVAC Insulation Upgrade]

    Cat3 --> M8[Cogeneration Tri Generation]
    Cat3 --> M9[Green Building Re Design]
    Cat3 --> M10[Renewable Integration Solar Wind]
    Cat3 --> M11[Fuel Switch to Cleaner Energy]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Measures of EEM (Metrics Hierarchy)

```mermaid
graph TD
    ME[Measures of EEM Block] --> L1[Macro Level National Economy]
    ME --> L2[Meso Level Sectoral]
    ME --> L3[Micro Level Firm or Equipment]

    L1 --> P1[Energy Intensity of GDP]
    L1 --> P2[Energy Productivity]
    L1 --> P3[ODEX Index]
    L1 --> P4[Kaya Decomposition]

    L2 --> S1[Specific Energy Consumption SEC]
    L2 --> S2[Sectoral Carbon Intensity]

    L3 --> U1[Energy Performance Indicator EnPI]
    U1 --> U2[Cost of Conserved Energy CCE]
    U1 --> U3[Rebound Effect Adjusted Savings]
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to use these diagrams in the KTU exam:** When a question asks "Discuss the measures of EEM", reproduce the **three-tier hierarchy (Macro / Meso / Micro)** and place each metric in its correct cell. This is the most examiner-friendly organisation and almost guarantees full content marks.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**

**Q1. Define Energy Efficiency Measure (EEM) and list any four categories of EEM with one example each. (3 Marks)**

*Model Answer:*
An Energy Efficiency Measure (EEM) is a specific, identifiable technological, operational, managerial, or behavioural intervention adopted to deliver the same energy service using less energy input. Four categories are:
1. **Housekeeping / Low-Cost** — e.g., switching off idle motors.
2. **Equipment Retrofit** — e.g., replacing old motors with IE3/IE4 high-efficiency motors.
3. **Process Optimisation** — e.g., installing a Variable Frequency Drive (VFD) on a pump.
4. **Systemic / Capital-Intensive** — e.g., cogeneration or trigeneration plant.

---

**Q2. Differentiate between Energy Intensity (EI) and Specific Energy Consumption (SEC). (3 Marks)**

*Model Answer:*

| Aspect | Energy Intensity (EI) | Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) |
|--------|----------------------|------------------------------------|
| **Level** | Macro / Sectoral | Plant / Equipment |
| **Denominator** | GDP or monetary output | Physical production (tonnes, units, m²) |
| **Use** | Cross-country / cross-sector benchmarking | Internal plant-level performance tracking |
| **Formula** | $EI = E / GDP$ | $SEC = E_{in} / P_{out}$ |

EI reflects **economic** efficiency; SEC reflects **engineering / physical** efficiency.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (Module Internal Choice: 14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style — Module 1 Internal Choice Pattern]**

### 🅰️ Question A (14 Marks)

**(a) Explain the drivers and barriers for the adoption of Energy Efficiency Measures (EEM) in industrial units. (7 Marks)**

*Model Solution:*

**Drivers of EEM Adoption:**
1. **Economic drivers** — Rising energy prices; lower Levelized Cost of Conserved Energy (LCCE) than grid tariff; ESCO business models.
2. **Policy / Regulatory drivers** — Perform-Achieve-Trade (PAT) cycle in India, Energy Conservation Act 2001, BEE star-labelling, carbon taxes, tax incentives (e.g., 80-IA accelerated depreciation for renewables).
3. **Information / Awareness drivers** — Mandatory energy audits (for designated consumers under PAT), ISO 50001 energy reviews, benchmarking, energy clubs.
4. **Organisational drivers** — Appointment of BEE-certified Energy Managers, top-management commitment, corporate ESG commitments.
5. **Behavioural / Social drivers** — Environmental values, peer pressure, customer demand for green products.
6. **Technological drivers** — Falling cost of efficient technologies, digital IoT-based monitoring, smart meters.

**Barriers to EEM Adoption:**
1. **Financial barrier** — High upfront capital, long payback periods, credit access for SMEs.
2. **Information / Asymmetry barrier** — Hidden costs, performance uncertainty, lack of reliable M&V.
3. **Organisational barrier** — Lack of management commitment, fragmented decision-making, *split-incentive problem* (landlord vs tenant).
4. **Behavioural barrier** — Status-quo bias, bounded rationality, "if it ain't broke, don't fix it" attitude.
5. **Technical barrier** — Integration complexity, risk of process disruption, skills gap.
6. **Policy / Institutional barrier** — Inconsistent regulations, weak enforcement, subsidy design flaws.

> Examiner's Note: A simple 6-vs-6 bullet format with 1-line explanation for each (≈ 1 mark per valid point) easily scores 7/7.

---

**(b) An aluminium smelter consumed 18,000 MWh in the year 2020. After implementing an EEM (replacement of rectifier transformers and improved cell control), the consumption in 2021 reduced to 15,300 MWh. The investment was ₹ 8 crore with annual O&M of ₹ 40 lakh. The life is 12 years; discount rate = 12% per annum. Calculate the annual energy savings, % savings, Capital Recovery Factor, and Cost of Conserved Energy (CCE) per kWh. (7 Marks)**

*Model Solution:*

**Step 1 — Annual Energy Savings.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{saved} &= 18{,}000 - 15{,}300 = 2{,}700 \text{ MWh / year} \\
         &= 27{,}00{,}000 \text{ kWh / year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — % Energy Savings.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%S &= \frac{2{,}700}{18{,}000} \times 100 = 15.00\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Capital Recovery Factor at $i = 0.12$, $n = 12$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(1.12)^{12} &\approx 3.8960 \\
CRF &= \frac{0.12 \times 3.8960}{3.8960 - 1} = \frac{0.46752}{2.8960} \approx 0.1614 \text{ year}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Cost of Conserved Energy.**

Total annualised cost = $I \times CRF + OM = 8{,}00{,}00{,}000 \times 0.1614 + 40{,}00{,}000$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&= 1{,}29{,}12{,}000 + 40{,}00{,}000 = 1{,}69{,}12{,}000 \\
CCE &= \frac{1{,}69{,}12{,}000}{27{,}00{,}000} \approx 6.26 \text{ ₹/kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Since CCE (₹ 6.26/kWh) is **less than** the typical industrial tariff (₹ 7–9/kWh), the EEM is **economically justified** and should be adopted.

---

### 🅱️ Question B (14 Marks)

**(a) Discuss the various measures (metrics) used to evaluate the effectiveness of Energy Efficiency Measures at the national, sectoral, and plant levels. (7 Marks)**

*Model Solution:*

**1. National (Macro) Level Measures:**
- **Energy Intensity (EI)** = $E / GDP$ (toe per million \$).
- **Energy Productivity (EP)** = $1 / EI$ = $GDP / E$.
- **ODEX (Odyssee Efficiency Index)** — composite sectoral efficiency index $< 1$ means improvement.
- **Kaya Identity decomposition** — disaggregates emissions into population, affluence, intensity, and carbonisation.
- **Avoided Emissions** = $E_{saved} \times EF_{grid}$.

**2. Sectoral (Meso) Level Measures:**
- **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** = energy per unit of physical output.
- **Sectoral carbon intensity** = CO$_2$ per unit output.
- **PAT (Perform-Achieve-Trade) Specific Energy Consumption norms** — e.g., SEC in kWh/tonne for cement, paper, fertiliser.

**3. Plant / Equipment (Micro) Level Measures:**
- **Energy Performance Indicator (EnPI)** = $E_{actual} / E_{baseline} \rightarrow < 1$ means EEM succeeded.
- **Cost of Conserved Energy (CCE)** — for project ranking.
- **Simple Payback, NPV, IRR** — financial measures of EEM.
- **Rebound Effect** — actual extra consumption vs expected extra consumption.
- **M&V savings per IPMVP** — Plan A (retrofit isolation), B (whole facility), C (whole building), D (calibrated simulation).

> Examiner's Note: A 3-level tabular answer with at least **2 metrics per level** and 1-line descriptions scores full marks.

---

**(b) A country consumed 800 Mtoe in 2018 with GDP of ₹ 200 lakh crore. In 2023, after a national EEM policy, consumption became 820 Mtoe and GDP rose to ₹ 260 lakh crore. Calculate the % change in Energy Intensity and Energy Productivity. Comment on the type of decoupling achieved. (7 Marks)**

*Model Solution:*

**Step 1 — Initial Energy Intensity (2018).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EI_{2018} &= \frac{800}{200} = 4.000 \text{ Mtoe / lakh crore}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Final Energy Intensity (2023).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EI_{2023} &= \frac{820}{260} = 3.1538 \text{ Mtoe / lakh crore}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — % Change in Energy Intensity.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\Delta EI &= \frac{3.1538 - 4.000}{4.000} \times 100 = -21.15\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Initial Energy Productivity.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EP_{2018} &= \frac{1}{4.000} = 0.2500 \text{ lakh crore / Mtoe}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Final Energy Productivity.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EP_{2023} &= \frac{1}{3.1538} = 0.3170 \text{ lakh crore / Mtoe}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — % Change in Energy Productivity.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\Delta EP &= \frac{0.3170 - 0.2500}{0.2500} \times 100 = +26.80\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Decoupling Commentary.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\Delta GDP &= \frac{260-200}{200}\times 100 = +30\% \\
\%\Delta E   &= \frac{820-800}{800}\times 100 = +2.5\%
\end{aligned}
$$

GDP grew at 30% while energy use grew at only 2.5% → **strong relative decoupling** is achieved. The EEM policy has clearly improved energy efficiency. Absolute decoupling is **not yet** achieved (energy use is still rising in absolute terms).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE marks in this topic:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch in CCE** — Mixing crore and lakh, MWh and kWh. Always convert to a *single* monetary unit and *single* energy unit *before* dividing.
> 2. **Forgetting the OM term in CCE** — Some students compute only $I \times CRF / E_{saved}$. Examiners explicitly deduct 1 mark for omitting the O&M component.
> 3. **Confusing EI with SEC** — EI uses *monetary* output; SEC uses *physical* output. Writing the wrong one = 0 marks for the formula.
> 4. **Writing Kaya Identity as a single equation without naming each factor** — Examiners expect **all four terms to be labelled** (population, affluence, energy intensity, carbon intensity of energy).
> 5. **Skipping the "comment" in decoupling questions** — Final numbers without interpretation fetch ≤ 50% marks. Always end with "**relative decoupling / absolute decoupling**" judgment.
> 6. **Spelling EEM as "EEN" or "EME"** — Technically a deduction under "presentation".

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> **Quick-Recall Box (for last-minute revision)**

- ⭐ **EEM =** Technology + Operations + Management + Behaviour changes to reduce energy input per unit service.
- ⭐ **Adoption =** 5-stage diffusion process: Awareness → Persuasion → Decision → Implementation → Routinisation.
- ⭐ **Adoption Gap ≈** 20–30% of technical potential (worldwide).
- ⭐ **Three Tiers of EEM:** Tier 1 (housekeeping), Tier 2 (retrofit), Tier 3 (systemic / capital).
- ⭐ **Macro measure:** Energy Intensity (EI = E / GDP), Energy Productivity (1/EI), ODEX, Kaya.
- ⭐ **Meso measure:** Specific Energy Consumption (SEC = E / physical output).
- ⭐ **Micro measure:** EnPI (E_actual / E_baseline), CCE, Rebound Effect.
- ⭐ **Kaya Identity:** $F = P \times (GDP/P) \times (E/GDP) \times (F/E)$ — four levers: population, affluence, intensity, carbonisation.
- ⭐ **Cost of Conserved Energy (CCE) formula:** $\dfrac{I \cdot CRF + OM}{E_{saved}}$ — compare with grid tariff to decide.
- ⭐ **Capital Recovery Factor:** $CRF = \dfrac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}$.
- ⭐ **% Savings Formula:** $\dfrac{E_{b} - E_{a}}{E_{b}} \times 100$.
- ⭐ **Relative Decoupling:** GDP grows faster than energy use. **Absolute Decoupling:** Energy use *falls* while GDP rises.
- ⭐ **PAT (Perform-Achieve-Trade)** = India's flagship EEM-adoption policy instrument for Designated Consumers.
- ⭐ **IPMVP (International Performance Measurement and Verification Protocol)** is the standard M&V framework used to confirm EEM savings.
- ⭐ **Indian context:** BEE (Bureau of Energy Efficiency), ECO Niwas Samhita, ECBC (for buildings), ISO 50001 (for industry).
- ⭐ **Rebound effect** = $\dfrac{\text{Actual extra consumption}}{\text{Expected extra consumption}} \times 100\%$. If > 100% → Jevons "backfire".
- ⭐ **Always state units** in CCE (₹/kWh) and SEC (kWh/tonne). Units earn 1 mark each in KTU.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Supply and Demand Analyses
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET526 - ENERGY ECONOMICS AND POLICY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Supply and Demand Analyses

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)
