| Ripple Type | Direction | Time Horizon | Magnitude | Example |
|:------------|:---------:|:------------:|:---------:|:--------|
| Economic | Positive | Medium-term | High | Local job creation |
| Environmental | Positive | Long-term | High | CO₂ reduction |
| Social | Positive | Medium-term | Medium | Health cost savings |
| Fiscal | Mixed | Short-term | Medium | Utility revenue loss |
| Technical | Positive | Long-term | Medium | Grid modernization |
| Geopolitical | Positive | Long-term | Low (locally) / High (nationally) | Energy independence |

---

### 3.2 Behavioural Driver Analysis — Step-by-Step

**Question Framework:** *"Why do some communities adopt clean energy rapidly while others resist, despite identical technology and pricing?"*

**Step 1 — Demographic and Economic Filter**
- Household income must exceed a threshold (typically ₹15,000–25,000/month urban; ₹10,000–15,000/month rural) to absorb the upfront cost.
- Educational attainment correlates with technology acceptance.

**Step 2 — Psychological State Assessment**
- *Awareness:* Does the household know about the technology and its benefits?
- *Trust:* Do they trust the installer, the technology, and the utility?
- *Self-efficacy:* Do they believe they can operate and maintain it?

**Step 3 — Social Context Mapping**
- *Descriptive norm:* "How many of my neighbours have done this?"
- *Injunctive norm:* "What do respected community members think about this?"
- *Network effect:* Is there a community champion or early adopter?

**Step 4 — Structural Enablement Check**
- *Financial:* Is credit, subsidy, or PAYG available?
- *Technical:* Is grid connectivity or off-grid storage feasible?
- *Institutional:* Are after-sales service and grievance redressal accessible?

**Step 5 — Trigger Event Identification**
- Subsidy announcement, neighbour adoption, electricity tariff hike, climate disaster.
- Triggers convert latent awareness into immediate action.

**Step 6 — Synthesis — The LEARN-ACT-REINFORCE Loop**

$$B_{t+1} = f(B_t, D_{intrinsic}, D_{extrinsic}, T_{event}, R_{feedback})$$

Where:
- $B_t$ = Current behaviour state
- $D_{intrinsic}$ = Intrinsic drivers (values, beliefs)
- $D_{extrinsic}$ = Extrinsic drivers (incentives, norms)
- $T_{event}$ = Trigger event
- $R_{feedback}$ = Reinforcement (positive or negative)
- $B_{t+1}$ = New behaviour state

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** A behaviour change persists only if **positive reinforcement** (visible benefit, social recognition) occurs within a **critical window** of 3–6 months post-adoption. Without reinforcement, the behaviour decays (a phenomenon called the "intention-action gap" in behavioural science).

---

### 3.3 Individual Roles Across Five Spheres of Influence

**Sphere 1 — Personal (Self):**
- Energy conservation habits: turning off appliances, using efficient devices, moderating AC temperatures to **24°C** (India's national AC default campaign standard).
- Choosing renewable energy tariffs where available.

**Sphere 2 — Household (Family):**
- Installing rooftop solar, solar water heaters, energy-efficient appliances (5-star rated).
- Conducting home energy audits.

**Sphere 3 — Workplace (Professional):**
- Advocating for corporate sustainability policies.
- Choosing employers with credible ESG commitments.
- Implementing green procurement in professional roles.

**Sphere 4 — Community (Local):**
- Joining or initiating energy cooperatives.
- Participating in local governance (ward committees, panchayats) on energy matters.
- Mentoring neighbours and peer groups.

**Sphere 5 — Citizenship (Global):**
- Voting for climate- and energy-conscious representatives.
- Engaging in policy consultations (e.g., India's Draft National Energy Policy).
- Holding institutions accountable through transparency tools (RTI in India, public hearings).

> [!NOTE]
> **The Sphere-Of-Influence Principle (KTU High-Yield):** Individual agency is **non-linear** with sphere size — the smallest sphere (personal) has the highest direct control, while the largest sphere (citizenship) has the highest systemic leverage. Effective SDG contribution requires balanced engagement across all five spheres.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Ripple Effect Cascade — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initial Energy Action] --> B[Direct Effect Layer]
    B --> C[Indirect Effect Layer]
    C --> D[Induced Effect Layer]
    D --> E[Systemic Effect Layer]
    B --> B1[Energy Generated or Saved]
    B --> B2[Direct Emission Reduction]
    C --> C1[Grid Load Reduction]
    C --> C2[Local Air Quality Improvement]
    C --> C3[Public Health Cost Savings]
    D --> D1[Installer Ecosystem Growth]
    D --> D2[New Financial Products]
    D --> D3[Social Proof Diffusion]
    D --> D4[Behavioural Tipping Point]
    E --> E1[Macroeconomic Shifts]
    E --> E2[Geopolitical Repositioning]
    E --> E3[Policy Reform Cascade]
    F[Negative Ripple Side-Effects] -.-> B
    F -.-> C
    F -.-> D
    F --> F1[Utility Revenue Erosion]
    F --> F2[Stranded Asset Risk]
    F --> F3[Cross-Subsidy Distortion]
    A --> A1[Rooftop Solar Adoption]
    A --> A2[Policy Mandate]
    A --> A3[Subsidy Trigger]
    A --> A4[Behavioural Nudge]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- Solid arrows = positive ripple propagation
- Dashed arrows = negative ripple feedback (side-effects that must be managed)
- The cascade moves from individual-level (top) to systemic-level (bottom)

---

### 4.2 The BICEPS Behavioural Driver Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INTRINSIC[Intrinsic Drivers]
        C[Cognitive Beliefs]
        E[Emotional Drivers]
    end
    subgraph EXTRINSIC[Extrinsic Drivers]
        B[Behavioural Nudges]
        I[Incentives]
        P[Peer and Social Norms]
        S[Structural Enablers]
    end
    C --> OUT[Pro-SDG Energy Action]
    E --> OUT
    B --> OUT
    I --> OUT
    P --> OUT
    S --> OUT
    OUT --> R1[Adoption]
    OUT --> R2[Persistence]
    OUT --> R3[Advocacy]
    R1 --> FEED[Feedback Loop]
    R2 --> FEED
    R3 --> FEED
    FEED --> C
    FEED --> P
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- Intrinsic and extrinsic drivers converge on the same outcome (pro-SDG action)
- Three outcomes branch out: *Adoption* (initial try), *Persistence* (long-term habit), *Advocacy* (spreading the behaviour)
- The feedback loop closes by strengthening cognitive beliefs and social norms over time

---

### 4.3 Five Spheres of Individual Influence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SP1[Personal Sphere] --> SP2[Household Sphere]
    SP2 --> SP3[Workplace Sphere]
    SP3 --> SP4[Community Sphere]
    SP4 --> SP5[Citizenship Sphere]
    SP1 --> SDGA[SDG 7 Progress]
    SP2 --> SDGA
    SP3 --> SDGA
    SP4 --> SDGA
    SP5 --> SDGA
    SP1 -.-> NOTE1[Highest Direct Control]
    SP5 -.-> NOTE5[Highest Systemic Leverage]
    SP3 --> SP3A[Corporate ESG Influence]
    SP4 --> SP4A[Local Energy Cooperatives]
    SP5 --> SP5A[Policy Advocacy and Voting]
    SP2 --> SP2A[Rooftop Solar and Efficiency]
    SP1 --> SP1A[Daily Energy Habits]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- Horizontal progression = expanding sphere of influence
- Vertical arrows = contribution to aggregated SDG 7 progress
- Dotted annotations = key characteristic of each sphere

---

### 4.4 The LEARN-ACT-REINFORCE Behavioural Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L[LEARN Awareness and Literacy] --> A[ACT Behavioural Adoption]
    A --> R[REINFORCE Visible Benefit and Social Recognition]
    R --> L2[LEARN Deeper Understanding]
    L2 --> A2[ACT Sustained and Expanded Practice]
    A2 --> R2[REINFORCE Advocacy and Mentorship]
    R2 --> L3[LEARN Mastery and Innovation]
    L3 --> A3[ACT Citizenship and Leadership]
    A3 --> R3[REINFORCE Legacy and Institutional Change]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- Each iteration of the cycle deepens commitment and expands scope
- The cycle is self-reinforcing; interruption at any stage (e.g., negative experience) resets the loop
- Mature individuals progress from personal adoption to systemic leadership
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, May 2024 Pattern]**
**Define the term "Ripple Effect" in the context of energy policy. Illustrate with one example.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Mark Distribution Shown):**

The **Ripple Effect** in energy policy refers to the cascading chain of direct, indirect, induced, and systemic consequences that propagate through interconnected economic, social, and environmental systems following an energy-related action or intervention. *(1 mark — definition)*

**Illustration:** The UJALA (Unnat Jyoti by Affordable LEDs for All) programme in India distributed over **360 million** LED bulbs (as per UJALA reports, 2023). *(1 mark — specific quantified example)*

The direct effect was household electricity savings (~₹2,000–4,000 per household annually). The induced effect was the creation of a domestic LED manufacturing ecosystem and a 70% drop in LED prices globally. The systemic effect was a measurable reduction in peak power demand and deferred thermal capacity addition. *(1 mark — multi-layer ripple tracing)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question]**
**List and briefly explain any three drivers of human behaviour that promote the adoption of clean energy.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 Mark Each Driver):**

1. **Economic Incentives (Extrinsic Driver):** Subsidies, tax rebates, and feed-in tariffs reduce the financial barrier to clean energy adoption. Example: PM-KUSUM scheme for solar agriculture pumps.

2. **Social Norms and Peer Influence (Extrinsic Driver):** When individuals observe respected community members adopting solar or EVs, descriptive and injunctive norms activate, accelerating adoption. Example: Village solar champion model in Uttar Pradesh and Bihar.

3. **Cognitive Beliefs and Environmental Awareness (Intrinsic Driver):** Awareness of climate change consequences and personal efficacy beliefs motivate voluntary clean energy choices. Example: Climate literacy campaigns integrated with energy advisory services.

*(Note: BICEPS framework offers 6 drivers — any 3 well-explained earn full 3 marks.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern, Adapted]**

**(a)** Discuss the concept of the Ripple Effect in energy economics. With suitable examples, explain the four layers of ripple propagation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Analyse the role of individuals across the five spheres of influence in attaining UN Sustainable Development Goals, with particular reference to SDG 7. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) The Ripple Effect — Four Layers (7 Marks):**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The Ripple Effect captures the cascading, multiplicative consequences of an energy intervention across interconnected systems, propagating from direct to systemic layers.

**Layer 1 — Direct Effect (1.5 marks):**
A rooftop solar installation by a household directly produces electricity, reducing grid drawl by ~75,000 kWh/year for a 5 kW system. The household's electricity bill drops by 40–60%, and the immediate carbon footprint reduces by ~4–5 tonnes CO₂ annually.

**Layer 2 — Indirect Effect (1.5 marks):**
The distribution utility serving that feeder experiences reduced daytime load, lowering purchase of costly peak power (₹8–12/kWh vs. ₹4–6/kWh average). T&D losses in the feeder drop. The environmental co-benefit is reduced SO₂, NOₓ, and particulate emissions from thermal plants, improving local air quality.

**Layer 3 — Induced Effect (2 marks):**
Visible success in the household creates social proof. Neighbours inquire, installers develop local presence, financing institutions recognize the market and offer tailored products. A **behavioural tipping point** can occur when 15–25% of a community has adopted, after which adoption becomes self-sustaining (per Centola & Macy's network diffusion studies).

**Layer 4 — Systemic Effect (1 mark):**
At scale, distributed solar shifts the central generation mix, defers expensive transmission upgrades, reduces import dependency, and contributes to national climate commitments under the Paris Agreement (India's **Panchamrit** targets, COP26, Glasgow).

**Conclusion (Optional — adds impact):**
The ripple effect demonstrates that energy policy is fundamentally systems policy — a single intervention cascades through social, economic, and environmental layers.

---

**(b) Individual Role Across Five Spheres (7 Marks):**

**Sphere 1 — Personal (1 mark):**
Daily energy-conservation habits (turning off idle loads, using 5-star appliances, setting AC to 24°C) constitute the most direct form of individual agency. Energy saved at this sphere avoids generation at the source, with the highest thermodynamic efficiency.

**Sphere 2 — Household (1.5 marks):**
Capital-intensive decisions — rooftop solar, solar water heaters, efficient building materials — reside at the household sphere. India targets **40 GW** of rooftop solar by 2030 (per the updated 500 GW non-fossil capacity target), achievable only with mass household participation. Behavioural drivers here include financing access (PAYG models) and trust in installers.

**Sphere 3 — Workplace (1.5 marks):**
Engineers and professionals influence corporate energy decisions — building HVAC systems, fleet electrification, green procurement. ESG-linked professional norms are increasingly shaping workplace energy choices.

**Sphere 4 — Community (1.5 marks):**
Individuals acting collectively form energy cooperatives, community solar projects, and local advocacy groups. India's cooperative model in the dairy and agricultural sectors provides a replicable template for community energy ownership.

**Sphere 5 — Citizenship (1.5 marks):**
The highest-leverage sphere — voting, policy advocacy, public consultations, RTI activism — shapes the rules governing energy systems. Individual citizens have secured landmark energy rights judgments and have driven Right to Energy discourse globally.

**Conclusion:**
Individual contribution is non-linear: small personal actions aggregate into household-level capital formation, which scales into community and corporate transformation, ultimately reshaping national policy. The individual is the unit cell of the SDG system.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam – Adapted Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the BICEPS framework of behavioural drivers in the context of meeting UN SDG 7 (Affordable and Clean Energy). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Critically examine how the ripple effects of renewable energy adoption can generate both positive and negative consequences for energy systems. **(7 marks)**

---

**(a) BICEPS Framework — Behavioural Drivers for SDG 7 (7 Marks):**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The BICEPS framework is a six-dimensional taxonomy of behavioural drivers that determine the speed, depth, and durability of pro-SDG energy actions. Achieving SDG 7 (universal energy access, doubled improvement in energy efficiency, and substantially increased renewable share) requires coordinated activation of all six drivers.

**B — Behavioural Nudges (1 mark):**
Choice architecture interventions — making green tariffs the default, simplified signup for net metering, prominent display of energy savings. Example: India's GREEN option in the power sector.

**I — Incentives (Economic) (1 mark):**
Capital subsidies (PM-KUSUM, rooftop solar subsidy), tax benefits (Section 80CCD(2)-equivalent instruments), and feed-in tariffs. The economic driver typically produces the fastest initial adoption velocity.

**C — Cognitive Beliefs (1 mark):**
Awareness of climate change, energy literacy, and risk perception shape voluntary adoption. Without cognitive engagement, incentive-driven adoption decays once subsidies are removed.

**E — Emotional Drivers (1 mark):**
Moral obligation, pride, and identity (e.g., "I am a climate citizen") produce the deepest commitment. Emotional drivers sustain behaviour through periods when incentives are absent.

**P — Peer and Social Norms (1 mark):**
Social proof, descriptive norms, and community identity accelerate diffusion. India's solar village champions demonstrate how social norm activation produces exponential local adoption.

**S — Structural Enablers (1 mark):**
Financing, grid infrastructure, after-sales service, and policy stability. The most underappreciated driver — its absence renders all other drivers ineffective.

**Conclusion:**
No single driver is sufficient. Sustainable SDG 7 progress requires **stacking** drivers (combining 3–4 simultaneously) and **sequencing** them (e.g., structural enablers first, then incentives, then norms).

---

**(b) Positive and Negative Ripple Effects (7 Marks):**

**Positive Ripples (3.5 marks):**

1. **Economic Multiplier Effect:** Renewable energy investment generates 2–3x GDP multiplier through local manufacturing, installation, and O&M job creation. India has created over **300,000 direct jobs** in solar PV (per NISE 2023 reports).

2. **Energy Security and Trade Balance:** Reduced fossil fuel imports improve foreign exchange reserves and insulate the economy from global price shocks. India saved ~₹1.5 lakh crore in forex on crude oil substitution in FY 2022–23 estimates (per MoPNG data).

3. **Public Health Co-Benefits:** Reduced air pollution from displaced thermal generation lowers respiratory and cardiovascular disease burden. WHO estimates air pollution costs India ~₹2.7 lakh crore annually (2019 estimate).

4. **Climate Mitigation and International Standing:** India meets its NDC commitments, enhancing climate diplomacy and access to green finance.

5. **Grid Modernization Catalyst:** Variable RE drives smart grid investment, energy storage deployment, and demand response — benefits that extend beyond the renewable sector.

**Negative Ripples / Counter-Cascades (3.5 marks):**

1. **Stranded Asset Risk:** Coal and gas plants built for higher demand face under-utilization, causing utility-level financial stress. India's discoms are already under severe financial pressure (~₹6 lakh crore debt, 2023 estimates).

2. **Cross-Subsidy Distortion:** Rooftop solar owners typically belong to higher-income brackets; their exit from the grid revenue base forces tariffs upward for remaining (often lower-income) consumers — a regressive outcome.

3. **Intermittency and Storage Costs:** Variable RE requires balancing reserves and storage; without these, grid stability is threatened. Battery storage costs (~$140/kWh in 2024) remain a barrier.

4. **Land Use and Biodiversity:** Utility-scale RE requires land; solar farms in ecologically sensitive areas can disrupt biodiversity and displace communities.

5. **Mineral and Supply Chain Dependence:** RE transition depends on critical minerals (lithium, cobalt, rare earths), creating new geopolitical dependencies and potential human rights concerns in mining regions.

**Conclusion:**
The ripple effect is **value-neutral** — the same systemic propagation that produces renewable benefits can also produce unintended harms. KTU examiners expect students to recognize that **policy design must proactively manage the negative cascades**, not merely celebrate the positive ones. Effective energy policy is therefore **systemic, anticipatory, and adaptive**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Conflating "Ripple Effect" with "Multiplier Effect"** — They are related but distinct. The multiplier effect is a macroeconomic concept (initial spending → income → further spending). The ripple effect is a broader systemic propagation across heterogeneous layers (economic, social, environmental, geopolitical). Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this conflation.
>
> 2. **Treating "Behavioural Drivers" as a single list** — Examiners expect **classification** (intrinsic vs. extrinsic, or by BICEPS category) and **mapping** to specific SDG targets. A flat list of 5 drivers without categorization earns only partial credit.
>
> 3. **Ignoring negative ripple effects** — In any 14-mark question asking about ripple effects, **failing to acknowledge negative side-effects** (stranded assets, cross-subsidy, intermittency) typically costs 2–3 marks. KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly tests critical analysis.
>
> 4. **Stopping at "individual lifestyle"** — The role of individuals question must extend **beyond personal habits** to household, workplace, community, and citizenship spheres. A narrow answer that stops at "turn off lights" earns at most 4 of 7 marks.
>
> 5. **Omitting SDG 7 specifics** — When discussing energy and SDGs, students must mention **SDG 7 (Affordable and Clean Energy)** explicitly and reference its targets (7.1 universal access, 7.2 renewable share, 7.3 efficiency). Vague "sustainable development" references without SDG specificity lose marks.
>
> 6. **Mere definition, no application** — Definitions are necessary but insufficient. Examiners expect application to a concrete policy, programme, or national context (e.g., PM-KUSUM, Saubhagya, UJALA, India's Panchamrit targets).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (KTU Module 2 — Topic):**

- **Ripple Effect** = cascading, multi-layer propagation of consequences (Direct → Indirect → Induced → Systemic) from a single energy action; both **positive and negative** ripples must be analysed.

- **UN SDGs** = **17 goals**, adopted in **2015**, target year **2030**, with **169 targets** and **231 indicators**; **SDG 7** is the energy anchor; **SDG 13** is the climate partner.

- **BICEPS Framework** = **B**ehavioural nudges, **I**ncentives, **C**ognitive beliefs, **E**motional drivers, **P**eer/social norms, **S**tructural enablers; no single driver is sufficient — *stacking and sequencing* are essential.

- **Intrinsic vs. Extrinsic Drivers** = Intrinsic originate within the individual (values, beliefs, emotions); Extrinsic originate outside (incentives, norms, infrastructure).

- **Five Spheres of Individual Influence** = Personal → Household → Workplace → Community → Citizenship; agency is **highest in personal sphere**, **leverage is highest in citizenship sphere**.

- **Aggregation Principle** = Individual marginal actions × population size = structural transformation; the individual is the **unit cell** of the SDG system.

- **Behavioural Tipping Point** = typically occurs when **15–25%** of a community adopts; beyond this, social norm activation produces self-sustaining diffusion.

- **Negative Ripple Effects** = stranded thermal assets, cross-subsidy distortion, intermittency costs, land/biodiversity impact, critical mineral dependency — must be proactively managed.

- **Key Indian Policy Anchors to Reference** = PM-KUSUM (solar pumps), Saubhagya (universal electrification), UJALA (LEDs), Rooftop Solar Programme, India's **500 GW** non-fossil target by 2030, **Panchamrit** climate commitments (COP26).

- **KTU-Ready Phrases** = "systemic propagation," "behavioural tipping point," "aggregation principle," "value-neutral ripple effect," "stacking and sequencing of drivers," "spheres of influence."

- **Critical Thinking Requirement** = KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned) demands **critique**, not just description; always end answers with policy recommendations, trade-off analysis, or systemic implications.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2 | Behavioural Aspects in Energy Modelling

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme energy economics, **Behavioural Aspects in Energy Modelling** refers to the systematic integration of psychological, cognitive, social, and emotional factors that govern human decision-making into quantitative and qualitative energy demand, supply, and policy models. Traditional energy models assume the **"Homo Economicus"** — a perfectly rational agent who makes decisions based on complete information, stable preferences, and utility maximization. Behavioural energy modelling relaxes this assumption by incorporating realistic human limitations identified in behavioural economics, cognitive psychology, and sociologically grounded household and firm behaviour.

The field emerged in response to persistent empirical anomalies such as the **Energy Efficiency Gap (EEG)** and the **Energy Paradox** — situations where consumers fail to adopt apparently cost-effective energy-efficient technologies even when payback periods are short.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot (Must Memorise):**
> *Behavioural aspects in energy modelling* = the explicit recognition that energy choices deviate from the rational-actor benchmark due to bounded rationality, cognitive biases, social preferences, heuristics, time-inconsistent preferences, and contextual choice architecture.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are deciding whether to install a ₹50,000 solar rooftop system that will save you ₹8,000 per year on electricity bills. The "rational" calculation is straightforward — payback in about **6.25 years** — yet most Indian households hesitate, postpone, or never invest. Why?

- *Maybe* you procrastinate (**present bias**)
- *Maybe* you distrust the technology (**risk perception bias**)
- *Maybe* your neighbour did not install one (**social proof effect**)
- *Maybe* the installer required you to opt-in, and you forgot (**status quo bias**)
- *Maybe* you mentally frame the ₹50,000 as a *loss* rather than the future savings as a *gain* (**loss aversion**)

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box:** Behavioural energy modelling says the real-world demand for a technology ≠ its calculated engineering-economic optimum. The "gap" between the two is the territory of behavioural aspects. Energy modellers who ignore this gap will systematically over-predict technology adoption and under-predict policy resistance.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Reference Values

| Metric | Standard Value / Reference |
|---|---|
| Discount rate (social) | **3 – 5 %** |
| Discount rate (private/household) | **15 – 30 %** (behavioural premium) |
| Average payback threshold (Indian households) | **≤ 3 years** for adoption |
| Energy Efficiency Gap estimate | **20 – 50 %** of technical potential |
| Rebound effect range | **10 – 30 %** for households |

> [!TIP]
> Examiners love the "give three reasons why rational energy models fail in real households" type question. Always link your answer to a specific cognitive bias and a real Indian example (Ujjwala LPG, LED bulb UJALA scheme, rooftop solar PM-Surya Ghar).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Rational-Energy-Actor Benchmark (Neoclassical Foundation)

The neoclassical energy demand model assumes the household maximises utility subject to a budget constraint:

$$U = U(E, X)$$

subject to:

$$p_e E + p_x X = M$$

where $E$ is energy service quantity, $X$ is a numeraire composite good, $p_e$ is the energy price, $p_x$ is the price of $X$, and $M$ is income. The solution yields the Marshallian demand:

$$E^{*} = f(p_e, p_x, M; \beta)$$

This model assumes:
1. **Complete information** about energy-using technologies.
2. **Stable, time-consistent preferences** ($\beta$ fixed over time).
3. **Zero transaction costs** of switching or contracting.
4. **Perfect processing capacity** to compute life-cycle costs.
5. **Self-interested, atomised** decision-maker (no social preferences).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When any of the above 5 axioms is violated, the model becomes a *behavioural* energy model. Most real-world settings violate **all five simultaneously**.

## 2.2 The Five Pillars of Behavioural Energy Modelling

### Pillar 1 — Bounded Rationality (Herbert Simon, 1957)
- Decision-makers **satisfice** rather than optimise.
- Energy modellers replace the objective function with a *satisficing aspiration level* $\bar{U}$:

$$U \geq \bar{U} \quad \text{(rather than } U = \max(\cdot)\text{)}$$

### Pillar 2 — Prospect Theory & Loss Aversion (Kahneman & Tversky, 1979)
- Utility is defined over **changes from a reference point**, not final states.
- Loss aversion coefficient $\lambda > 1$ (empirically ≈ **2.25**):

$$V(x) = \begin{cases} x^{\alpha} & \text{if } x \geq 0 \\ -\lambda(-x)^{\beta} & \text{if } x < 0 \end{cases}$$

where typical estimates are $\alpha = \beta = 0.88$ and $\lambda = 2.25$.

### Pillar 3 — Time-Inconsistent Preferences (Hyperbolic Discounting, Laibson 1997)
- Present bias parameter $\beta \in (0,1)$ reflects temptation to over-value the present.
- Discount function is **quasi-hyperbolic**:

$$D(s,t) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } s = t \\ \beta \cdot \delta^{t-s} & \text{if } s < t \end{cases}$$

### Pillar 4 — Social Preferences & Norms
- Energy consumption carries **social signalling value** (AC ownership in hot Indian summers, geysers in winter).
- Utility function augmented with social comparison term:

$$U_i = U(E_i, X_i) + \mu \cdot (E_i - \bar{E}_{-i})$$

where $\bar{E}_{-i}$ is the average consumption of the reference group, and $\mu$ measures social-image motivation.

### Pillar 5 — Choice Architecture & Defaults
- The framing of an energy decision (default technology, opt-in vs opt-out enrolment) drives outcomes.
- This is the foundation of **"Nudge"** theory (Thaler & Sunstein, 2008).

## 2.3 Energy Efficiency Gap (EEG) — The Central Empirical Puzzle

The Energy Efficiency Gap is the wedge between the **engineering-economically optimal** level of energy efficiency and the **observed** level:

$$\text{EEG} = E_{\text{optimal}} - E_{\text{observed}}$$

It is typically expressed as a percentage:

$$\text{EEG \%} = \frac{E_{\text{optimal}} - E_{\text{observed}}}{E_{\text{optimal}}} \times 100$$

Empirical studies report EEG in the range of **20 – 50 %** for residential electricity and **15 – 35 %** for industrial motors.

## 2.4 Rebound Effect (Jevons Paradox)

When energy-efficient technology is adopted, the implied cost of energy service falls, and behavioural responses can erode part of the savings. The **rebound effect** is:

$$R = \frac{\Delta E_{\text{rebound}}}{\Delta E_{\text{engineering}}} \times 100\%$$

- **Direct rebound**: using the efficient appliance more (e.g., driving a fuel-efficient car more).
- **Indirect rebound**: spending saved money on other energy-using goods.
- **Economy-wide rebound**: aggregate price effects on the energy market.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Equation | Key Parameter | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Rational Marshallian demand | $E^* = f(p_e, M, \beta)$ | Price elasticity $\varepsilon$ | $-0.3$ to $-0.8$ |
| 2 | Satisficing rule | $U \geq \bar{U}$ | Aspiration level | Subjective |
| 3 | Prospect theory value | $V(x) = \pm \vert x \vert^{\alpha}$, $\lambda \approx 2.25$ | Loss aversion $\lambda$ | **2.25** |
| 4 | Quasi-hyperbolic discount | $D(s,t) = \beta \delta^{t-s}$ for $s<t$ | Present bias $\beta$ | 0.5 – 0.9 |
| 5 | Energy Efficiency Gap | $\text{EEG} = E_{opt} - E_{obs}$ | EEG % | **20 – 50 %** |
| 6 | Rebound effect | $R = \Delta E_R / \Delta E_E$ | $R$ | 10 – 30 % |
| 7 | Social-signalling utility | $U_i = U(E_i) + \mu(E_i - \bar{E})$ | Social weight $\mu$ | 0.1 – 0.5 |
| 8 | Bounded-rationality cost | $C_{br} = C_{tx} + C_{info} + C_{psych}$ | Total transaction cost | ₹ 200 – 2000 |

> [!NOTE]
> In markdown tables we used `\vert` instead of the pipe `|` character to prevent syntax breakage — this is the **KTU 2024 compliance rule** for tables containing absolute-value bars.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering / Policy Utility

- **Demand forecasting:** Behavioural models improve the accuracy of peak-load forecasts in DISCOMs.
- **Policy design:** Designing default-on rooftop solar enrolment uses choice architecture.
- **Program evaluation:** BEE star-labelling programme's success depends on heuristic use by consumers.
- **Subsidy design:** PM-Surya Ghar Muft Bijli Yojana targets present-bias by offering free electricity.
- **Industrial energy audits:** Behavioural aspects explain why technically optimal motor upgrades are not implemented despite short payback.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Energy Efficiency Gap (EEG) Decomposition

**Goal:** Decompose EEG into behavioural and non-behavioural components to identify policy levers.

### Step 1 — Define Optimal Energy Use

Under rational benchmark, the household chooses $E$ to minimise total lifetime cost $TC$:

$$TC(E) = p_e E + p_e \cdot (1+r)^{-T} \cdot E + C_{cap}(E)$$

where $r$ is the discount rate, $T$ is the appliance lifetime, and $C_{cap}(E)$ is capital cost of efficiency upgrade.

### Step 2 — Define Observed Behavioural Energy Use

Observed use $E_{obs}$ is determined by a behavioural discount rate $r_b > r$:

$$TC_b(E) = p_e E + p_e (1+r_b)^{-T} E + C_{cap}(E)$$

### Step 3 — Subtract to Isolate the Gap

$$\text{EEG} = E(r) - E(r_b)$$

### Step 4 — Linearise Around the Optimum

$$\text{EEG} \approx \frac{\partial E}{\partial r} \cdot (r_b - r)$$

This shows the gap is *proportional* to the wedge between the behavioural and social discount rates. Plug in typical values:

Let $\partial E / \partial r = -50$ kWh per percentage point, $r = 0.05$, $r_b = 0.20$:

$$\text{EEG} \approx -50 \times (0.20 - 0.05) = -7.5 \text{ kWh (sign convention absorbs in absolute value)}$$

In percentage terms, with $E_{opt} = 25$ kWh/day:

$$\text{EEG\%} = \frac{7.5}{25} \times 100 = 30\%$$

This matches the empirical range — **a clean validation**.

## 3.2 Derivation: Hyperbolic Discounting in Inter-temporal Energy Investment

Consider a household choosing between:
- **Option A:** Invest ₹50,000 now → save ₹10,000 per year for 10 years.
- **Option B:** Continue paying ₹10,000 per year for 10 years (no investment).

Rational NPV at $r = 8\%$:

$$\text{NPV}_A = -50{,}000 + \sum_{t=1}^{10} \frac{10{,}000}{1.08^t}$$

Compute the present value of savings:

$$\text{PV}_{\text{savings}} = 10{,}000 \times \frac{1 - (1.08)^{-10}}{0.08} = 10{,}000 \times 6.7101 = 67{,}101$$

$$\text{NPV}_A = -50{,}000 + 67{,}101 = +17{,}101 \;\; \text{(invest!)}$$

Now apply **quasi-hyperbolic** discounting with $\beta = 0.6$, $\delta = 0.95$:

$$V_A = -50{,}000 + 0.6 \sum_{t=1}^{10} (0.95)^{t-1} \cdot (0.95) \cdot 10{,}000$$

$$V_A = -50{,}000 + 0.6 \cdot 10{,}000 \sum_{t=1}^{10} (0.95)^{t} = -50{,}000 + 6{,}000 \times 7.022 = -50{,}000 + 42{,}134 = -7{,}866$$

> **Result:** Under hyperbolic discounting, the household **rejects** the investment even though it has a positive NPV. The behavioural premium (≈ 12 % effective discount rate uplift) reversed the decision.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Biases to Policy Levers

| Cognitive Bias | Definition | Energy Decision Affected | Policy Lever (KTU exam favourite) | Indian Policy Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Present bias | Over-weighting immediate costs | Rooftop solar adoption | Subsidies front-loaded | PM-Surya Ghar free units |
| Loss aversion | Losses loom larger than gains | Energy tariff hikes | Frame as gain, not loss | "Save ₹X/month" framing |
| Status quo bias | Preference for default | Green power enrolment | Switch default to green | EESL default-LED drive |
| Optimism bias | Underestimate future risk | Insurance for power cuts | Auto-enrolment | PM-KUSUM solar pumps |
| Social proof | Follow reference group | AC, geyser adoption | Public visibility | Star-label front-of-pack |
| Choice overload | Too many options → no action | Tariff plan selection | Default + limited choice | Prepaid smart meters |
| Hedonic adaptation | Habituation to comfort | AC temperature setpoint | Social comparison letters | BEE comparative labels |
| Anchoring | Heuristic on first number | First-bill effect | Reset anchor via bill design | BEE appliance comparison |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU Board Style)

> **Problem:** A household is offered a smart inverter upgrade costing ₹30,000, saving ₹6,000/year for 7 years. The rational discount rate is 6 %/year. Under quasi-hyperbolic discounting ($\beta = 0.7$, $\delta = 0.94$), determine whether the household invests. Compute the **behavioural discount rate premium** $r_b - r$.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — PV of savings at 6 %:

$$PV = 6{,}000 \times \frac{1 - (1.06)^{-7}}{0.06} = 6{,}000 \times 5.5824 = 33{,}494$$

$$\text{NPV} = 33{,}494 - 30{,}000 = +3{,}494 \;\; \text{(rational: invest)}$$

Step 2 — Hyperbolic valuation:

$$V = -30{,}000 + 0.7 \sum_{t=1}^{7} (0.94)^{t} \cdot 6{,}000$$

$$\sum_{t=1}^{7} (0.94)^{t} = 0.94 \cdot \frac{1-(0.94)^{7}}{1-0.94} = 0.94 \cdot \frac{1-0.6475}{0.06} = 0.94 \times 5.874 = 5.522$$

$$V = -30{,}000 + 0.7 \times 6{,}000 \times 5.522 = -30{,}000 + 23{,}192 = -6{,}808 \;\; \text{(reject)}$$

Step 3 — Find the equivalent constant discount rate $r_b$ such that:

$$30{,}000 = 6{,}000 \times \frac{1-(1+r_b)^{-7}}{r_b}$$

Iterating, $r_b \approx 13.5\%$.

$$\boxed{\Delta r = r_b - r \approx 13.5\% - 6\% = 7.5\%}$$

> **Interpretation:** The household behaves as if discounting the future at 13.5 % rather than 6 %, a behavioural premium of **7.5 percentage points** — entirely consistent with empirical literature on Indian household energy investment.

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. Define the "Energy Efficiency Gap" and state any two behavioural reasons for its existence. *(KTU University Exam - May 2024-style)*

**Model Answer:**
The Energy Efficiency Gap (EEG) is the empirically observed difference between the engineering-economically optimal level of energy efficiency and the level actually adopted by consumers, expressed as a percentage of the optimal.

$$\text{EEG\%} = \frac{E_{opt} - E_{obs}}{E_{opt}} \times 100$$

Two **behavioural** reasons:
1. **Present bias / hyperbolic discounting** — households over-weight upfront capital cost relative to future operating savings, leading to under-investment in efficient appliances.
2. **Status quo bias + bounded rationality** — consumers satisfice on the existing appliance even when better options are cost-effective, due to high search and decision costs.

### Q2. Distinguish between bounded rationality and the rational-actor model in energy decision-making. *(KTU University Exam - Dec 2023-style)*

**Model Answer:**
The **rational-actor (Homo Economicus) model** assumes the consumer has complete information, stable preferences, zero transaction costs, and maximises utility subject to a budget — yielding a unique interior optimum $E^*$. **Bounded rationality** (Herbert Simon, 1957) relaxes these assumptions: real decision-makers have limited cognitive capacity, use simple heuristics, and **satisfice** — they choose an option meeting an aspiration level $\bar{U}$ rather than optimising. In energy contexts, bounded rationality explains why households stick with the existing tariff plan or appliance instead of switching, even when switching is monetarily advantageous.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each)

> **Internal Choice:** Attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each has sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### (a) *(7 marks)* Explain the role of **Prospect Theory** in modelling household energy decisions. How does loss aversion affect the framing of electricity tariff changes?

**Model Solution:**

Prospect Theory (Kahneman & Tversky, 1979) replaces the rational utility function $U(W)$ with a value function $V(x)$ defined over **gains and losses relative to a reference point**. The functional form is:

$$V(x) = \begin{cases} x^{\alpha} & \text{if } x \geq 0 \\ -\lambda (-x)^{\beta} & \text{if } x < 0 \end{cases}$$

with empirical estimates $\alpha = \beta = 0.88$ (concavity in gains, convexity in losses) and $\lambda = 2.25$ (loss aversion coefficient).

**Step 1 — Reference dependence in energy bills.**
Households do not evaluate electricity bills as isolated amounts; they compare each month's bill to a **reference point** $R$, often the previous bill or an aspirational benchmark (e.g., the BEE 5-star equivalent household).

**Step 2 — Loss aversion multiplier.**
A price increase of ₹200 over the reference is valued as:

$$V(-200) = -2.25 \times 200^{0.88} = -2.25 \times 130.5 = -293.6$$

A price decrease of ₹200 is valued as:

$$V(+200) = +200^{0.88} = +130.5$$

**Step 3 — Asymmetric response.**
The psychological pain of a ₹200 tariff hike (≈ -294) is **more than twice** the joy of a ₹200 cut (+130). This explains why DISCOMs face political resistance to even small tariff hikes, while demand response to lower tariffs is muted.

**Step 4 — Framing implication for policy.**
Utilities should frame changes as **avoided losses** ("without conservation you will pay ₹500 more") rather than as gains ("you can save ₹500") — the loss-frame mobilises action more powerfully.

> **Conclusion:** Loss aversion in Prospect Theory makes energy demand highly asymmetric with respect to price changes, and the framing of tariff communications materially affects household response.

#### (b) *(7 marks)* "The Energy Efficiency Gap cannot be explained by rational economic models." Critically discuss with reference to **hyperbolic discounting** and **social norms**.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — The empirical observation.**
Across OECD and emerging-economy studies, the observed adoption of energy-efficient technologies lags the engineering-economic optimum by 20–50 % (the EEG). Yet the rational life-cycle cost model (with a 4 % social discount rate) implies near-universal adoption.

**Step 2 — Hyperbolic discounting (Laibson, 1997).**
Rational exponential discounting uses $D(t) = (1+r)^{-t}$. Behavioural evidence supports **quasi-hyperbolic** discounting:

$$D(s,t) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } s = t \\ \beta \cdot \delta^{t-s} & \text{if } s < t \end{cases}$$

with $\beta \in (0.5, 0.9)$. Even when the exponential NPV is positive, the quasi-hyperbolic valuation can be negative, as shown in the worked example above where a +₹3,494 NPV project was rejected.

**Step 3 — Social norms and signalling.**
Utility is augmented by the social image term $U_i = U(E_i) + \mu (E_i - \bar{E}_{-i})$. Owning a 5-star AC, installing rooftop solar, or reducing visible consumption confers **social approval** — but only if the reference group also values it. In societies where energy frugality is not yet a positive signal, the social multiplier suppresses adoption.

**Step 4 — Synthesis.**
The EEG is jointly produced by inter-temporal inconsistency (hyperbolic discounting) and by the social meaning attached to energy-using goods. Rational models ignore both channels. Behavioural models that incorporate them restore explanatory power and yield testable policy implications: default-enrolment, peer-comparison letters, and front-loaded subsidies.

> **Conclusion:** Hyperbolic discounting and social-norm effects together account for a substantial fraction of the observed EEG. Pure rational models therefore systematically mis-predict technology adoption — a fact with direct implications for India's BEE, UJALA, and PM-Surya Ghar programmes.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

#### (a) *(7 marks)* With a suitable diagram, explain the **Nudge Theory** framework. How can it be applied to reduce peak electricity demand in Indian households?

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition.**
**Nudge Theory** (Thaler & Sunstein, 2008) is a framework of *libertarian paternalism* that alters people's behaviour by changing the **choice architecture** without forbidding options or significantly changing economic incentives.

**Step 2 — The Nudge framework — diagrammatic representation.**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Choice Architecture] --> B[Default Option]
    A --> C[Framing of Information]
    A --> D[Feedback and Salience]
    A --> E[Social Comparison]
    B --> F[Behavioural Outcome]
    C --> F
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[Reduced Peak Demand]
```

**Step 3 — Application to Indian peak demand.**

| Nudge Lever | Mechanism | Indian Implementation |
|---|---|---|
| Default option | Opt-out green tariff | EESL smart-meter default TOU pricing |
| Framing | Bill in kWh + ₹ with comparison | BEE comparative bill disclosure |
| Feedback | Real-time display | SAIL smart-meter in-home display |
| Social comparison | Compare to similar households | Utility "neighbourhood" reports |
| Commitment devices | Pre-commit to lower AC setpoint | App-based challenges during summer |

**Step 4 — Empirical support.**
The 2015 OPower experiment across 600,000 US households showed **1.4 – 3.3 %** peak-demand reduction from comparative bills alone. Translated to India's 250 million electrified households, even a 2 % reduction yields gigawatt-scale peak-shaving.

> **Conclusion:** Nudge-based interventions offer a low-cost, scalable complement to price-based and infrastructural measures for peak-demand management.

#### (b) *(7 marks)* Compute the rebound effect for the following case: A household replaces a 60 W bulb used 4 hours/day with a 9 W LED. Post-replacement, usage rises to 5 hours/day. Quantify the engineering savings, the actual savings, and the rebound percentage.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Engineering (technical) savings.**
Daily energy before: $60 \text{ W} \times 4 \text{ h} = 240 \text{ Wh/day}$
Daily energy after: $9 \text{ W} \times 5 \text{ h} = 45 \text{ Wh/day}$
Engineering saving: $\Delta E_E = 240 - 45 = 195 \text{ Wh/day}$

**Step 2 — Actual saving.**
$$\Delta E_{\text{actual}} = 240 - 45 = 195 \text{ Wh/day}$$

**Step 3 — Compute rebound.**
The rebound is the *rebound energy* (the extra usage beyond the technical minimum caused by behavioural response). Technical saving *if usage had stayed at 4 h*:
$$\Delta E_{\text{tech, fixed}} = 240 - (9 \times 4) = 240 - 36 = 204 \text{ Wh/day}$$

Rebound energy = technical saving (fixed-use) − actual saving:

$$\Delta E_{\text{rebound}} = 204 - 195 = 9 \text{ Wh/day}$$

**Step 4 — Rebound percentage.**

$$R = \frac{\Delta E_{\text{rebound}}}{\Delta E_{\text{tech, fixed}}} \times 100 = \frac{9}{204} \times 100 \approx 4.41\%$$

> **Conclusion:** The LED's efficiency gain is partially eroded by 4.4 % rebound from increased usage, which is well within the typical 10–30 % household rebound range — a behaviourally modest but economically real effect.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (KTU Board Examiner Reports):**
> 1. **Conflating bounded rationality with irrationality** — students write "consumers are stupid". The correct framing is that consumers are *constrained* by information, time, and cognitive bandwidth. *Loss: 1 mark.*
> 2. **Quoting formulas without units or values** — writing $V(x) = x^{\alpha}$ without specifying $\alpha = 0.88$ and $\lambda = 2.25$ is considered incomplete. *Loss: 1–2 marks.*
> 3. **Ignoring Indian context** — generic OECD examples are acceptable, but students who cite **UJALA, PM-Surya Ghar, BEE star labels, or EESL** get appreciation marks.
> 4. **Forgetting the difference between direct and indirect rebound** — examiners specifically test this distinction. Always state *both* and give a numerical example.
> 5. **Confusing energy efficiency gap with rebound effect** — EEG is a *static* deviation from optimum; rebound is a *dynamic* re-expansion of consumption after efficiency gains. *Loss: 2 marks if swapped.*
> 6. **Not drawing diagrams in 14-mark answers** — Part B answers without a flow chart / block diagram lose 1–2 marks by default, per KTU valuation key.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Behavioural aspects** = deviations from Homo Economicus in energy decision-making.
- **Five pillars:** bounded rationality, prospect theory, hyperbolic discounting, social norms, choice architecture.
- **Energy Efficiency Gap (EEG)** = $E_{opt} - E_{obs}$; typically **20 – 50 %**; key empirical motivation for behavioural modelling.
- **Prospect Theory value function** = $V(x) = \pm \vert x \vert^{\alpha}$; **loss aversion $\lambda = 2.25$**.
- **Quasi-hyperbolic discount** = $D(s,t) = \beta \cdot \delta^{t-s}$ with $\beta \in (0.5, 0.9)$ — the formal workhorse of present-bias analysis.
- **Rebound effect** = $R = \Delta E_R / \Delta E_E$; direct + indirect + economy-wide channels.
- **Nudge Theory** = libertarian paternalism via choice architecture; defaults, framing, feedback, social comparison.
- **Indian applications:** UJALA (LED), PM-Surya Ghar (solar), BEE star labels (appliances), EESL smart meters.
- **Key behavioural levers for policy:** default-on enrolment, gains-framed messaging, peer-comparison feedback, front-loaded subsidies, simplified tariff menus.
- **Always quantify:** A 14-mark answer must contain at least one numerical example, one diagram, and one Indian policy reference.
- **Examiner buzzwords to use:** *bounded rationality, satisficing, reference dependence, loss aversion, present bias, choice architecture, libertarian paternalism, rebound effect, social norms, energy efficiency gap.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Behaviour Changes for Sustainable Development, Energy Efficiency and Emission Abatement Relations, and Abatement of Emissions through Energy Efficiency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET526 - ENERGY ECONOMICS AND POLICY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Behaviour Changes for Sustainable Development, Energy Efficiency and Emission Abatement Relations, and Abatement of Emissions through Energy Abatement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1. Behaviour Changes for Sustainable Development

### Formal KTU Definition
**Sustainable Development** (per the Brundtland Commission, 1987, adopted globally in KTU 2024 Energy Policy syllabus) is "development that meets the needs of the present without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own needs." **Behaviour changes for sustainable development** refer to the deliberate, measurable shifts in individual, organisational, and societal conduct — including consumption patterns, mobility choices, dietary habits, and energy-use practices — that reduce the ecological footprint while maintaining or improving human well-being.

In the KTU 2024 PEMET526 syllabus, behavioural change is studied as a *soft-policy instrument* that complements hard-policy mechanisms (taxes, regulations, subsidies). It is operationalised through three theoretical lenses:

1. **Theory of Planned Behaviour (TPB)** — Ajzen, 1991: Attitude + Subjective Norms + Perceived Behavioural Control → Intention → Behaviour.
2. **Nudge Theory** — Thaler & Sunstein, 2008: Choice architecture modifications that predictably alter behaviour without forbidding options.
3. **Social Practice Theory** — Shove et al., 2012: Behaviour emerges from the dynamic interaction of materials, competencies, and meanings.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Behaviour change is **not a substitute for technology or policy** — it acts as a force multiplier. A 1 % behavioural shift across 1.4 billion Indians can offset emissions equivalent to dozens of thermal power stations.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine your household electricity bill. You could install a **5-star inverter AC** (technology intervention), the government could raise the **electricity tariff** (policy intervention), OR you could simply **switch off the AC at 26 °C and use a ceiling fan** (behavioural intervention). The first costs ₹40,000, the second may face political resistance, but the third is free, immediate, and scalable. **Behavioural change is the "free-lunch" lever of sustainable development** — it costs little but requires cultural acceptance.

---

## 2. Energy Efficiency and Emission Abatement Relations

### Formal Definition
**Energy Efficiency (EE)** is the ratio of useful energy output (or service) to energy input. It is commonly expressed as:

$$\eta = \frac{E_{\text{useful output}}}{E_{\text{input}}} \times 100\%$$

**Emission Abatement** is any deliberate action (technological, behavioural, or structural) that reduces the quantity of greenhouse gases (GHGs) — primarily **CO₂, CH₄, N₂O** — released into the atmosphere per unit of economic activity or per unit of energy consumed.

The **Energy Efficiency–Emission Abatement Relation** describes the quantitative linkage: improvements in energy efficiency reduce specific energy consumption, which (all else equal) lowers fuel combustion and thereby **abates CO₂ emissions**. The mathematical identity linking them is the **Kaya Identity** (Kaya & Yokobori, 1997):

$$CO_2 = P \times \frac{GDP}{P} \times \frac{E}{GDP} \times \frac{CO_2}{E}$$

Where $P$ = Population, $E$ = Primary Energy. The term $\frac{CO_2}{E}$ is the **carbon intensity of energy**, and $\frac{E}{GDP}$ is the **energy intensity of GDP** (the inverse of energy efficiency).

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of your car. **Energy efficiency** is the *miles per litre* (kmpl) your car delivers. **Emission abatement** is the *CO₂ coming out of the tailpipe*. If you improve kmpl from 12 to 18 (a 50 % efficiency gain), for the same distance travelled, you burn 33 % less fuel, and the tailpipe CO₂ drops by 33 % (assuming the same fuel). The Kaya Identity simply unpacks **who** (population), **how rich** (GDP/capita), **how efficiently** (E/GDP), and **how cleanly** (CO₂/E) we produce emissions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric (KTU 2024):** India reports emissions in **tonnes of CO₂-equivalent (tCO₂e)** under the UNFCCC framework. The standard GWP-100 values are **CO₂ = 1, CH₄ = 28, N₂O = 265** (IPCC AR5, used in India's 2022 National Inventory).

---

## 3. Abatement of Emissions through Energy Efficiency

### Formal Definition
**Abatement of Emissions through Energy Efficiency (AEEE)** is the deliberate set of measures — technological upgrades, process redesign, waste-heat recovery, building retrofits, efficient appliances, and behavioural conservation — that reduce energy input for the same useful output, thereby proportionally reducing combustion-related GHG emissions.

Mathematically, the *avoided emissions* from an EE intervention are:

$$\Delta CO_2 = \eta_{\text{grid}} \times EF_{\text{grid}} \times \Delta E_{\text{conserved}}$$

Where:
- $\Delta CO_2$ = avoided emissions (tCO₂/year)
- $\eta_{\text{grid}}$ = share of fossil-fuel-based generation in the grid mix
- $EF_{\text{grid}}$ = emission factor of the grid (typically **0.71 tCO₂/MWh** for India, CEA 2023)
- $\Delta E_{\text{conserved}}$ = energy conserved (MWh/year) due to the EE measure

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine a leaky water tap wasting 10 litres/hour. The "behavioural fix" is to remember to tighten it. The "energy efficiency fix" is to replace the washer so the tap drips zero. The "abatement" is the water you **stopped wasting** — directly visible on your water bill. Similarly, every unit of energy you stop wasting translates into a unit of CO₂ you stopped emitting. AEEE is therefore the **cheapest, fastest, and most politically palatable** decarbonisation lever — a 2022 IEA report states that **energy efficiency alone could deliver over 40 % of the emissions reductions needed by 2040**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Focus:** The *abatement cost* of EE measures (₹/tCO₂e avoided) is typically **negative** — meaning EE *saves money* while abating emissions. This is the "**negawatt**" concept (Lovins, 1976) — a unit of conserved energy is cheaper than a unit of generated energy.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Behavioural Change for Sustainability

Behavioural change for sustainable development rests on three interacting pillars (per KTU Module 2 lecture framework):

### Pillar 1 — Cognitive (Information & Awareness)
- Knowledge about climate change, energy labels, carbon footprint.
- **Limitation:** Information alone rarely changes behaviour — the **"Knowledge–Action Gap"** (Kollmuss & Agyeman, 2002).
- *KTU example:* BEE star-label awareness is high (78 %, BEE 2022 survey) but actual purchase of 5-star appliances remains ~32 % due to higher upfront cost.

### Pillar 2 — Structural (Choice Architecture)
- Default options, framing, salience, simplification.
- **Nudges** (Thaler & Sunstein) are low-cost interventions that alter choices predictably.
- *KTU example:* Defaulting household electricity bills to **e-bills + auto-pay** reduced paper use by 41 % in Kerala DISCOM pilots (2021).

### Pillar 3 — Social (Norms & Identity)
- Descriptive norms ("most people do X"), injunctive norms ("people should do X"), and identity.
- *KTU example:* Showing a household its **electricity consumption vs. its neighbours' average** ("social comparison bill") cut consumption by **1.8 – 2.0 %** in OPower-style field trials globally.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Whenever you write about behavioural change, cite the **3-pillar framework** (Cognitive–Structural–Social). It is a high-yield mark-scoring structure in 14-mark answers.

---

## 2.2 Energy Efficiency — Definitions and Metrics

### First-Law (Thermodynamic) Efficiency
$$\eta_1 = \frac{E_{\text{useful}}}{E_{\text{in}}} \quad \text{(dimensionless, 0 to 1)}$$

### Second-Law (Exergy) Efficiency
$$\eta_2 = \frac{Ex_{\text{useful}}}{Ex_{\text{in}}}$$

Exergy-based EE is always ≤ First-Law EE and is the more honest indicator because it accounts for the *quality* of energy.

### Economy-Wide Energy Intensity
$$EI = \frac{\text{Total Primary Energy Supply (TPES)}}{GDP} \quad \text{(toe / million ₹ or MJ/₹)}$$

- Lower $EI$ ⇒ higher macro-level energy efficiency.
- India (2022): ~0.20 kgoe / $ GDP-PPP (2017 prices) — among the highest (least efficient) globally.
- EU average: ~0.11 kgoe / $ GDP-PPP.

### ODEX (ODYSSEE Energy Efficiency Index)
An aggregated, weighted index where a decrease of 1 point = **1 % energy saving** vs. a baseline year. India is not yet an ODEX member, but EU uses it for policy monitoring.

---

## 2.3 The Kaya Identity — The Heart of EE–Emissions Relation

The Kaya Identity decomposes CO₂ emissions into four multiplicative drivers:

$$CO_2 = P \times g \times e \times c$$

Where:
| Symbol | Variable | Unit | KTU Notation |
|--------|----------|------|--------------|
| $CO_2$ | Total CO₂ emissions | GtCO₂/yr | $F$ |
| $P$ | Population | billion | $P$ |
| $g$ | Per-capita GDP | $/person | $\frac{GDP}{P}$ |
| $e$ | Energy intensity of GDP | MJ/$ or toe/$ | $\frac{E}{GDP}$ |
| $c$ | Carbon intensity of energy | tCO₂/toe | $\frac{CO_2}{E}$ |

**Growth-rate form** (logarithmic differentiation, used in KTU numericals):

$$r_{CO_2} = r_P + r_g + r_e + r_c$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trick Question Alert:** If population is constant and GDP grows 5 %/yr, energy intensity improves (drops) 2 %/yr, and carbon intensity is constant, what is $r_{CO_2}$? Answer: $0 + 5 - 2 + 0 = +3\%$/yr. **Energy efficiency gains are often *swallowed* by economic growth** — this is the *rebound effect in macro form*.

---

## 2.4 The Rebound Effect — The "Hidden Tax" on EE

When EE improves, the *effective cost* of energy services falls, which induces **additional consumption**. This is the **Rebound Effect**, quantified as:

$$RE = \frac{\text{Additional energy consumption due to behavioural response}}{\text{Engineering energy savings}} \times 100\%$$

### Three Sub-Categories

| Sub-Category | Mechanism | Typical Magnitude | KTU Example |
|--------------|-----------|-------------------|-------------|
| **Direct Rebound** | Same equipment used more intensively | 10 – 30 % | Buying a fuel-efficient car → driving more |
| **Indirect Rebound** | Saved money spent on energy-using goods | 20 – 50 % | LED bulb savings spent on a new fridge |
| **Economy-Wide Rebound** | Aggregate demand stimulus across economy | 50 – 100 %+ | Lower national energy demand → lower prices → industrial expansion |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** A naïve 50 % energy-saving measure does NOT yield 50 % emission cuts. If the economy-wide rebound is 60 %, the **net climate benefit is only 20 %**. Always include rebound in long-run emission projections.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | $\eta = \frac{E_{\text{out}}}{E_{\text{in}}}$ | Energy efficiency (First Law) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $EI = \frac{TPES}{GDP}$ | Energy intensity of economy | kgoe/$ or MJ/₹ |
| 3 | $CO_2 = P \cdot g \cdot e \cdot c$ | Kaya Identity | GtCO₂/yr |
| 4 | $r_{CO_2} = r_P + r_g + r_e + r_c$ | Kaya growth decomposition | %/yr |
| 5 | $RE = \frac{\Delta E_{\text{induced}}}{\Delta E_{\text{engineering}}}$ | Rebound effect | % |
| 6 | $\Delta CO_2 = \eta_{\text{fossil}} \cdot EF_{\text{grid}} \cdot \Delta E_{\text{saved}}$ | Abatement from EE | tCO₂/yr |
| 7 | $ACS = \frac{C_{\text{EE}} - C_{\text{baseline}}}{E_{\text{saved}} \cdot EF}$ | Abatement cost (negative = profitable) | ₹/tCO₂ |
| 8 | $\text{COP} = \frac{Q_{\text{heating/cooling}}}{W_{\text{electrical}}}$ | Coefficient of Performance (HVAC) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $E_{\text{saved}} = E_{\text{baseline}} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{\eta_{\text{old}}}{\eta_{\text{new}}}\right)$ | Energy saved from retrofit | kWh/yr |
| 10 | $D = 1 - \frac{e_t}{e_0}$ | Decoupling index (relative) | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** These equations are used in (a) **PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade) scheme** of BEE, (b) **ESCO (Energy Service Company)** business models, (c) **Carbon Credit** project design under CDM/Article 6.4, and (d) **NDC (Nationally Determined Contribution)** reporting by India to the UNFCCC.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Kaya Identity from First Principles

The Kaya Identity is not an axiom — it is a **definitional rearrangement** of three fundamental ratios. We derive it step-by-step.

**Step 1 — Total emissions equal carbon intensity times total energy.**

$$CO_2 = \left(\frac{CO_2}{E}\right) \times E = c \cdot E$$

**Step 2 — Total energy equals energy intensity times GDP.**

$$E = \left(\frac{E}{GDP}\right) \times GDP = e \cdot GDP$$

**Step 3 — GDP equals per-capita GDP times population.**

$$GDP = \left(\frac{GDP}{P}\right) \times P = g \cdot P$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Steps 2 and 3 into Step 1.**

$$CO_2 = c \cdot (e \cdot GDP) = c \cdot e \cdot (g \cdot P) = P \cdot g \cdot e \cdot c$$

$$\boxed{CO_2 = P \times g \times e \times c}$$

**Step 5 — Take natural logarithms to obtain the additive (growth-rate) form.**

$$\ln(CO_2) = \ln(P) + \ln(g) + \ln(e) + \ln(c)$$

**Step 6 — Differentiate with respect to time** (note: $r_x = \frac{1}{x} \frac{dx}{dt} = \frac{d \ln x}{dt}$):

$$\frac{d \ln CO_2}{dt} = \frac{d \ln P}{dt} + \frac{d \ln g}{dt} + \frac{d \ln e}{dt} + \frac{d \ln c}{dt}$$

**Step 7 — Recognise each term as a percentage growth rate.**

$$\boxed{r_{CO_2} = r_P + r_g + r_e + r_c}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The first form (multiplicative) is used for *absolute* level decomposition. The second form (additive) is used for *growth attribution* — i.e., "How much of India's emissions growth is due to population vs. affluence vs. inefficiency vs. carbon-intensity?" This is the standard tool of the **IPCC** and **IEA** in national-emissions accounting.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical — Kaya Decomposition (India 2010 → 2020)

**Given Data (India, 2010 vs. 2020):**

| Variable | 2010 | 2020 | CAGR (%) |
|----------|------|------|----------|
| Population $P$ (billion) | 1.181 | 1.380 | +1.57 |
| GDP $g$ (₹ lakh crore, 2011-12 prices) | 72.0 | 105.0 | +3.85 |
| TPES $E$ (Mtoe) | 549 | 695 | +2.39 |
| CO₂ emissions $F$ (MtCO₂) | 1693 | 2400 | +3.55 |
| Carbon intensity $c$ (tCO₂/toe) | 3.08 | 3.45 | +1.14 |

**Step 1 — Compute carbon intensity 2010.**

$$c_{2010} = \frac{CO_2}{E} = \frac{1693 \text{ MtCO₂}}{549 \text{ Mtoe}} = 3.084 \text{ tCO₂/toe}$$

**Step 2 — Compute carbon intensity 2020.**

$$c_{2020} = \frac{2400}{695} = 3.453 \text{ tCO₂/toe}$$

**Step 3 — Compute energy intensity 2010 and 2020.**

$$e_{2010} = \frac{549 \text{ Mtoe}}{72.0 \text{ ₹ lakh crore}} = 7.625 \text{ Mtoe per ₹ lakh crore}$$

$$e_{2020} = \frac{695}{105.0} = 6.619 \text{ Mtoe per ₹ lakh crore}$$

**Step 4 — Compute per-capita GDP 2010 and 2020.**

$$g_{2010} = \frac{72.0}{1.181} = 60.96 \text{ ₹ lakh crore per billion person}$$

$$g_{2020} = \frac{105.0}{1.380} = 76.09 \text{ ₹ lakh crore per billion person}$$

**Step 5 — Apply multiplicative Kaya check for 2010.**

$$P \times g \times e \times c = 1.181 \times 60.96 \times 7.625 \times 3.084$$

$$= 1.181 \times 60.96 = 71.99$$

$$= 71.99 \times 7.625 = 548.9$$

$$= 548.9 \times 3.084 = 1692.8 \approx 1693 \text{ MtCO₂} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 6 — Apply multiplicative Kaya check for 2020.**

$$1.380 \times 76.09 \times 6.619 \times 3.453 = 2400.0 \text{ MtCO₂} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 7 — Growth-rate decomposition (Kaya additive form).**

$$r_{CO_2} = r_P + r_g + r_e + r_c$$

$$+3.55 = +1.57 + r_g + r_e + +1.14$$

We need $r_g$ and $r_e$:

$$r_g = \frac{76.09 - 60.96}{60.96 \times 10} = \frac{15.13}{609.6} = +2.48 \text{ \%/yr}$$

$$r_e = \frac{6.619 - 7.625}{7.625 \times 10} = \frac{-1.006}{76.25} = -1.32 \text{ \%/yr}$$

**Step 8 — Verify the decomposition.**

$$r_P + r_g + r_e + r_c = 1.57 + 2.48 - 1.32 + 1.14 = 3.87 \text{ \%/yr}$$

This is close to (but not exactly equal to) the observed $r_{CO_2} = 3.55$ %/yr — the residual (~0.32 %/yr) is the **rounding/CAGR interaction term** typical of discrete Kaya decompositions.

**Interpretation (KTU Board Answer Frame):**
- *Population growth contributed +1.57 %/yr* — the smallest driver.
- *Affluence (g) contributed +2.48 %/yr* — the largest push, reflecting rising Indian incomes.
- *Energy intensity improved by 1.32 %/yr* — this is the EE dividend.
- *Carbon intensity WORSENED by +1.14 %/yr* — because India added more coal in 2010–2020.
- *Net: Emissions grew 3.55–3.87 %/yr.* Even strong EE gains were outpaced by growth + dirtier fuel mix.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Insight:** This is why **EE alone is insufficient** — India must also pursue *carbon-intensity reduction* (renewables, fuel-switching). The Kaya Identity is the **policy-target framework** India used to set its NDC (Panchamrit, COP-26, Glasgow 2021): 45 % reduction in *emissions intensity of GDP* by 2030 vs. 2005.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Behavioural Levers vs. Abatement Levers

This matrix maps which behavioural and technological levers address which Kaya-driver:

| Kaya Driver | Behavioural Lever | Technological / Policy Lever | EE–Emission Impact |
|-------------|-------------------|------------------------------|---------------------|
| $r_P$ (population) | Family-planning education, women's empowerment | — (slow lever) | Very long-term |
| $r_g$ (affluence) | Sufficiency culture, degrowth advocacy | Green fiscal policy (progressive carbon tax) | Indirect, contested |
| $r_e$ (energy intensity) | Switching off, set-point changes (AC at 26 °C), public transport, walking/cycling, plant-based diet | 5-star appliances, LED retrofit, BEE standards, PAT scheme | **Direct, highest leverage** |
| $r_c$ (carbon intensity) | Choosing RE tariffs, EV adoption | Solar/wind, nuclear, hydrogen, coal retirement | **Direct, highest leverage** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Strategic Takeaway for KTU:** India's NDC primarily attacks $r_e$ and $r_c$ through EE and renewables. Behavioural change is treated as an *accelerant* — multiplying the technical impact at low cost.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Abatement-Cost Equation

**Step 1 — Define the Baseline and Project scenarios.**

Baseline annual energy use: $E_0$ (kWh/yr).
Post-EE retrofit annual energy use: $E_1 = E_0(1 - s)$ where $s$ is the fractional saving (e.g., 0.30 for 30 % saving).

**Step 2 — Annual energy saved.**

$$\Delta E = E_0 - E_1 = E_0 \cdot s$$

**Step 3 — Annual emissions abated.**

$$\Delta CO_2 = \Delta E \cdot EF_{\text{grid}}$$

**Step 4 — Annualised cost of the EE measure.**

Let $C_0$ = capital cost (₹), $n$ = equipment life (yr), $r$ = discount rate, $M$ = annual O&M (₹).

Annualised cost:

$$AC = C_0 \cdot CRF + M$$

where the **Capital Recovery Factor** is:

$$CRF = \frac{r(1+r)^n}{(1+r)^n - 1}$$

**Step 5 — Abatement cost (₹ per tCO₂e avoided).**

$$ACS = \frac{AC - S_{\text{energy bill}}}{\Delta CO_2}$$

where $S_{\text{energy bill}} = \Delta E \cdot P_{\text{electricity}}$ is the annual bill saving.

**Step 6 — Interpretation:**
- $ACS < 0$ ⇒ the EE measure is **profitable** (negawatt concept).
- $ACS > 0$ ⇒ the measure is *technically* effective but *economically* expensive — needs subsidy.
- $ACS$ is the *shadow price* used in carbon markets and CDM projects.

**Worked Example:** LED replacement in a household.

- $E_0 = 1000$ kWh/yr (incandescent), $s = 0.80$ → $\Delta E = 800$ kWh/yr.
- $EF_{\text{grid}} = 0.71$ tCO₂/MWh = 0.00071 tCO₂/kWh.
- $\Delta CO_2 = 800 \times 0.00071 = 0.568$ tCO₂/yr.
- $P_{\elec} = ₹7$/kWh → $S = 800 \times 7 = ₹5{,}600$/yr.
- LED cost: ₹1,500 for 10 bulbs, life = 8 yr, $r$ = 8 %, O&M = ₹0.
- $CRF = \frac{0.08(1.08)^8}{(1.08)^8 - 1} = \frac{0.08 \times 1.8509}{0.8509} = 0.1740$.
- $AC = 1500 \times 0.1740 = ₹261$/yr.
- $ACS = \frac{261 - 5600}{0.568} = \frac{-5339}{0.568} = -₹9{,}400$/tCO₂e.
- **Negative ACS** → highly profitable emission abatement.

## 4.3 The Rebound Effect Cascade

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    eng[EE IMPROVEMENT] --> save[ENGINEERING ENERGY SAVING 100 PCT]
    save --> d1[DIRECT REBOUND 10 TO 30 PCT]
    save --> d2[INDIRECT REBOUND 20 TO 50 PCT]
    save --> d3[ECONOMY WIDE REBOUND 50 TO 100 PCT]
    d1 --> net1[NET SAVING 70 TO 90 PCT]
    d2 --> net2[NET SAVING 30 TO 70 PCT]
    d3 --> net3[NET SAVING 0 TO 30 PCT]
    net3 --> outcome[FINAL CLIMATE BENEFIT]
    net2 --> outcome
    net1 --> outcome
    style eng fill:#0d6efd,color:#fff
    style save fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd
    style d1 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107
    style d2 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545
    style d3 fill:#f5c2c7,stroke:#b02a37
    style outcome fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Engineering savings are *eroded* by three layers of behavioural response. Economy-wide rebound can **completely cancel** the direct EE benefit, which is why KTU emphasises **complementary carbon pricing** to neutralise rebound.

---

## 4.4 EE-to-Abatement Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[ENERGY CONSUMPTION] --> B[USEFUL OUTPUT SERVICE]
    A --> C[WASTE HEAT LOSS]
    A --> D[INEFFICIENCY LOSS]
    B --> E[DESIRED SERVICE]
    C --> F[UNCONTROLLED EMISSIONS]
    D --> F
    P[EE INTERVENTION] --> B
    P --> C
    P --> D
    P --> G[REBOUND RESPONSE]
    G --> B
    P --> H[NET ABATEMENT]
    F --> H
    style A fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd
    style P fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107
    style H fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754
    style G fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545
    style F fill:#f5c2c7,stroke:#b02a37
```

> **Reading the diagram:** EE intervention reduces inputs to service. But it also triggers a rebound loop that re-expands service demand. The **net abatement** is the difference between *avoided emissions* and *rebound-induced emissions*. A well-designed AEEE policy couples EE measures with **carbon pricing or caps** to suppress the rebound loop.
