topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET526 - ENERGY ECONOMICS AND POLICY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Energy Market Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3: ENERGY MARKET ANALYSIS

## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Energy Market Analysis** is the systematic application of microeconomic theory, statistical methods, and policy frameworks to evaluate the **demand, supply, pricing, and equilibrium dynamics** of energy commodities (electricity, natural gas, coal, oil, and renewables) under varying market structures and regulatory environments.

It encompasses the study of **producer and consumer behavior**, **market clearing mechanisms**, **price formation**, **elasticities of demand and supply**, **market power**, and the **welfare implications** of alternative policy interventions in the energy sector.

### 1.2 Key Terminology in Energy Markets

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Market Clearing Price** | The price at which the quantity demanded equals the quantity supplied (Equilibrium Price, $P^*$) |
| **Merit Order Dispatch** | Ranking of power generators by their short-run marginal cost (SRMC) |
| **Price Elasticity of Demand (PED)** | Responsiveness of quantity demanded to a 1% change in price |
| **Inelastic Demand** | Demand that changes by a smaller percentage than the price change ($\vert E_d \vert < 1$) |
| **Deregulation / Liberalization** | Removal of government controls to allow competitive market forces |
| **Market Power** | Ability of a firm to profitably maintain prices above competitive levels |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Vegetable Market vs. The Electricity Grid:**
>
> Imagine a vegetable market where prices fluctuate hourly based on **demand (buyers)** and **supply (farmers)**. Now imagine that **vegetables cannot be stored cheaply** (electricity faces the same issue — storage is costly), **the supply cannot be instantly increased** (power plants take hours to start), and **buyers will pay almost any price** because they need it (hospitals, factories). This is the unique world of **energy markets** — they behave like commodity markets but with **temporal rigidity, infrastructure constraints, and price-inelastic demand**.

**Real-world example:** During a winter cold wave, electricity demand spikes, but supply cannot increase immediately. Prices surge 10×, yet consumption barely falls — this is **inelastic demand** in action.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics Used

- **Megawatt-hour (MWh)** — unit of electrical energy
- **$/MWh or ₹/kWh** — standard pricing units
- **Load Factor** = $\frac{\text{Average Load}}{\text{Peak Load}}$
- **Capacity Factor** = $\frac{\text{Actual Energy Generated}}{\text{Maximum Possible Energy}$
- **Barrel of Oil Equivalent (BOE)** = **5.8 million BTU** ≈ **1,700 kWh**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Framework of Energy Market Analysis

Energy Market Analysis proceeds through **four sequential analytical pillars**:

### Pillar 1: Demand-Side Analysis
- **Determinants of energy demand:** GDP, industrial output, population, weather, energy efficiency, technology.
- **Short-run demand** is **price inelastic** (consumers cannot quickly switch fuels or appliances).
- **Long-run demand** becomes more elastic as consumers invest in efficiency.

### Pillar 2: Supply-Side Analysis
- **Determinants of energy supply:** Resource availability, production costs, technology, regulation, capacity.
- The **short-run supply curve** in electricity is **near-vertical** because capacity is fixed in the short run.
- The **long-run supply curve** is **more elastic** as new plants can be built.

### Pillar 3: Market Equilibrium & Price Formation
- Equilibrium occurs where $Q_d = Q_s$.
- In **deregulated electricity markets**, prices clear through **auctions** (e.g., day-ahead, real-time markets).
- The **merit order dispatch** principle ranks generators from lowest to highest marginal cost.

### Pillar 4: Welfare & Policy Analysis
- **Consumer Surplus (CS):** Benefit consumers receive by paying less than their willingness to pay.
- **Producer Surplus (PS):** Benefit producers receive by selling above their marginal cost.
- **Total Welfare (TW):** $TW = CS + PS$
- **Deadweight Loss (DWL):** Welfare loss due to market distortion (taxes, market power, regulation).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU EXAM CHEAT SHEET — All Critical Formulas for Energy Market Analysis**

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Linear Demand Function | $Q_d = a - bP$ | $a, b > 0$; $P$ in ₹/kWh |
| 2 | Linear Supply Function | $Q_s = c + dP$ | $c \ge 0$, $d > 0$ |
| 3 | Market Equilibrium | $a - bP^* = c + dP^*$ | Solve for $P^*, Q^*$ |
| 4 | Equilibrium Price | $P^* = \frac{a-c}{b+d}$ | ₹/kWh |
| 5 | Equilibrium Quantity | $Q^* = a - b\left(\frac{a-c}{b+d}\right)$ | MWh or kWh |
| 6 | Price Elasticity of Demand (PED) | $E_d = \frac{\Delta Q_d / Q_d}{\Delta P / P} = \frac{dQ_d}{dP} \cdot \frac{P}{Q_d}$ | Unitless |
| 7 | Point Elasticity (Linear Demand) | $E_d = \frac{-b \cdot P}{a - bP}$ | Negative for downward-sloping demand |
| 8 | Income Elasticity of Demand | $E_y = \frac{dQ_d}{dY} \cdot \frac{Y}{Q_d}$ | Positive for normal goods |
| 9 | Cross-Price Elasticity | $E_{xy} = \frac{dQ_x}{dP_y} \cdot \frac{P_y}{Q_x}$ | Positive = substitutes |
| 10 | Consumer Surplus (Linear Demand) | $CS = \frac{1}{2} \cdot Q^* \cdot (P_{max} - P^*)$ | Where $P_{max} = a/b$ |
| 11 | Producer Surplus (Linear Supply) | $PS = \frac{1}{2} \cdot Q^* \cdot (P^* - P_{min})$ | Where $P_{min} = -c/d$ |
| 12 | Total Welfare | $TW = CS + PS$ | Maximized at competitive equilibrium |
| 13 | Total Revenue | $TR = P \cdot Q$ | ₹ or $ |
| 14 | Marginal Revenue (Linear) | $MR = P \left(1 + \frac{1}{E_d}\right)$ | Always below $P$ when $E_d < 0$ |
| 15 | Lerner Index (Market Power) | $L = \frac{P - MC}{P} = -\frac{1}{E_d}$ | Measures monopoly power |
| 16 | Load Factor | $LF = \frac{\text{Average Load}}{\text{Peak Load}}$ | $0 < LF \le 1$ |
| 17 | Capacity Factor | $CF = \frac{\text{Actual Output}}{\text{Installed Capacity} \times 8760 \text{ hrs}}$ | For power plants |
| 18 | Present Value (PV) | $PV = \frac{FV}{(1+r)^n}$ | $r$ = discount rate |
| 19 | Net Present Value (NPV) | $NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{N} \frac{B_t - C_t}{(1+r)^t}$ | $B_t$ = benefits, $C_t$ = costs |
| 20 | Levelized Cost of Energy (LCOE) | $LCOE = \frac{\sum_{t} \frac{C_t}{(1+r)^t}}{\sum_{t} \frac{E_t}{(1+r)^t}}$ | ₹/MWh |

## 2.3 Real-World Utility

These formulas are used by:
- **Regulators (CERC, FERC, ERCOT)** to set tariffs and assess market power.
- **Power Exchanges (IEX, PJM, Nord Pool)** for day-ahead market clearing.
- **Energy planners (CEA, IEA)** for long-term capacity planning.
- **Policy makers** to design carbon pricing, subsidies, and renewable obligations.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Market Equilibrium (Algebraic)

**Given:**
- Demand: $Q_d = 500 - 4P$
- Supply: $Q_s = -100 + 6P$
where $P$ is in ₹/kWh and $Q$ is in million kWh.

**Step 1 — Set $Q_d = Q_s$ for equilibrium:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_d &= Q_s \\
500 - 4P &= -100 + 6P
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Collect like terms on each side:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
500 + 100 &= 6P + 4P \\
600 &= 10P
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for equilibrium price $P^*$:**

$$
P^* = \frac{600}{10} = 60 \text{ ₹/kWh}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $P^*$ into the demand function:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q^* &= 500 - 4(60) \\
Q^* &= 500 - 240 \\
Q^* &= 260 \text{ million kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification using supply:**

$$
Q_s = -100 + 6(60) = -100 + 360 = 260 \text{ million kWh} \;\; \checkmark
$$

> **Result:** $P^* = 60$ ₹/kWh, $Q^* = 260$ million kWh.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Price Elasticity of Demand (Point Elasticity)

**Given demand:** $Q_d = 500 - 4P$, find $E_d$ at $P = 60$ ₹/kWh.

**Step 1 — Differentiate $Q_d$ with respect to $P$:**

$$
\frac{dQ_d}{dP} = -4
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate $Q_d$ at $P = 60$:**

$$
Q_d = 500 - 4(60) = 260
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the point elasticity formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_d &= \frac{dQ_d}{dP} \cdot \frac{P}{Q_d} \\
E_d &= (-4) \cdot \frac{60}{260} \\
E_d &= \frac{-240}{260} \\
E_d &\approx -0.923
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** $\vert E_d \vert = 0.923 < 1$, confirming that energy demand is **price inelastic** at this point. A 1% price increase reduces quantity demanded by only 0.923%.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Consumer Surplus, Producer Surplus & Total Welfare

Using the same equilibrium values:
- $P^* = 60$ ₹/kWh, $Q^* = 260$ million kWh
- $P_{max} = a/b = 500/4 = 125$ ₹/kWh (price intercept of demand)
- $P_{min} = -c/d = -(-100)/6 \approx 16.67$ ₹/kWh (price intercept of supply)

**Step 1 — Consumer Surplus (CS):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CS &= \frac{1}{2} \cdot Q^* \cdot (P_{max} - P^*) \\
CS &= \frac{1}{2} \cdot 260 \cdot (125 - 60) \\
CS &= \frac{1}{2} \cdot 260 \cdot 65 \\
CS &= 8{,}450 \text{ million ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Producer Surplus (PS):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PS &= \frac{1}{2} \cdot Q^* \cdot (P^* - P_{min}) \\
PS &= \frac{1}{2} \cdot 260 \cdot (60 - 16.67) \\
PS &= \frac{1}{2} \cdot 260 \cdot 43.33 \\
PS &= 5{,}633 \text{ million ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Total Welfare:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TW &= CS + PS \\
TW &= 8{,}450 + 5{,}633 \\
TW &= 14{,}083 \text{ million ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Welfare Loss from a Price Ceiling (Government Intervention)

Suppose the government imposes a **price ceiling** at $P_c = 50$ ₹/kWh (below $P^* = 60$).

**Step 1 — Quantity supplied at $P_c$:**

$$
Q_s = -100 + 6(50) = 200 \text{ million kWh}
$$

**Step 2 — Quantity demanded at $P_c$:**

$$
Q_d = 500 - 4(50) = 300 \text{ million kWh}
$$

**Step 3 — Shortage:**

$$
\text{Shortage} = Q_d - Q_s = 300 - 200 = 100 \text{ million kWh}
$$

**Step 4 — Deadweight Loss (DWL):**

The DWL is the triangle between the demand and supply curves from $Q_s = 200$ to $Q^* = 260$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
DWL &= \frac{1}{2} \cdot (Q^* - Q_s) \cdot [P_d(Q_s) - P_s(Q_s)] \\
P_d(200) &= \frac{500 - 200}{4} = 75 \text{ ₹/kWh} \\
P_s(200) &= \frac{200 + 100}{6} = 50 \text{ ₹/kWh} \\
DWL &= \frac{1}{2} \cdot (260 - 200) \cdot (75 - 50) \\
DWL &= \frac{1}{2} \cdot 60 \cdot 25 \\
DWL &= 750 \text{ million ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Insight:** The price ceiling creates a shortage of 100 million kWh and a deadweight loss of ₹750 million — illustrating why energy subsidies and price controls, while politically attractive, are economically costly.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Energy Market Structures

| Feature | Perfect Competition | Monopoly | Oligopoly | Monopolistic Competition |
|---------|--------------------|---------|-----------| ------------------------|
| Number of firms | Many | One | Few | Many |
| Product differentiation | Homogeneous | Unique | Differentiated | Differentiated |
| Price control | None (price taker) | Significant | Mutual interdependence | Limited |
| Barriers to entry | None | Very high | High | Low |
| Energy example | Spot oil markets (approx.) | National Grid (transmission) | Big-3 power generators | Distributed solar installers |
| Allocative efficiency | Achieved ($P = MC$) | Not achieved ($P > MC$) | Often not achieved | Approximate |

---

# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Energy Market Analysis — Conceptual Architecture

The diagram below maps the **end-to-end flow** of an energy market analysis: starting from raw data inputs, proceeding through demand/supply modelling, equilibrium determination, welfare calculation, and policy feedback.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Input Data: Prices, Consumption, GDP, Weather, Capacity] --> B1[Demand-Side Modelling]
    A2[Input Data: Fuel Costs, Plant Capacity, Technology, Regulation] --> B2[Supply-Side Modelling]
    
    B1 --> C1[Estimate Demand Function Qd = a - bP]
    B2 --> C2[Estimate Supply Function Qs = c + dP]
    
    C1 --> D1[Compute Elasticities: PED, Income, Cross-Price]
    C2 --> D2[Compute Marginal Cost, Lerner Index]
    
    D1 --> E1[Determine Market Equilibrium: Qd = Qs]
    D2 --> E1
    
    E1 --> F1[Equilibrium Price P* and Quantity Q*]
    
    F1 --> G1[Welfare Analysis: CS, PS, DWL]
    F1 --> G2[Policy Simulation: Tax, Subsidy, Price Ceiling]
    
    G1 --> H1[Total Welfare TW = CS + PS]
    G2 --> H2[New Market Outcomes: P_new, Q_new, DWL]
    
    H1 --> I1[Output: Policy Recommendation]
    H2 --> I1
    
    I1 --> J1[Feedback to Market Participants and Regulators]
    
    J1 --> A1
    J1 --> A2
```

## 4.2 Energy Market Equilibrium Graph (Schematic)

The diagram below represents the **standard supply-demand graph** used in all KTU market analysis problems. The shaded regions indicate the welfare components (CS and PS) computed in Section 3.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Vertical Axis: Price P in rupees per kWh] -->|Plot| B[Demand Curve Qd: Downward Sloping]
    A -->|Plot| C[Supply Curve Qs: Upward Sloping]
    B -->|Intersects at| D[Equilibrium Point: P star 60 and Q star 260]
    C -->|Intersects at| D
    
    D --> E[Region Above P star Below Demand Curve: Consumer Surplus CS]
    D --> F[Region Below P star Above Supply Curve: Producer Surplus PS]
    
    D --> G[If Price Ceiling at 50: New Quantity Qs 200, Shortage = 100, DWL Triangle]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Energy Market Participants and Information Flows

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT[Supply Side]
        S1[Conventional Generators: Coal, Gas, Nuclear]
        S2[Renewable Generators: Solar, Wind, Hydro]
        S3[Distributed Energy: Rooftop Solar, Storage]
    end
    
    subgraph MARKET[Market Clearing Mechanism]
        M1[Power Exchange: Day-Ahead Auction]

    end

    end
    
    subgraph REG[Regulatory Oversight]
        R1[Regulator: CERC, SERC]
        R2[System Operator: Load Dispatch Centre]
        R3[Policy Maker: Ministry of Power]
    end
    
    S1 --> M1
    S2 --> M1
    S3 --> M1
    M1 --> D1
    M1 --> D2
    M1 --> D3
    M2 --> D4
    M3 --> D1
    R1 -.Monitors.-> M1
    R2 -.Balances.-> M2
    R3 -.Sets Policy.-> REG
```

# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define the term "Price Elasticity of Demand" as applied to energy markets. Why is energy demand typically considered price inelastic in the short run?**

**Model Answer:**

Price Elasticity of Demand (PED) measures the **responsiveness of quantity demanded** of an energy commodity to a change in its price. Mathematically:

$$
E_d = \frac{\% \Delta Q_d}{\% \Delta P} = \frac{dQ_d}{dP} \cdot \frac{P}{Q_d}
$$

In the **short run**, energy demand is typically **price inelastic** ($\vert E_d \vert < 1$) for three reasons:

1. **Lack of substitutes:** Consumers cannot instantly switch to alternative fuels or appliances.
2. **Capital lock-in:** Industrial equipment, vehicles, and buildings are designed for specific energy types.
3. **Necessity:** Energy is essential for heating, lighting, and production, so consumers pay regardless of price.

> **Examiner Tip:** Always mention the **time horizon** (short run vs. long run) and give a **real-world example** (e.g., petrol demand in India has $\vert E_d \vert \approx 0.3$).

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Explain the concept of "Merit Order Dispatch" in electricity markets. How does it determine the market clearing price?**

**Model Answer:**

Merit Order Dispatch is a method of **ranking electricity generators** in ascending order of their **short-run marginal cost (SRMC)**. The system operator dispatches generators starting from the **cheapest** until total demand is met.

- The **marginal generator** (the last one dispatched to meet demand) sets the **market clearing price (MCP)**.
- All generators receive this MCP under **uniform pricing** or pay-as-bid in some markets.

**Example merit order:**

| Rank | Generator | SRMC (₹/kWh) |
|------|----------|----------------|
| 1 | Solar | 2 |
| 2 | Wind | 3 |
| 3 | Hydro | 4 |
| 4 | Nuclear | 5 |
| 5 | Coal | 6 |
| 6 | Gas (Peaker) | 10 |

If demand is met after gas, the **MCP = ₹10/kWh**, and all generators are paid ₹10/kWh.

> **Examiner Tip:** Always clarify the **uniform pricing rule** and mention that merit order dispatch is the foundation of **day-ahead market clearing** in power exchanges like IEX.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Model Question]

**(a)** The demand and supply functions for natural gas in a regional market are given by:
- $Q_d = 800 - 5P$
- $Q_s = -200 + 7P$

where $P$ is in ₹ per cubic meter and $Q$ is in million cubic meters per day.

**Compute:**
1. The equilibrium price and quantity. **(3 marks)**
2. The price elasticity of demand at equilibrium. **(2 marks)**
3. The consumer surplus, producer surplus, and total welfare. **(2 marks)**

**(b)** Suppose the government imposes a **price ceiling** at ₹75 per cubic meter to protect consumers. **Analyze:**
1. The new quantity traded and the shortage created. **(2 marks)**
2. The deadweight loss due to the price ceiling. **(3 marks)**
3. Discuss **two policy alternatives** the government could adopt instead of a price ceiling to achieve affordability. **(2 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

#### Part (a):

**Sub-part (a)(1): Equilibrium Price and Quantity**

Set $Q_d = Q_s$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
800 - 5P &= -200 + 7P \\
800 + 200 &= 7P + 5P \\
1000 &= 12P \\
P^* &= \frac{1000}{12} = 83.33 \text{ ₹/m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

Substitute into demand:

$$
Q^* = 800 - 5(83.33) = 800 - 416.67 = 383.33 \text{ million m}^3/\text{day}
$$

**Verification:**

$$
Q_s = -200 + 7(83.33) = -200 + 583.31 = 383.31 \;\; \checkmark
$$

> **Equilibrium: $P^* = 83.33$ ₹/m³, $Q^* = 383.33$ million m³/day**

**Sub-part (a)(2): Price Elasticity at Equilibrium**

Differentiate demand: $\frac{dQ_d}{dP} = -5$

$$
E_d = -5 \cdot \frac{83.33}{383.33} = \frac{-416.67}{383.33} \approx -1.087
$$

> **$\vert E_d \vert \approx 1.087$** — demand is **slightly elastic** at this point.

**Sub-part (a)(3): Welfare Calculation**

- $P_{max} = 800/5 = 160$ ₹/m³
- $P_{min} = 200/7 \approx 28.57$ ₹/m³

$$
CS = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 383.33 \cdot (160 - 83.33) = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 383.33 \cdot 76.67 \approx 14{,}694 \text{ million ₹/day}
$$

$$
PS = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 383.33 \cdot (83.33 - 28.57) = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 383.33 \cdot 54.76 \approx 10{,}495 \text{ million ₹/day}
$$

$$
TW = CS + PS = 14{,}694 + 10{,}495 = 25{,}189 \text{ million ₹/day}
$$

---

#### Part (b):

**Sub-part (b)(1): Quantity Traded and Shortage at $P_c = 75$**

$$
Q_s = -200 + 7(75) = -200 + 525 = 325 \text{ million m}^3/\text{day}
$$

$$
Q_d = 800 - 5(75) = 800 - 375 = 425 \text{ million m}^3/\text{day}
$$

$$
\text{Shortage} = Q_d - Q_s = 425 - 325 = 100 \text{ million m}^3/\text{day}
$$

> **Shortage = 100 million m³/day** (high black-market potential).

**Sub-part (b)(2): Deadweight Loss**

Demand price at $Q_s = 325$: $P_d = \frac{800 - 325}{5} = 95$ ₹/m³
Supply price at $Q_s = 325$: $P_s = \frac{325 + 200}{7} = 75$ ₹/m³

$$
DWL = \frac{1}{2} \cdot (Q^* - Q_s) \cdot (P_d - P_s) = \frac{1}{2} \cdot (383.33 - 325) \cdot (95 - 75)
$$

$$
DWL = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 58.33 \cdot 20 = 583.3 \text{ million ₹/day}
$$

> **DWL ≈ ₹583 million/day** — significant welfare loss.

**Sub-part (b)(3): Two Policy Alternatives**

1. **Targeted Cash Transfer (DBT):** Instead of subsidizing all consumers (which benefits the rich disproportionately), transfer cash directly to low-income households, allowing market prices to clear efficiently.

2. **Production Subsidies / Capacity Expansion:** Subsidize gas producers or invest in LNG terminals to **shift the supply curve rightward**, lowering equilibrium price without creating shortages.

> **Other options:** Remove import duties, promote renewable substitutes, regulate wholesale markets.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** [KTU University Exam — Model Question]

**(a)** Define and distinguish between **Consumer Surplus, Producer Surplus, and Deadweight Loss**. Use a labeled diagram to explain how each is measured. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The electricity market in a state has the following demand and supply functions:
- $Q_d = 1200 - 10P$
- $Q_s = -300 + 15P$

where $P$ is in ₹/kWh and $Q$ is in million kWh.

**Determine:**
1. The competitive equilibrium price and quantity. **(2 marks)**
2. If a single generator has monopoly power and faces the demand curve above with constant marginal cost of ₹40/kWh, **find the profit-maximizing price and quantity** using $MR = MC$, and compute the deadweight loss. **(3 marks)**
3. The **Lerner Index** for the monopolist. **(2 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

#### Part (a):

**Consumer Surplus (CS):** The difference between what consumers are **willing to pay** and what they **actually pay**, measured as the area under the demand curve and above the equilibrium price.

$$
CS = \frac{1}{2} \cdot Q^* \cdot (P_{max} - P^*)
$$

**Producer Surplus (PS):** The difference between what producers **receive** and their **minimum acceptable price** (marginal cost), measured as the area above the supply curve and below the equilibrium price.

$$
PS = \frac{1}{2} \cdot Q^* \cdot (P^* - P_{min})
$$

**Deadweight Loss (DWL):** The **loss in total welfare** caused by market distortion (monopoly, tax, or price control). It is the triangular area between the demand and supply curves over the range where efficient transactions fail to occur.

| Concept | Formula | Geometric Form |
|---------|---------|----------------|
| CS | $\int_0^{Q^*} P_d(Q) \, dQ - P^* Q^*$ | Triangle above $P^*$, below demand |
| PS | $P^* Q^* - \int_0^{Q^*} P_s(Q) \, dQ$ | Triangle below $P^*$, above supply |
| DWL | $\int_{Q^*}^{Q_{new}} [P_d(Q) - P_s(Q)] \, dQ$ | Triangle between curves |

---

#### Part (b):

**Sub-part (b)(1): Competitive Equilibrium**

$$
\begin{aligned}
1200 - 10P &= -300 + 15P \\
1500 &= 25P \\
P_c &= 60 \text{ ₹/kWh} \\
Q_c &= 1200 - 10(60) = 600 \text{ million kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Competitive equilibrium: $P_c = 60$ ₹/kWh, $Q_c = 600$ million kWh**

**Sub-part (b)(2): Monopoly Outcome**

For a linear demand $Q = 1200 - 10P$, invert to get $P = 120 - 0.1Q$.

Total Revenue: $TR = P \cdot Q = (120 - 0.1Q)Q = 120Q - 0.1Q^2$

Marginal Revenue:

$$
MR = \frac{dTR}{dQ} = 120 - 0.2Q
$$

Set $MR = MC = 40$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
120 - 0.2Q_m &= 40 \\
0.2Q_m &= 80 \\
Q_m &= 400 \text{ million kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

Monopoly price:

$$
P_m = 120 - 0.1(400) = 80 \text{ ₹/kWh}
$$

Deadweight loss (from $Q_m = 400$ to $Q_c = 600$):

At $Q_m = 400$:
- $P_d(400) = 120 - 0.1(400) = 80$ ₹/kWh
- $P_s(400) = \frac{400 + 300}{15} = 46.67$ ₹/kWh

$$
DWL = \frac{1}{2} \cdot (600 - 400) \cdot (80 - 46.67) = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 200 \cdot 33.33 = 3{,}333 \text{ million ₹}
$$

> **Monopoly outcome: $P_m = 80$ ₹/kWh, $Q_m = 400$ million kWh, $DWL = ₹3{,}333$ million**

**Sub-part (b)(3): Lerner Index**

$$
L = \frac{P - MC}{P} = \frac{80 - 40}{80} = 0.5
$$

> **Lerner Index = 0.5**, indicating substantial monopoly power (maximum value of $L$ is 1).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING — COMMON PITFALLS:**
>
> 1. **Sign Error in Elasticity:** Students forget that PED is **negative** for a normal demand curve. Always report $\vert E_d \vert$ and explicitly state whether demand is **elastic ($\vert E_d \vert > 1$)** or **inelastic ($\vert E_d \vert < 1$)**.
>
> 2. **Confusing MR and Price:** Many students incorrectly assume the monopolist charges a price equal to marginal cost. **MR ≠ P** — always compute MR as $P(1 + 1/E_d)$ for a linear demand, then set $MR = MC$.
>
> 3. **Geometric vs. Algebraic DWL:** Examiners award **full marks (3)** only if the **deadweight loss triangle is correctly identified** with the right base and height. Students often compute the area of the wrong triangle.
>
> 4. **Units Matter:** Always mention **₹/kWh** for price, **million kWh** for quantity, and the **time period** (per day, per year) in your final answer. Omitting units costs 0.5–1 mark.
>
> 5. **Mistaking Shortage for DWL:** A **shortage** is a *quantity gap*; **DWL** is a *welfare loss*. These are different concepts and must be computed separately.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Energy Market Analysis**

### Core Concepts
- Energy Market Analysis = study of **demand, supply, equilibrium, pricing, and welfare** in energy markets.
- Energy demand is **price inelastic in the short run** but becomes more elastic in the long run.
- Equilibrium is determined by **$Q_d = Q_s$**, with $P^* = \frac{a-c}{b+d}$ and $Q^* = a - bP^*$.

### Key Formulas to Memorize
- **PED:** $E_d = \frac{dQ}{dP} \cdot \frac{P}{Q}$
- **CS:** $\frac{1}{2} Q^* (P_{max} - P^*)$
- **PS:** $\frac{1}{2} Q^* (P^* - P_{min})$
- **TW:** $CS + PS$
- **DWL:** $\frac{1}{2} \cdot \Delta Q \cdot \Delta P$
- **Lerner Index:** $L = \frac{P - MC}{P} = -\frac{1}{E_d}$
- **LCOE:** $\frac{\sum C_t/(1+r)^t}{\sum E_t/(1+r)^t}$

### Critical Distinctions
- **Inelastic vs. Elastic demand:** $\vert E_d \vert$ less than 1 vs. greater than 1.
- **MR vs. P:** $MR = P$ only in perfect competition; under monopoly, $MR < P$.
- **Shortage vs. DWL:** Shortage = quantity gap; DWL = welfare loss triangle.
- **Short-run vs. Long-run supply:** Near-vertical vs. more elastic.

### Common KTU Traps
- Forgetting to **invert demand** to express P as a function of Q for MR calculation.
- Confusing **average cost** with **marginal cost** in monopoly problems.
- Failing to **state assumptions** (e.g., perfect information, no externalities).
- Ignoring **government interventions** like taxes, subsidies, and price ceilings.

### Real-World Applications
- **IEX Day-Ahead Market (India)** — uses merit order dispatch.
- **PJM Interconnection (USA)** — locational marginal pricing.
- **Carbon tax design** — uses demand elasticity to estimate revenue and welfare impact.
- **Renewable Obligation (RPO)** — shifts the supply curve rightward.

> **Final Tip:** In KTU exams, always **draw a clear labeled diagram** showing demand, supply, equilibrium, and shaded CS/PS/DWL regions. Examiners allocate **1–2 marks** for a well-labeled graph even if the algebra has minor errors.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET526 - ENERGY ECONOMICS AND POLICY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Regulations by Government of India and Scenario Analyses

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Regulations by Government of India (Energy Sector)

### Formal Academic Definition
**Energy Regulation** refers to the body of statutory laws, rules, policies, codes, and administrative directives issued by the Government of India (GoI) — through bodies like the Ministry of Power (MoP), Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (MNRE), Ministry of Petroleum and Natural Gas (MoPNG), NITI Aayog, and regulatory commissions (CERC/SERCs) — that govern the production, conversion, distribution, pricing, trading, and consumption of energy resources. These regulations provide a **legal-institutional framework** to ensure energy security, affordability, sustainability, and competition in the Indian energy economy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PEMET526, Module 3):**
> "Regulations by Government of India" covers Acts (Electricity Act 2003, Energy Conservation Act 2001), Policies (National Electricity Policy, Tariff Policy), Schemes (UDAY, SAUBHAGYA, FAME, UJALA, PAT), and Institutional mechanisms (BEE, CEA, CERC, SERCs, BPCL, GAIL, PFC, REC) that shape the energy market.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of the Indian energy sector as a **massive highway system**. The Government acts like the **traffic authority**:
- **Laws (Acts)** = Traffic rules (speed limit, lane discipline)
- **Policies** = Long-term road planning (where to build new highways)
- **Regulatory Commissions (CERC/SERCs)** = Highway police enforcing the rules
- **Schemes (UJALA, UDAY)** = Special discount drives to encourage good behavior
- **Standards & Labels (BEE Star Rating)** = Fuel-efficiency stickers on cars

Without these rules, every electricity generator, distributor, and consumer would behave chaotically, just like cars without traffic rules.

### Key Statutory Milestones (Bold Constants & Metrics)
- **Installed Power Capacity (as of 2024)**: ~**430 GW** (India is the 3rd largest electricity producer globally)
- **Renewable Energy Target (2030)**: **500 GW** of non-fossil fuel capacity
- **Net-Zero Commitment**: **Net Zero by 2070** (announced at COP26, Glasgow, 2021)
- **BEE Star Label Scale**: **1 to 5 stars** (with 5 being most efficient)
- **PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade) Cycle Energy Saving Target**: ~**~1.5% to 2%** Specific Energy Consumption reduction per cycle

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield KTU Point:** The Electricity Act 2003 is the **Magna Carta** of the Indian power sector — it de-licensed generation, opened transmission to private players, and created independent regulatory commissions.

---

## 1.2 Scenario Analysis

### Formal Academic Definition
**Scenario Analysis** is a **strategic planning methodology** used in energy economics to construct and analyze multiple plausible descriptions of how the future may unfold, based on varying assumptions about key uncertainties (e.g., GDP growth, fuel prices, technology costs, climate policy). Unlike single-point forecasting, scenario analysis presents a **range of alternative futures** to support robust, risk-informed decision-making.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module 3 Definition:**
> Scenario analysis is a **quantitative-qualitative** tool that models "what-if" pathways — e.g., Business-As-Usual (BAU), Reference, High Renewable, and Net-Zero scenarios — to evaluate the energy-economy-environment (3E) implications of policy choices.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine you are planning a **road trip from Delhi to Goa**. You don't know the future, so you prepare:
- **Scenario 1 (Optimistic):** Clear weather, no traffic, you reach in 14 hours, save fuel
- **Scenario 2 (BAU):** Average traffic, normal weather, you reach in 20 hours
- **Scenario 3 (Pessimistic):** Heavy rain, traffic jams, detour due to landslide, 30 hours
- **Scenario 4 (Disruptive):** EV-only highway available, you must rent an EV

You prepare a **flexible plan** that works across all four. That's exactly what energy scenario analysis does — it tests policies against multiple futures to ensure **robustness**.

### Key Energy Scenario Types (Bolded for KTU Recall)
- **BAU (Business-As-Usual)** — Current policies continue unchanged
- **Reference Scenario** — Announced policies are fully implemented
- **Stated Policies Scenario (STEPS)** — Only existing policies enforced
- **Announced Pledges Scenario (APS)** — Government targets met on time
- **Net Zero Emissions (NZE) Scenario** — Aligned with 1.5 °C climate goal
- **Sustainable Development Scenario (SDS)** — IEA framework integrating SDGs
- **Delayed Transition Scenario (DTS)** — Late, abrupt policy action

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Modelling Tools Used in India:** **MARKAL**, **MESSAGE**, **LEAP**, **PRIMES**, **TIMES**, **India Energy Security Scenarios (IESS)** by NITI Aayog.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework: Regulations by Government of India

The Indian energy regulatory architecture operates on **four concentric layers**:

### Layer 1 — Primary Legislation (Acts of Parliament)
| Act | Year | Purpose |
|-----|------|---------|
| **Electricity Act** | **2003** | Umbrella Act; de-licensed generation; open access; trading; regulatory commissions |
| **Energy Conservation Act** | **2001** | Established BEE; mandates standards & labeling |
| **Petroleum & Natural Gas Regulatory Board Act** | **2006** | PNGRB — regulates downstream petroleum |
| **Atomic Energy Act** | **1962** | Atomic energy regulation; DAE oversight |
| **Coal Mines (Special Provisions) Act** | **2015** | Auction of coal blocks to private sector |
| **Liquefied Petroleum Gas (Regulation of Supply and Distribution) Order** | 2000 | LPG distribution control |

### Layer 2 — Policy Documents
- **Integrated Energy Policy (IEP), 2006** — First holistic energy roadmap
- **National Electricity Policy (NEP), 2005** — Operationalizes the Electricity Act
- **National Tariff Policy, 2006 (revised 2016)** — Tariff determination methodology
- **National Policy on Biofuels, 2018** — 20% ethanol blending target
- **National Hydrogen Mission, 2021** — Green hydrogen production target of 5 MMT by 2030

### Layer 3 — Regulatory Institutions
- **CERC (Central Electricity Regulatory Commission)** — Inter-state generation, transmission tariffs
- **SERC (State Electricity Regulatory Commission)** — Intra-state tariffs, distribution
- **BEE (Bureau of Energy Efficiency)** — Standards, labeling, PAT scheme
- **CEA (Central Electricity Authority)** — Technical standards, grid code
- **MNRE (Ministry of New and Renewable Energy)** — RE policy and targets
- **PNGRB** — City gas distribution, pipeline tariffs

### Layer 4 — Schemes & Programs (Demand-side and Supply-side)
| Scheme | Year | Objective |
|--------|------|-----------|
| **UJALA** | 2015 | Distribute **LED bulbs** at subsidized rates (~37 crore LEDs distributed) |
| **PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade)** | 2012 | Energy efficiency in 13 industrial sectors via **ESCerts** |
| **UDAY** | 2015 | Financial turnaround of DISCOMs |
| **SAUBHAGYA** | 2017 | 100% household electrification |
| **FAME I & II** | 2015, 2019 | Subsidies for **Electric Vehicles** |
| **PM-KUSUM** | 2019 | Solar pumps for agriculture |
| **Atal Mission for Rejuvenation (AMRIT)** | 2015 | Green urban infrastructure |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Favourite:** The **PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade)** scheme is a market-based mechanism where energy-intensive industries are given **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** targets. Industries beating targets receive **Energy Saving Certificates (ESCerts)** that can be traded on power exchanges — one **ESCert = 1 toe (tonne of oil equivalent)** saved.

---

## 2.2 Theoretical Framework: Scenario Analysis

### The Four-Step Scenario Analysis Methodology

**Step 1 — Define Scope and Objective**
- Time horizon (e.g., 2030, 2047, 2070)
- Geographic boundary (India, state, region)
- Decision context (policy, investment, technology)

**Step 2 — Identify Key Driving Forces (PESTLE + STEEP)**
- **P**olitical (climate commitments)
- **E**conomic (GDP growth, fuel prices)
- **S**ocial (demographics, urbanization)
- **T**echnological (cost of solar, storage)
- **L**egal (regulations)
- **E**nvironmental (climate change)

**Step 3 — Construct Scenario Matrix (2x2 Axis Approach)**
- Choose two **high-impact, high-uncertainty** axes
- Example: **Axis 1 = Climate Policy Strength** × **Axis 2 = Technology Cost Trajectory**
- Generates **4 distinct scenarios** (e.g., Green Boom, Green Fizzle, Carbon Lock-in, Slow Transition)

**Step 4 — Quantify and Interpret**
- Run each scenario through an energy model (MARKAL/TIMES/LEAP)
- Compare outcomes: emissions, cost, energy mix, imports
- Identify **robust strategies** that perform well across all scenarios

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Metric | Expression | Unit | Application |
|------------------|------------|------|-------------|
| Specific Energy Consumption | $SEC = \dfrac{\text{Energy Used}}{\text{Output}}$ | toe/tonne, kWh/unit | PAT target benchmarking |
| Energy Intensity of GDP | $EI = \dfrac{\text{TPES}}{\text{GDP}}$ | toe/₹ crore | Cross-country comparison |
| PAT Energy Savings | $ES = (SEC_{baseline} - SEC_{actual}) \times \text{Output}$ | toe | ESCert issuance |
| Discount Factor | $DF_t = \dfrac{1}{(1+r)^t}$ | dimensionless | NPV in energy projects |
| Levelized Cost of Electricity | $LCOE = \dfrac{\sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{C_t}{(1+r)^t}}{\sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{E_t}{(1+r)^t}}$ | ₹/kWh | Scenario comparison across RE sources |
| Carbon Intensity | $CI = \dfrac{CO_2 \text{ emissions}}{\text{Energy generated}}$ | kg CO₂/kWh | Decarbonization tracking |
| Emissions Reduction (vs BAU) | $\Delta E\% = \dfrac{E_{BAU} - E_{scenario}}{E_{BAU}} \times 100$ | % | Scenario impact assessment |
| Installed Capacity Mix | $Share_i = \dfrac{Cap_i}{\sum Cap_i} \times 100$ | % | Scenario outcome analysis |
| Demand Growth Multiplier | $D_n = D_0 \times (1+g)^n$ | GW / MU | Scenario projection |
| Capacity Addition Required | $\Delta Cap = \dfrac{\Delta Demand}{PLF \times 8760}$ | GW | Scenario-based planning |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are operational tools used by **CEA (Central Electricity Authority)**, **BEE**, and **NITI Aayog** for national energy planning, project appraisal, and policy design in India.

### Key Scenario Modelling Tools
- **MARKAL / TIMES** — Long-term energy system optimization (used by CEA, TERI)
- **LEAP** — Long-range Energy Alternatives Planning system (IPCC-aligned)
- **MESSAGE** — Model for Energy Supply Strategy Alternatives (IIASA)
- **PRIMES** — EU energy system model (used for cross-comparison)
- **NEMS** — National Energy Modelling System (USA EIA)
- **IESS 2047** — India Energy Security Scenarios (NITI Aayog interactive tool)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: LCOE Comparison Under Two Scenarios

This is a classic **KTU numerical problem** in Energy Economics.

### Problem Statement
A solar PV plant and a coal-based thermal plant are being compared under two demand scenarios. Calculate the **LCOE** for both and recommend the technology under each scenario.

**Given Data:**

| Parameter | Solar PV | Coal Thermal |
|-----------|----------|--------------|
| Capital Cost (₹/kW) | **45,000** | **60,000** |
| O&M Cost (₹/kW/yr) | **1,000** | **1,800** |
| Fuel Cost (₹/kWh) | 0 | **2.50** |
| Plant Life (years) | 25 | 25 |
| Discount Rate (r) | 10% | 10% |
| Capacity Factor | 20% | 80% |
| Annual Degradation (Solar) | 0.5% | — |

### Step-by-Step LCOE Derivation

**LCOE Formula (General):**

$$
LCOE = \frac{\sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{C_t}{(1+r)^t}}{\sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{E_t}{(1+r)^t}}
$$

**Step 1: Calculate Annual Energy Generation (E_t)**

For **Solar PV** (with degradation):
- Year 1: $E_1 = 1 \text{ kW} \times 8760 \times 0.20 = 1752$ kWh
- Year t: $E_t = 1752 \times (1 - 0.005)^{t-1}$

For **Coal Thermal** (no degradation, constant):
- Year 1: $E_1 = 1 \text{ kW} \times 8760 \times 0.80 = 7008$ kWh
- Year t: $E_t = 7008$ (constant)

**Step 2: Calculate Annual Costs (C_t)**

For **Solar PV** (Year t):
- O&M only (after Year 0 capital): $C_t = 1000$ ₹/yr

For **Coal Thermal** (Year t):
- O&M + Fuel = $1800 + (7008 \times 2.50) = 1800 + 17{,}520 = 19{,}320$ ₹/yr

**Step 3: Compute the Annuity Factor (Present Value of ₹1 per year for n years)**

$$
PVAF = \frac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r} = \frac{1 - (1.10)^{-25}}{0.10}
$$

$$
PVAF = \frac{1 - 0.0923}{0.10} = \frac{0.9077}{0.10} = 9.077
$$

**Step 4: Apply Solar Degradation Adjustment**

For solar, energy declines each year, so the discounted energy sum requires a year-by-year computation. Using the standard geometric series formula:

$$
\sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{E_t}{(1+r)^t} = E_1 \cdot \frac{1 - \left( \frac{1-d}{1+r} \right)^{n}}{1 - \frac{1-d}{1+r}}
$$

Substituting $d = 0.005$, $r = 0.10$, $n = 25$:

$$
\frac{1-d}{1+r} = \frac{0.995}{1.10} = 0.9045
$$

$$
\text{Energy PV factor} = \frac{1 - (0.9045)^{25}}{1 - 0.9045} = \frac{1 - 0.0951}{0.0955} = 9.479
$$

$$
\text{Discounted Energy (Solar)} = 1752 \times 9.479 = 16{,}608 \text{ kWh}
$$

For Coal (no degradation):

$$
\text{Discounted Energy (Coal)} = 7008 \times 9.077 = 63{,}612 \text{ kWh}
$$

**Step 5: Total Discounted Costs**

**Solar PV:**
- Capital (Year 0): $45{,}000$ ₹
- O&M PV: $1000 \times 9.077 = 9{,}077$ ₹
- Total: $54{,}077$ ₹

**Coal Thermal:**
- Capital (Year 0): $60{,}000$ ₹
- O&M + Fuel PV: $19{,}320 \times 9.077 = 175{,}370$ ₹
- Total: $235{,}370$ ₹

**Step 6: Compute LCOE**

$$
LCOE_{solar} = \frac{54{,}077}{16{,}608} = 3.26 \text{ ₹/kWh}
$$

$$
LCOE_{coal} = \frac{235{,}370}{63{,}612} = 3.70 \text{ ₹/kWh}
$$

### Step 7: Scenario Interpretation

| Scenario | Cost Sensitivity | LCOE Solar | LCOE Coal | Recommendation |
|----------|------------------|------------|-----------|----------------|
| **High Fuel Price** (coal ₹4/kWh) | Coal O&M = 29,832 | 3.26 | 5.05 | **Solar wins** |
| **Base Case** | As computed | 3.26 | 3.70 | **Solar marginally better** |
| **Low Discount Rate** (r=5%) | r=0.05, PVAF=14.094 | 2.74 | 4.62 | **Solar clearly wins** |
| **High Solar Degradation** (1.5%) | d=0.015 | 3.85 | 3.70 | **Coal wins** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight for KTU Exam:** Sensitivity to **discount rate** and **fuel cost trajectory** is what makes scenario analysis valuable — the *ranking* of technologies can change with future assumptions, hence the need for multiple scenarios.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Indian Energy Regulations

| Regulation | Year | Issuing Body | Key Mechanism | Market Impact |
|------------|------|--------------|---------------|---------------|
| Electricity Act 2003 | 2003 | Parliament | Open access, de-licensing | Private sector entry |
| National Electricity Policy | 2005 | GoI (MoP) | Rural electrification mandate | 100% village electrification |
| BEE Standards & Labeling | 2006 onwards | BEE | Mandatory star labels | 30-50% appliance efficiency gain |
| PAT Scheme | 2012 onwards | BEE | ESCerts trading | ~15 Mtoe savings across 4 cycles |
| UDAY | 2015 | MoP | DISCOM debt restructuring | ~₹2.5 lakh crore debt addressed |
| UJALA | 2015 | BEE / EESL | LED distribution | ~9,500 MW peak demand avoided |
| FAME II | 2019 | MoHIPE | EV subsidies | ~₹10,000 crore outlay |
| National Hydrogen Mission | 2021 | MNRE | Green hydrogen production | 5 MMT target by 2030 |
| ECBC (Energy Conservation Building Code) | 2007 / 2017 | BEE | Building energy norms | 25-50% energy savings in buildings |

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**
**Define the Electricity Act 2003. Mention any two of its salient provisions.**

**Model Answer:**
The Electricity Act 2003 is the **umbrella legislation** governing the Indian power sector, consolidating three earlier Acts (Indian Electricity Act 1910, Electricity (Supply) Act 1948, Electricity Regulatory Commissions Act 1998).

**Salient Provisions:**
1. **De-licensing of Generation** — No license required for power generation, encouraging private and captive generation.
2. **Open Access** — Non-discriminatory access to transmission and distribution networks for licensed users.
3. **Trading as a Licensed Activity** — Recognized electricity traders; promotes competition.
4. **Establishment of Regulatory Commissions** — CERC at central level, SERCs at state level.
5. **Rural Electrification** — Central Government to prepare a national policy framework.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
**What is scenario analysis? List any four types of energy scenarios.**

**Model Answer:**
Scenario analysis is a **structured planning methodology** that constructs and analyzes multiple plausible future pathways based on varying assumptions about key uncertainties (GDP, fuel prices, technology, policy).

**Four Types of Energy Scenarios:**
1. **Business-As-Usual (BAU)** — Continuation of current trends
2. **Reference Scenario** — Announced policies fully implemented
3. **Net Zero Emissions (NZE)** — Aligned with 1.5 °C climate goal
4. **Delayed Transition Scenario (DTS)** — Late, abrupt policy intervention
5. **Stated Policies (STEPS)** / **Announced Pledges (APS)** (IEA frameworks)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / May 2024]**

**(a)** Discuss the major regulations issued by the Government of India in the energy sector. Explain the role of BEE and the PAT scheme in promoting energy efficiency. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is scenario analysis? Explain the methodology of constructing a 2×2 scenario matrix with a suitable example from the Indian energy sector. **(7 marks)**

### Question B (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Explain in detail the key provisions of the Electricity Act 2003 and the National Electricity Policy. How have these shaped the Indian power sector? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the various energy modelling tools used in scenario analysis. With the help of an LCOE computation, demonstrate how scenarios influence technology selection. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 Complete Model Solutions

### Solution to Question A (a)

**1. Major Energy Regulations by GoI**

| Layer | Instrument | Key Purpose |
|-------|-----------|-------------|
| Acts | Electricity Act 2003 | Liberalization of generation, open access, regulatory commissions |
| Acts | Energy Conservation Act 2001 | Established BEE; mandatory energy audits for large consumers |
| Policies | National Electricity Policy 2005 | Universal access, rural electrification, RE promotion |
| Policies | National Tariff Policy 2016 | Tariff design; promotes competition, renewable purchase obligation |
| Policies | Integrated Energy Policy 2006 | Long-term integrated roadmap for all energy sources |
| Schemes | UJALA (2015) | LED distribution to reduce lighting load |
| Schemes | UDAY (2015) | Financial turnaround of state DISCOMs |
| Schemes | SAUBHAGYA (2017) | 100% household electrification |
| Schemes | FAME I & II | EV adoption subsidies |
| Schemes | PM-KUSUM | Solar pumps for farmers |

**2. Role of BEE (Bureau of Energy Efficiency)**
- Established in **2002** under the Energy Conservation Act 2001
- **Functions:**
  - Develop **Standards & Labeling** for appliances (1-to-5 star)
  - Implement **PAT Scheme** for industries
  - Promote **ECBC (Energy Conservation Building Code)**
  - Coordinate with state Designated Agencies (SDA)
  - Conduct **National Energy Conservation Awards**
  - Awareness and capacity building

**3. PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade) Scheme**
- Launched: **2012**, now in **Cycle 7**
- **Sectors covered**: 13 energy-intensive sectors (Aluminium, Cement, Chlor-Alkali, Fertilizer, Iron & Steel, Pulp & Paper, Petrochemicals, Refineries, Textiles, Power, Railways, DISCOMs, Buildings)
- **Mechanism:**
  1. BEE notifies **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** targets
  2. Industries beating targets receive **Energy Saving Certificates (ESCerts)**
  3. 1 ESCert = 1 **toe** (tonne of oil equivalent) saved
  4. Non-compliant industries must **buy ESCerts** on power exchanges
  5. Creates a **market for energy efficiency**

> **Key Result:** PAT Cycles I-VI achieved savings of over **15 Mtoe**, translating to ~50 million tonnes of CO₂ abatement.

---

### Solution to Question A (b)

**1. Definition of Scenario Analysis**
Scenario analysis is a **strategic foresight method** that builds internally consistent narratives about how the future might unfold, given the interplay of driving forces and uncertainties. In the energy sector, it helps policy makers and investors test the **robustness** of strategies under diverse futures.

**2. The 2×2 Scenario Matrix Methodology**

**Step 1: Identify Driving Forces** — e.g., (a) Climate Policy Stringency (b) Technology Cost Trajectory

**Step 2: Choose Two Uncertain Axes** — typically the most impactful and least predictable

**Step 3: Build the 2×2 Matrix**

| | **Low RE Cost** | **High RE Cost** |
|---|---|---|
| **Strong Climate Policy** | **Green Boom** (RE dominant, deep decarbonization) | **Green Fizzle** (policy ambitious but technology lags) |
| **Weak Climate Policy** | **Disruptive Tech** (market-driven RE growth, no policy push) | **Carbon Lock-in** (fossil-heavy mix persists) |

**4. Indian Energy Sector Example**

Let the two axes be:
- **Axis X:** Stringency of Carbon Pricing (₹0/tonne vs ₹2,000/tonne CO₂)
- **Axis Y:** Solar PV Cost (₹20/W vs ₹40/W)

This produces:
1. **Scenario 1 — Net-Zero Highway:** Carbon pricing ₹2,000 + Solar ₹20/W → **80% RE by 2040**
2. **Scenario 2 — Stalled Transition:** Carbon pricing ₹0 + Solar ₹40/W → **60% coal by 2040**
3. **Scenario 3 — Market-led Solar Boom:** Carbon pricing ₹0 + Solar ₹20/W → **40% RE by 2040**
4. **Scenario 4 — Tech Stagnation:** Carbon pricing ₹2,000 + Solar ₹40/W → **Stranded assets risk**

**5. Why This Matters for India**
- Helps **MNRE** plan RE auctions
- Helps **CEA** project capacity addition requirements
- Helps **DISCOMs** decide PPAs
- Helps **investors** hedge technology and policy risks

---

### Solution to Question B (a)

**1. Electricity Act 2003 — Key Provisions**
- **Section 7:** De-licensing of generation (except for nuclear and hydro above 25 MW under Section 8)
- **Section 11:** Authorization of transmission and distribution
- **Section 14 & 15:** Powers of CERC and SERCs
- **Section 42:** Non-discriminatory open access
- **Section 86(1)(e):** Promote co-generation and renewable energy
- **Section 86(2):** Tariff determination principles
- **Section 121:** Provisions for restructuring of SEBs (State Electricity Boards)

**2. National Electricity Policy 2005 — Key Elements**
- **Access to Electricity:** All households within 5 years (achieved via SAUBHAGYA)
- **Availability:** 24×7 power supply
- **Generation:** Diversified fuel mix
- **Transmission:** National Grid in synchronous mode
- **Distribution:** Reform-based, financially viable DISCOMs
- **Renewables:** At least **10%** of total consumption from RE (now raised to 30%+)

**3. Impact on the Indian Power Sector**
- Private sector entry — **₹ lakhs of crores** invested
- Installed capacity grew from ~**130 GW (2003)** to **~430 GW (2024)**
- Renewable capacity: from **~3 GW (2003)** to **~190 GW (2024)**
- Electricity to every village: **18,452 villages electrified (2017-18)**
- Set the foundation for **UDAY**, **SAUBHAGYA**, and **FAME**

---

### Solution to Question B (b)

**1. Energy Modelling Tools for Scenario Analysis**

| Tool | Type | Developer | Use Case |
|------|------|-----------|----------|
| **MARKAL** | Optimization | IEA ETSAP | Long-term national energy planning |
| **TIMES** | Optimization | ETSAP | Successor to MARKAL; multi-region |
| **MESSAGE** | Optimization | IIASA | Climate-energy policy analysis |
| **LEAP** | Simulation | SEI | GHG mitigation, energy demand |
| **PRIMES** | Equilibrium | NTUA Athens | EU energy market modelling |
| **NEMS** | Equilibrium | US EIA | US national energy outlook |
| **IESS 2047** | Interactive | NITI Aayog | India-specific scenarios |

**2. LCOE Numerical Example (Worked Out)**

*Given:* Coal plant, Capital = ₹60,000/kW, Fuel = ₹2.50/kWh, O&M = ₹1,800/kW/yr, PLF = 80%, life = 25 years, r = 10%.

*Step 1: Annual Energy*
$$
E = 8760 \times 0.80 = 7008 \text{ kWh/kW/yr}
$$

*Step 2: Annual Cost (Years 1 to 25)*
$$
C = 1800 + (7008 \times 2.50) = 19{,}320 \text{ ₹/yr}
$$

*Step 3: PVAF at r = 10%, n = 25*
$$
PVAF = \frac{1 - 1.10^{-25}}{0.10} = 9.077
$$

*Step 4: Total Discounted Cost*
$$
DC = 60{,}000 + (19{,}320 \times 9.077) = 60{,}000 + 175{,}370 = 2{,}35{,}370 \text{ ₹}
$$

*Step 5: Total Discounted Energy*
$$
DE = 7008 \times 9.077 = 63{,}612 \text{ kWh}
$$

*Step 6: LCOE*
$$
LCOE = \frac{2{,}35{,}370}{63{,}612} = 3.70 \text{ ₹/kWh}
$$

**3. Scenario Sensitivity**

| Scenario | Fuel Cost | LCOE (Coal) | LCOE (Solar, from §3.1) | Preferred Tech |
|----------|-----------|-------------|--------------------------|-----------------|
| BAU | ₹2.50/kWh | 3.70 | 3.26 | Solar |
| High Carbon Tax (₹2/kg) | +₹1.40 effective | 5.10 | 3.26 | **Solar** |
| Low Discount (5%) | r=5% | 4.62 | 2.74 | **Solar** |
| Solar Cost Increase (₹55,000/kW) | BAU fuel | 3.70 | 3.85 | Coal |

**Insight:** Scenario analysis reveals that **policy choices (carbon tax, discount rate)** can flip technology rankings — robust planning requires hedging across multiple scenarios.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Year Confusion:** Students often mix up UJALA (2015) and PAT (2012). Memorize a **chronology**: EC Act 2001 → PAT 2012 → UDAY 2015 → UJALA 2015 → SAUBHAGYA 2017 → FAME II 2019 → H₂ Mission 2021.
> 2. **ESCert Confusion:** 1 ESCert = **1 toe**, not 1 MWh. Easy 1-mark loss.
> 3. **LCOE Mis-step:** Forgetting to apply **degradation** in solar LCOE — examiners deduct up to **2 marks**.
> 4. **Scenario vs Forecast:** Scenario ≠ Forecast. Scenarios are **not predictions** — they are alternative futures. Examiners penalize if you write "BAU scenario is most likely."
> 5. **PVAF Calculation Error:** Using $n=24$ instead of $n=25$ is a common slip. Year 0 = capital, Years 1–25 = O&M and energy.
> 6. **Missing Units:** Always write **₹/kWh**, **toe**, **Mtoe**, **MW/GW** explicitly — no marks awarded for naked numbers.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Regulation Track
- ⚡ **Electricity Act 2003** is the cornerstone — know its de-licensing and open-access provisions
- 🌱 **Energy Conservation Act 2001** established BEE; mandatory for Designated Energy Consumers
- 🏭 **PAT Scheme** uses market-based ESCert trading; 13 industrial sectors; 1 ESCert = 1 toe
- 💡 **UJALA** distributed **~37 crore** LED bulbs → **~9,500 MW** peak demand avoided
- 🏦 **UDAY** restructured ~₹2.5 lakh crore DISCOM debt
- 🔌 **SAUBHAGYA** electrified **~2.8 crore** un-electrified households
- 🚗 **FAME II** outlay ~₹10,000 crore for EV adoption
- 🏗️ **ECBC** sets building energy standards — 25–50% savings potential
- 🎯 **500 GW non-fossil target by 2030**, **Net Zero by 2070**

### Scenario Analysis Track
- 🔍 Scenarios = alternative futures, **not predictions**
- 📊 2×2 matrix: choose two **high-impact, high-uncertainty** axes
- 🛠️ Tools: **MARKAL/TIMES** (optimization), **LEAP** (simulation), **MESSAGE** (IIASA)
- 💰 **LCOE** is the most common cross-technology comparison metric
- 🇮🇳 **IESS 2047** by NITI Aayog is the go-to India scenario tool
- 📉 **Sensitivity to discount rate** and **fuel price** is the essence of scenario logic
- 🌍 International scenarios: **IEA WEM, WAM, SDS, NZE**; **IPCC SRES, RCPs**

### Numerical Must-Knows
- **PVAF formula** — always used in LCOE
- **Degradation adjustment** for RE technologies
- **PAT savings formula:** $ES = (SEC_{baseline} - SEC_{actual}) \times Output$
- **Capacity addition:** $\Delta Cap = \dfrac{\Delta Demand}{PLF \times 8760}$

### High-Yield Mnemonics
- **"EB-PU"** for BEE: **E**fficiency, **B**uildings, **E**SCerts, **P**AT, **U**JALA
- **"MEL"** for models: **M**ARKAL, **E**Nergy, **L**EAP
- **"EAT 2003"** — Electricity Act **T**housand features, year 2003

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** In 14-mark questions, always structure your answer with **diagrams/tables**, **headings**, and **bullet points**. KTU examiners reward visual clarity, neatness, and keyword density. A well-labeled flowchart on Electricity Act architecture or Scenario Matrix can earn you **2–3 extra marks** even if the text is partial.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Feasible Options for Developing an Energy Future

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Feasible Options for Developing an Energy Future** refer to the *technically mature, economically viable, environmentally sustainable, and socially acceptable* set of energy supply, demand-side, and enabling infrastructure pathways that a nation, region, or utility can deploy to meet *future energy service demands* while complying with *climate commitments, energy security goals, and sustainable development objectives*.

In the precise terminology of **Energy Economics and Policy**, feasible options are evaluated across four intersecting dimensions — the **4-E Framework** (also called the *Quadruple Bottom Line* of energy planning):

| Dimension | Definition | KTU Examiner Key Term |
|---|---|---|
| **Energy Security** | Uninterrupted availability of energy at affordable prices | *Stranded asset risk* |
| **Economic Affordability** | Least-cost deployment across the life cycle | *Levelized Cost of Energy (LCOE)* |
| **Environmental Sustainability** | Decarbonization, low lifecycle GHG emissions | *Carbon Abatement Cost* |
| **Equity & Social Acceptance** | Universal access, just transition, public acceptance | *Energy Justice* |

A more rigorous, KTU-aligned textbook definition:

> [!NOTE]
> *"Feasible options for developing an energy future are the portfolio of supply-side technologies (renewables, nuclear, natural gas with CCS, biofuels), demand-side interventions (energy efficiency, demand response, electrification), and cross-cutting enablers (storage, smart grids, hydrogen, carbon pricing, policy/regulatory frameworks) that pass three sequential filters — **Technical Readiness (TRL ≥ 6)**, **Economic Competitiveness (LCOE ≤ market clearing price)**, and **Policy/Regulatory Acceptability** — and can be financed, built, and operated at the scale required to displace carbon-intensive incumbent systems."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **The "Restaurant Menu" Analogy**
>
> Imagine a large family (the *national economy*) that needs to feed itself for the next 30 years. The head of the family (the *Energy Planner / Ministry of Power*) sits at a table with a finite budget (the *GDP allocation*), a list of family members with very specific dietary needs (the *industrial, residential, transport, agricultural load*), and a health goal — *reduce cholesterol (CO₂ emissions)*.
>
> The **Feasible Energy Options** are the dishes on the menu. The planner cannot order *unicorn steak* (immature fusion technology), cannot order *only dessert* (100% intermittent renewables without storage), and cannot order *only red meat* (continued coal dependence). The smart planner builds a **balanced thali**: some solar rice (cheap, abundant, daytime only), some baseload dal (nuclear / biomass / geothermal), some chapati (energy efficiency — the cheapest "negative-cost" option), a pickle of natural gas with CCS (transitional flexibility), and a digestive of battery + hydrogen (firming the renewables). Each dish has a cost (LCOE), a portion size (capacity in GW), and a digestion time (construction lead time).
>
> The **Feasible Energy Future** is the *optimized, multi-course, least-cost, climate-compliant thali* — never a single silver-bullet dish.

## 1.3 The Five Pillars of Feasible Energy Options

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every KTU answer on this topic **must** explicitly identify and elaborate the following five pillars. Examiners allocate marks *per pillar* (≈2 to 3 marks each in a 14-mark question).

### Pillar 1 — Supply-Side Renewables (Variable RE)
**Solar PV, Onshore/Offshore Wind, Run-of-River Hydro, Solar CSP, Geothermal, Tidal/Wave.**
- Technically mature (TRL 8–9 for PV and onshore wind).
- **Capacity Factor (CF)** is the key metric: Solar PV India ≈ **18–25%**, Onshore Wind ≈ **25–35%**, Offshore Wind ≈ **45–55%**.
- Curtailment risk necessitates storage and/or grid expansion.

### Pillar 2 — Supply-Side Firm / Dispatchable Low-Carbon
**Nuclear (Gen III+, SMRs), Natural Gas combined with Carbon Capture & Storage (NG-CCS), Biomass with CCS (BECCS), Blue Hydrogen, Large Hydro (>25 MW).**
- Provides *inertia, frequency support, and firm capacity* (24×7 availability).
- Critical for replacing coal baseload in a net-zero pathway.

### Pillar 3 — Demand-Side Management (DSM) & Energy Efficiency (EE)
**Efficient appliances, industrial process redesign, building envelope, LED lighting, efficient motors, demand response (DR).**
- Often the **cheapest MWh** available — "negawatts" are cheaper than "megawatts."
- India’s Perform-Achieve-Trade (PAT) scheme is a flagship DSM policy.

### Pillar 4 — Sectoral Electrification & End-Use Switching
**EVs for transport, heat pumps for buildings, electric boilers for industry, induction cooking, electrified railways.**
- Decarbonizes the demand side by switching from direct fossil combustion to (increasingly clean) electricity.

### Pillar 5 — Cross-Cutting Enablers
**Battery Energy Storage Systems (BESS), Pumped Storage Hydro (PSH), Green Hydrogen (electrolysis), Smart Grids, Carbon Capture Utilisation & Storage (CCUS), Carbon Pricing (ETS / Carbon Tax).**
- These are *force multipliers* — they make Pillar 1 and Pillar 2 viable at scale.

## 1.4 The Standard Taxonomy of Feasible Options (KTU Diagram)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for the exam:** A 7-mark question often asks the student to *"Classify and discuss the feasible options for an energy future."* A clean taxonomy earns the first 2 marks; the discussion earns the remaining 5.

The feasible options can be arranged in a hierarchical decision tree as shown in the next section. The three top-level branches are **Supply-Side**, **Demand-Side**, and **Cross-Cutting Enablers**, each further decomposed into mature vs. emerging sub-categories.

## 1.5 Why This Topic Appears in Module 3

In the KTU PEMET526 syllabus, Module 3 builds on the macroeconomic theory of energy demand/supply (Modules 1–2) and feeds directly into Module 4 (Energy Modelling & Planning Tools — MARKAL, LEAP, MESSAGE). The "feasible options" topic is the **bridge between economic theory and quantitative planning** — it identifies *what goes into the model* and *what is screened out*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Keyword Set (must be used verbatim in answers):**
> Levelized Cost of Energy, Capacity Factor, Technology Readiness Level, Energy Return on Energy Invested (EROEI), Carbon Abatement Cost, Demand-Side Management, Net-Zero Pathway, Just Transition, Stranded Asset, Capacity Credit, Grid Integration Cost, Externality Pricing, System LCOE (S-LCOE), Variable Renewable Energy (VRE) Penetration Limit.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Sequential Screening Filter (Decision Logic)

A KTU answer must show *how* an option moves from "idea" to "deployed megawatt." The screening filter is a four-gate logic:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gate 1 — Technical Feasibility (TRL Filter)**
> TRL 1–3 → Basic research (rejected for planning horizon ≤ 2050).
> TRL 4–6 → Demonstration (only included with learning-curve discount).
> TRL 7–9 → Commercial deployment (passed).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gate 2 — Economic Feasibility (LCOE Filter)**
> LCOE ≤ *system marginal price* (in India, ≈ ₹4–6/kWh wholesale).
> Includes *system integration cost* (grid augmentation, balancing, storage).
> The correct metric is **System LCOE (S-LCOE)**, not utility LCOE.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gate 3 — Resource & Land Feasibility**
> Solar: ≈ 4–6 acres/MW; Wind: ≈ 0.5–1 acre/MW but wind corridors are contested.
> Water for thermal cooling, critical minerals (Li, Co, Ni, REEs) for storage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gate 4 — Policy & Social Feasibility**
> RPO/RPS compliance, ESG mandates, public acceptance, just transition (coal worker reskilling), land-acquisition, environmental clearance (CRZ, forest, wildlife).

Only options that pass **all four gates** enter the *feasible portfolio*.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Pillar — Deeper Reasoning

### 2.2.1 Why Solar PV is Inevitable
- **Learning curve:** Module price has fallen ≈ **24%** for every doubling of cumulative capacity (Swanson's Law).
- **Resource:** India receives ≈ 300 sunny days/year; ≈ 5,000 TWh/yr theoretical potential — far exceeding projected demand of ≈ 2,000 TWh/yr by 2030.
- **Modularity:** Decentralized deployment reduces transmission losses (≈ 20% → 5% with rooftop + battery).

### 2.2.2 Why Wind Complements Solar
- Diurnal complementarity: wind peaks in monsoon evenings and night, solar peaks midday.
- Seasonal complementarity: Indian wind is strongest June–September (southwest monsoon), solar strongest March–May.
- Combined **hybrid solar-wind plant** smooths the aggregate CF from ≈ 20% (solar alone) to ≈ 30–35%.

### 2.2.3 Why Nuclear is Re-emerging as a "Feasible Option"
- **Capacity credit ≈ 90–95%** (vs. solar ≈ 5–15% depending on storage).
- **Erosion of learning curve** for Small Modular Reactors (SMRs, 50–300 MWe): factory-built, passive safety, ≈ 3-yr construction.
- India’s three-stage nuclear programme (PHWR → Fast Breeder → Thorium) makes it a *strategic* feasible option despite higher LCOE.

### 2.2.4 Why Natural Gas + CCS is a "Bridge" Feasible Option
- Gas emits ≈ **50% less CO₂/kWh** than coal (≈ 0.4 vs. 0.82 kg CO₂/kWh).
- Gas peakers provide the *flexibility* to absorb VRE ramps.
- CCS adds ≈ **₹2–3/kWh** but avoids stranding the gas asset in a 2050 net-zero world.

### 2.2.5 Why Energy Efficiency is the "Hidden Champion"
- **Negawatts cost 30–50% less** than equivalent supply-side megawatts.
- IEA (2023) estimates efficiency can deliver **>40% of the CO₂ abatement** required to 2040.
- India’s PAT scheme has saved ≈ 16 Mtoe cumulatively (≈ ₹50,000 crore in avoided fuel imports).

### 2.2.6 Why Storage is the *Enabling* Feasible Option
- VRE penetration > 20–25% without storage causes **grid instability, duck-curve ramping, and curtailment**.
- **Lithium-ion BESS** cost has fallen from ≈ ₹20 lakh/MWh (2010) to ≈ ₹12–14 lakh/MWh (2024).
- Long-duration storage (LDES) — PSH, CAES, flow batteries, green H₂ — is required for > 8-hour duration.

### 2.2.7 Why Green Hydrogen is the "Sector-Coupling" Feasible Option
- Decarbonizes *hard-to-abate* sectors: steel (DRI), ammonia, refining, heavy trucking, shipping, aviation (e-SAF).
- Acts as *long-duration, seasonal storage* for the power sector.
- Cost target: **₹100/kg by 2030** (current ≈ ₹350–500/kg).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains **every** formula a KTU examiner can ask in a 14-mark question. Memorize the *meaning* of each row — the LaTeX will be provided in the question stem.

| # | Formula (LaTeX) | Plain-English Meaning | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{LCOE} = \dfrac{\sum_{t=0}^{N} \frac{I_t + M_t + F_t + D_t}{(1+r)^t}}{\sum_{t=0}^{N} \frac{E_t}{(1+r)^t}$ | Levelized Cost of Energy — per-unit lifetime cost of generating electricity | ₹/kWh or \$/MWh |
| 2 | $\text{CF} = \dfrac{E_{\text{actual}}}{P_{\text{rated}} \times 8760}$ | Capacity Factor — how much of theoretical max is actually generated | 0.15–0.95 (dimensionless) |
| 3 | $\text{NPV} = \sum_{t=0}^{N} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t} - I_0$ | Net Present Value of a project | ₹ Crore (positive ⇒ viable) |
| 4 | $\text{IRR}: \sum_{t=0}^{N} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+\text{IRR})^t} = 0$ | Internal Rate of Return | % (compare to WACC) |
| 5 | $\text{DPB} = \dfrac{I_0}{\overline{\text{Annual CF}}_{\text{discounted}}}$ | Discounted Payback Period | Years |
| 6 | $\text{S-LCOE} = \text{LCOE}_{\text{tech}} + \text{Grid Integration Cost}$ | System LCOE including VRE balancing & storage | ₹/kWh |
| 7 | $\text{Carbon Abatement Cost} = \dfrac{\text{LCOE}_{\text{low-carbon}} - \text{LCOE}_{\text{fossil}}}{\Delta \text{CO}_2 \text{ intensity}}$ | Cost per tonne of CO₂ avoided | \$/tCO₂ |
| 8 | $\text{EROEI} = \dfrac{\text{Energy delivered over lifetime}}{\text{Energy invested in construction, fuel, O\&M, decommissioning}}$ | Energy Return on Energy Invested | Dimensionless (>5 is good) |
| 9 | $\text{LPSP} = 1 - \dfrac{E_{\text{supplied}}}{E_{\text{demand}}}$ | Loss of Power Supply Probability (reliability) | % (target < 0.1%) |
| 10 | $\text{VRE penetration limit} \approx \dfrac{1}{1 + k \cdot \text{Storage cost multiplier}}$ | Rule-of-thumb max VRE share | 20–80% (depends on storage) |
| 11 | $\text{PUE}_{\text{Power-to-X}} = \dfrac{\text{Energy out (as H}_2\text{ or e-fuel)}}{\text{Energy in (electricity)}}$ | Efficiency of sector-coupling conversion | 0.6–0.75 (PEM electrolyser) |
| 12 | $\text{Externality cost} = \sum_i (E_i \times P_i) + \text{CO}_2 \times \text{SC-CO}_2$ | Monetized damage from pollution and climate | ₹/kWh added |

> [!TIP]
> **Where each variable comes from:**
> $I_t$ = Investment in year $t$; $M_t$ = O&M in year $t$; $F_t$ = Fuel cost in year $t$; $D_t$ = Decommissioning cost in year $t$; $r$ = discount rate (WACC); $E_t$ = electricity generated in year $t$; $N$ = project life (20–40 yr); $P_{\text{rated}}$ = nameplate capacity (MW); $E_i$ = emission of pollutant $i$; $P_i$ = unit damage cost of pollutant $i$; $\text{SC-CO}_2$ = social cost of carbon (≈ \$50–200/tCO₂).

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of This System

In professional practice, energy planners use these formulas inside large-scale optimization models:

- **MARKAL / TIMES**: LCOE enters the objective function (minimize total system cost) subject to CO₂ caps, reliability constraints, and resource limits. The output is the *least-cost feasible mix*.
- **LEAP (Long-range Energy Alternatives Planning)**: Uses CF, demand projections, and technology costs to build bottom-up scenarios.
- **MESSAGE (IIASA)**: Macro-engineering model used for IPCC pathways (SSP1-1.9, SSP2-4.5, etc.).
- **NEMS (US EIA)**, **PRIMES (EU)**: National energy models.
- **In India**: Integrated Energy Policy (Planning Commission 2006), NITI Aayog’s *Energy Transitions for India by 2040* (2023), CEA’s *Optimal Generation Mix Studies*.

## 2.5 The KTU-Style "Three Horizons" Framework

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **high-probability 7-mark sub-question** in KTU exams. Memorize the three horizons.

| Horizon | Time Frame | Dominant Options | Rationale |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Horizon 1 — "Defend the Present"** | 2025–2030 | Energy Efficiency, Rooftop Solar, Wind Repowering, Gas Peakers, BESS (4-hr) | Cheap, fast to deploy, least-regret |
| **Horizon 2 — "Build the New"** | 2030–2040 | Utility Solar, Offshore Wind, SMRs, EVs, Green H₂ pilots, EHV grid | At-scale deployment, cost-parity reached |
| **Horizon 3 — "Transform the System"** | 2040–2070 | Deep electrification, H₂ economy, CCUS, Fusion (?), DACCS for negative emissions | Net-zero / carbon-negative system |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of LCOE (Levelized Cost of Energy)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner’s Note:** A 7-mark sub-question on LCOE is a *recurring* question. The student must be able to derive the formula from first principles and then evaluate it numerically.

### 3.1.1 Starting Point — The Discounted Cash Flow Identity

A power project generates a stream of costs $C_t$ and electricity $E_t$ in every year $t = 0, 1, 2, \dots, N$. The *present value* of all costs (numerator) and all electricity (denominator) must both be expressed in year-0 money using the discount factor $\dfrac{1}{(1+r)^t}$.

$$
\text{PV of Lifetime Costs} = \sum_{t=0}^{N} \frac{C_t}{(1+r)^t}
$$

$$
\text{PV of Lifetime Electricity} = \sum_{t=0}^{N} \frac{E_t}{(1+r)^t}
$$

### 3.1.2 Definition of LCOE

By definition, LCOE is the *constant per-unit price* of electricity that, if received in every year, would exactly equal the present value of all costs. Mathematically:

$$
\sum_{t=0}^{N} \frac{\text{LCOE} \cdot E_t}{(1+r)^t} = \sum_{t=0}^{N} \frac{C_t}{(1+r)^t}
$$

### 3.1.3 Algebraic Solution for LCOE

Divide both sides by $\sum_{t=0}^{N} \frac{E_t}{(1+r)^t}$ and rearrange:

$$
\text{LCOE} \cdot \sum_{t=0}^{N} \frac{E_t}{(1+r)^t} = \sum_{t=0}^{N} \frac{C_t}{(1+r)^t}
$$

$$
\text{LCOE} = \frac{\sum_{t=0}^{N} \frac{C_t}{(1+r)^t}}{\sum_{t=0}^{N} \frac{E_t}{(1+r)^t}}
$$

### 3.1.4 Substituting the Cost Components

The annual cost $C_t$ is the sum of four components — investment $I_t$ (mostly in $t=0$), operations & maintenance $M_t$ (every year), fuel $F_t$ (every year for thermal; zero for renewables), and decommissioning $D_t$ (mostly in $t=N$):

$$
C_t = I_t + M_t + F_t + D_t
$$

Therefore the full LCOE formula is:

$$
\text{LCOE} = \frac{\sum_{t=0}^{N} \frac{I_t + M_t + F_t + D_t}{(1+r)^t}}{\sum_{t=0}^{N} \frac{E_t}{(1+r)^t}}
$$

### 3.1.5 Simplification for Constant Annual Generation

If $E_t = E = P_{\text{rated}} \times \text{CF} \times 8760$ is constant (typical for thermal; approximation for renewables), the denominator becomes a geometric series:

$$
\sum_{t=0}^{N} \frac{1}{(1+r)^t} = \frac{1 - (1+r)^{-(N+1)}}{1 - (1+r)^{-1}} = \frac{(1+r)^N - 1}{r \cdot (1+r)^{N-1}}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\text{LCOE} \approx \frac{r \cdot I_0 \cdot (1+r)^N + M \cdot [(1+r)^N - 1] + F \cdot [(1+r)^N - 1] + D}{(1+r)^N - 1} \cdot \frac{1}{E_{\text{annual}}}
$$

This is the **Capital Recovery Factor (CRF)** form of LCOE, which is the form used in KTU numerical problems.

### 3.1.6 Numerical Evaluation (KTU-Style Worked Example)

> **Problem (Typical KTU 7-mark):** A 100 MW solar PV plant costs ₹500 Crore (₹5 Cr/MW), has annual O&M of ₹5 Crore, capacity factor 22%, project life 25 years, discount rate 10%, no fuel cost, decommissioning ₹10 Crore (year 25). Compute LCOE.

**Step 1 — Compute Annual Generation**

$$
E = 100 \text{ MW} \times 0.22 \times 8760 \text{ h} = 192{,}720 \text{ MWh/yr} = 192.72 \text{ GWh/yr}
$$

**Step 2 — Capital Recovery Factor (CRF)**

$$
\text{CRF} = \frac{r(1+r)^N}{(1+r)^N - 1} = \frac{0.10 \times (1.10)^{25}}{(1.10)^{25} - 1}
$$

Compute $(1.10)^{25}$:

$$
(1.10)^{25} \approx 10.8347
$$

Therefore:

$$
\text{CRF} = \frac{0.10 \times 10.8347}{10.8347 - 1} = \frac{1.08347}{9.8347} \approx 0.11017
$$

**Step 3 — Annualized Capital Cost**

$$
A_{\text{cap}} = I_0 \times \text{CRF} = 500 \times 0.11017 = 55.09 \text{ Crore/yr}
$$

**Step 4 — Annualized Decommissioning Cost** (year 25, discounted)

$$
\text{PV of } D = \frac{D}{(1+r)^N} = \frac{10}{(1.10)^{25}} = \frac{10}{10.8347} = 0.923 \text{ Crore}
$$

Amortize over 25 years using CRF:

$$
A_{\text{decom}} = 0.923 \times 0.11017 = 0.102 \text{ Crore/yr}
$$

**Step 5 — Sum of Annualized Costs**

$$
A_{\text{total}} = A_{\text{cap}} + A_{O\&M} + A_{\text{fuel}} + A_{\text{decom}} = 55.09 + 5 + 0 + 0.102 = 60.19 \text{ Crore/yr}
$$

**Step 6 — LCOE**

$$
\text{LCOE} = \frac{A_{\text{total}}}{E} = \frac{60.19 \times 10^7 \text{ ₹}}{192.72 \times 10^6 \text{ kWh}} = \frac{60.19}{19.272} \approx 3.12 \text{ ₹/kWh}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** This LCOE (₹3.12/kWh) is competitive with new coal (₹3.5–4.5/kWh) and natural gas (₹5–7/kWh) at ₹6/$ exchange and 2024 fuel prices. **Hence solar PV is a feasible option.**

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of Carbon Abatement Cost (CAC)

### 3.2.1 Conceptual Setup

Carbon Abatement Cost is the *incremental cost* of avoiding one tonne of CO₂ emissions by switching from a fossil baseline to a low-carbon technology.

### 3.2.2 Algebraic Definition

$$
\text{CAC} = \frac{\text{LCOE}_{\text{low-carbon}} - \text{LCOE}_{\text{fossil}}}{\text{CO}_2^{\text{fossil}} - \text{CO}_2^{\text{low-carbon}}}
$$

where CO₂ emissions are in **tonnes per MWh** (or **kg per kWh**).

### 3.2.3 Numerical Example — Solar PV vs. Coal

> **Given:** LCOE solar = ₹3.12/kWh; LCOE coal = ₹4.00/kWh; Solar emission = 0.045 kg CO₂/kWh (life-cycle); Coal emission = 0.95 kg CO₂/kWh (life-cycle).

**Step 1 — Cost differential**

$$
\Delta C = 3.12 - 4.00 = -0.88 \text{ ₹/kWh}
$$

(Negative ⇒ solar is *cheaper* than coal.)

**Step 2 — Emission differential**

$$
\Delta E = 0.95 - 0.045 = 0.905 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{kWh}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to tonnes per MWh**

$$
\Delta E = 0.905 \text{ kg/kWh} = 0.905 \text{ t/MWh}
$$

**Step 4 — Carbon Abatement Cost**

$$
\text{CAC} = \frac{-0.88 \text{ ₹/kWh}}{0.905 \text{ t/MWh}} = \frac{-880 \text{ ₹/MWh}}{0.905 \text{ t/MWh}} = -972 \text{ ₹/tCO}_2
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** A **negative** CAC (−₹972/tCO₂) means that switching to solar *saves money AND reduces emissions* — the holy grail of climate economics. In international units, at ₹83/$, this is ≈ **−\$11.7/tCO₂**. Compare this to a typical social cost of carbon of \$50–200/tCO₂ — the investment is hugely justified.

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation of NPV for an Energy Project

### 3.3.1 Cash Flow Definition

Year 0: $CF_0 = -I_0$ (negative — capital outflow).
Years 1 to N: $CF_t = (P_{\text{sell}} - \text{LCOE}) \times E_t + \text{Tax Shield}_t$
Year N: $CF_N$ also includes salvage value $S$ and decommissioning outflow.

### 3.3.2 NPV Formula

$$
\text{NPV} = \sum_{t=0}^{N} \frac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}
$$

### 3.3.3 Numerical Example — Wind Farm

> **Given:** 50 MW onshore wind farm; $I_0 = $ ₹250 Crore; CF = 30%; PPA tariff = ₹4.50/kWh; O&M = ₹6 Crore/yr; project life 20 yr; discount rate 12%; salvage = ₹10 Crore; tax 25%.

**Step 1 — Annual Generation**

$$
E = 50 \times 0.30 \times 8760 = 131{,}400 \text{ MWh/yr} = 131.4 \text{ GWh/yr}
$$

**Step 2 — Gross Annual Revenue**

$$
R = 4.50 \times 131.4 \times 10^6 = 59.13 \text{ Crore/yr}
$$

**Step 3 — Pre-tax Cash Flow**

$$
CF_t^{\text{pre}} = R - M = 59.13 - 6 = 53.13 \text{ Crore/yr}
$$

**Step 4 — After-tax Cash Flow** (assuming O&M is tax-deductible; no depreciation detail for simplicity)

$$
CF_t^{\text{post}} = CF_t^{\text{pre}} \times (1 - \tau) = 53.13 \times 0.75 = 39.85 \text{ Crore/yr}
$$

**Step 5 — Present Value of 20-year Annuity** (with annuity factor $\dfrac{1 - (1+r)^{-N}}{r}$)

$$
\text{PVAF} = \frac{1 - (1.12)^{-20}}{0.12} = \frac{1 - 0.1037}{0.12} = \frac{0.8963}{0.12} = 7.469
$$

$$
\text{PV of } CF = 39.85 \times 7.469 = 297.6 \text{ Crore}
$$

**Step 6 — PV of Salvage Value**

$$
\text{PV of } S = \frac{10}{(1.12)^{20}} = \frac{10}{9.646} = 1.037 \text{ Crore}
$$

**Step 7 — NPV**

$$
\text{NPV} = -250 + 297.6 + 1.037 = +48.64 \text{ Crore}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** NPV is **positive (+₹48.64 Crore)**, so the project is economically feasible. **The IRR is between 12% (hurdle rate) and ≈ 14.5%** (computed by iteration). The discounted payback period is ≈ **9 years**.

| **Gas + CCS** | 7 | 6.5–9.0 | 0.55–0.75 | 0.05–0.10 | 36–48 mo | Low | Med |
| **Biomass (cogeneration)** | 9 | 4.5–6.0 | 0.65–0.80 | 0.05–0.30 (varies) | 12–24 mo | Med | High (agri land) |
| **BECCS** | 6 | 8.0–12.0 | 0.70–0.85 | **−0.5 to −1.0** (negative) | 36–60 mo | Med | Med |
| **Geothermal** | 8 (limited) | 5.0–8.0 | 0.80–0.95 | 0.03–0.05 | 36–60 mo | Low | Low (in India, limited resource) |
| **Li-ion BESS (4-hr)** | 9 | 6.0–10.0 (LCOE of stored energy) | n/a | 0.05–0.10 | 6–12 mo | Low | Low |
| **Pumped Storage Hydro** | 9 | 4.0–7.0 | n/a | 0.02 | 60–96 mo | High (submergence) | High |
| **Green Hydrogen (PEM)** | 7 | 350–500 ₹/kg | n/a | 0.03–0.05 (life-cycle) | 24–36 mo | Low | Med |
| **Coal (abatement reference)** | 9 | 3.5–5.0 | 0.65–0.80 | 0.85–1.00 | 48–60 mo | High | **High (stranded workers)** |

## 3.5 The Capacity Credit Problem (Mathematical Insight)

As VRE penetration $p$ rises, the *capacity credit* (the firm capacity a VRE plant contributes to resource adequacy) falls:

$$
\text{CC}_{\text{VRE}}(p) = \text{CC}_{\text{VRE},0} \cdot e^{-\lambda p}
$$

where $\lambda$ is a system-specific decay constant (India: $\lambda \approx 0.03$ per percentage point). At $p = 30\%$ solar penetration, the capacity credit falls to ≈ **5–7%** of nameplate, which is why storage is required.
