Individual Cost] C --> D[Aggregate Use Rises] D --> E[Resource Stock Falls] E --> F[Regeneration Rate Declines] F --> G[Sustainability Threshold Breached] G --> H[Resource Collapse] H --> I[All Users Lose] A1[No defined property rights]:::cause --> A C1[Negative externality
not internalized]:::cause --> C classDef cause fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#B22222,color:#000000 ``` --- ### 4.4 McKelvey Diagram — Resource/Reserve Classification (Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph EconomicFeasibility[Economic Feasibility Vertical Axis] direction TB H1[Economically Recoverable Now - R1 Reserves]:::reserve H2[Economically Recoverable with Price Rise - R2 Reserves]:::reserve H3[Subeconomic - R3 Resources]:::reserve end subgraph GeologicalCertainty[Geological Certainty Horizontal Axis] direction LR V1[Identified and Demonstrated]:::geology V2[Inferred]:::geology V3[Hypothetical and Speculative]:::geology end H1 --> H2 --> H3 V1 --> V2 --> V3 classDef reserve fill:#D4EDDA,stroke:#155724,color:#000000 classDef geology fill:#FFF3CD,stroke:#856404,color:#000000 ``` --- ### 4.5 Depleting Resource Decision Framework ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Decision: Extract or Conserve] --> B{Compare PV of Net Revenue} B -->|Higher today| C[Extract Now] B -->|Higher tomorrow| D[Conserve in Ground] B -->|Equal| E[Hotelling Equilibrium] C --> F[Revenue Realized] D --> G[Future Scarcity Rent Rises] E --> H[Path of P minus c
grows at rate r] F --> I[Check Externality Cost] G --> I H --> I I --> J{Social Cost of Carbon} J -->|High| K[Substitute to Backstop] J -->|Low| L[Continue Extraction] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **"State and explain Hotelling's Rule for the optimal extraction of a non-renewable resource."** **Model Answer:** Hotelling's Rule, formulated by Harold Hotelling in 1931, states that in a competitive market, the **net price of an exhaustible resource (market price minus marginal extraction cost) must rise at a rate equal to the real interest rate (discount rate) in equilibrium**. $$P_{t+1} - P_t = r(P_t - c) \quad \text{or} \quad \frac{\dot{P}}{P - c} = r$$ **Key points:** - It reflects the opportunity cost of leaving the resource in the ground. - Owners are indifferent between extracting one more unit today and one more unit tomorrow. - The scarcity rent (Marginal User Cost, $\lambda = P - c$) is a stock variable whose present value is constant over time. - The rule provides the optimal intertemporal extraction path that maximizes the present value of total profit. - It links resource economics with capital theory via the discount rate $r$. - It explains why real oil prices have historically shown a long-term upward trend. *(3 marks: definition 1, formula 1, economic meaning 1)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **"What is the Tragedy of the Commons? Explain with reference to energy or environmental resources."** **Model Answer:** The Tragedy of the Commons, articulated by Garrett Hardin (1968), describes a situation in which **individual users, acting independently and rationally according to their own self-interest, deplete a shared limited resource, contrary to the collective long-term interest of the group**. In the energy/environmental context, classic examples include: 1. **Atmospheric carbon sink** — each emitter adds CO₂ for private benefit (e.g., cheap fossil energy), but the cumulative effect is global climate change (a negative externality borne by all). 2. **Groundwater aquifers** — farmers pumping water for irrigation deplete the common aquifer faster than recharge. 3. **Overfishing in international waters** — each fleet catches as much as possible before others do, collapsing fish stocks. 4. **Grazing commons** — the original Hardin example. **Solution mechanisms** include: property rights assignment, Pigouvian taxes (carbon tax), tradable permits, community self-regulation, and international treaties (e.g., the Paris Agreement). *(3 marks: definition 1, energy example 1, solution 1)* --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) > **ESE Module Internal Choice Rule (KTU 2024):** *Answer any ONE full question from the module. Each question carries 14 marks and has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.* --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Hotelling's Rule & Oil Sector **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]** **(a)** Derive Hotelling's Rule from the optimization of an exhaustible resource owner. Clearly state the Lagrangian setup, the FOCs, and the economic interpretation of the multiplier $\lambda$. (7 marks) **(b)** The marginal extraction cost of crude oil in a competitive market is $c = \$20$/barrel. The current price is $P_0 = \$60$/barrel and the real interest rate is $r = 5\%$. Assuming Hotelling's Rule holds, calculate: (i) The scarcity rent in year 0. (ii) The expected price in year 5 and year 10. (iii) The total present value of the resource stock if 1 billion barrels remain, and the owner extracts them uniformly over 10 years (assume price follows the Hotelling path). (iv) Comment on what would happen if a *backstop* technology with constant marginal cost $c_b = \$120$/barrel becomes available. (7 marks) --- **Model Solution (a):** **Step 1 — Setup the optimization problem.** The owner chooses extraction $Q_t$ in each period to maximize: $$PV = \sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \frac{(P_t - c)Q_t}{(1+r)^t}$$ subject to the stock constraint $\sum_{t=0}^{\infty} Q_t = S$. **Step 2 — Form the Lagrangian.** $$\mathcal{L} = \sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \frac{(P_t - c)Q_t}{(1+r)^t} - \lambda \left( \sum_{t=0}^{\infty} Q_t - S \right)$$ **Step 3 — FOC for each period:** $$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial Q_t} = \frac{P_t - c}{(1+r)^t} - \lambda = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lambda = \frac{P_t - c}{(1+r)^t}$$ **Step 4 — Economic meaning of $\lambda$:** $\lambda$ is the **shadow price of the stock** — the marginal value of having one more barrel in the ground. It equals the present value of the net price of extraction in every period. **Step 5 — Derive Hotelling's Rule:** Equating the FOCs for $t$ and $t+1$: $$\frac{P_t - c}{(1+r)^t} = \frac{P_{t+1} - c}{(1+r)^{t+1}}$$ $$\Rightarrow P_{t+1} - c = (P_t - c)(1+r)$$ $$\Rightarrow P_{t+1} = P_t + r(P_t - c)$$ **Interpretation:** The net price $(P_t - c)$ — the scarcity rent — grows at the rate $r$. Higher discount rates imply faster extraction; lower discount rates imply slower extraction and conservation. --- **Model Solution (b):** **(i) Scarcity rent in year 0:** $$\lambda_0 = P_0 - c = 60 - 20 = \$40/\text{barrel}$$ **(ii) Expected price in year 5 and year 10:** Using the Hotelling path: $P_t = c + (P_0 - c)(1+r)^t = 20 + 40 \times (1.05)^t$ Year 5: $P_5 = 20 + 40 \times (1.05)^5 = 20 + 40 \times 1.2763 = 20 + 51.05 = \$71.05/\text{barrel}$ Year 10: $P_{10} = 20 + 40 \times (1.05)^{10} = 20 + 40 \times 1.6289 = 20 + 65.16 = \$85.16/\text{barrel}$ **(iii) Total PV of stock (uniform extraction over 10 years):** Annual extraction: $q = 1{,}000{,}000{,}000 / 10 = 100{,}000{,}000$ barrels/year. Net revenue in year $t$: $(P_t - c) \times 100{,}000{,}000$. $$PV = \sum_{t=0}^{9} \frac{(P_t - 20) \times 10^8}{(1.05)^t}$$ Compute the discount factors and revenues: | $t$ | $P_t - c$ | DF $(1.05)^{-t}$ | PV contribution (in \$million) | |---|---|---|---| | 0 | 40.00 | 1.0000 | 4000.00 | | 1 | 42.00 | 0.9524 | 4000.00 | | 2 | 44.10 | 0.9070 | 4000.00 | | 3 | 46.31 | 0.8638 | 4000.00 | | 4 | 48.62 | 0.8227 | 4000.00 | | 5 | 51.05 | 0.7835 | 4000.00 | | 6 | 53.60 | 0.7462 | 4000.00 | | 7 | 56.28 | 0.7107 | 4000.00 | | 8 | 59.10 | 0.6768 | 4000.00 | | 9 | 62.05 | 0.6446 | 4000.00 | **Key insight:** Because $(P_t - c) \times (1.05)^{-t}$ is constant (= 40) under the Hotelling path, each year contributes exactly the same **\$4,000 million** to the present value. $$PV = 10 \times 4000 = \$40{,}000 \text{ million} = \$40 \text{ billion}$$ This is the **total scarcity rent** embedded in the stock — exactly $S \times \lambda_0 = 10^9 \times 40 = \$40$ billion. The mathematics is a beautiful confirmation of Hotelling's logic. **(iv) Effect of a backstop technology at $c_b = \$120/\text{barrel}$:** Set $P_t = c_b$ to find the year when the resource is abandoned: $$120 = 20 + 40(1.05)^t \quad \Rightarrow \quad (1.05)^t = \frac{100}{40} = 2.5$$ $$t = \frac{\log 2.5}{\log 1.05} = \frac{0.3979}{0.02119} \approx 18.78 \text{ years}$$ After about **19 years**, the oil price equals the backstop cost, and the resource is effectively abandoned in favor of the cheaper, abundant alternative. The owner would accelerate extraction before this date if the backstop is anticipated. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Market Penetration & Resource Overuse **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]** **(a)** Explain the concept of market penetration pathways for renewable energy technologies. Discuss the role of the **experience curve** and **learning rate** in driving the falling costs of solar PV. (7 marks) **(b)** A fishery has logistic growth with parameters $\alpha = 0.4$ (per year) and $\beta = 0.001$ (per year per unit of stock). The current stock is $q_0 = 300$ units and the cost of fishing is $C_f = \$50$/unit caught. The fish price is $P_f = \$200$/unit. (i) Compute the Maximum Sustainable Yield (MSY) and the corresponding stock level. (ii) Determine whether the current stock is being over-harvested or under-harvested. (iii) If the open-access harvest level is $h_{OA} = 350$ units/year, calculate the resource rent at the socially optimal level of harvest and discuss the **Tragedy of the Commons** in this context. (7 marks) --- **Model Solution (a):** **Market Penetration Pathways** trace how a new energy technology progressively captures market share from incumbent technologies through a sequence of overlapping stages: 1. **R&D and Demonstration** (high cost, niche markets, public R&D funding). 2. **Early Adoption** (visionary users, subsidies like feed-in tariffs, demonstration projects). 3. **Take-off and Growth** (learning-by-doing kicks in, costs fall steeply, policy scaled up). 4. **Maturity and Mass Market** (grid parity achieved, technology self-sustaining, subsidies withdrawn). 5. **Saturation and Displacement** (dominant market share, begins to displace fossil incumbents). **The Experience Curve** quantifies the cost decline. The unit cost falls as a power function of cumulative production: $$C(Q) = C_0 \left( \frac{Q}{Q_0} \right)^{-b}$$ The **learning rate (LR)** is the fractional cost reduction per doubling of cumulative output: $$LR = 1 - 2^{-b} \quad \Rightarrow \quad b = -\frac{\log(1-LR)}{\log 2}$$ **Solar PV Case Study:** - Solar PV has an empirical learning rate of **~24%** — i.e., every doubling of cumulative global PV deployment reduces module costs by 24%. - Between 1976 and 2020, cumulative PV deployment grew from ~10 MW to ~760 GW (factor 76,000). - The corresponding cost decline: $76000^{-0.911} \approx 0.000003$, i.e., **module costs fell by 99.9997%** — from ~$76/W to ~$0.30/W. - This dramatic cost decline enabled solar PV to reach **grid parity** in most sunny countries by the early 2010s, triggering the rapid market penetration observed in the 2010s–2020s. **Policy Implications:** Governments can accelerate the experience curve through: - Deployment subsidies (every installed MW builds the knowledge base). - R&D funding (shifts the curve downward). - Standards and mandates (ensure minimum deployment volume). --- **Model Solution (b):** **(i) MSY and corresponding stock level:** $$q_{MSY} = \frac{\alpha}{2\beta} = \frac{0.4}{2 \times 0.001} = 200 \text{ units}$$ $$h_{MSY} = \frac{\alpha^2}{4\beta} = \frac{(0.4)^2}{4 \times 0.001} = \frac{0.16}{0.004} = 40 \text{ units/year}$$ **(ii) Is the current stock being over-harvested?** Current stock: $q_0 = 300$ units. The natural growth rate at this stock is: $$\dot{q}_{growth} = \alpha q_0 - \beta q_0^2 = 0.4 \times 300 - 0.001 \times 300^2 = 120 - 90 = 30 \text{ units/year}$$ This is the **maximum sustainable harvest** at $q_0 = 300$. If harvest exceeds 30 units/year, the stock will decline. The biological sustainable harvest at $q_0$ is **30 units/year**, well below the open-access level of **350 units/year** (from the problem). Therefore, the fishery is being **massively over-harvested**, and the stock is collapsing. Also note: the stock is *above* the MSY level ($q_0 = 300 > q_{MSY} = 200$), so biologically the stock is on the high side — but economically, the open-access harvest rate is so high that the stock is depleted. **(iii) Resource Rent at the Socially Optimal Harvest:** The socially optimal harvest maximizes the resource rent (difference between total revenue and total cost), evaluated along the steady-state growth curve. The open-access condition equates price to average cost. For a Gordon-Schaefer model with cost $C_f$ per unit: At steady state, the open-access stock $q_{OA}$ satisfies: $$P_f = \frac{C_f}{q} \quad \Rightarrow \quad q_{OA} = \frac{C_f}{P_f} = \frac{50}{200} = 0.25 \text{ units}$$ (Wait — this is dimensionally inconsistent. In a properly specified model, the open-access condition would be a bioeconomic equilibrium. Let me re-frame for clarity in the exam context.) **Simplified exam-style answer:** At the **socially optimal** level, harvest equals the *growth* at the optimal stock. Taking a stock level that maximizes economic rent: If the MSY stock is $q_{MSY} = 200$ with $h_{MSY} = 40$ units/year: $$\text{Resource Rent} = h_{MSY} \times (P_f - C_f) = 40 \times (200 - 50) = 40 \times 150 = \$6{,}000/\text{year}$$ **Tragedy of the Commons Discussion:** - The open-access harvest (350 units/year) is **8.75 times** the sustainable level. - At this rate, the stock declines every year (harvest > natural growth at any stock). - Eventually, the stock falls to a level where fishing becomes unprofitable ($q = 50/200 = 0.25$, but realistically the stock crashes to near zero before this point). - **All fishers lose** their livelihood — a classic Tragedy of the Commons. - **Solution:** Individual Transferable Quotas (ITQs), Marine Protected Areas (MPAs), or community-based fisheries management. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Deductions:** > 1. **Hotelling's Rule confusion:** Students often confuse "net price grows at rate $r$" with "price grows at rate $r$". The correct statement is that **$(P_t - c)$** grows at rate $r$, not $P_t$ itself. Losing 1 mark for this. > 2. **Forgetting units:** Always quote $LR$ as a percentage (e.g., 24%) and $r$ as a fraction (e.g., 0.05). Examiner deducts 0.5 marks for unit confusion. > 3. **In MSY problems:** Many students forget that the *economically* optimal harvest is **less than** the *biologically* optimal MSY. Use this insight to score full marks in the discussion. > 4. **Experience curve:** Don't confuse *learning rate* with *learning index $b$*. Show the conversion: $b = -\log(1-LR)/\log 2$. > 5. **Tragedy of the Commons:** State the *cause* (open access + negative externality) and a *policy solution* — not just describe the problem. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - [x] **Hotelling's Rule:** Net price of exhaustible resource rises at the discount rate $r$. The scarcity rent $\lambda = P - c$ is a *stock* value whose *present value* is constant over time. - [x] **Marginal User Cost (MUC):** Equals $P - c$; represents opportunity cost of leaving resource in the ground. - [x] **Backstop technology:** Abundant alternative with constant marginal cost; resource is abandoned when $P = c_b$. - [x] **Experience curve:** $C(Q) = C_0(Q/Q_0)^{-b}$; learning rate $LR = 1 - 2^{-b}$. - [x] **Solar PV learning rate:** ~24% (canonical value for KTU problems). - [x] **MSY (Logistic):** $h_{MSY} = \alpha^2/(4\beta)$, $q_{MSY} = \alpha/(2\beta)$. - [x] **Reserve-to-Production (R/P) ratio:** Years of remaining supply at current production rate. - [x] **Tragedy of the Commons:** Open-access + negative externality → overuse; solutions include Pigouvian tax, property rights, tradable permits, community governance. - [x] **Hubbert's Peak:** Logistic model of oil production; global peak often predicted around 2005–2020. - [x] **Social Cost of Carbon (SCC):** \$50–200/tCO₂; internalizes the externality of fossil fuel use. - [x] **McKelvey Diagram:** Classifies resources by geological certainty × economic feasibility. - [x] **Key policy levers for market penetration:** Feed-in tariffs, RPS (Renewable Portfolio Standards), carbon tax, cap-and-trade, R&D subsidies. - [x] **Common exam trap:** "Net price grows at $r$" — NEVER just "price grows at $r$". The marginal extraction cost must be subtracted. - [x] **Numerical must-knows:** $r = 5\% \Rightarrow (1.05)^{10} \approx 1.6289$; $(1.05)^{20} \approx 2.6533$.

topics.title: Combating Resource Depletion, Misuse, and Pollution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET526 - ENERGY ECONOMICS AND POLICY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Combating Resource Depletion, Misuse, and Pollution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Defining Resource Depletion, Misuse, and Pollution (KTU Terminology)

In the precise language of **Energy Economics and Policy (KTU 2024 Scheme, PEMET526)**, the three intertwined challenges threatening the long-run energy-economy-environment nexus are defined as follows:

> [!IMPORTANT] Core KTU Definitions
> - **Resource Depletion:** The progressive exhaustion of finite, non-renewable energy stocks (coal, crude oil, natural gas, uranium) and the over-harvesting of renewable energy flows beyond their natural regeneration rates (e.g., aquifer-fuelled geothermal, deep old-growth forests used as fuel-wood).
> - **Resource Misuse:** The economic inefficiency in energy consumption arising from wasteful conversion, poor pricing signals, subsidisation of carbon-intensive fuels, technological lock-in, and thermodynamic losses (exergy destruction) in extraction, refining, and end-use.
> - **Pollution:** The discharge of contaminants (GHGs, SOx, NOx, PM2.5, oil spills, thermal pollution) into air, water, and soil, whose social cost is not reflected in the private market price — a classic *externality* in welfare economics.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Earth as a Bank Vault" Intuition

Imagine the planet as a **bank vault** containing two types of wealth:
1. **A fixed safe** (non-renewable stocks — coal, oil, gas) — once cracked open, you cannot put the contents back.
2. **A monthly salary** (renewable flows — solar, wind, hydro) — what you earn depends on how much of it you actually collect (technology permitting) and you cannot spend more than your salary without going broke.

- **Depletion** = breaking open the safe too fast and ignoring the salary.
- **Misuse** = buying a high-power bulb when a LED would do the same job, or subsidising petrol so people drive inefficient cars.
- **Pollution** = burning the money from the safe to make smoke; the smoke dirties the air inside the vault, making the salary harder to collect (climate change damages solar yield, hydropower, etc.).

The economic objective of *combating* these three problems is therefore to **maximise the present value of welfare** subject to keeping the "real wealth" of the vault non-declining across generations.

> [!NOTE] Why this matters in KTU
> Module 3 of PEMET526 explicitly links resource economics with environmental economics. The "combat" theme requires a balanced toolkit of **Hotelling-style optimal extraction rules**, **Pigouvian taxes**, **tradable permits**, and **Genuine Savings / Green GDP** accounting to test whether policy interventions are actually sustainable.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics a KTU Student MUST Know

- **Reserve-to-Production (R/P) Ratio:** years of remaining supply at current extraction rate.
- **Energy Returned on Energy Invested (EROEI):** ratio of energy delivered to energy used to obtain it.
- **Social Cost of Carbon (SCC):** monetary damage of emitting one additional tonne of CO₂.
- **Genuine Savings (GS):** Net savings adjusted for resource depletion and pollution damages.
- **Hotelling Rent:** the in-situ scarcity rent of an exhaustible resource.
- **Carbon Intensity (CI):** CO₂ emitted per unit of GDP (kg CO₂ / USD).
- Standard physical constant: **GWP of CO₂ = 1**, CH₄ = **27–30** (100-year horizon, IPCC AR6), N₂O = **273**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of the "Combat" Framework

Combat policy is built on three pillars. A KTU 14-mark answer should be organised around them.

**Pillar A — Efficient Allocation (Economic Instruments)**
- Pigouvian tax equal to marginal external damage.
- Tradable Emission Permits (cap-and-trade).
- Removal of fossil-fuel subsidies.
- Full-cost pricing of electricity (internalising health + climate externality).

**Pillar B — Technological Substitution (Innovation)**
- Switch from high-carbon to low-carbon primary energy.
- Improve EROEI of renewable harvesting.
- Carbon Capture, Utilisation & Storage (CCUS).
- Energy-efficiency standards and labelling (e.g., BEE Star Rating in India).

**Pillar C — Institutional / Regulatory Reform**
- Command-and-control (emission standards, technology mandates).
- Information disclosure (ESG reporting, TCFD).
- Property rights reform (common-pool resources).
- International agreements (UNFCCC, Paris Agreement NDCs).

### 2.2 Hotelling's Rule — The Bedrock of Combating Depletion

For an exhaustible resource owned by a profit-maximising monopolist or social planner with constant marginal extraction cost *c* and constant interest rate *r*:

$$\frac{\partial P(t)}{\partial t} = r \cdot P(t) \quad \text{(net-price version)}$$

Equivalently, the **gross price** must rise at:

$$\frac{\partial P_g(t)}{\partial t} = r \cdot \left(P_g(t) - c\right)$$

**Intuitive "Why":** A unit of resource held in the ground is an *asset* that must earn a return at least equal to the financial market rate *r*, otherwise the owner would sell it today and invest the cash. Hence the scarcity rent (price − marginal cost) grows at rate *r*. The market price path that satisfies this condition **minimises the present value of total social cost** of using up the resource.

### 2.3 Hartwick Rule — Sustainability in One Equation

> [!IMPORTANT] Hartwick's Rule
> "Invest the rents from exhaustible resources into reproducible capital such that the value of total capital is non-declining." If the rule is obeyed, constant per-capita consumption is possible even with finite resources.

Mathematically, if *R* is the Hotelling rent (resource rent) and *K* is man-made capital:

$$S_{genuine} = S_{conventional} - \delta K - D_{resource} - D_{pollution} \ge 0$$

where $D_{resource}$ and $D_{pollution}$ are the monetised depletion and pollution damage terms.

### 2.4 Environmental Kuznets Curve (EKC)

A hypothesised **inverted-U** relationship between per-capita income and environmental degradation:

$$D(y) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 y + \beta_2 y^2 + \beta_3 y^3 + \varepsilon$$

- For low $y$: $\beta_1 > 0$ (degradation rises with industrialisation).
- Past turning point: $\beta_2 < 0$ (degradation falls as income rises and clean-tech demand kicks in).

> [!CAUTION] KTU Pitfall
> The EKC is **descriptive, not normative**. It does *not* guarantee that growth will clean itself; it is an empirical regularity and policy can flatten the curve (shift the turning point leftward) via taxes and standards.

### 2.5 Pigouvian Tax and Tradable Permits

For an externality with marginal damage $MD$ at emission level $e$:

$$t^{opt} = MD(e^*)$$

For a tradable permit system with cap $\bar{E}$ and *n* firms with MAC (Marginal Abatement Cost):

$$\text{Equilibrium} : \;\; MAC_1 = MAC_2 = \cdots = MAC_n = p_{permit}$$

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Variables & Units | Key Insight |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hotelling Rule (net price) | $\dot{P}(t) / P(t) = r$ | $P$ = net price (USD/GJ), $r$ = real interest rate (yr⁻¹) | Scarcity rent grows at discount rate |
| 2 | Hotelling Rule (gross price) | $\dot{P}_g = r(P_g - c)$ | $c$ = marginal extraction cost (USD/GJ) | Resource price path is monotonically rising if $r>0$ |
| 3 | Reserve-to-Production Ratio | $R/P = S_0 / Q_0$ | $S_0$ = proven reserve, $Q_0$ = current annual production (yr) | Static depletion index; ignores demand growth |
| 4 | EROEI | $EROEI = E_{out} / E_{in}$ | $E_{out}$, $E_{in}$ in MJ or kWh | If $EROEI < 1$, the resource is an *energy sink* |
| 5 | Hotelling Rent | $\pi_t = P_t - c$ | $\pi$ = rent (USD/unit) | Captures opportunity cost of leaving in ground |
| 6 | Present Value of Resource | $PV = \int_0^T (P_t - c) q_t e^{-rt}\, dt$ | $q_t$ = extraction rate | Maximise subject to $\int_0^T q_t\, dt = S_0$ |
| 7 | Pigouvian Tax | $t^{*} = MD(e^*)$ | $MD$ = marginal damage (USD/tonne) | Internalises externality |
| 8 | Tradable Permits | $MAC_1 = MAC_2 = \ldots = p$ | $p$ = permit price | Cost-effective across heterogeneous firms |
| 9 | EKC Polynomial | $D = \beta_0 + \beta_1 y + \beta_2 y^2 + \beta_3 y^3$ | $y$ = per-capita GDP, $D$ = pollution | Inverted-U empirical regularity |
| 10 | Genuine Savings (World Bank) | $GS = GNS - C_{nrg} - C_{poll} + Ed$ | GNS = gross national savings | $GS \ge 0$ ⇒ weakly sustainable |
| 11 | Hartwick Investment Rule | $I_K \ge R_{resource}$ | $I_K$ = investment in reproducible capital | Constant consumption path possible |
| 12 | Social Cost of Carbon | $SCC = \int_0^\infty \Delta C(t) e^{-rt}\, dt$ | $\Delta C$ = damage pulse (USD) | Used in CBA of climate policy |
| 13 | Carbon Intensity | $CI = CO_2 / GDP$ | kg CO₂ per USD | KTU relevant for India target $\approx 45\%$ reduction by 2030 (vs 2005) |
| 14 | Coase Theorem | $Pareto\,eff.\,iff\,tr.\,costs \approx 0$ | Liable rule + small N | Justifies property-rights pollution solutions |
| 15 | PM2.5 Health Cost | $HC = \Delta m \cdot VSL$ | $\Delta m$ = premature mortality, $VSL$ = value of statistical life | Direct WTP-based damage monetisation |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering and Policy Utility

- The **Hotelling price path** is used in long-run oil & gas fiscal modelling (Norway, Qatar).
- **Carbon pricing** has been operationalised in 75+ jurisdictions covering ~24% of global emissions (World Bank Carbon Pricing Dashboard 2024).
- **Genuine Savings** is published by the **World Bank** for nearly every country and is the UN's recommended headline indicator for SDG 8.4 (decoupling economic growth from environmental degradation).
- **EKC analysis** underlies India's NDC commitments under the Paris Agreement — the implicit claim is that per-capita income growth plus a carbon tax can deliver an EKC turning point.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Hotelling's Rule from First Principles

**Setup.** A social planner must allocate a finite stock $S_0$ of an exhaustible energy resource over time horizon $[0, T]$. Let $q(t)$ be the extraction rate and $P(t)$ the market price. Assume constant marginal extraction cost $c$ and constant discount rate $r$.

**Step 1 — State the planner's objective.** Maximise the present value of social welfare:

$$W = \int_0^T \big[ P(t) - c \big] q(t) \, e^{-rt} \, dt$$

**Step 2 — State the resource stock constraint.**

$$\int_0^T q(t) \, dt = S_0 \quad ; \quad q(t) \ge 0$$

**Step 3 — Define the current-value Hamiltonian** (because the constraint is integral / infinite-dimensional we switch to a stock variable).

Let $S(t)$ be the remaining reserve with $\dot{S} = -q(t)$. The Hamiltonian in current value is:

$$H = \big[ P(t) - c \big] q(t) + \lambda(t) \big[ - q(t) \big] = q(t) \big[ P(t) - c - \lambda(t) \big]$$

**Step 4 — First-order condition with respect to $q(t)$:**

$$\frac{\partial H}{\partial q} = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; P(t) - c - \lambda(t) = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; \lambda(t) = P(t) - c$$

**Step 5 — Costate (shadow-price) equation.** In current-value form, the shadow price $\lambda$ must grow at the rate of discount:

$$\dot{\lambda}(t) = r \, \lambda(t)$$

**Step 6 — Substitute Step 4 into Step 5.**

$$\frac{d}{dt}\big[ P(t) - c \big] = r \big[ P(t) - c \big]$$

**Step 7 — Recognise that $c$ is constant, hence:**

$$\frac{dP(t)}{dt} = r \big[ P(t) - c \big]$$

**Step 8 — Rearrange to the canonical Hotelling form (net price version).** Define net price $\tilde P(t) = P(t) - c$:

$$\frac{d\tilde P(t)}{dt} = r \, \tilde P(t) \;\Longrightarrow\; \tilde P(t) = \tilde P(0) e^{rt}$$

This is the **net-price Hotelling rule**: the scarcity rent must grow at the rate of discount. ∎

**Step 9 — Intuitive re-statement.** A unit of resource left in the ground today is *equivalent* to a financial asset paying the rent $\tilde P$. To keep extraction and saving equally attractive, $\tilde P$ must compound at $r$.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Optimal Pigouvian Tax

**Step 1.** Consider a competitive market where a representative firm emits $e$ units of pollutant per period and the marginal private cost is $MPC(e) = MC_{private}(e)$.

**Step 2.** The social cost is $MSC(e) = MPC(e) + MD(e)$ where $MD(e)$ is the marginal damage to third parties.

**Step 3.** Unregulated equilibrium: firm sets $MPC(e_u) = 0$ (or chooses $e$ to maximise private profit), so $e_u$ is the unregulated emission.

**Step 4.** Social optimum: choose $e^*$ such that $MSC(e^*) = 0$, i.e.:

$$\frac{dMPC}{de} \bigg\vert_{e^*} = - \frac{dMD}{de} \bigg\vert_{e^*}$$

**Step 5 — Construct a tax.** Impose a per-unit tax $t$ so that the firm's private cost becomes $MPC(e) + t$. The new optimum satisfies:

$$MPC'(e_t) + t = 0$$

**Step 6 — Equate to social optimum:** $e_t = e^*$ requires $t = MD(e^*)$. ∎

> [!NOTE] Second-best caveat: If the damage function is not perfectly known, the Weitzman (1974) result shows that **prices (taxes)** are preferable when marginal damages are steep, while **quantities (permits)** are preferable when marginal abatement costs are steep.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Policy Instruments

| Instrument | Cost-Effectiveness | Environmental Certainty | Information Requirement | Political Feasibility | Equity Concern | KTU 3-Mark Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Emission Standard | Low (one-size-fits-all) | High | Low | High | Low | CPCB norms for SOx in India |
| Pigouvian Tax | High | Low–Medium | Very High (needs MD) | Low (tax-averse) | Regressive (must recycle) | India Coal Cess → GST Compensation Cess |
| Tradable Permits | High | High (cap known) | Medium (needs MAC) | Moderate | Allocation rules matter | EU ETS, China National ETS |
| Subsidies for Clean Tech | Medium | Low | Medium | High (visible) | Fiscal cost | PM-KUSUM solar pumps |
| Information Disclosure | Medium | Indirect | Low | Very High | Low | BEE Star labelling |
| R&D Investment | High (long-run) | High | Strategic | High | Low | National Hydrogen Mission |

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Hotelling Price Path

**Problem (typical KTU 5-Mark).** A barrel of crude oil has a current net price (price − marginal cost) of ₹2,000. Real interest rate is 6 % per year. Marginal extraction cost is constant at ₹1,500 per barrel. Find the net and gross price in 5 years.

**Step 1 — Apply net-price Hotelling rule.**

$$\tilde P(t) = \tilde P(0) \cdot e^{rt} = 2000 \cdot e^{0.06 \times 5}$$

$$e^{0.30} = 1.34986$$

$$\tilde P(5) = 2000 \times 1.34986 = \text{₹}2{,}699.72$$

**Step 2 — Compute gross price.**

$$P_g(5) = \tilde P(5) + c = 2699.72 + 1500 = \text{₹}4{,}199.72$$

**Step 3 — Verify growth check.** Gross price growth check:

$$P_g(0) = 2000 + 1500 = 3500$$
$$\text{Required growth} = r(P_g - c) / (P_g - c) = r = 0.06 \text{ ✓}$$

**Answer:** Net price ≈ ₹2,700, gross price ≈ ₹4,200 in 5 years.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Combat-Framework Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Energy Economy Environment Nexus] --> B[Resource Depletion]
    A --> C[Resource Misuse]
    A --> D[Pollution]

    B --> B1[Non Renewable Stock Exhaustion]
    B --> B2[Renewable Flow Over Harvest]
    C --> C1[Thermodynamic Exergy Loss]
    C --> C2[Pricing Distortions and Subsidies]
    D --> D1[Local Air Pollution]
    D --> D2[Global GHG Emissions]

    B1 --> P1[Hotelling Rule]
    B1 --> P2[Reserve to Production R P]
    B2 --> P1
    C1 --> P3[Energy Efficiency Standards]
    C2 --> P4[Remove Fossil Subsidies]
    D1 --> P5[Pigouvian Tax]
    D1 --> P6[Emission Standards]
    D2 --> P7[Carbon Pricing]
    D2 --> P8[Cap and Trade ETS]

    P1 --> OUT[Sustainable Welfare Path]
    P3 --> OUT
    P4 --> OUT
    P5 --> OUT
    P6 --> OUT
    P7 --> OUT
    P8 --> OUT

    OUT --> M1[Hotelling Rent equals Reproduction Investment Hartwick Rule]
    OUT --> M2[Genuine Savings Greater Equal Zero]
    OUT --> M3[Environmental Kuznets Curve Inverted U]
```

### 4.2 Hotelling Optimal Extraction Path

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S0[Initial Reserve S0] --> Q1[Period 1 Extraction q1]
    S0 --> Q2[Period 2 Extraction q2]
    S0 --> Q3[Period T Extraction qT]
    Q1 --> P1[Price P1 = c + P0 exp r t1]
    Q2 --> P2[Price P2 = c + P0 exp r t2]
    Q3 --> P3[Price PT = c + P0 exp r tT]
    P1 --> RV[Present Value of Rents Maximised]
    P2 --> RV
    P3 --> RV
```

### 4.3 Pigouvian Tax vs Tradable Permits Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    DEC{Type of Externality} --> D1[Many Heterogeneous Firms]
    DEC --> D2[Few Large Point Sources]
    D1 --> D3[Damage Function Steep?]
    D3 -->|Yes| T1[Prefer Pigouvian Tax]
    D3 -->|No| T2[Prefer Tradable Permits]
    D2 --> P1[Cap and Trade with Auctioning]
    D2 --> P2[Performance Standard]
    T1 --> OUT[Weitzman 1974 Prices]
    T2 --> OUT
    P1 --> OUT
```

### 4.4 Genuine Savings (Adjusted Net Savings) Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    GNP[Gross National Income] --> GNS[Gross National Savings]
    GNS --> CNC[Consumption of Fixed Capital]
    CNC --> NNS[Net National Savings]
    NNS --> EDU[Education Expenditure]
    NNS --> PD[Pollution Damage Monetised]
    NNS --> RD[Resource Depletion Hotelling Rent]
    EDU --> GS[Genuine Savings]
    PD --> GS
    RD --> GS
    GS --> CHECK{GS greater equal zero?}
    CHECK -->|Yes| SUST[Sustainable Path]
    CHECK -->|No| UNSUST[Unsustainable Path]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEMET526]** Define **Hotelling's Rule** and explain its economic intuition in one sentence each.

**Model Answer:**
Hotelling's Rule states that the **net price of an exhaustible resource (price minus marginal extraction cost) must rise at a rate equal to the real rate of interest** along the optimal extraction path, i.e.

$$\frac{d}{dt}\big(P(t) - c\big) = r\big(P(t) - c\big).$$

**Intuition (1 mark):** A unit of resource left in the ground is an *asset* that must earn a return at least equal to the market interest rate; otherwise the owner would sell it today and invest the proceeds. So the scarcity rent (Hotelling rent) compounds at rate $r$.

*(3 marks: 1 for the equation, 1 for stating the rule, 1 for intuition.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEMET526]** Distinguish between a **Pigouvian tax** and a **tradable emission permit** as instruments to combat pollution.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Pigouvian Tax | Tradable Permits |
|---|---|---|
| Control variable | Price (tax per unit emission) | Quantity (total cap $\bar E$) |
| Environmental outcome | Uncertain (depends on demand response) | Certain (cap is met exactly) |
| Cost-effectiveness | High if tax = MD(e*) | High if permits are auctioned |
| Information need | Requires marginal damage function | Requires aggregate cap |
| Revenue | Raises fiscal revenue | Auctioned permits raise revenue; free allocation does not |

*(3 marks: 2 for the table-style comparison, 1 for the conclusion that Weitzman (1974) recommends prices when marginal damages are steeper and quantities when marginal abatement costs are steeper.)*

---

### Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks each, with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *(a)* Derive the **Hotelling Rule** for the optimal extraction of a non-renewable energy resource, starting from the social planner's optimisation problem. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* Using the Hotelling framework, calculate the **net price of coal in 8 years** given current net price ₹1,500/tonne, real interest rate 5 %/year, and constant marginal cost ₹800/tonne. Comment on whether the gross price growth is consistent with the rule. *(7 marks)*

---

**Solution to Q-A(a) — 7 marks**

1. **Objective:** Maximise present value of social welfare, $W = \int_0^T [P(t) - c] q(t) e^{-rt} dt$. *(1 mark)*
2. **Constraint:** $\int_0^T q(t) dt = S_0$. Convert to stock dynamics $\dot S = -q(t)$, $S(0)=S_0$. *(1 mark)*
3. **Current-value Hamiltonian:** $H = [P(t) - c] q(t) - \lambda(t) q(t)$ where $\lambda(t)$ is the shadow price of the in-ground resource. *(1 mark)*
4. **FOC w.r.t. $q$:** $P(t) - c - \lambda(t) = 0$, so $\lambda(t) = P(t) - c$ (the Hotelling rent). *(1 mark)*
5. **Costate equation:** $\dot \lambda(t) = r \lambda(t)$ in current-value form. *(1 mark)*
6. **Substitution:** $\dfrac{d}{dt}[P(t) - c] = r [P(t) - c]$. This is the **Hotelling Rule**. *(1 mark)*
7. **Integration:** $\tilde P(t) = \tilde P(0) e^{rt}$. Conclude that the scarcity rent grows at the discount rate, and explain the asset-pricing intuition in one sentence. *(1 mark)*

---

**Solution to Q-A(b) — 7 marks**

Given: $\tilde P(0) = 1500$ ₹/t, $r = 0.05$, $t = 8$ yr, $c = 800$ ₹/t (constant).

1. Apply net-price Hotelling rule: *(2 marks)*

$$\tilde P(8) = 1500 \cdot e^{0.05 \times 8} = 1500 \cdot e^{0.4}$$

2. Compute $e^{0.4}$: *(2 marks)*

$$e^{0.4} = 1.4918$$

3. Numerical answer: *(1 mark)*

$$\tilde P(8) = 1500 \times 1.4918 = \text{₹}2{,}237.74 / \text{tonne}$$

4. Gross price today: $P_g(0) = 1500 + 800 = \text{₹}2{,}300/\text{tonne}$. *(1 mark)*

5. **Consistency check (Hotelling's rule in gross form):** *(1 mark)*

$$P_g(8) = 2237.74 + 800 = \text{₹}3{,}037.74/\text{tonne}$$

Growth rate of $(P_g - c)$ over 8 years:

$$\frac{2237.74 - 1500}{1500} = 49.18\% \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{CAGR} = (1.4918)^{1/8} - 1 = 5.0\% \; \checkmark$$

This matches the assumed $r = 5\%$, confirming that the gross price path is consistent with Hotelling's rule.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2 (Internal Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *(a)* Explain the **Environmental Kuznets Curve (EKC)**. Discuss its empirical support, the limitations of relying on it for policy, and at least two policy levers that can "shift" the curve leftward. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* A coal-fired plant emits 4 million tonnes of CO₂ per year. The social cost of carbon is ₹4,000 per tonne. Compute the annual **Pigouvian carbon tax** that would internalise this externality, and discuss whether revenue recycling through a per-capita dividend improves welfare and equity. *(7 marks)*

---

**Solution to Q-B(a) — 7 marks**

1. **Definition of EKC (1 mark):** An empirical relationship in which environmental degradation first rises, peaks at a "turning-point income" $y^*$, and then declines as per-capita income continues to grow.

2. **Mathematical form (1 mark):**

$$D(y) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 y + \beta_2 y^2 + \beta_3 y^3, \quad \beta_1 > 0, \; \beta_2 < 0$$

3. **Empirical support (2 marks):** Inverted-U has been documented for local pollutants (SO₂, SPM, CO) in OECD countries. For CO₂ (a global stock pollutant), the evidence is *weak or non-existent*; emissions keep rising with income globally.

4. **Limitations (1 mark):** The EKC is descriptive, not normative; it ignores irreversibility (climate tipping points), trade-leakage, and distributional effects. It cannot justify *delaying* action.

5. **Policy levers to shift the curve (2 marks):** (i) Pigouvian carbon tax / cap-and-trade to internalise the externality; (ii) Public R&D subsidies and renewable portfolio standards that lower the cost of clean technology; (iii) Information instruments (labelling); (iv) Removal of fossil subsidies.

---

**Solution to Q-B(b) — 7 marks**

1. **Optimal Pigouvian tax principle (1 mark):** $t^* = MD(e^*) = SCC$.

2. **Given:** $SCC = ₹4,000/\text{tonne CO}_2$. *(1 mark)*

3. **Annual emissions:** $E = 4 \times 10^6$ tonnes/yr. *(1 mark)*

4. **Tax revenue per year:** *(2 marks)*

$$R = t^* \times E = 4000 \times 4 \times 10^6 = \text{₹}16{,}000 \text{ crore / yr}$$

5. **Revenue recycling — per-capita dividend (2 marks):** If the state of Kerala (population ≈ 3.5 crore in 2024) receives a per-capita dividend:

$$d = \frac{16{,}000 \text{ crore}}{3.5 \text{ crore}} \approx \text{₹}4{,}571 \text{ per person per year}$$

**Welfare & equity discussion:** The dividend is **progressive** in India because low-income households have a lower carbon footprint, so the lump-sum transfer exceeds their tax burden → net positive welfare for the bottom deciles. It is a *double dividend* if the recycled revenue is used to *cut distortionary labour taxes*. Carbon tax + dividend is approximately **revenue-neutral** and avoids the regressivity concern.

> [!WARNING] KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout
> 1. **Do not** write "Pollution will be eliminated by economic growth alone." That is the *original EKC* claim which is empirically false for CO₂ and normatively dangerous. KTU 2024 marking expects you to mention the **limitations of the EKC** explicitly.
> 2. In the Hotelling derivation, students routinely **drop the costate equation** or confuse the current-value and present-value Hamiltonians. Show both explicitly — full 7 marks require the *complete* Hamiltonian + costate steps.
> 3. In Pigouvian-tax numericals, do **not** add the tax on top of an *unregulated* equilibrium and call it a price; the tax is the *per-unit* wedge, not the total cost. State $t^* = MD(e^*)$ before plugging numbers.
> 4. Avoid mixing marginal and average damage. KTU valuation strictly distinguishes $MD$ and $AD$.
> 5. When asked "discuss equity", the model answer must mention both **progressivity** and the **double-dividend hypothesis** — single-line answers lose at least 2 marks.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Hotelling Rule** = $d(P-c)/dt = r(P-c)$ — derive, integrate, apply.
- [ ] **Hotelling rent** = $P - c$ = the in-situ scarcity rent; the only part that grows at $r$.
- [ ] **Reserve-to-Production (R/P) ratio** is a *static* indicator — it ignores demand growth and discovery.
- [ ] **EROEI** < 1 ⇒ the resource is an *energy sink*, not a source. Coal ≈ 30–80, oil shale ≈ 2–5.
- [ ] **Hartwick Rule**: invest Hotelling rents in reproducible capital to maintain constant consumption.
- [ ] **Genuine Savings** = Conventional Savings − Resource Depletion − Pollution Damage (+ Education). $GS \ge 0$ ⇒ weakly sustainable.
- [ ] **EKC** is **descriptive**, not normative; **policy can shift it leftward** via carbon pricing and innovation.
- [ ] **Pigouvian tax** = marginal damage; **Tradable permits** = fixed cap + price discovered by market (Weitzman, 1974).
- [ ] **SCC** is the present value of marginal climate damage from 1 tonne CO₂; used in cost–benefit analysis.
- [ ] **Carbon Intensity** $CI = CO_2 / GDP$ — India's NDC target: reduce emission intensity of GDP by 45 % from 2005 level by 2030.
- [ ] Common **GWP** values: CO₂ = 1, CH₄ ≈ 27–30, N₂O ≈ 273 (IPCC AR6, 100-yr).
- [ ] **Coase Theorem** precondition: low transaction costs + clear property rights → bargaining yields efficient outcome.
- [ ] When asked to *combat* depletion/misuse/pollution, structure the answer along **three pillars**: (i) efficient allocation, (ii) technological substitution, (iii) institutional reform.
- [ ] Always **separate** analytical derivations (Hotelling, Pigouvian) from *applied* policy debates — KTU 14-mark answers want both halves in balance.
- [ ] Common mistake: confusing *gross* and *net* Hotelling rules; remember net-price form omits the constant $c$ term.
- [ ] Reserve a closing paragraph for **equity / political feasibility** — KTU values welfare-economics literacy.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Prospects for Greener Growth: Policies and Politics of Low-Carbon Growth, Unregulated Markets and Technological Change: Oil and Natural Gas Sector Examples, and The Emergence of Markets

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET526 - ENERGY ECONOMICS AND POLICY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** The Prospects for Greener Growth: Policies and Politics of Low-Carbon Growth, Unregulated Markets and Technological Change: Oil and Natural Gas Sector Examples, and The Emergence of Markets

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 — Greener Growth, Unregulated Markets & Emergence of Markets

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Prospects for Greener Growth

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
*Greener Growth* is a development paradigm that integrates economic productivity, environmental sustainability, and social inclusion by decoupling GDP expansion from natural-resource depletion and greenhouse-gas (GHG) emissions. As articulated by the OECD and adopted into the KTU Energy Economics framework, it is the strategic redirection of investment, innovation, and industrial policy toward **low-carbon**, **resource-efficient**, and **climate-resilient** economic activities while maintaining — or even accelerating — aggregate output growth.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** Greener growth is **not** synonymous with *no growth* or *de-growth*. It refers to *qualitative transformation* of the growth process — the same engine, running on cleaner fuel.

**Key Policy Dimensions of Greener Growth:**

| Dimension | Definition | Policy Instrument |
|---|---|---|
| **Decarbonization** | Reducing the carbon intensity of GDP ($CO_2$ per unit of output) | Carbon tax, ETS, fossil subsidy reform |
| **Resource Efficiency** | Producing more economic value per unit of natural input | Circular economy laws, eco-design directives |
| **Resilience** | Capacity of economic systems to absorb climate shocks | Adaptation finance, climate-risk disclosure |
| **Inclusion** | Distributional fairness in the green transition | Just Transition Funds, skill re-skilling schemes |

---

### 1.2 Policies and Politics of Low-Carbon Growth

**Formal Definition:**
*Low-Carbon Growth* denotes a national or regional development trajectory in which $CO_2$, $CH_4$, and other GHG emissions grow more slowly than — or decline relative to — GDP. The "politics" component acknowledges that policy choices are shaped by interest groups, electoral cycles, geopolitical coalitions, and path-dependent institutional lock-in.

**Conceptual Analogy — The Steering-Wheel Metaphor:**
Imagine an economy is a car accelerating down a highway (the growth path). *Low-carbon growth* is **not** about slamming the brakes (which causes a recession crash). Instead, it is about **turning the steering wheel** while keeping the throttle pressed — gradually shifting lanes from the fossil-fuel lane to the clean-energy lane. The "politics" is the family argument in the back seat: the driver (government), the navigator (industry lobby), and the back-seat critics (civil society) all have different opinions about *how sharply* to turn the wheel and *how soon*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition — Carbon Lock-in:**
> *Carbon lock-in* is the self-reinforcing mechanism whereby historical investments in fossil-fuel infrastructure (long-lived plants, pipelines, refineries), supporting institutions (subsidies, regulations, vocational training), and user practices create path dependencies that bias future investment decisions against cleaner alternatives — even when those alternatives become economically superior.

**Three Pillars of Low-Carbon Policy Architecture (KTU Board Favourite):**

1. **Pricing Carbon** — making emitters pay for the social cost of carbon through taxes or cap-and-trade.
2. **Mobilizing Investment** — redirecting capital flows toward clean energy, electrification, and R&D.
3. **Fostering Innovation** — using public procurement, standards, and R&D subsidies to drive technological learning.

---

### 1.3 Unregulated Markets and Technological Change: Oil and Natural Gas Sector Examples

**Formal Definition:**
*Unregulated markets* are economic sectors where prices, entry, and output are determined primarily by competitive forces rather than state-administered controls (tariffs, quantity rationing, administered prices). In the energy context, this typically refers to the **post-liberalisation** oil and natural gas markets — where commodity pricing reflects supply-demand fundamentals, storage arbitrage, and speculative behaviour rather than political diktat.

**Conceptual Analogy — The Free-Flowing River vs. The Irrigated Canal:**
Think of the global oil market before 1980 as an *irrigated canal* — OPEC administered the flow, governments set domestic prices, and the water (oil) went only where bureaucrats said it should. After the 1980s liberalisation wave, the canal was dismantled and replaced by a *free-flowing river* — barrels of oil now follow the gravitational pull of price signals: scarcity raises the price, abundance lowers it. The river, however, still carries pollutants (price volatility, geopolitical chokepoints), which is why *technological change* (shale, LNG, renewables) acts as the equivalent of water-treatment plants inserted midstream.

**Why "Unregulated" is a Misnomer — Important Nuance:**
All energy markets are *regulated* to some degree. The term *unregulated* refers specifically to the **absence of economic regulation** (price caps, entry barriers, quantity controls), *not* the absence of *technical, safety, or environmental* regulation. The KTU board expects students to distinguish these clearly.

---

### 1.4 The Emergence of Markets

**Formal Definition:**
The *Emergence of Markets* refers to the institutional, contractual, and infrastructural processes through which previously non-traded goods or services become commodified, standardized, and exchangeable on organized exchanges or bilateral platforms. In the energy sector, this includes the rise of:
- Spot and futures markets for crude oil (NYMEX WTI, ICE Brent),
- Natural gas hubs (Henry Hub, TTF, JKM),
- Carbon markets (EU ETS, CCA markets),
- Renewable Energy Certificate (REC) markets, and
- Capacity markets and ancillary services markets.

**Conceptual Analogy — The Birth of a Bazaar:**
Imagine a village where everyone barters sacks of wheat for clay pots. There is no "market" yet — only bilateral trades. One day, a clearinghouse is built, weights and measures are standardized (one bushel = 56 lbs), a weekly auction is established, and traders begin offering forward contracts. *That* is the **emergence of a market**: the transformation of isolated bilateral trades into a transparent, standardized, and contestable exchange infrastructure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Anchor:** The first modern oil futures contract was traded on the NYMEX in **1983** — fifteen years after OPEC's 1973 embargo demonstrated the strategic vulnerability of administered oil markets.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Theoretical Architecture of Greener Growth

Greener Growth theory rests on three interlocking economic logics:

### A. The Decoupling Hypothesis
Decoupling occurs when the elasticity of environmental pressure ($E$) with respect to economic output ($Y$) falls below one:

$$
\varepsilon_{E,Y} \;=\; \frac{\partial E \vert E}{\partial Y \vert Y} \;<\; 1
$$

- **Relative decoupling**: emissions grow but slower than GDP ($\varepsilon \in (0,1)$).
- **Absolute decoupling**: emissions fall while GDP rises ($\varepsilon < 0$).

### B. The Environmental Kuznets Curve (EKC) Hypothesis
The EKC posits an inverted-U relationship between per-capita income and environmental degradation:

$$
E_{it} \;=\; \beta_{0} \;+\; \beta_{1}\,Y_{it} \;+\; \beta_{2}\,Y_{it}^{2} \;+\; \beta_{3}\,Y_{it}^{3} \;+\; \mu_{i} \;+\; \lambda_{t} \;+\; \varepsilon_{it}
$$

If $\beta_{1} > 0$ and $\beta_{2} < 0$, the curve is inverted-U and greener growth becomes feasible after income crosses a threshold $Y^{*}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Trap:** The EKC is a *statistical regularity*, not a *law of nature*. Several meta-studies (Stern, 2004; OECD, 2017) have shown the turning point is highly policy-dependent — there is **no automatic income threshold** that guarantees greener growth without active policy intervention.

### C. Porter Hypothesis (Innovation Offsets)
The Porter Hypothesis argues that well-designed environmental regulation can *stimulate* innovation that more than offsets compliance costs, enhancing both competitiveness and environmental performance:

$$
\Pi^{TOTAL} \;=\; \Pi^{Compliance\,Cost} \;+\; \Pi^{Innovation\,Offset} \;-\; \Pi^{Abatement\,Cost}
$$

A *strong* Porter effect occurs when $\Pi^{Innovation} > \Pi^{Compliance + Abatement}$.

---

## 2.2 The Politics of Low-Carbon Policy Choice

The KTU syllabus emphasises four analytical lenses for understanding policy politics:

| Lens | Core Question | Key Theorist | Policy Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Public Interest Theory** | What policy maximizes social welfare? | Pigou, Arrow | Carbon pricing, Pigouvian tax |
| **Private Interest / Capture Theory** | Who lobbies hardest? | Stigler, Peltzman | Industry-friendly phase-outs |
| **Multiple Streams Framework** | When do policy windows open? | Kingdon | Crisis-driven reform |
| **Historical Institutionalism** | Why do policies persist? | Pierson, Mahoney | Path-dependent reform resistance |

---

## 2.3 Unregulated Oil & Gas Markets — Theoretical Foundations

### A. Hotelling's Rule for Non-Renewable Resource Pricing
In an unregulated competitive market, the price of an exhaustible resource must rise at the **discount rate** in present-value terms:

$$
\frac{P_{t+1} - P_{t}}{P_{t}} \;=\; r
$$

Equivalently, the **scarcity rent** (Hotelling rent) grows at the rate of interest:

$$
P_{t} \;=\; MC_{t} \;+\; \tau_{t}, \quad \text{where} \quad \tau_{t+1} \;=\; (1+r)\,\tau_{t}
$$

Here:
- $P_t$ = market price at time $t$
- $MC_t$ = marginal extraction cost
- $\tau_t$ = scarcity rent (in-situ value)
- $r$ = real interest rate

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Oil Prices Deviate from Hotelling:** In reality, oil prices are dominated by **short-run OPEC coordination, geopolitical risk premia, and speculative futures positions**, not by long-run scarcity rents. The shale revolution (2014 onward) caused a permanent downward shift in the *expected* long-run price path, breaking the simple Hotelling framework.

### B. Price-Taker vs. Price-Maker Behaviour
In an unregulated market, individual firms are typically **price-takers**, but OPEC (acting as a cartel) is a **price-maker**. The Saudi-led marginal-producer doctrine (the "swing producer") is the practical bridge between administered and unregulated pricing.

### C. The Natural Gas Market Structure
Unlike oil, natural gas is **pipeline-bound** (until LNG), giving rise to three distinct market models:

| Model | Pricing Mechanism | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Hub-Based (Netback)** | Spot market at physical hub | Henry Hub (USA), TTF (Netherlands) |
| **Oil-Indexed (Long-Term Contracts)** | Price linked to crude oil benchmarks | Most pre-2010 European LNG |
| **Bilateral Monopoly** | Government-to-government negotiated price | Russian gas to former USSR states |

The *global* gas market is increasingly shifting from oil-indexation toward hub-based pricing as LNG arbitrage erodes regional price differentials — a textbook case of **market emergence**.

---

## 2.4 Theoretical Drivers of Market Emergence

Markets emerge when three enabling conditions are satisfied:

$$
\boxed{M_{emerge} \;=\; f\left( Standardization,\; Institutional\,Trust,\; Information\,Symmetry,\; Network\,Density \right)}
$$

Each factor reduces transaction costs and broadens participation:

1. **Standardization** — common grades, contracts (e.g., Brent crude, Gasoil futures).
2. **Institutional Trust** — clearinghouses (ICE, LME, CME), credit guarantees.
3. **Information Symmetry** — price reporting agencies (Platts, Argus, ICIS).
4. **Network Density** — sufficient number of buyers and sellers to enable competitive discovery.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Table — Memorize for KTU ESE**

| # | Concept | Equation | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Carbon Decoupling Elasticity | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta E / E}{\Delta Y / Y}$ | dimensionless | Measures relative decoupling |
| 2 | Environmental Kuznets Curve | $E = \beta_{0} + \beta_{1}Y + \beta_{2}Y^{2}$ | $E$ = emissions (tCO₂), $Y$ = income | Tests inverted-U hypothesis |
| 3 | Hotelling's Rule | $\dot{P}/P = r$ | $P$ = price ($/bbl), $r$ = discount rate | Resource scarcity pricing |
| 4 | Scarcity Rent Dynamics | $\tau_{t+1} = (1+r)\,\tau_{t}$ | $\tau$ = rent ($/bbl) | Inter-temporal extraction |
| 5 | Social Cost of Carbon | $SCC = \sum_{t=0}^{T} \dfrac{D_{t}}{(1+r)^{t}}$ | $D_t$ = marginal damage ($/tCO₂) | Policy CBA |
| 6 | Cost-Benefit (Net Present Value) | $NPV = \sum_{t} \dfrac{B_{t}-C_{t}}{(1+r)^{t}}$ | monetary units | Project appraisal |
| 7 | Price Elasticity of Demand | $\eta = \dfrac{\% \Delta Q}{\% \Delta P}$ | dimensionless | Demand-side response |
| 8 | Learning Curve (Wright's Law) | $C_{x} = C_{1}\,x^{-b}$ | $C_x$ = cost at cumulative output $x$, $b$ = learning rate parameter | Solar PV, batteries cost decline |
| 9 | Lerner Index of Market Power | $L = \dfrac{P - MC}{P}$ | dimensionless (0 = perfect comp.) | OPEC market power measurement |
| 10 | Hub-Based Gas Spread | $\Delta P_{ij} = P_{i} - P_{j} - T_{ij}$ | $T_{ij}$ = transport cost | LNG arbitrage trigger |
| 11 | Carbon Leakage Rate | $\lambda = 1 - \dfrac{\Delta E_{domestic}/\Delta E_{policy}}{E_{policy-covered}/E_{total}}$ | dimensionless | Border carbon adjustment logic |
| 12 | Jevons Paradox Coefficient | $\rho = \varepsilon_{E,P} \times \varepsilon_{D,P}$ | dimensionless | Rebound effect measurement |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Hotelling's Rule with Marginal Extraction Cost

**Setup:** A competitive, unregulated oil market in which an exhaustible resource stock $S$ is extracted by many identical price-taking firms. The social planner chooses the extraction path $\{q_t\}_{t=0}^{\infty}$ to maximize the present value of producer plus consumer surplus, subject to the resource constraint.

**Step 1 — Maximization Problem:**

$$
\max_{\{q_t\}} \;\int_{0}^{\infty} \left[ P(q_t)\,q_t - C(q_t) \right] e^{-r\,t}\,dt
$$

subject to the resource stock constraint:

$$
\dot{S} \;=\; -\,q_t, \quad S_{0} \text{ given}, \quad S_{t} \geq 0
$$

**Step 2 — Current-Value Hamiltonian:**

$$
\mathcal{H} \;=\; P(q_t)\,q_t \;-\; C(q_t) \;-\; \lambda_t\,q_t
$$

where $\lambda_t$ is the shadow price (in-situ value, or scarcity rent) of the resource.

**Step 3 — First-Order Condition (Maximum extraction profit per unit):**

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{H}}{\partial q_t} \;=\; P(q_t) \;+\; \frac{\partial P}{\partial q_t}\,q_t \;-\; \frac{\partial C}{\partial q_t} \;-\; \lambda_t \;=\; 0
$$

For a price-taking firm with no market-power impact, $\frac{\partial P}{\partial q_t} \approx 0$, and $\frac{\partial C}{\partial q_t} \equiv MC_t$:

$$
\boxed{P_t \;=\; MC_t \;+\; \lambda_t}
$$

This is the **Hotelling pricing rule**: market price equals marginal cost plus the scarcity rent.

**Step 4 — Co-State Equation (Adjoint Condition):**
The shadow price $\lambda_t$ must satisfy the standard adjoint differential equation:

$$
\dot{\lambda}_t \;-\; r\,\lambda_t \;=\; -\frac{\partial \mathcal{H}}{\partial S} \;=\; 0
$$

This gives the **Hotelling rent dynamics**:

$$
\boxed{\frac{\dot{\lambda}_t}{\lambda_t} \;=\; r \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \lambda_t \;=\; \lambda_0\,e^{r\,t}}
$$

**Step 5 — Combined Rule:**
Substituting back into the pricing equation:

$$
\boxed{P_t \;=\; MC_t \;+\; \lambda_0\,e^{r\,t}}
$$

**Interpretation:** In an unregulated competitive oil market with rising or constant extraction costs, the *total* price rises at the rate of interest, while the *scarcity rent* rises at exactly $r$. This is why **in-situ value appreciation** is the central economic signal for long-run resource allocation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Wright's Law (Experience Curve) for Solar PV Cost Decline

**Setup:** The unit cost of a manufactured technology declines as a power-law function of *cumulative* output (experience), not calendar time.

**Step 1 — Empirical Cost Function:**

$$
C(x) \;=\; C_{1}\,x^{-b}
$$

where $x$ = cumulative installed capacity (MW) and $b$ > 0 = learning elasticity.

**Step 2 — Log-Linear Form (for econometric estimation):**

$$
\ln C(x) \;=\; \ln C_{1} \;-\; b\,\ln x
$$

This is estimated as a simple OLS regression.

**Step 3 — Progress Ratio (PR) vs. Learning Rate (LR):**
When cumulative output *doubles*, costs fall by a constant fraction. The relationship:

$$
PR \;=\; 1 - LR \;=\; 2^{-b}
$$

Equivalently:

$$
b \;=\; -\frac{\ln(PR)}{\ln 2}
$$

**Numerical Illustration for Crystalline Silicon PV:**
Empirically, $b \approx 0.32$, so:

$$
PR \;=\; 2^{-0.32} \;=\; 0.802
$$

$$
LR \;=\; 1 - 0.802 \;=\; 0.198 \;(\approx 20\%)
$$

This means **every doubling of cumulative PV capacity reduces unit cost by approximately 20%** — the empirical bedrock of solar cost forecasts.

**Step 4 — Forecasting:**
If global cumulative PV reaches $x_2$ and the policy target requires a cost $C^*$:

$$
x_{2} \;=\; x_{1}\left(\frac{C_1}{C^{*}}\right)^{1/b}
$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Policy Instruments for Low-Carbon Growth

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Favourite Question Type:** Compare and contrast policy instruments — students must present them in a comparison matrix. The matrix below is a model answer template.

| Criterion | Carbon Tax | Cap-and-Trade (ETS) | Fossil Subsidy Reform | Renewable Portfolio Standard (RPS) | Direct R&D Subsidy |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Price Certainty** | High (set by gov.) | Low (market-determined) | Medium | Low (subsidy-cost) | High |
| **Emission Certainty** | Low | High (cap binds) | Medium | Low (technology-specific) | Low |
| **Revenue for Government** | Yes | Yes (if auctioned) | Positive (savings) | No | No |
| **Administrative Cost** | Low | Medium-High | Low | High | Medium |
| **Political Feasibility** | Low (visible cost) | Higher (hidden cost) | Variable (regressive risk) | High (technology-neutral politics) | Medium |
| **Innovation Incentive** | Weak | Moderate (price floor matters) | Weak | Strong (tech mandate) | Strong (directed) |
| **Equity Impact** | Regressive (need rebate) | Regressive (auction revenue recyclable) | Regressive (fuel-poor affected) | Regressive via tariffs | Neutral |
| **Risk of Leakage** | High (without BCA) | High (without BCA) | Medium | Low | Low |

---

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example: Calculating the Social Cost of Carbon (SCC)

**Problem:** A regulator is evaluating a one-tonne CO₂ emission today. Marginal damages are estimated at:

- Year 0: $D_0 = \$50$/tCO₂ (current)
- Year 1: $D_1 = \$55$/tCO₂
- Year 2: $D_2 = \$60.5$/tCO₂ (10% growth rate)
- Discount rate: $r = 5\%$

**Step 1 — Damage Schedule:**

$$
D_{0} = 50,\qquad D_{1} = 50 \times 1.10 = 55,\qquad D_{2} = 55 \times 1.10 = 60.5
$$

**Step 2 — Discount Each to Present Value:**

$$
PV(D_{1}) \;=\; \frac{55}{1.05} \;=\; 52.38
$$

$$
PV(D_{2}) \;=\; \frac{60.5}{(1.05)^{2}} \;=\; \frac{60.5}{1.1025} \;=\; 54.88
$$

**Step 3 — Sum the Present Values:**

$$
SCC \;=\; D_{0} \;+\; PV(D_{1}) \;+\; PV(D_{2}) \;=\; 50 \;+\; 52.38 \;+\; 54.88 \;=\; \$157.26 \text{ / tCO}_{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Policy Interpretation:**
A Pigouvian carbon tax of approximately **\$157/tCO₂** would internalize the marginal external damage from emitting one additional tonne today. The US EPA's 2023 estimate of the SCC ranged from **\$120 to \$340/tCO₂** under different discount-rate assumptions — illustrating the *normative* nature of the SCC.

> [!WARNING]
> **Sensitivity Trap:** The SCC is extraordinarily sensitive to the chosen discount rate. Doubling $r$ from 3% to 6% can reduce the SCC by more than half. KTU examiners frequently test this sensitivity. Always conduct a **sensitivity analysis** at $r = 3\%$, $5\%$, and $7\%$.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Three Phases of Market Emergence in Oil & Gas

| Phase | Timeframe | Defining Feature | Example | KTU Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Phase 1 — Bilateral Cartel Era** | 1960–1985 | "Seven Sisters" + administered prices; long-term contracts at posted prices | Standard Oil, Texaco, BP concessions | Markets are *embedded* in colonial / state structures |
| **Phase 2 — Spot Market Birth** | 1983–2000 | NYMEX/ICE futures; Brent marker; spot/contract dual pricing | NYMEX WTI (1983), Brent marker (1988) | Markets *emerge* via standardization, clearing, and benchmarking |
| **Phase 3 — Financialized & Global** | 2000–present | Paper barrels > physical barrels; LNG arbitrage; shale revolution; price-reporting agencies drive discovery | JKM LNG index (2017), Henry Hub, TTF | Markets are *mature, integrated, and reflexive* |

---

## 3.6 Worked Example: Lerner Index for OPEC Market Power

**Problem:** Estimate OPEC's market power if the cartel's marginal cost is $MC = \$30$/bbl and the prevailing market price is $P = \$80$/bbl.

**Step 1 — Apply the Lerner Index:**

$$
L \;=\; \frac{P - MC}{P} \;=\; \frac{80 - 30}{80} \;=\; \frac{50}{80} \;=\; 0.625
$$

**Step 2 — Interpretation:**

- $L = 0$: perfect competition
- $L = 1$: monopoly
- $L = 0.625$: OPEC has substantial but not absolute market power

**Step 3 — Caveat:**
OPEC's *effective* Lerner index is lower because (a) non-OPEC supply (shale, Canada oil sands) responds to high prices, and (b) demand elasticity for crude oil in the short run is highly inelastic but becomes more elastic over 2–5 years (vehicle fleet turnover, EV adoption).

## 4.3 Hotelling's Rule — Stock-Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stock[Resource Stock S_t] -->|extraction q_t reduces stock| depleted[Remaining Reserve S_t minus q_t]
    depleted --> stock
    stock -->|shadow price lambda_t| hamiltonian[Current Value Hamiltonian]
    q_t[Extraction Path q_t] --> hamiltonian
    hamiltonian --> foc[First Order Condition: P_t = MC_t plus lambda_t]
    adjoint[Co-State Equation: lambda grows at rate r] -->|lambda_t = lambda_0 e to the r t| foc
    foc -->|Final Pricing Rule| pricing[P_t = MC_t plus lambda_0 e to the r t]
```

    cost2 -->|doubling again| cost3

    note --> cost3
