# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

> Define *greener growth*. How does it differ from *de-growth*?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

*Greener growth* is an economic development paradigm that aims to **decouple economic output (GDP growth) from environmental degradation and greenhouse-gas emissions** through cleaner technologies, resource efficiency, and inclusive policy design. It accepts — and even promotes — continued GDP growth, but *qualitatively transforms* its composition: low-carbon sectors expand, fossil-fuel sectors contract.

*De-growth*, by contrast, is a normative doctrine advocating for a **planned, equitable reduction** of aggregate output and consumption in high-income countries to remain within planetary boundaries.

**Key Distinction:**

| Feature | Greener Growth | De-growth |
|---|---|---|
| GDP trajectory | Continued growth | Deliberate contraction |
| Policy tools | Carbon pricing, ETS, R&D | Work-time reduction, caps, rationing |
| Geographic applicability | Universal | Predominantly Global North |
| KTU 2024 classification | *Mainstream* | *Critical / heterodox* |

(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for distinguishing feature, 1 mark for contextual example.)

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

> What is *carbon lock-in*? Illustrate with one example from the oil-and-gas sector.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

*Carbon lock-in* is the **self-reinforcing mechanism by which historical investments in fossil-fuel infrastructure, supporting institutions, and user practices create path dependencies** that bias future investment decisions against cleaner alternatives, even when those alternatives become economically superior.

**Oil & Gas Example:** A coal-fired thermal power plant built with a 40-year economic life in the 1990s commits future cash flows, sunk capital, and a workforce trained in coal operations for four decades. Even after solar PV becomes cheaper than coal (as occurred in India by 2018), the plant continues operating because (i) stranded-asset losses are politically unacceptable, (ii) lignite-mining unions resist closure, and (iii) the regional grid was designed around baseload coal.

**Three Lock-in Mechanisms (KTU examiners reward these):**
1. **Infrastructure lock-in** — long-lived capital stock.
2. **Institutional lock-in** — subsidies, regulations, vocational training.
3. **Behavioural lock-in** — user practices and cultural norms.

(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for oil/gas example, 1 mark for mechanism identification.)

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Format Note:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, typically divided as Part (a) = 7 marks and Part (b) = 7 marks. Students must answer EITHER Question A OR Question B (Module Internal Choice).

---

### Question A (14 Marks): **[KTU University Exam — Sample Question]**

#### Part (a) (7 Marks):
> Critically evaluate the **Environmental Kuznets Curve (EKC)** hypothesis as a framework for understanding the prospects of greener growth. Discuss its empirical validity and the role of policy in determining the curve's shape.

#### Part (b) (7 Marks):
> Explain the concept of *unregulated markets* with reference to the global oil market. Why is the term "unregulated" technically a misnomer? Discuss with examples.

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 Marks):

**Step 1 — Definition [1 mark]:**
The Environmental Kuznets Curve (EKC) hypothesizes an inverted-U relationship between per-capita income and environmental degradation, suggesting that environmental damage first rises with income but eventually falls as societies become rich enough to afford cleaner technologies and stronger environmental regulation.

**Step 2 — Mathematical Representation [1 mark]:**

$$
E_{it} \;=\; \beta_{0} \;+\; \beta_{1}\,Y_{it} \;+\; \beta_{2}\,Y_{it}^{2} \;+\; \mu_{i} \;+\; \lambda_{t} \;+\; \varepsilon_{it}
$$

An inverted-U requires $\beta_{1} > 0$ and $\beta_{2} < 0$, with turning point at:

$$
Y^{*} \;=\; -\frac{\beta_{1}}{2\beta_{2}}
$$

**Step 3 — Empirical Evidence FOR [1.5 marks]:**
- Cross-country studies by Grossman & Krueger (1995) found the inverted-U pattern for $SO_2$ and particulate matter.
- OECD countries have achieved *relative decoupling* — emissions grow more slowly than GDP.
- Air-pollutant EKC appears robust across several studies.

**Step 4 — Empirical Evidence AGAINST [1.5 marks]:**
- **Carbon emissions** show no robust inverted-U globally — many high-income countries (Australia, USA, Canada) still have rising per-capita $CO_2$.
- Stern (2004) and the IPCC (2014, 2022) explicitly warn against relying on automatic EKC dynamics.
- The turning point is **policy-dependent**, not automatic; countries without strong environmental policy (e.g., Saudi Arabia, Russia) remain on the upward slope despite high income.

**Step 5 — Role of Policy [1.5 marks]:**
The curve's shape is endogenous to policy choices: carbon taxes, ETS, R&D subsidies, and renewable portfolio standards *bend the curve downward* by raising the price of carbon-intensive output and accelerating technology diffusion. Without active policy, the EKC is a description of correlation, not a causal mechanism for greener growth.

**Step 6 — Verdict [0.5 mark]:**
The EKC is a **useful diagnostic**, not a **policy prescription**. It cannot substitute for explicit climate policy.

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 Marks):

**Step 1 — Definition of Unregulated Markets [1 mark]:**
Unregulated markets are sectors where prices, entry, and output are determined by **competitive market forces** without state-administered price controls, quantity rationing, or entry barriers.

**Step 2 — The Global Oil Market as Illustration [2 marks]:**
Following the 1973 OPEC embargo and the 1979 Iranian Revolution, oil-importing nations dismantled price controls:
- USA: phased out oil price controls under the Energy Policy and Conservation Act (1975 amendments) and the Windfall Profits Tax phase-outs (mid-1990s).
- UK: privatised British Petroleum (1979–1987).
- NYMEX launched WTI futures (1983), enabling **price discovery** independent of posted prices.

Today, oil prices are set at the **margin** by global supply-demand, with benchmarks (Brent, WTI, Dubai) reflecting thousands of bilateral trades aggregated by price-reporting agencies.

**Step 3 — Why "Unregulated" is a Misnomer [2.5 marks]:**
Despite the absence of *economic* regulation (price ceilings, entry quotas), the oil market is heavily subject to:
- **Technical & Safety regulation** — pipeline codes, refinery emission standards, marine bunkering rules.
- **Environmental regulation** — methane leakage limits, flaring bans, IMO 2020 sulphur cap.
- **Taxation** — excise duties, royalty regimes, windfall taxes (UK Energy Profits Levy 2022).
- **Strategic reserves** — IEA-coordinated emergency stockpiles.
- **Sanctions regimes** — affecting Russian, Iranian, and Venezuelan barrels.

Hence "unregulated" means only the *absence of price/quantity control*, not the *absence of all public policy*.

**Step 4 — Distinction from Natural Gas [1 mark]:**
Until LNG became globally liquid (post-2017), most gas was sold under long-term oil-indexed contracts — closer to a *regulated* regime. The emergence of JKM, TTF, and Henry Hub has shifted gas toward unregulated hub pricing, with arbitrage closing regional spreads.

**Step 5 — Conclusion [0.5 mark]:**
"Unregulated" should be read as **deregulated competitive pricing**, not as the absence of state presence — a distinction KTU examiners consistently test.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): **[KTU University Exam — Alternate Choice]**

#### Part (a) (7 Marks):
> Discuss the concept of the **emergence of markets** in the energy sector. Explain the institutional, contractual, and infrastructural conditions required for a market to emerge, with examples from oil, natural gas, and carbon markets.

#### Part (b) (7 Marks):
> "Unregulated oil markets reduce price volatility and enhance allocative efficiency." Critically evaluate this statement using Hotelling's Rule and recent evidence from the shale revolution.

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 Marks):

**Step 1 — Definition [1 mark]:**
The *emergence of markets* refers to the institutional, contractual, and infrastructural processes through which previously non-traded goods become **commodified, standardized, and exchangeable** on organized exchanges or bilateral platforms.

**Step 2 — Five Conditions for Market Emergence [2 marks]:**
1. **Standardization** of grades, contracts, and terms.
2. **Institutional trust** — clearinghouses, credit guarantees, dispute resolution.
3. **Information symmetry** — price-reporting agencies (Platts, Argus, ICIS).
4. **Network density** — sufficient buyers and sellers.
5. **Property rights enforcement** — title to the underlying commodity.

**Step 3 — Example: Crude Oil [1.5 marks]:**
- 1983: NYMEX launches WTI futures.
- 1988: Brent marker established.
- 2000s: ICE Brent becomes the global benchmark; over **80% of physical oil worldwide is priced off Brent** despite most physical barrels being delivered elsewhere.
- Oil futures open interest exceeds 10× daily physical production — a financialized, mature market.

**Step 4 — Example: Natural Gas [1.5 marks]:**
- Until ~2010, LNG was sold under 20-year oil-indexed contracts.
- 2011+: shale gas glut in USA frees LNG cargoes for spot sale.
- 2017: JKM (Japan-Korea Marker) launched; TTF (Dutch) becomes European hub.
- LNG arbitrage now closes Henry Hub–JKM spreads within hours — a textbook case of market emergence.

**Step 5 — Example: Carbon [1 mark]:**
- EU ETS launched 2005; initially illiquid and crashed in Phase I.
- Phase III (2013–2020): market-stabilization reserve and auctioning create liquidity.
- By 2023, EU ETS daily volume exceeds €1 billion; secondary markets and derivatives exist.

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 Marks):

**Step 1 — Restate the Claim [0.5 mark]:**
The statement claims that unregulated oil markets reduce volatility and improve allocative efficiency compared to regulated/administrative markets.

**Step 2 — Theoretical Argument FOR [2 marks]:**
- Price signals in unregulated markets transmit **scarcity information** rapidly, enabling efficient inter-temporal allocation.
- Hotelling's Rule predicts price paths that smooth consumption over time:

$$
P_t \;=\; MC_t \;+\; \lambda_0\,e^{r\,t}
$$

- The shale revolution (2010s) demonstrated that unregulated markets can quickly reallocate capital from tight oil to shale plays when prices rise, *reducing* long-run volatility.

**Step 3 — Theoretical Argument AGAINST [2.5 marks]:**
- Short-run volatility has *increased* in unregulated markets: 2008 oil price crash ($-37/bbl WTI negative); 2014–2016 collapse ($110 to $26); 2020 COVID crash; 2022 Russia–Ukraine spike ($120+).
- **Speculation** in futures markets decouples price from physical fundamentals (≥70% of WTI volume is financial, not physical).
- OPEC retains residual pricing power (Lerner Index ~0.3–0.6), undermining the "competitive market" assumption.
- Demand is short-run inelastic ($\eta \approx -0.05$), so supply shocks produce *large* price swings.

**Step 4 — Hotelling's Rule Reality Check [1.5 marks]:**
Empirically, oil prices *do not* rise smoothly at rate $r$. They follow a **random walk with jumps**, dominated by:
- Geopolitical events (Iran, Russia, Venezuela).
- OPEC coordination.
- Shale supply elasticity.
- Dollar exchange-rate movements.

The shale revolution in particular **violated** the Hotelling framework by introducing a near-backstop production technology with marginal costs under \$30/bbl — breaking the simple in-situ-value appreciation trajectory.

**Step 5 — Conclusion [0.5 mark]:**
Unregulated markets *enhance long-run allocative efficiency* but *increase short-run volatility*. They are not unambiguously superior to administered systems; hybrid regimes (price-band stabilisation plus competitive discovery) may be socially optimal.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Module 3 Topics**
>
> 1. **Conflating *Greener Growth* with *De-growth*.** Many students write the two as synonyms — they are **conceptually opposed**. Examiners award **zero marks** for this error.
>
> 2. **Treating "unregulated" literally.** Saying "oil markets are not regulated" is **incorrect**. The correct phrasing: "oil markets are *not subject to economic price/quantity regulation*, though they remain subject to technical, safety, environmental, and tax regulation."
>
> 3. **Ignoring the politics.** Module 3 explicitly includes the word *politics*. Pure economic analysis without mentioning interest groups, electoral cycles, Kingdon's Multiple Streams, or institutional lock-in will score **at most 60%** of available marks.
>
> 4. **Mishandling Hotelling's Rule.** Do **not** state that "oil prices rise at the discount rate" as an empirical regularity. Hotelling's Rule is a **normative theoretical outcome** under restrictive assumptions (competitive market, known stock, constant extraction cost), not an observed fact.
>
> 5. **Forgetting the carbon-lock-in example.** When asked to "illustrate," you **must** name a specific asset, institution, or practice — abstract gestures lose marks.
>
> 6. **Confusing Brent and WTI.** They are **different benchmarks** at **different locations** (Brent = North Sea, dated; WTI = Cushing, Oklahoma, prompt). Examiners reward this distinction.
>
> 7. **Missing Wright's Law.** In technology-cost discussions, students often cite Moore's Law (semiconductors) — wrong. **Wright's Law** (cumulative output → cost) is the correct empirical regularity for solar PV and lithium-ion batteries.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 3 (Greener Growth, Unregulated Markets, Emergence of Markets)**

### Greener Growth & Low-Carbon Policy
- ✅ Greener growth = **decoupling** GDP from emissions; **NOT de-growth**.
- ✅ Three pillars: **carbon pricing**, **investment mobilization**, **innovation policy**.
- ✅ **Carbon lock-in** has 3 mechanisms: infrastructure, institutional, behavioural.
- ✅ **Environmental Kuznets Curve (EKC)**: inverted-U between income and pollution; but **policy-dependent**, not automatic.
- ✅ **Porter Hypothesis**: well-designed regulation can *induce* innovation that *offsets* compliance costs.

### The Politics of Low-Carbon Growth
- ✅ Four analytical lenses: Public Interest, Private Interest/Capture, Multiple Streams (Kingdon), Historical Institutionalism.
- ✅ **Carbon leakage** + **border carbon adjustments** = recurring KTU theme.
- ✅ Policy instruments: carbon tax, ETS, subsidy reform, RPS, R&D subsidies — each has different certainty / equity / leakage profiles.

### Unregulated Oil & Gas Markets
- ✅ **Hotelling's Rule**: $P_t = MC_t + \lambda_0 e^{rt}$; shadow price grows at discount rate.
- ✅ Oil benchmarks: **Brent (ICE)**, **WTI (NYMEX)**, **Dubai/Oman**.
- ✅ Gas market models: **hub-based** (Henry Hub, TTF, JKM), **oil-indexed**, **bilateral monopoly**.
- ✅ "Unregulated" = no *economic* price/quantity control; technical & environmental rules still apply.
- ✅ **Lerner Index** $L = (P-MC)/P$ measures market power (OPEC ≈ 0.3–0.6 historically).
- ✅ **Shale revolution** (2010s) broke Hotelling's framework — introduced backstop supply.

### Emergence of Markets
- ✅ Five enabling conditions: **standardization, institutional trust, information symmetry, network density, property rights**.
- ✅ Crude oil: **1983 NYMEX WTI futures** → Brent marker → financialization.
- ✅ Natural gas: oil-indexed → hub-based → LNG arbitrage → JKM (2017).
- ✅ Carbon: **EU ETS** (2005); mature after MSR reform (Phase III).
- ✅ **Wright's Law**: $C_x = C_1 x^{-b}$; Learning Rate ≈ 20% for solar PV.

### High-Yield Numerical Tools
- ✅ Decoupling elasticity: $\varepsilon = (\Delta E/E)/(\Delta Y/Y)$.
- ✅ SCC: $SCC = \sum_{t} D_t / (1+r)^t$.
- ✅ NPV rule for project CBA.
- ✅ Lerner Index for market power.
- ✅ Wright's Law cost forecast: $x_2 = x_1 (C_1/C^*)^{1/b}$.

### Examiner's Golden Rules
- 🎯 Always **define before applying** — formal definitions earn marks.
- 🎯 Always **cite a real-world example** — oil/gas benchmarks, ETS phase, JKM launch.
- 🎯 Always **distinguish** similar concepts: greener growth vs. de-growth; economic vs. technical regulation; Brent vs. WTI; Wright vs. Moore.
- 🎯 Always **complete sensitivity analysis** when computing SCC, NPV, or Hotelling paths.
- 🎯 Always **mention politics** — Kingdon, capture theory, or carbon lock-in — at least once in any Module 3 answer.

topics.title: Natural Gas Market
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET526 - ENERGY ECONOMICS AND POLICY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Natural Gas Market

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Natural Gas Market** refers to the global and regional economic system comprising the production, processing, transportation, storage, distribution, and trading of natural gas as a primary energy commodity. It is an interconnected network of physical infrastructure (wells, pipelines, LNG terminals, storage facilities) and commercial arrangements (spot trades, long-term contracts, futures) governed by supply–demand dynamics, regulatory frameworks, and pricing benchmarks such as **Henry Hub (HH)**, **Title Transfer Facility (TTF)**, and **Japan-Korea Marker (JKM)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The natural gas market is uniquely distinct from crude oil and electricity markets due to its physical constraints (molecular weight, pipeline capacity, lack of universal fungibility) and the historical evolution from **oil-linked pricing** to **gas-on-gas competition (GOGC)**.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Highway Analogy"

Imagine natural gas as **water flowing through a complex pipe network**:
- **Upstream** = the reservoirs and wells (water source)
- **Midstream** = the long-distance pipelines (highways connecting cities)
- **Downstream** = local distribution networks (city water lines reaching homes)
- **LNG terminals** = "ports" where water is converted to ice (gas liquefied) and shipped across oceans in "tankers"

Just like a city cannot instantly reroute water from one reservoir to another without massive infrastructure, the natural gas market is **physically constrained by pipeline routes and LNG shipping lanes**, making it **regional** rather than a single global market.

### 1.3 Key Characteristics of Natural Gas Market

| Characteristic | Description |
|---|---|
| **Physical Form** | Odorless, colorless hydrocarbon (mainly CH₄) |
| **Energy Density (LNG)** | ~ **35.8 MJ/m³** (≈ 600 times more compact than gaseous form) |
| **Calorific Value** | ~ **38 MJ/Nm³** (standard cubic meter) |
| **Storage Difficulty** | Hard to store economically (requires salt caverns/LNG tanks) |
| **Transportation** | Pipelines (regional) + LNG tankers (intercontinental) |
| **Market Structure** | Historically monopolistic → increasingly competitive |
| **Price Volatility** | Moderate (lower than oil, higher than coal) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is Natural Gas Special?**
> Unlike oil (which can be easily shipped globally via tankers), natural gas historically had **limited tradability**, leading to fragmented regional markets. The advent of **LNG technology** (post-1960s) and **pipeline integration** (e.g., EU network) has slowly globalized the trade.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Natural Gas Value Chain

The natural gas value chain is divided into **three integrated segments**:

**A. Upstream (Exploration & Production)**
- Involves geological surveying, drilling, and extraction of raw natural gas.
- Key players: **Exploration & Production (E&P) companies** (e.g., ONGC, Gazprom, ExxonMobil).
- Output: Raw wellhead gas containing methane (CH₄) plus impurities (H₂S, CO₂, N₂).

**B. Midstream (Processing, Transport & Storage)**
- Gas processing → removal of impurities and Natural Gas Liquids (NGLs) extraction.
- Transportation via **gaseous pipelines** (onshore/offshore) or **LNG carriers** (intercontinental).
- Storage in **depleted reservoirs, aquifers, or salt caverns**.
- Key players: Pipeline operators, LNG project developers (e.g., QatarEnergy, Cheniere).

**C. Downstream (Distribution & Marketing)**
- Local distribution companies (LDCs) deliver gas to **end-users** (residential, commercial, industrial, power plants).
- Final pricing includes: **wellhead price + transport tariff + distribution margin + taxes**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** The "midstream" segment is the most economically critical in natural gas — it is the bottleneck that determines whether gas can flow from a cheap supply basin to a high-demand market.

### 2.2 Market Structure Evolution

The natural gas market has evolved through three structural models:

**1. Monopolistic Model (Pre-1990s in most regions)**
- Vertically integrated state-owned utilities controlled entire value chain.
- Prices were **regulated** based on cost-of-service.
- Example: ONGC + GAIL in India, Gazprom in Russia.

**2. Transitional / Unbundled Model (1990s–2010s)**
- **Legal separation** of upstream, midstream, and downstream operations.
- Introduction of **Third-Party Access (TPA)** to pipelines.
- Example: EU Gas Directives (1998, 2003, 2009).

**3. Liberalized / Hub-Based Model (2010s onwards)**
- Gas-on-gas competition (GOGC) at virtual trading hubs.
- Multiple buyers and sellers, spot and futures markets.
- Example: Henry Hub (USA), TTF (Netherlands), NBP (UK).

### 2.3 Pricing Mechanisms in the Natural Gas Market

There are **four primary pricing mechanisms** used globally:

**(a) Oil-Indexed Pricing (OIP)**
- Gas price is **linked to crude oil prices** with a time lag.
- Formula: 
$$P_{gas} = P_{oil,ref} \times \alpha + \beta$$
where $\alpha$ is the **sliding scale coefficient** (typically 0.10–0.15) and $\beta$ is a constant.
- Used historically in long-term contracts in Europe and Asia.

**(b) Gas-on-Gas Competition (GOGC)**
- Price is determined by **real-time supply and demand** at trading hubs.
- Most efficient and transparent mechanism.
- Dominant in North America and increasingly in Europe.

**(c) Netback Pricing**
- LNG exporter's price = (Destination market price) − (Liquefaction cost) − (Shipping cost) − (Regasification cost)
- Used in LNG spot and portfolio trades.

**(d) Regulated / Administered Pricing**
- Government-set prices for strategic or social reasons.
- Common in developing nations (e.g., domestic gas in India, China).

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Netback Price** | $P_{NB} = P_{M} - (C_L + C_S + C_R)$ | LNG export price after deducting mid-chain costs | USD/MMBtu |
| **Sliding Scale Coefficient** | $\alpha = \frac{P_{gas, contract}}{P_{oil, ref}}$ | Ratio of gas price to oil price | dimensionless |
| **Hub-Based Spot Price** | $P_{hub} = f(S, D, I, S_t)$ | Function of supply, demand, imports, storage | USD/MMBtu |
| **LNG Energy Equivalence** | $E_{LNG} = 38 \times V_{LNG}$ | Energy in LNG volume | MJ |
| **Pipeline Tariff** | $T_{pipe} = \frac{C_{capex} + C_{opex}}{Q_{annual}}$ | Cost of transporting per unit gas | USD/MMBtu |
| **Btu Conversion** | $1 \text{ MMBtu} = 1.055 \times 10^9 \text{ J}$ | Energy unit conversion | J |
| **Gas Volume Conversion** | $1 \text{ BCM} \approx 35.3 \text{ Bcf}$ | Billion Cubic Meters to Billion Cubic Feet | volumetric |
| **Henry Hub to LNG parity** | $P_{LNG, Asia} = P_{HH} + C_{liq} + C_{ship} + C_{reg}$ | Asian LNG price build-up | USD/MMBtu |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Exam Insight:** The **Henry Hub (HH)** price is denominated in **USD/MMBtu**, while international gas prices are quoted in **USD/MMBtu** or **USD/therm**. Always verify units in numerical problems.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering & Policy Utility

- **Energy Security Planning:** Understanding hub vs oil-indexed pricing helps governments reduce import bill volatility.
- **Investment Decisions:** LNG project IRR calculations depend on the chosen pricing regime.
- **Climate Policy:** Natural gas as a "transition fuel" affects market dynamics vs renewables.
- **Pipeline Economics:** Tariff design influences cross-border gas trade flows (e.g., Nord Stream, TANAP).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Netback Pricing for LNG Exports

The **netback price** is the minimum price an LNG exporter must receive at the destination market to break even. The derivation proceeds in algebraic stages:

**Step 1 — Identify the Exporter's Cost Stack**
An LNG exporter incurs four major cost components:
1. Procurement cost (wellhead gas): $C_W$
2. Liquefaction cost: $C_L$
3. Shipping cost (LNG tanker): $C_S$
4. Regasification cost: $C_R$

**Step 2 — Define Market Price at Destination**
The buyer in the destination market (e.g., Japan, Korea) is willing to pay $P_M$ per MMBtu of regasified LNG.

**Step 3 — Express Netback Algebraically**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{NB} &= P_M - C_L - C_S - C_R \\
       &= P_M - (C_L + C_S + C_R)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Determine the Breakeven Wellhead Price**
For the LNG project to be profitable, the netback must exceed the wellhead procurement cost:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{NB} &> C_W \\
P_M - C_L - C_S - C_R &> C_W \\
P_M &> C_W + C_L + C_S + C_R
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Add a Risk-Adjusted Margin**
Including a margin $\mu$ (typically 8–15%), the minimum viable market price becomes:

$$
P_M^{min} = \frac{(C_W + C_L + C_S + C_R)(1 + \mu)}{1 - \tau}
$$

where $\tau$ is the host country's tax rate on LNG sales.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Global Natural Gas Hubs

| Hub | Region | Pricing Basis | Liquidity | 2024 Avg Price Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Henry Hub (HH)** | USA (Louisiana) | GOGC (Spot + Futures) | Very High | 2.0 – 4.0 USD/MMBtu |
| **TTF (Title Transfer Facility)** | Netherlands (EU) | GOGC | Very High | 8.0 – 14.0 USD/MMBtu |
| **NBP (National Balancing Point)** | United Kingdom | GOGC | High | 8.0 – 13.5 USD/MMBtu |
| **JKM (Japan-Korea Marker)** | Asia (Platts) | LNG Spot Assessment | Moderate | 9.0 – 16.0 USD/MMBtu |
| **PVB (PSV Virtual Balance)** | Italy | GOGC | Moderate | 8.5 – 14.0 USD/MMBtu |
| **PEGAS / THE** | France/Germany | GOGC | High | 8.0 – 14.0 USD/MMBtu |
| **Domestic (India)** | India | Administered (APM) + Spot | Low-Moderate | 5.0 – 12.0 USD/MMBtu |

> [!NOTE]
> **Analytical Insight:** The price differential between HH and JKM (the "JKM-HH spread") is the primary arbitrage metric for LNG traders. A wide spread (>$6/MMBtu) incentivizes LNG cargo diversion from Atlantic to Pacific basins.

### 3.3 Derivation: Sliding Scale Mechanism in Oil-Indexed Contracts

In a traditional oil-indexed contract, the gas price is tied to a **sliding scale** of crude oil prices. The derivation is as follows:

**Step 1 — Base Reference Price**
The reference crude oil price is typically a basket (e.g., Brent, or a marker like Dated Brent).

**Step 2 — Linear Sliding Scale Equation**

$$
P_{gas} = A \cdot P_{oil} + B
$$

where $A$ is the slope (e.g., 0.13) and $B$ is the intercept (often 0 or a flat charge).

**Step 3 — Multiple Slope (S-Curve) Formulation**
For more sophisticated contracts, a piecewise function is used:

$$
P_{gas} = 
\begin{cases}
A_1 \cdot P_{oil} + B_1, & P_{oil} \leq P_1 \\
A_2 \cdot P_{oil} + B_2, & P_1 < P_{oil} \leq P_2 \\
A_3 \cdot P_{oil} + B_3, & P_{oil} > P_2
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 4 — Example Numerical Evaluation**
Let $P_{oil} = \$80/\text{bbl}$, $A_1 = 0.12$, $B_1 = 0.50$, $A_2 = 0.15$, $B_2 = -1.00$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{gas} &= 0.12 \times 80 + 0.50 \\
        &= 9.60 + 0.50 \\
        &= 10.10 \text{ USD/MMBtu}
\end{aligned}
$$

This shows the gas price becomes $\approx 12.6\%$ of crude oil price — typical of mid-2010s European contracts.

### 3.4 Step-by-Step: Supply–Demand Equilibrium in a Gas Market

**Step 1 — Define Linear Supply Function**

$$
Q_S = a + b \cdot P
$$

where $a, b > 0$.

**Step 2 — Define Linear Demand Function**

$$
Q_D = c - d \cdot P
$$

where $c, d > 0$.

**Step 3 — Equilibrium Condition ($Q_S = Q_D$)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
a + b \cdot P^* &= c - d \cdot P^* \\
b \cdot P^* + d \cdot P^* &= c - a \\
P^* \cdot (b + d) &= c - a \\
P^* &= \frac{c - a}{b + d}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute to Find Equilibrium Quantity**

$$
Q^* = a + b \cdot \left(\frac{c - a}{b + d}\right) = \frac{ad + bc}{b + d}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Shortcut:** In KTU 12-mark analytical questions, examiners often provide numerical values for $a, b, c, d$ and ask students to compute $P^*$ and $Q^*$. Always show all four algebraic steps.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Natural Gas Value Chain Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR

```

```mermaid
    start --> netback
    start --> regulated
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**
*Define the term "Gas-on-Gas Competition" (GOGC) and mention two global hubs where it is practiced.*

**Model Answer:**
Gas-on-Gas Competition (GOGC) refers to a market-based pricing mechanism in which the price of natural gas is determined by the real-time interaction of multiple buyers and sellers at a virtual trading hub, rather than being administratively fixed or linked to oil prices. It requires market liberalization, third-party pipeline access, and sufficient liquidity.
**Two global hubs:** (1) **Henry Hub (HH)** in Louisiana, USA, and (2) **Title Transfer Facility (TTF)** in the Netherlands, EU. *(3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**
*List the three segments of the natural gas value chain and state the primary activity of each.*

**Model Answer:**
The three segments are:
1. **Upstream** — Exploration, drilling, and production of raw natural gas from wells.
2. **Midstream** — Processing, transportation through pipelines, liquefaction, shipping, and storage.
3. **Downstream** — Local distribution to end-users (residential, commercial, industrial, and power sectors).
*(3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option A**

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Module 3, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the four major pricing mechanisms used in the global natural gas market, with at least one real-world example of each. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the evolution of the natural gas market structure from a monopolistic model to a liberalized hub-based model, with specific reference to the European Union's Gas Directives. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (a):**

The four primary pricing mechanisms are:

1. **Oil-Indexed Pricing (OIP):** Historically used in long-term contracts (LTCs) in continental Europe and Asia. The gas price is linked to a basket of crude oil prices (e.g., Dated Brent) with a time lag of 3–6 months. *Example: Pre-2020 Russian gas contracts to Germany via Yamal pipeline.*

2. **Gas-on-Gas Competition (GOGC):** Spot and forward trading at virtual hubs where multiple buyers/sellers meet. *Example: Henry Hub (USA), TTF (Netherlands), NBP (UK).* This is the most transparent and market-driven mechanism.

3. **Netback Pricing:** Used for LNG trade — the price is built up from the wellhead + liquefaction + shipping + regasification costs, anchored to a destination market price (e.g., JKM in Asia). *Example: Spot LNG cargoes from USA to Japan.*

4. **Regulated/Administered Pricing:** Government sets prices for strategic or equity reasons. *Example: India's Administered Pricing Mechanism (APM) for domestic gas from ONGC/Reliance fields.*

*(7 marks — 1.5 for each mechanism + 1 for example + 0.5 for relevance)*

**Model Solution (b):**

The natural gas market structure has evolved through **five distinct stages**:

1. **Monopolistic Era (Pre-1990s):** Vertically integrated state utilities (e.g., Ruhrgas in Germany, GdF in France) controlled the entire chain. Prices were set by cost-of-service regulation.

2. **First EU Gas Directive (1998):** Mandated **legal unbundling** of supply, transport, and distribution activities. Required non-discriminatory third-party access (TPA) to pipelines.

3. **Second Gas Directive (2003):** Strengthened TPA rules; required accounting separation and creation of regulatory authorities in each member state.

4. **Third Gas Directive (2009):** Mandated **ownership unbundling** — pipeline operators could not simultaneously own production/supply businesses. This was a landmark shift toward full liberalization.

5. **Hub-Based Trading (2010s onwards):** Emergence of TTF, NBP, and PSV hubs in Europe with GOGC. By 2020, more than 70% of EU gas was traded on hubs.

The transition was driven by the **1996 Energy Charter Treaty**, market liberalization pressures, and the goal of EU energy security and competition. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option B (Internal Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Module 3, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the LNG value chain from wellhead to burner tip. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A European utility has a long-term LNG import contract from Qatar with the following parameters: wellhead cost = $1.50/MMBtu, liquefaction cost = $2.20/MMBtu, shipping cost = $1.00/MMBtu, regasification cost = $0.40/MMBtu, required margin = 12%, and destination tax = 5%. Calculate the minimum viable price at the European hub. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (a):**

The LNG value chain consists of the following sequential blocks:

1. **Upstream Production:** Gas is extracted from onshore/offshore wells.
2. **Gas Processing:** Removal of impurities (H₂S, CO₂) and extraction of NGLs (ethane, propane).
3. **Pipeline Transport to Liquefaction Plant:** Gas transported via dedicated pipelines to coastal LNG terminal.
4. **Liquefaction:** Gas cooled to **−162 °C**, reducing volume by **~600 times** to form LNG.
5. **LNG Storage and Loading:** Stored in insulated tanks; loaded onto LNG carriers.
6. **LNG Shipping:** Cryogenic tankers transport LNG across oceans (~15,000 km typical).
7. **Regasification:** At import terminal, LNG is warmed back to gaseous form.
8. **Distribution to End Users:** Via local distribution networks to power plants, industries, and households.

**Block Diagram (Textual Representation):**
```
[Wellhead] → [Processing] → [Pipeline] → [Liquefaction] → [LNG Storage] 
→ [Tanker] → [Regasification] → [Pipeline Grid] → [End User]
```
*(7 marks — 6 stages × 1 mark + 1 mark for diagram)*

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given:**
- Wellhead cost ($C_W$) = $1.50/MMBtu
- Liquefaction cost ($C_L$) = $2.20/MMBtu
- Shipping cost ($C_S$) = $1.00/MMBtu
- Regasification cost ($C_R$) = $0.40/MMBtu
- Margin ($\mu$) = 12% = 0.12
- Tax ($\tau$) = 5% = 0.05

**Step 1 — Sum of Costs:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{total} &= C_W + C_L + C_S + C_R \\
          &= 1.50 + 2.20 + 1.00 + 0.40 \\
          &= 5.10 \text{ USD/MMBtu}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Margin and Tax:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{min} &= \frac{C_{total} \cdot (1 + \mu)}{1 - \tau} \\
        &= \frac{5.10 \times (1 + 0.12)}{1 - 0.05} \\
        &= \frac{5.10 \times 1.12}{0.95} \\
        &= \frac{5.712}{0.95} \\
        &= 6.0126 \text{ USD/MMBtu}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** The minimum viable price at the European hub = **$6.01/MMBtu** (approximately).

**Interpretation:** If the TTF hub price is currently trading at $9.00/MMBtu, the importer earns a profit margin of approximately ($9.00 − $6.01) = $2.99/MMBtu per cargo.
*(7 marks — 3 for setup, 2 for calculation, 2 for interpretation)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Unit Inconsistency Pitfall:** Many students mix **MMBtu** with **MTPA** (million tonnes per annum) or **Bcm**. Always state units clearly in every line.
> - **Sign Error in Netback:** A very common mistake is to **add** shipping cost to market price instead of **subtracting** it. Remember: Netback = Market price − All mid-chain costs.
> - **Tax Placement:** Tax $\tau$ is applied as a **divisor** $(1-\tau)$, not added. Adding it directly is a typical 2-mark deduction.
> - **Missing Margin:** Forgetting to apply the $\mu$ margin is a 1-mark loss. Examiners expect students to explicitly state the margin assumption.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- The **natural gas market** is a **physically constrained, regionally fragmented** market that is gradually globalizing through LNG trade.
- The **value chain** has three segments: **Upstream → Midstream → Downstream**, with midstream being the critical bottleneck.
- Four **pricing mechanisms**: **OIP, GOGC, Netback, Regulated** — each dominates in different regions and time periods.
- **Key global hubs:** **Henry Hub (HH)**, **TTF**, **JKM**, **NBP** — these are the price reference points for global trade.
- **Netback price formula:** $P_{NB} = P_M - C_L - C_S - C_R$ (always subtract mid-chain costs from market price).
- **Supply-demand equilibrium:** $P^* = \frac{c-a}{b+d}$ for linear functions — useful for demand forecasting problems.
- **LNG** is natural gas cooled to **−162 °C**, reducing volume by ~600×, enabling intercontinental trade.
- **Calorific value** of natural gas ≈ **38 MJ/Nm³**; **1 MMBtu** = **1.055 GJ**.
- **Market evolution**: Monopoly → Unbundling → TPA → Hub Trading → Global LNG Market.
- **Arbitrage metric:** The **JKM–HH spread** is the primary signal for LNG cargo diversion between Atlantic and Pacific basins.
- For **KTU 14-mark questions**, always draw a **labeled block diagram** for value chain/process questions — it earns 1–2 marks.
- Numerical problems require **explicit unit statements** in every line and a **final interpretation** in 1–2 sentences.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transportation Market
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET526 - ENERGY ECONOMICS AND POLICY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Transportation Market

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of the Transportation Market

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Transportation Market** is the economic system in which energy carriers (predominantly petroleum-based liquid fuels such as gasoline, diesel, and jet fuel, alongside natural gas, electricity, biofuels, and hydrogen) are demanded by mobility end-users (passenger and freight) and supplied by a vertically integrated chain of crude oil producers, refiners, distributors, and retail fuel outlets. From an energy economics perspective, it is a **derived demand market** — transportation energy is demanded not for its own sake but as an *intermediate input* into the production of mobility services (passenger-km and tonne-km).

In the KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, the transportation market is studied as a **structurally imperfect, policy-laden, and strategically critical sub-sector of the global energy economy**, characterized by:

- **High oil dependence** (~**96%** of transport energy globally still derives from oil, per IEA benchmarks).
- **Low short-run price elasticity** of demand (typically between **-0.1 and -0.3**).
- **Significant negative externalities**: local air pollution, greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions, congestion, and accident costs.
- **Strong policy intervention**: fuel taxes, vehicle efficiency standards, low-emission zones, and EV subsidies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The transportation market is the single largest end-use sector of global oil consumption (≈ **65%** of total oil demand). Any analysis of energy security, climate policy, or peak oil must begin here.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Lifeblood Pipeline"

Think of the transportation market as the **circulatory system of a modern economy**:

- **Crude oil** is the *raw oxygen* sucked in by producers (OPEC, US shale, Russia, etc.).
- **Refineries** are the *lungs* that convert crude into usable "blood" — gasoline, diesel, jet-A.
- **Pipelines, tankers, and trucks** are the *arteries and veins* carrying fuel to retail outlets.
- **Vehicles (cars, trucks, planes, ships)** are the *organs* that consume this fuel to deliver mobility.
- **Fuel taxes and emission norms** act as the *immune system* — selectively regulating flow to limit pollutants (GHG, NOx, particulates).

Just as a clot in an artery cripples the body, a supply shock (e.g., 1973 OPEC embargo, 2022 Russia-Ukraine war) can paralyze an entire economy. Policymakers therefore treat the transportation market as **strategic infrastructure** rather than a free-market commodity.

## 1.3 Key Metrics & Physical Constants

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics Used in Transportation Energy Economics**

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Energy intensity of road transport | $\eta$ | ~**2.5 MJ/passenger-km** (passenger car) |
| Well-to-Wheel efficiency (ICE) | $\eta_{wtw}$ | ~**20–25%** |
| Well-to-Wheel efficiency (BEV) | $\eta_{wtw}$ | ~**70–80%** |
| Global transport oil share | $s_{oil}$ | ~**96%** |
| Long-run price elasticity of gasoline demand | $\varepsilon^{LR}_p$ | ~**-0.6 to -0.8** |
| Short-run price elasticity of gasoline demand | $\varepsilon^{SR}_p$ | ~**-0.1 to -0.3** |
| Income elasticity of vehicle ownership | $\varepsilon^{LR}_y$ | ~**1.0 to 1.5** |
| Social cost of carbon (transport sector) | $SCC$ | ~**$50–$200 / tCO₂** |

These constants will reappear in Section 2's formula sheet and Section 3's derivations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Structural Anatomy of the Transportation Market

The transportation market is best understood as a **two-sided derived-demand system** with feedback loops between macro drivers, vehicle stock, fuel choice, and policy.

### 2.1.1 Demand Side

Transportation energy demand $D_T$ is a function of four core drivers:

$$D_T = f(Y, P_f, P_v, T, N, \varepsilon)$$

Where:
- $Y$ = real per-capita income (GDP)
- $P_f$ = real fuel price (ex-tax and including tax)
- $P_v$ = real vehicle purchase price
- $T$ = urban density and public-transport quality
- $N$ = population and household size
- $\varepsilon$ = vector of elasticities (price, income, cross)

**Why these matter:** Income growth raises vehicle ownership (income elasticity > 1 historically). Fuel prices affect vehicle-km traveled. Urban form shifts demand between modes (car vs. rail).

### 2.1.2 Supply Side

The supply chain is **vertical and oligopolistic**:

1. **Upstream**: Crude oil production (OPEC+ vs. non-OPEC, shale revolution).
2. **Midstream**: Refining (cracking crude into diesel, gasoline, jet fuel at yield ratios of ~**40% diesel / 30% gasoline / 10% jet / 20% others**).
3. **Downstream**: Wholesale distribution and retail (often branded oligopolies — Shell, BP, IOC, BPCL).

> [!NOTE]
> **Refining Yield Reality:** A barrel of crude (~**159 L**) yields only ~**70 L of gasoline-equivalent** after refining losses. This is why refining margins ("crack spreads") are a *second-order* price driver on top of crude.

### 2.1.3 Policy & Externalities Layer

The market is overlaid with corrective instruments:

- **Pigouvian taxes** on fuel (carbon tax + excise).
- **CAFE / fuel economy standards** on vehicles.
- **Subsidies** for EVs, biofuels, and public transit.
- **Regulation**: low-emission zones, BS-VI / Euro-VI norms, IMO shipping fuel caps.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they form 80% of Part B derivations.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Price Elasticity of Fuel Demand | $\varepsilon_p = \dfrac{\%\Delta Q_f}{\%\Delta P_f} = \dfrac{dQ_f}{dP_f}\cdot\dfrac{P_f}{Q_f}$ | $SR \in [-0.3, -0.1]$, $LR \in [-0.8, -0.6]$ |
| 2 | Income Elasticity | $\varepsilon_y = \dfrac{dQ_f}{dY}\cdot\dfrac{Y}{Q_f}$ | Vehicle ownership: 1.0–1.5 |
| 3 | Total Revenue Test | Sign of $(P + Q/\varepsilon_p)$ | Determines max-revenue price |
| 4 | Log-Linear Demand | $\ln Q_f = \alpha + \beta_1 \ln P_f + \beta_2 \ln Y + u$ | $\beta_1 = \varepsilon_p$, $\beta_2 = \varepsilon_y$ |
| 5 | Energy Demand Identity (Kaya) | $CO_2 = P \times \dfrac{GDP}{P} \times \dfrac{E}{GDP} \times \dfrac{CO_2}{E}$ | $P$=population, $E$=energy |
| 6 | Rebound Effect | $\eta_R = \dfrac{\%\Delta E_{actual}}{\%\Delta E_{engineered}}$ | Typically **10–30%** for road transport |
| 7 | Pigouvian Tax (optimal) | $t^* = MEC \text{ at } Q^*$ | $MEC$ = marginal external cost |
| 8 | Deadweight Loss (no tax) | $DWL = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot t \cdot \Delta Q$ | Triangle area of welfare loss |
| 9 | Consumer Surplus | $CS = \int_{P_0}^{P_1} D(P)\,dP$ | Welfare gain to buyers |
| 10 | Fuel Tax Incidence (pass-through) | $\rho = \dfrac{\varepsilon_s}{\varepsilon_s - \varepsilon_d}$ | $\rho$ = share borne by consumers |
| 11 | Vehicle-km Demand (gravity) | $VKT_i = k \cdot \dfrac{Y_i \cdot N_j}{d_{ij}^\gamma}$ | $\gamma \approx 1.5$–$2.0$ |
| 12 | Well-to-Wheel CO₂ (ICE) | $CO_2^{wtw} = \dfrac{E_f}{LHV_f} \cdot C_c \cdot \dfrac{1}{\eta_{wtw}}$ | $E_f$=fuel energy, $C_c$=carbon content |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering & Policy Utility

- **Oil-importing nations** (India, Japan, EU) use transportation demand models to project import bills and design strategic petroleum reserves.
- **Refiners** use **crack-spread forecasting** to decide refinery configuration (diesel-heavy vs. gasoline-heavy).
- **City planners** use **elasticity values** to forecast transit ridership under congestion pricing.
- **Climate policy** (e.g., India's Panchamrit, EU Fit-for-55) directly leverages transport-sector price elasticities to set carbon-price trajectories.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Insight:** Singapore's Electronic Road Pricing (ERP) and London's Congestion Charge are textbook Pigouvian tax applications — they internalize congestion externalities and have reduced traffic by **15–20%** while raising revenues for transit.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Long-Run Fuel Demand Function from a Cobb-Douglas Utility Framework

We derive a tractable transport-fuel demand function that KTU examiners frequently use as a **14-mark derivation**.

### Step 1: Specify the representative consumer's utility

The household gains utility from three goods: (i) **vehicle-mobility services** $M$ (passenger-km), (ii) **other consumption** $C$, and (iii) **leisure** $L$ (held fixed in the short run). Utility is Cobb-Douglas:

$$
U(C, M) = C^{\alpha} \cdot M^{\,1-\alpha}, \quad 0 < \alpha < 1
$$

### Step 2: Specify the budget constraint

The household spends income $Y$ on other goods at price $P_C = 1$ (numeraire) and on fuel at price $P_f$ per litre. Mobility services $M$ are produced by combining fuel with a vehicle stock at constant returns:

$$
M = A \cdot F^{\beta} \cdot V^{1-\beta}
$$

where $F$ is fuel litres, $V$ is vehicle services (vehicle-km), and $A$ is a productivity parameter. The full budget is:

$$
Y = P_C \cdot C + P_f \cdot F + P_v \cdot V
$$

### Step 3: Substitute the production function

Substitute $F = \left(\dfrac{M}{A \cdot V^{1-\beta}}\right)^{1/\beta}$ into the budget:

$$
Y = C + P_f \cdot \left(\dfrac{M}{A \cdot V^{1-\beta}}\right)^{\!1/\beta} + P_v \cdot V
$$

### Step 4: Maximize utility with respect to $C$ and $M$

The consumer maximizes $U$ subject to the budget. The First Order Condition (FOC) for $M$ is:

$$
\dfrac{\partial U}{\partial M} = \lambda \cdot P_f \cdot \dfrac{1}{\beta} \cdot \left(\dfrac{M}{A \cdot V^{1-\beta}}\right)^{\!(1-\beta)/\beta} \cdot \dfrac{1}{A \cdot V^{1-\beta}}
$$

and the FOC for $C$ is:

$$
\dfrac{\partial U}{\partial C} = \lambda
$$

Dividing the two FOCs yields the **marginal rate of substitution condition**:

$$
\dfrac{(1-\alpha)\,C}{\alpha\,M} = \dfrac{P_f}{\beta} \cdot \left(\dfrac{M}{A \cdot V^{1-\beta}}\right)^{\!(1-\beta)/\beta} \cdot \dfrac{1}{A \cdot V^{1-\beta}}
$$

### Step 5: Solve for the long-run demand for fuel

Using $C = \alpha Y$ (standard Cobb-Douglas result) and rearranging, we get the **fuel demand function**:

$$
F^* = K \cdot Y^{\beta} \cdot P_f^{-\beta} \cdot P_v^{-\beta(1-\beta)} \cdot A^{\beta}
$$

where $K$ is a constant. Taking natural logs gives the **estimable form**:

$$
\ln F^* = \ln K + \beta \ln Y - \beta \ln P_f - \beta(1-\beta) \ln P_v
$$

**Interpretation:** The coefficient on $\ln P_f$ is the **price elasticity of fuel demand**, equal to $-\beta$ (typically $-0.3$ to $-0.7$ in the long run). The coefficient on $\ln Y$ is the **income elasticity**, equal to $\beta$ (typically $0.3$ to $0.7$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note:** This derivation is the theoretical backbone of empirical studies (e.g., Gately & Kahn 2010 on oil demand). In the exam, you are usually asked to derive up to the log-linear form and *interpret* the elasticities.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Optimal Pigouvian Carbon Tax on Transportation Fuel

We derive the welfare-maximizing carbon tax that internalizes the GHG externality from gasoline.

### Step 1: Define marginal external cost

The marginal external cost (MEC) of an additional litre of fuel equals the CO₂ emitted times the social cost of carbon:

$$
MEC = C_c \cdot \rho_f \cdot SCC
$$

where $C_c$ is the carbon content of gasoline (~**2.31 kg CO₂/litre** for petrol), $\rho_f$ is fuel density, and $SCC$ is the social cost of carbon.

### Step 2: Set MEC equal to marginal damage

The efficient level of fuel consumption $Q^*$ is where **marginal social cost (MSC) = marginal social benefit (MSB)**:

$$
MSC = MC_{private} + MEC = MSB
$$

### Step 3: Solve for the optimal tax

The optimal Pigouvian tax $t^*$ equals MEC at $Q^*$:

$$
t^* = MEC(Q^*) = C_c \cdot \rho_f \cdot SCC
$$

### Step 4: Compute deadweight loss without the tax

If the unregulated equilibrium quantity is $Q^u > Q^*$, the **deadweight loss (DWL)** triangle is:

$$
DWL = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot t^* \cdot (Q^u - Q^*)
$$

### Step 5: Numerical example (India, gasoline)

Take:
- $C_c = 2.31 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{L}$
- $SCC = \$70 / \text{tCO}_2 = \$0.07 / \text{kgCO}_2$

$$
t^* = 2.31 \times 0.07 = \$0.162 / \text{L} \approx \text{₹}13.5 / \text{L} \text{ (at } \$1 = ₹83\text{)}
$$

If $Q^u - Q^* = 10\% \text{ of consumption}$ and total fuel = **30 MT** annually:

$$
DWL = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 13.5 \times 3 = ₹20.25 \text{ crore per million litres}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often write $DWL$ as a rectangle. It is a *triangle* — area of the welfare loss between MSC and MSB from $Q^*$ to $Q^u$.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Policy Instruments

| Instrument | Mechanism | Elasticity Targeted | Welfare Efficiency | Implementation Difficulty |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Fuel excise tax | Price signal | $\varepsilon_p$ | High (first-best) | Low (already in use) |
| Carbon tax | External cost pricing | $\varepsilon_p$ | Highest (Pigouvian) | Medium |
| CAFE / fuel-economy standard | Mandate on $VKT/\eta$ | Technology choice | Medium (second-best) | Medium |
| EV subsidy | Shifts $P_v$ | Cross-elasticity | Medium (distortionary) | High (fiscal cost) |
| Low-emission zones | Restricts access | Behavioral | Low (command-control) | High (enforcement) |
| Congestion pricing | Internalizes congestion | Time-of-day elasticity | High (Pigouvian) | High (technology) |

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example: Price Elasticity of Fuel Demand

**Question:** Suppose fuel demand is $Q_d = 100 - 5P$ (in million litres), and supply is $Q_s = 20 + 5P$. Compute equilibrium, tax incidence, and deadweight loss from a ₹10/litre tax.

### Step 1: Equilibrium without tax

Set $Q_d = Q_s$:

$$
100 - 5P = 20 + 5P \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 80 = 10P \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; P^* = 8, \quad Q^* = 60
$$

### Step 2: Apply a ₹10/litre tax on consumers

New demand: $Q_d^t = 100 - 5(P - 10) = 150 - 5P$. New equilibrium:

$$
150 - 5P = 20 + 5P \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; P_c = 13, \quad P_s = 3, \quad Q^t = 50
$$

### Step 3: Tax incidence

Consumers bear $\Delta P_c = 13 - 8 = ₹5$; producers bear $\Delta P_s = 8 - 3 = ₹5$. Pass-through = 50% (symmetric slopes).

### Step 4: Deadweight loss

$$
DWL = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 10 \times (60 - 50) = ₹50 \text{ million}
$$

### Step 5: Verify using elasticity formula

At equilibrium, $\varepsilon_d = -5 \cdot \dfrac{8}{60} = -0.67$. Tax share on consumer:

$$
\rho = \dfrac{\varepsilon_s}{\varepsilon_s - \varepsilon_d} = \dfrac{0.67}{0.67 - (-0.67)} = 0.5 \;\;\checkmark
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Pattern:** This exact 5-step structure (equilibrium → tax shock → incidence → DWL → verification) recurs in nearly every KTU energy-economics past paper.
