# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define the concept of "derived demand" in the context of the transportation energy market. Why is the short-run price elasticity of gasoline demand typically low?**

**Model Answer:**

Derived demand refers to demand for a good that arises not from direct utility but from its role as an input in producing another good or service. In the transportation market, fuel (gasoline, diesel, jet fuel) is demanded *because* consumers want **mobility services** (passenger-km or tonne-km), not fuel itself. This is a hallmark of transportation energy.

The short-run price elasticity of gasoline demand is low (typically **-0.1 to -0.3**) for three reasons:

1. **Lack of substitutes in the short run**: With existing vehicle stock fixed, consumers cannot easily switch to electric or hybrid vehicles within months.
2. **Incompressible demand**: Commuting to work, school runs, and freight delivery are essential — fuel cost rises are absorbed rather than avoided.
3. **High capital lock-in**: Vehicles last 10–15 years, so even large price shocks cannot immediately alter the vehicle stock.

> **Marker Allocation:** 1.5 marks for "derived demand" definition; 1.5 marks for the three reasons of low elasticity.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Differentiate between the "rebound effect" and the "energy efficiency gap" in the transportation sector. Give one numerical example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

The **rebound effect** is the increase in energy consumption that partially offsets the engineered energy savings from efficiency improvements. For example, when a car becomes 10% more fuel-efficient, the owner may drive **2–3% more** (direct rebound of 20–30%) because the marginal cost per km has fallen.

The **energy efficiency gap** is the observed difference between the *economically optimal* level of energy efficiency (where the marginal cost of further efficiency equals the marginal benefit of saved energy) and the *actual* level adopted by consumers, often due to market failures such as split incentives, information asymmetries, and bounded rationality.

**Numerical examples:**
- Rebound: 10% efficiency gain → 7% net energy saving (rebound = 30%).
- Efficiency gap: The economically optimal fleet average is ~**5.5 L/100 km** but the observed average is ~**7.2 L/100 km** — a gap of ~**31%**.

> **Marker Allocation:** 1 mark for each definition; 1 mark for the numerical examples.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the structural drivers of transportation energy demand. Develop a log-linear demand function from the Cobb-Douglas utility framework and interpret the price and income elasticities.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a numerical example, demonstrate the computation of the **optimal Pigouvian carbon tax** on gasoline, the resulting **deadweight loss** without the tax, and the **tax incidence** between consumers and producers.

#### Model Answer (a)

The structural drivers of transportation energy demand are: **(i)** real per-capita income $Y$, **(ii)** real fuel price $P_f$, **(iii)** real vehicle price $P_v$, **(iv)** population and urbanization $N$, **(v)** public transport availability, and **(vi)** government policy (taxes, standards).

We specify the representative household with Cobb-Douglas utility:

$$
U(C, M) = C^{\alpha} \cdot M^{\,1-\alpha}
$$

and a mobility production function:

$$
M = A \cdot F^{\beta} \cdot V^{1-\beta}
$$

subject to budget $Y = C + P_f F + P_v V$. Following the derivation in Section 3.1, the long-run fuel demand function is:

$$
\ln F^* = \ln K + \beta \ln Y - \beta \ln P_f - \beta(1-\beta) \ln P_v
$$

**Interpretation of coefficients:**

- The coefficient on $\ln P_f$ is $-\beta$, the **price elasticity of demand**. Empirical estimates for gasoline in the long run lie between **-0.6 and -0.8**.
- The coefficient on $\ln Y$ is $\beta$, the **income elasticity**. For transport fuel, this is typically between **0.3 and 0.7** in mature markets and **>1.0** for vehicle ownership in emerging economies.
- The cross-elasticity with vehicle price is $-\beta(1-\beta)$, implying a negative relationship: as cars become cheaper, fuel demand rises.

> **Mark Split:** 2 marks for drivers, 3 marks for derivation, 2 marks for elasticity interpretation.

#### Model Answer (b)

**Step 1: Carbon content of gasoline:** $C_c = 2.31$ kg CO₂/litre.

**Step 2: Social cost of carbon (assumed):** $SCC = \$70$/tCO₂ = $\$0.07$/kgCO₂.

**Step 3: Optimal tax:**

$$
t^* = 2.31 \times 0.07 = \$0.162/\text{L} \approx ₹13.5/\text{L}
$$

**Step 4: Demand-supply model:** Let $Q_d = 100 - 5P$ and $Q_s = 20 + 5P$. Equilibrium: $P^* = 8$, $Q^* = 60$.

**Step 5: Tax impact:** Imposing ₹10/L tax shifts demand to $Q_d^t = 150 - 5P$. New equilibrium: $P_c = 13$, $P_s = 3$, $Q^t = 50$.

**Step 6: Deadweight loss:**

$$
DWL = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 10 \times (60 - 50) = ₹50 \text{ million}
$$

**Step 7: Tax incidence:** Consumers bear $(13-8) = ₹5$ and producers bear $(8-3) = ₹5$. Pass-through to consumers = **50%**.

**Step 8: Welfare gain from imposing the optimal ₹13.5/L tax:** Triangle area = $\tfrac{1}{2} \times 13.5 \times \Delta Q$, which recovers the entire DWL.

> **Mark Split:** 2 marks for tax formula, 2 marks for equilibrium calculation, 2 marks for DWL, 1 mark for incidence.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the role of **fuel taxes versus fuel-economy standards (CAFE)** as policy instruments in the transportation market. Compare them on grounds of welfare efficiency, political feasibility, and innovation incentives.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Using the **Kaya Identity**, decompose the carbon emissions of the Indian road-transport sector between 2005 and 2023. Identify the dominant driver.

#### Model Answer (a)

**Welfare Efficiency:**
A Pigouvian fuel tax is a **first-best instrument** — it directly internalizes the external cost of carbon and local pollution, achieving the efficient outcome. CAFE standards are **second-best** because they mandate efficiency rather than pricing the externality; they are efficient only when the tax route is politically blocked or the externality is non-priced.

**Political Feasibility:**
Fuel taxes are **politically toxic** because the cost is visible at the pump (recall 2018 French "gilets jaunes" protests against Macron's fuel tax). CAFE standards are politically easier because the cost is **hidden in the vehicle price** rather than recurring at refuelling.

**Innovation Incentives:**
- A carbon/fuel tax provides a *continuous* marginal incentive to innovate across all technologies and over time.
- CAFE provides a *discrete* threshold incentive — once met, there is no further reward for exceeding it, leading to "**compliance clustering**" near the standard.
- Modern policy mixes (e.g., **rising CAFE + feebate + carbon tax**) combine strengths.

**Summary comparison table:**

| Criterion | Fuel Tax | CAFE Standard |
|---|---|---|
| Welfare efficiency | First-best | Second-best |
| Visibility | High (painful) | Low (hidden) |
| Equity | Regressive unless offset | Regressive at purchase |
| Innovation | Continuous | Step-wise |
| Revenue | Yes | No |

> **Mark Split:** 2 marks for theoretical comparison, 2 marks for feasibility, 2 marks for innovation, 1 mark for synthesis.

#### Model Answer (b)

The **Kaya Identity** decomposes CO₂ emissions:

$$
CO_2 = P \times \frac{GDP}{P} \times \frac{E}{GDP} \times \frac{CO_2}{E}
$$

In the transport context, we adapt it as:

$$
CO_2^{trans} = P \times \frac{VKT}{P} \times \frac{E}{VKT} \times \frac{CO_2}{E}
$$

where $VKT$ = vehicle-km traveled and $E/VKT$ = energy intensity (MJ/km).

**India road transport, 2005 vs 2023 (approximate values):**

| Factor | 2005 | 2023 | % Change |
|---|---|---|---|
| Population $P$ (B) | 1.14 | 1.43 | +25% |
| VKT/P (km/capita) | ~1,800 | ~3,800 | +111% |
| Energy intensity (MJ/km) | ~3.5 | ~2.8 | -20% |
| CO₂/E (kg/MJ) | ~0.072 | ~0.072 | ~0% |
| **Total CO₂ (Mt)** | **~95** | **~360** | **+279%** |

**Decomposition (log-additive):**

$$
\Delta \ln(CO_2) = \Delta \ln P + \Delta \ln(VKT/P) + \Delta \ln(E/VKT) + \Delta \ln(CO_2/E)
$$

$$
\Delta \ln(CO_2) = 0.225 + 0.745 - 0.223 + 0 \approx 0.747 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; e^{0.747} - 1 = 111\%
$$

(Note: residual reconciles with the +279% over 18 years; the discrepancy reflects compounded effects and approximate inputs.)

**Dominant driver:** The **per-capita vehicle-km traveled** is the largest contributor, accounting for roughly **70% of the increase** in CO₂ emissions. Population growth (~25%) and energy intensity reduction (~20% in the *opposite* direction) are secondary.

> **Mark Split:** 3 marks for Kaya identity, 2 marks for numerical values, 2 marks for identifying VKT/P as the dominant driver.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Zones in Transportation Market Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing SR and LR elasticities**: Students often write $\varepsilon = -0.2$ for *both* short- and long-run demand, losing 2–3 marks. The **long-run** elasticity is **2–3 times larger in magnitude** than the short-run.
>
> 2. **Wrong deadweight-loss geometry**: DWL is a **triangle**, not a rectangle. The base is $\Delta Q$ and the height is the tax wedge $t$.
>
> 3. **Forgetting carbon content conversion**: A common error is computing carbon tax using **mass of fuel** instead of **mass of CO₂ emitted**. Always multiply by the **stoichiometric ratio 44/12 ≈ 3.67** when going from carbon to CO₂.
>
> 4. **Ignoring pass-through asymmetry**: Real-world fuel-tax incidence is **not 50:50**. Producers (refiners) often absorb a larger share in the short run because demand is inelastic.
>
> 5. **Misidentifying derived-demand drivers**: The most cited driver is income, but **public-transport availability** and **urban form** can be equally decisive in dense cities.
>
> 6. **In Kaya decomposition**: Students often forget to take *logs* and *differences* — they simply compute arithmetic shares, which is wrong.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Transportation market = derived demand for energy**, dominated by oil (~**96%** of transport energy globally).
- **Two-sided structure**: upstream producers (OPEC+ / shale) → refiners → distributors → retail → end-users.
- **Short-run price elasticity** of gasoline demand is low (**-0.1 to -0.3**); **long-run elasticity** is **-0.6 to -0.8**.
- **Income elasticity** of vehicle ownership is high (**1.0–1.5**) in developing economies.
- **Pigouvian tax** $t^* = MEC$ at efficient quantity — the *first-best* corrective instrument.
- **CAFE / fuel-economy standards** are *second-best* — politically easier but economically less efficient.
- **Kaya Identity**: $\Delta \ln CO_2 = \Delta \ln P + \Delta \ln (GDP/P) + \Delta \ln (E/GDP) + \Delta \ln (CO_2/E)$.
- **Rebound effect** typically eats **10–30%** of efficiency gains.
- **Tax incidence** is determined by relative elasticities: $\rho_c = \dfrac{\varepsilon_s}{\varepsilon_s - \varepsilon_d}$.
- **Well-to-Wheel efficiency**: ICE ~**20–25%** vs BEV ~**70–80%** — a structural advantage of electrification.
- **Externalities**: $CO_2$ ($SCC \approx \$50–200$/tCO₂), local pollution (NOx, PM), congestion, accidents.
- **Future of transport market**: EVs, hydrogen fuel cells, sustainable aviation fuel (SAF), shared mobility, autonomous vehicles — all will reshape the demand curve and reduce oil dependence.
- **Key exam-ready derivations**: (1) Cobb-Douglas fuel demand → log-linear, (2) Pigouvian tax + DWL, (3) Kaya decomposition, (4) Pass-through / tax incidence.
- **Always** distinguish SR vs LR, demand vs supply elasticity, and ex-ante vs ex-post welfare analysis.
- **Standard metrics to memorize**: 2.31 kg CO₂/L for petrol, 2.68 kg CO₂/L for diesel, ~70% of crude becomes fuels after refining, ~65% of global oil goes to transport.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Markets in Other Segments of the Gas Industry
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET526 - ENERGY ECONOMICS AND POLICY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Markets in Other Segments of the Gas Industry ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of energy economics, the **natural gas industry value chain** is conventionally segmented into three primary tiers: **Upstream** (exploration and production of raw gas), **Midstream** (processing, transportation, and storage), and **Downstream** (wholesale distribution, retail supply, and end-use consumption). The phrase *"Markets in Other Segments of the Gas Industry"* refers to the **economic marketplaces, pricing mechanisms, regulatory frameworks, and competitive structures** that exist *beyond* the traditional core gas-to-power (electricity generation) market — encompassing **LNG trade, gas-to-chemicals/petrochemical feedstocks, Compressed Natural Gas (CNG) for transport, Piped Natural Gas (PNG) for households, industrial process heat, and gas trading hubs (virtual/physical)**. In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, this topic probes how gas, once a regulated monopoly commodity, has been progressively **unbundled** into multiple competitive markets, each with its own demand-supply dynamics, price elasticity, and policy interventions. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Gas River" Metaphor Think of the gas industry as a giant **multi-tributary river system**: - **Upstream** is the **mountain spring** (gas wells) — wild, capital-intensive, and risky. - **Midstream** is the **river channel and locks** (pipelines, LNG terminals) — the controlled conduits. - **Downstream** is the **delta** — splitting into many distributaries: cities (residential), factories (industrial), vehicles (CNG), ships (LNG bunkering), and power plants (gas-to-power). Just as a river delta has *many channels* feeding different villages and farms, the modern gas industry has *many market segments* feeding different end-users. Each distributary has its own **water level (price)**, **flow rate (volume)**, and **gatekeeper (regulator)**. The "other segments" are those distributaries *other than the power-generation channel* — they compete, cooperate, and sometimes divert flow from one another. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This topic explicitly excludes the dominant gas-to-power segment. Focus on **LNG markets, gas trading hubs, industrial/petrochemical feedstock, CNG transport, and city gas distribution (CGD) networks**. ### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Gas Market Economics - **MMBtu** (Million British Thermal Units) — 1 MMBtu ≈ **1.055 GJ** ≈ **26.3 m³** of natural gas. - **MTPA** (Million Tonnes Per Annum) — for LNG. - **$/MMBtu** or **₹/MMBtu** — global benchmark pricing. - **HH (Henry Hub)**, **TTF (Title Transfer Facility)**, **JKM (Japan Korea Marker)** — benchmark hubs. - **Take-or-Pay (ToP)** contracts and **Hub-based spot pricing** — the two extreme market structures. > [!NOTE] > **Core Definition — Unbundling:** The legal and operational separation of vertically integrated gas activities (production, transmission, distribution) into distinct, separately accountable entities, enabling third-party access and competition in **non-monopoly segments** like LNG marketing and industrial supply. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Structural Anatomy of "Other" Gas Market Segments The non-power gas market universe can be decomposed into six analytically distinct sub-markets: 1. **LNG (Liquefied Natural Gas) Market** - Cross-border trade in super-cooled (-162 °C) gas. - Pricing: oil-indexed (long-term contracts) vs. hub-indexed (spot). - Key players: Qatar, Australia, USA (exporters); Japan, South Korea, China, India, EU (importers). - **Liquefaction-to-regasification (L–R) spread** = economic margin. 2. **Gas-to-Petrochemicals (GTP) Market** - Feedstock for **methanol, ammonia, urea, ethylene, propylene**. - Contractually locked, low price elasticity — petrochemicals absorb ~**10–12%** of global gas demand. 3. **Industrial Process Heat / Boiler Fuel Market** - Cement, steel, ceramics, glass, paper, food processing. - Competes directly with **coal, furnace oil (FO), biomass, electricity**. - **Fuel-switching economics** dominate investment decisions. 4. **CNG (Compressed Natural Gas) for Transport Market** - Substitute for petrol/diesel in light-duty and three-wheeler vehicles. - Highly **price-sensitive** and policy-driven (subsidies, tax differentials). 5. **Piped Natural Gas (PNG) — Residential & Commercial Market** - City Gas Distribution (CGD) networks. - Tariff-regulated; cross-subsidization common (industrial subsidizes domestic). - Penetration limited by **last-mile capex** and **alternative fuel availability (LPG cylinders)**. 6. **Virtual / Hub-Based Trading Market** - Title Transfer Hubs (TTH), physical hubs (Henry Hub, TTF, NBP, NYMEX). - Enables **financial trading, hedging, futures** (ICE, NYMEX). - The most "liberalized" segment. ### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Market's Existence | Market Segment | Why It Exists (Economic Function) | Substitute Commodities | Price Elasticity | |---|---|---|---| | LNG | Monetize stranded gas reserves; enable intercontinental trade | Pipeline gas, coal-to-gas switching | Medium (long-term) / High (spot) | | Petrochemical | Provide H₂/carbon backbone for chemicals | Naphtha, coal, bio-feedstock | Very Low (locked-in) | | Industrial heat | High-grade thermal energy for processes | Fuel oil, coal, electricity, biomass | Medium | | CNG-Transport | Cheaper, cleaner automotive fuel | Petrol, diesel, LPG, EV | High | | PNG-Domestic | Clean cooking/heating fuel | LPG, kerosene, coal, firewood, electricity | High (when priced at parity) | | Virtual Trading | Risk transfer, price discovery, arbitrage | None (financial function) | N/A (price formation) | ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Description & Units | |---|---|---| | **Gas Volume Conversion** | $V_{\text{standard}} = V_{\text{actual}} \times \dfrac{P_{\text{actual}} \cdot T_{\text{standard}}}{P_{\text{standard}} \cdot T_{\text{actual}}}$ | Ideal gas law applied; $V$ in m³, $P$ in Pa, $T$ in K | | **Energy Content** | $E = V \times \text{GCV}$ | $E$ in MJ or MMBtu; GCV ≈ **38 MJ/m³** for pipeline gas | | **LNG Energy Density** | $1 \text{ tonne LNG} \approx 52 \text{ MMBtu} \approx 1,360 \text{ m}^3$ gas | Conversion constant for MTPA ↔ BCM/yr | | **Hub Indexation** | $P_{\text{gas}} = \alpha \cdot P_{\text{oil}} + \beta$ | $\alpha$ = slope (typically 0.10–0.16), $\beta$ = constant; oil in \$/bbl, gas in \$/MMBtu | | **Take-or-Pay Ratio** | $\text{ToP\%} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{contracted}}}{Q_{\text{annual demand}}} \times 100$ | Typically **80–90%** in legacy LNG contracts | | **Netback Pricing** | $P_{\text{wellhead}} = P_{\text{hub}} - T - L - P_{\text{processing}}$ | $T$ = transport, $L$ = losses, $P$ = processing fee | | **Switching Parity Price** | $P_{\text{gas}}^{*} = P_{\text{alt}} \times \dfrac{\eta_{\text{alt}}}{\eta_{\text{gas}}} \times \dfrac{\text{GCV}_{\text{alt}}}{\text{GCV}_{\text{gas}}}$ | $\eta$ = thermal efficiency | | **Arbitrage Condition** | $P_{\text{market A}} + C_{\text{transport}} < P_{\text{market B}}$ | Drives inter-segment and inter-hub flows | | **Demand Elasticity** | $\varepsilon_{d} = \dfrac{\%\Delta Q_{d}}{\%\Delta P}$ | Negative for normal goods; high in transport segment | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Boundary Condition:** Hub-based spot markets only function efficiently when (i) pipeline capacity is **non-discriminatory (open-access)**, (ii) multiple shippers exist, and (iii) a **liquid financial market** (futures/derivatives) exists at the hub. Without all three, the "market" is functionally a monopoly. ### 2.4 Real-World Utility These formulas underpin: - **LNG contract negotiations** between Qatar Petroleum and Indian importers (Petronet LNG). - **CGD tariff setting** by PNGRB (Petroleum and Natural Gas Regulatory Board, India). - **Industrial fuel-switching decisions** in Gujarat ceramics clusters. - **Government policy** on subsidies for CNG vs. petrol parity. - **Investment banking valuations** of LNG regasification terminals. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Gas Volume to Energy Conversion (Ideal Gas Law Application) **Step 1:** Start with the combined gas law for two states (actual and standard conditions). $$\dfrac{P_1 V_1}{T_1} = \dfrac{P_2 V_2}{T_2}$$ **Step 2:** Identify the variables. State 1 = actual pipeline conditions; State 2 = standard conditions (**15 °C / 288.15 K** and **1.01325 bar** per ISO standards). $$ \begin{aligned} P_1 &= \text{actual pipeline pressure (typically 40–70 bar)} \\ T_1 &= \text{actual gas temperature (typically 20–40 °C = 293.15–313.15 K)} \\ P_2 &= 1.01325 \text{ bar} \\ T_2 &= 288.15 \text{ K} \\ V_2 &= \text{volume at standard conditions (what we want)} \\ V_1 &= \text{volume at actual conditions (measured)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3:** Rearrange to solve for $V_2$. $$ \begin{aligned} V_2 &= V_1 \times \dfrac{P_1 \cdot T_2}{P_2 \cdot T_1} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4:** Multiply by the **Gross Calorific Value (GCV)** to get energy. $$ \begin{aligned} E &= V_2 \times \text{GCV} \\ &= V_1 \times \dfrac{P_1 \cdot T_2}{P_2 \cdot T_1} \times \text{GCV} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5:** Apply typical numbers — let $V_1 = 1000 \text{ m}^3$ at $P_1 = 50$ bar and $T_1 = 303.15$ K, with $\text{GCV} = 38 \text{ MJ/m}^3$. $$ \begin{aligned} V_2 &= 1000 \times \dfrac{50 \times 288.15}{1.01325 \times 303.15} \\ &= 1000 \times \dfrac{14407.5}{307.18} \\ &= 1000 \times 46.90 \\ &= 46{,}900 \text{ m}^3 \text{ (at standard conditions)} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} E &= 46{,}900 \times 38 \\ &= 1{,}782{,}200 \text{ MJ} \\ &= 1{,}782.2 \text{ TJ} \\ &= 1{,}688.3 \text{ MMBtu} \quad (\text{since } 1 \text{ TJ} = 947.817 \text{ MMBtu}) \end{aligned} $$ **Conclusion:** One thousand cubic meters of pipeline gas at 50 bar contains the energy equivalent of approximately **1,688 MMBtu**, which is the trading unit on the international gas market. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Hub-Indexed Gas Price from Oil Parity **Step 1:** Assume legacy oil-indexed contract: $P_{\text{gas}} = \alpha \cdot P_{\text{oil}} + \beta$. **Step 2:** Historical regression for Asia (JCC crude vs. LNG Japan import) gives $\alpha = 0.145$ and $\beta = 0.30$ \$/MMBtu (simplified; real coefficients are time-varying). **Step 3:** Suppose $P_{\text{oil}} = 80$ \$/bbl. $$ \begin{aligned} P_{\text{gas}} &= 0.145 \times 80 + 0.30 \\ &= 11.60 + 0.30 \\ &= 11.90 \text{ \$/MMBtu} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4:** Now suppose hub-based spot price (TTF or JKM) is $9.50 \$/\text{MMBtu}$. Compare and infer arbitrage. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Spread} &= 11.90 - 9.50 = 2.40 \text{ \$/MMBtu} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5:** At annual volume of 5 MTPA, energy content is: $$ \begin{aligned} E_{\text{annual}} &= 5 \times 10^6 \text{ tonnes} \times 52 \text{ MMBtu/tonne} \\ &= 260 \times 10^6 \text{ MMBtu} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6:** Annual savings from switching to hub-based pricing: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Savings} &= 260 \times 10^6 \times 2.40 \\ &= 624 \times 10^6 \text{ \$} \\ &= \$624 \text{ million per year} \end{aligned} $$ **Conclusion:** This is precisely why mature buyers (Japan, South Korea, India) are renegotiating legacy oil-indexed contracts to **hub-indexed or hybrid** pricing. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Switching Parity Price (Industrial Fuel Choice) **Step 1:** Industrial boiler can use either **gas** (efficiency 90%) or **FO (furnace oil)** (efficiency 85%). The user is indifferent when cost per unit of useful heat is equal. $$ \begin{aligned} \dfrac{P_{\text{gas}}}{\text{GCV}_{\text{gas}} \cdot \eta_{\text{gas}}} &= \dfrac{P_{\text{FO}}}{\text{GCV}_{\text{FO}} \cdot \eta_{\text{FO}}} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2:** Solve for $P_{\text{gas}}$ (the parity price above which FO is preferred). $$ \begin{aligned} P_{\text{gas}}^{*} &= P_{\text{FO}} \times \dfrac{\text{GCV}_{\text{gas}} \cdot \eta_{\text{gas}}}{\text{GCV}_{\text{FO}} \cdot \eta_{\text{FO}}} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3:** Insert typical values — $P_{\text{FO}} = 60$ \$/bbl FO (FO is heavier; ~6.6 MMBtu/bbl), $\text{GCV}_{\text{gas}} = 38 \text{ MJ/m}^3$, $\text{GCV}_{\text{FO}} = 40 \text{ MJ/kg} \approx 10.5 \text{ MMBtu/bbl approx (using } 1 \text{ bbl} = 159 \text{ L, FO density } 0.96)$, $\eta_{\text{gas}} = 0.90$, $\eta_{\text{FO}} = 0.85$. For simplicity, use FO energy ≈ **6.287 MMBtu/bbl** (industry standard): $$ \begin{aligned} P_{\text{gas}}^{*} &= 60 \times \dfrac{0.90 \cdot (1/38 \text{ in MMBtu per m}^3)}{0.85 \cdot 6.287 / 6.287 \text{ [per MMBtu basis]}} \\ \end{aligned} $$ Using consistent units (cost per MMBtu of useful heat): $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Cost of FO per MMBtu} &= \dfrac{60}{6.287} = 9.54 \text{ \$/MMBtu of FO} \\ \text{Cost of FO per MMBtu of useful heat} &= \dfrac{9.54}{0.85} = 11.22 \text{ \$/MMBtu} \\ \text{Therefore gas parity price} &= 11.22 \times 0.90 = 10.10 \text{ \$/MMBtu} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4:** Interpret: if gas exceeds **\$10.10/MMBtu** at the burner-tip, the industrial user economically switches to FO (assuming FO is available and not regulated out). **Conclusion:** This **switching parity price** is the upper bound of the industrial segment's willingness to pay — a critical input to CGD tariff design and gas allocation policy. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Market Characteristics | Attribute | LNG Market | Petrochemical | Industrial Heat | CNG Transport | PNG Domestic | Virtual Trading | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Market Structure** | Oligopoly (exporters) / competitive (importers) | Bilateral monopoly (locked) | Competitive (multi-fuel) | Quasi-monopolistic (city-wise) | Licensed monopoly (CGD) | Highly competitive | | **Price Discovery** | Hub (JKM) + oil index | Long-term contract | Spot / quarterly | Administered (govt-linked) | Tariff (PNGRB) | Continuous (futures) | | **Contract Length** | 15–25 yrs (legacy) | 10–20 yrs | Spot/annual | Annual / monthly | Tariff cycle (5 yr) | Intraday to 10 yrs | | **Switching Risk** | Low (destination-specific) | Very low (asset-locked) | Medium-high | Very high (vehicle-based) | Very low (appliance-based) | N/A (financial) | | **Regulatory Intensity** | High (cross-border) | Medium | Low-medium | High (subsidy) | Very high (welfare) | Medium (SEBI/regulator) | | **Demand Driver** | Industrial growth (Asia) | Chemical demand | Manufacturing cycle | Auto-fuel price gap | Urbanization | Macroeconomic | | **India Market Share** | ~50% of gas imports | ~10% of gas demand | ~25% of gas demand | ~6% of gas demand | ~5% of gas demand | Emerging | ## 4. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** *Define "Unbundling" in the natural gas industry. Why is it a prerequisite for the development of gas markets in non-power segments?* **Model Answer:** **Unbundling** is the legal, functional, and/or ownership separation of the vertically integrated gas value chain into distinct entities handling production, transmission, distribution, and marketing. It is a prerequisite because: 1. **Anti-discrimination** — A pipeline operator that *also* sells gas at the city gate will favour its own downstream arm and squeeze third-party marketers, killing competition in CNG, PNG, and industrial segments. 2. **Third-Party Access (TPA)** — Unbundling enables non-discriminatory access to pipelines and LNG terminals, allowing new entrants (e.g., CGD entities, industrial consumers) to procure gas. 3. **Tariff transparency** — Regulators (like PNGRB) can set cost-reflective tariffs only when accounts are separated. 4. **Market liquidity** — Open-access hubs require multiple shippers; unbundling creates them. Without unbundling, gas remains a captive input of a monopoly — no "market" can form in LNG, CGD, or trading segments. **(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for prerequisites)**. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** *Distinguish between "Hub-based spot pricing" and "Oil-indexed pricing" of natural gas. Which is more appropriate for the LNG spot market today?* **Model Answer:** | Dimension | Oil-Indexed Pricing | Hub-Based Spot Pricing | |---|---|---| | **Anchor** | Crude oil price (Brent / JCC) | Supply-demand at a physical/ virtual hub | | **Discovery** | Lagged (oil price first) | Real-time, continuous | | **Volatility** | Inherits oil volatility | Reflects gas-specific fundamentals | | **Contract form** | Long-term (15–25 yr) Take-or-Pay | Spot/short-term cargoes, futures | | **Geographic fit** | Suitable when gas competes with oil | Suitable where gas competes with gas | **Today, hub-based pricing dominates the LNG spot market** — represented by **JKM (Japan Korea Marker)**, **TTF (Title Transfer Facility)**, and increasingly **HH (Henry Hub) — JKM spreads**. The 2020–2024 period saw oil-indexation de-linkage accelerate due to US LNG exports, shale gas, and the European gas crisis, which decoupled gas from oil fundamentals. **(3 marks: 2 for distinction + 1 for conclusion)**. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU ESE – Module 3 – Model Paper Pattern]** **(a) [7 marks]** *Explain in detail the structure and pricing mechanism of the global LNG market. Discuss the shift from oil-indexed long-term contracts to hub-indexed spot pricing with suitable economic reasoning.* **(b) [7 marks]** *With a numerical example, derive the switching parity price of natural gas for an industrial consumer who can substitute furnace oil. State the policy implications for CGD tariff design in India.* --- **Model Solution — Part A(a):** **Structure of the Global LNG Market:** 1. **Liquefaction Plants (Export Terminals)** — Convert gas to liquid at -162 °C, reducing volume by **~610 times**. Examples: Ras Laffan (Qatar), Gorgon (Australia), Sabine Pass (USA), Sabine Pass capacity ~**30 MTPA**. 2. **LNG Carriers** — Specialized cryogenic ships, capacity typically **150,000–270,000 m³** (Q-Flex, Q-Max classes). 3. **Regasification Terminals (Import)** — Convert LNG back to gas. Examples: Dahej, Hazira (India); Sokhna (Egypt); Gate (Netherlands). 4. **Market Participants** — International Oil Companies (IOCs), National Oil Companies (NOCs), traders (Shell, BP, Vitol, Gunvor), and end-users. **Pricing Mechanisms:** **(i) Oil-Indexed Long-Term Contracts:** - Formula: $P_{\text{LNG}} = \alpha \cdot P_{\text{oil}} + \beta$ - $\alpha$ (slope) typically **0.10–0.16** in Asia - Contract length: **15–25 years** - **Take-or-Pay** clause: Buyer pays for **80–90%** of contracted volume whether or not it lifts - **Destination restriction** clause - Used to underwrite massive liquefaction capex **(ii) Hub-Indexed / Spot Pricing:** - Anchored to **JKM, TTF, or Henry Hub** plus shipping/regas cost - Liquefaction-to-regasification (L–R) spread captures liquefier's margin - **S-curve (Brent) → Hub pricing transition** post-2010 in Asia **Why the shift?** - **Shale revolution (USA)** created gas-on-gas competition and global LNG oversupply (2015–2020). - **Decoupling of oil and gas fundamentals** — gas markets became oversupplied independently of oil. - **Buyers' market** — Japanese, Korean, and Indian utilities renegotiated down. - **European gas crisis (2022)** made hub pricing the global benchmark. - **Spot JKM** in 2022 reached **\$70+/MMBtu** while oil-indexed prices lagged — demonstrating the inadequacy of oil-indexation. **Conclusion:** The LNG market is in a **hybrid transition** — long-term contracts are now **S-curve / hub-index-linked** with **flexible destination and lower ToP**, blending security with market responsiveness. **(7 marks: 2 for structure, 3 for pricing, 2 for shift reasoning)**. --- **Model Solution — Part A(b):** **Derivation of Switching Parity Price:** A factory has two boiler options: - **Gas-fired boiler** with efficiency $\eta_{\text{gas}} = 0.90$ and gas GCV = 38 MJ/m³ ≈ **1.055 MMBtu/m³ × 0.038 ≈ standard conversion used below** - **Furnace oil boiler** with efficiency $\eta_{\text{FO}} = 0.85$ and FO energy ≈ **6.287 MMBtu/bbl** Let $P_{\text{FO}} = 60$ \$/bbl. **Step 1:** Cost per MMBtu of FO at burner tip: $$ \begin{aligned} C_{\text{FO, useful}} &= \dfrac{P_{\text{FO}}}{\text{GCV}_{\text{FO}} \cdot \eta_{\text{FO}}} \\ &= \dfrac{60}{6.287 \times 0.85} \\ &= \dfrac{60}{5.344} \\ &= 11.23 \text{ \$/MMBtu of useful heat} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2:** For gas to be equally attractive on a useful-heat basis: $$ \begin{aligned} P_{\text{gas}}^{*} &= C_{\text{FO, useful}} \times \eta_{\text{gas}} \\ &= 11.23 \times 0.90 \\ &= 10.11 \text{ \$/MMBtu} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3:** Converting to ₹ (assume ₹/\$ = 83): $$ \begin{aligned} P_{\text{gas}}^{*} &= 10.11 \times 83 \\ &= 839.1 \text{ ₹/MMBtu} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4:** Per standard cubic meter (scm) equivalent: $$ \begin{aligned} P_{\text{gas, scm}}^{*} &= 839.1 \times \dfrac{1}{1.055 \times 10.55} \text{ [adjusting for GCV per m}^3] \\ \text{Using } 1 \text{ MMBtu} &= 26.85 \text{ m}^3 \text{ for typical pipeline gas:}\\ P_{\text{gas, scm}}^{*} &= \dfrac{839.1}{26.85} \\ &= 31.25 \text{ ₹/scm} \end{aligned} $$ **Policy Implications for CGD Tariff Design in India:** 1. **Ceiling Pricing** — The PNGRB must keep industrial CNG/PNG tariffs **below ~₹31/scm-equivalent** (or below \$10/MMBtu) to retain the industrial segment; otherwise, fuel-switching to FO/biomass occurs. 2. **Subsidy Targeting** — Cross-subsidization of domestic PNG by industrial users is sustainable only if industrial price stays below the switching parity. If it crosses, industries exit the gas market. 3. **Demand Forecasting** — Gas allocation policy should reserve adequate volumes for the "must-run" industrial segment to prevent self-displacement. 4. **Strategic Importance** — Industrial switching reduces gas demand elasticity but also reduces India's gas-share-of-energy, contradicting the policy goal of raising gas to **15% of energy mix by 2030**. **(7 marks: 3 for derivation, 2 for numerical result, 2 for policy)**. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]** **(a) [7 marks]** *Discuss the role of City Gas Distribution (CGD) networks in developing the residential PNG and CNG-transport markets in India. What are the regulatory and economic challenges?* **(b) [7 marks]** *Explain the concept of "Virtual Trading Hubs" with reference to Henry Hub, TTF, and emerging Asian hubs. Why are virtual hubs critical for the development of competitive gas markets?* --- **Model Solution — Question B(a):** **Role of CGD Networks:** 1. **Last-Mile Delivery Infrastructure** — CGD entities (Indraprastha Gas, Mahanagar Gas, Adani Gas, GAIL Gas) build the **medium-density polyethylene (MDPE) and steel pipelines** that bring PNG to households and CNG to refueling stations. 2. **Market Aggregation Point** — CGDs aggregate thousands of small-volume customers (domestic PNG users) into a viable offtake that justifies upstream pipeline and LNG terminal investment. 3. **Geographical Authorization Areas (GAAs)** — PNGRB auctions exclusive GAA licences (e.g., 1 district or city) to CGD operators for **25-year exclusivity**, creating semi-monopolistic local markets. 4. **Industrial/Commercial Anchor Loads** — Within the GAA, CGDs also serve commercial (hotels, hospitals) and small industrial customers, providing them with a cheaper alternative to LPG and FO. 5. **Transport Fuel Backbone** — CNG outlets (4500+ across India) are a CGD's most visible face, displacing petrol/diesel in public transport (Delhi model) and three-wheelers. **Regulatory and Economic Challenges:** | Challenge | Description | |---|---| | **High capex, slow payback** | Last-mile PNG capex is ₹20,000–50,000 per household with payback > 7 years | | **Cross-subsidy tensions** | Industrial/commercial subsidize domestic; industrial customers may switch out | | **Alternate fuel competition** | LPG cylinders (subsidized historically) compete with PNG; EVs compete with CNG | | **Volume risk** — CGDs must meet Minimum Work Programme targets or face cancellation | | **Pipeline connectivity gaps** — Many GAAs lack upstream pipeline access, requiring expensive virtual pipelines (LNG trucks) | | **Tariff determination** — PNGRB uses regulated tariff methodology, but delays cause financial strain | | **Domestic penetration plateau** — Slow shift from LPG to PNG despite subsidies | **Policy levers to address:** - **Priority access to APM gas** for CGD (4.6 MMSCMD reserved). - **Promote CNG in public transport** (Delhi model replicated in 50+ cities). - **DBC (Domestic Bulk Consumer) PNG** for housing societies as a transition step. **(7 marks: 4 for role, 3 for challenges)**. --- **Model Solution — Question B(b):** **Concept of Virtual Trading Hubs:** A **Virtual Trading Hub (VTH)** is a notional point on a gas pipeline system where title to gas can be transferred between shippers without physical delivery. It is the **economic nerve centre** of a deregulated gas market. **Major Hubs:** 1. **Henry Hub (Louisiana, USA):** - Anchor for **NYMEX natural gas futures** (the most liquid gas benchmark globally). - Volume traded: **~50 BCF/day**. - Hub pricing dominated by **shale gas** fundamentals. 2. **TTF (Title Transfer Facility, Netherlands):** - Virtual hub; pricing benchmark for **Northwest European gas**. - Became the **global marginal price** after Russia–Ukraine crisis (2022). - Deeply liquid: ~**\$1 trillion+** of gas traded annually. 3. **NBP (National Balancing Point, UK):** - Mature European hub; historically leading but now secondary to TTF. 4. **JKM (Japan-Korea Marker):** - **Platts-assessed** spot LNG price in Asia. - Increasingly liquid, especially post-2022. 5. **Emerging Hubs: India Gas Hub, Singapore (PVG), China's Chongqing hub.** **Why Virtual Hubs Are Critical for Competitive Gas Markets:** 1. **Price Discovery** — Continuous trading produces transparent, supply-demand-reflective prices — replacing administered tariffs. 2. **Risk Transfer** — Futures, swaps, and options enable hedging of price risk for buyers and sellers, attracting long-term investment. 3. **Market Liquidity** — A hub allows small players to enter/exit positions, creating depth that contract-based markets cannot. 4. **Spatial Arbitrage** — Gas can be bought cheaply at one hub and sold expensively at another, equalizing prices. 5. **Disintermediation** — Buyers can procure directly from producers/marketers, reducing monopoly rents of integrated utilities. 6. **Investment Signal** — Hub prices reflect true scarcity, guiding LNG terminal, storage, and pipeline investment. 7. **Hub-and-Spoke Market Architecture** — Replaces rigid contract networks with flexible, modular, scalable market architecture. **Challenges to Hub Development in India:** - Limited pipeline interconnectivity. - Lack of common carrier access culture. - Low trading volumes (institutional and regulatory). - **No official Indian gas hub** yet — though GAIL and IEX have initiated pilots. **(7 marks: 3 for concept and hubs, 4 for criticality and challenges)**. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Warning:** > 1. **Do NOT confuse "Gas-to-Power" with "Other Segments"** — Examiners will instantly deduct marks if your answer drifts into the power-generation segment. The 2024 Scheme clearly demarcates this topic. > 2. **Avoid vague answers** like *"gas is important for the economy"* — examiners expect **specific market names, prices, formulas, and policy references** (PNGRB, CGD, GAA, JKM, TTF, Henry Hub, ToP). > 3. **Numerical questions require full step-by-step substitution** — skipping intermediate algebra steps (e.g., showing only final \$/MMBtu) is penalized. > 4. **Cite real benchmark hubs** by name, not generically ("a hub in Europe"). > 5. **Distinguish "long-term contract" from "spot"** clearly — many students mix them up. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3: Markets in Other Segments of the Gas Industry** - ✅ The gas value chain is **Upstream → Midstream → Downstream**, and the "other segments" refer to **all downstream markets except power generation**. - ✅ The **six core sub-markets** are: **LNG, Petrochemical, Industrial Heat, CNG-Transport, PNG-Domestic, and Virtual Hub Trading**. - ✅ **Unbundling** (legal, functional, ownership) is the **necessary precondition** for any competitive market to form. - ✅ **Take-or-Pay (ToP)** contracts of 80–90% volume + 15–25 yr duration characterize **legacy oil-indexed LNG contracts**. - ✅ **Hub-based spot pricing** is the modern norm: **Henry Hub (USA)**, **TTF (Europe)**, **JKM (Asia)**. - ✅ **Oil-indexation formula**: $P_{\text{gas}} = \alpha \cdot P_{\text{oil}} + \beta$ with $\alpha \approx 0.10$–$0.16$. - ✅ **Switching parity price** sets the ceiling for industrial gas pricing; above it, users revert to FO/coal. - ✅ **CGD networks in India** are regulated by **PNGRB** under the **PNGRB Act, 2006**, with **Geographical Area (GAA) exclusivity** for 25 years. - ✅ **CNG competes with petrol/diesel** in transport — highly price-sensitive, subsidy-driven. - ✅ **PNG competes with LPG** in households — penetration depends on cross-subsidy, last-mile capex, and convenience. - ✅ **Virtual Trading Hubs** enable price discovery, risk transfer, and arbitrage — critical for mature gas markets. - ✅ **LNG L–R spread** (Liquefaction-to-Regasification margin) is the key economic indicator of LNG market health. - ✅ **Standard energy unit for gas trading is MMBtu**; **1 MMBtu ≈ 1.055 GJ ≈ 26.85 m³** of pipeline gas. - ✅ **India's policy goal** of raising gas's share of the energy mix to **15% by 2030** depends on developing these "other" segments — particularly CGD and industrial use. - ✅ **Real-world case studies** to remember: **Petronet LNG Dahej** (long-term Qatar contract), **Indian Gas Exchange (IGX)** (emerging hub), **Delhi CNG programme** (success model), **European TTF crisis of 2022** (hub pricing dominance).

topics.title: Trading Models in the Deregulated Natural Gas Industry
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET526 - ENERGY ECONOMICS AND POLICY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Trading Models in the Deregulated Natural Gas Industry # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition **Deregulation** of the natural gas industry refers to the structural separation of the vertically integrated gas value chain — production, gathering, processing, storage, transmission, distribution, and retail supply — into independent competitive segments, where market forces rather than a single regulated monopoly determine price, volume, and access. A **Trading Model** in this deregulated environment is the institutional, contractual, and market framework that defines *how* natural gas is bought, sold, priced, transported, and delivered among producers, shippers, marketers, utilities, and end-users across physical and financial markets. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Per the PEMET526 (2024 Scheme) Module-3 syllabus, students must be able to differentiate between **monopoly**, **oligopoly**, and **perfectly competitive** trading structures and analyze the role of **gas hubs**, **spot markets**, **futures markets**, and **bilateral contracting** in price discovery. ### Key Structural Components of a Deregulated Gas Industry | Component | Function | Market Power | |---|---|---| | **Producers / Upstream** | Exploration & production (E&P) | Often oligopolistic (e.g., 5–10 majors) | | **Gatherers & Processors** | Wellhead → processing plant | Regional competition | | **Pipelines / Transmission** | Long-haul transport | Natural monopoly (regulated access) | | **Storage Operators** | Buffer between supply and seasonal demand | Mixed; often competitive | | **LDCs (Local Distribution Co.)** | City-gate → end consumer | Monopoly franchise (regulated retail) | | **Marketers / Gas Traders** | Buy-bundle-sell gas | Competitive (wholesale) | | **End-Users** | Industrial, commercial, residential, power | Price-takers | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Gas Stock Exchange" Imagine the natural gas industry as a **grocery supply chain** that was once run by a single company owning the farm, the trucks, the warehouse, *and* the corner store. You had no choice but to buy from them at *their* price. **Deregulation** is like splitting that company into independent players: - The **farmer** (producer) just grows the gas. - The **trucking company** (pipeline) just transports it — anyone can hire them at a published tariff. - The **wholesalers** (marketers) buy in bulk, store it, and resell. - The **retailers** (LDCs) compete for your kitchen connection. A **Trading Model** is essentially the set of "rules of the marketplace" — like the rules of a stock exchange — that decides: - **Where** deals happen (virtual hubs, physical exchanges). - **When** deals happen (real-time spot, next-day, monthly forwards, years-ahead futures). - **How** prices are set (bid-ask, index-based, cost-plus, netback). > [!NOTE] > The benchmark price in the U.S. deregulated market is the **Henry Hub** price in Louisiana, quoted in **USD per MMBtu (Million British Thermal Units)**. Europe's equivalent is the **TTF (Title Transfer Facility)** at the Dutch virtual hub, and the UK uses the **NBP (National Balancing Point)**. ## 1.3 The Three Pillars of Gas Trading > [!NOTE] > **Core Definitions to Memorize for KTU Exams:** 1. **Spot Market** — A market for immediate or near-term (next-day, same-day) physical delivery of gas at a published hub index price. 2. **Forward / Futures Market** — Standardized contracts for delivery at a future date, used for hedging price risk. The **NYMEX Henry Hub Futures** contract is the global benchmark. 3. **Bilateral Contract** — A private, bespoke long-term agreement (15–25 years) between a producer and a buyer/utility, often indexed to a hub price plus a fixed basis differential. ## 1.4 The Standard Units of Natural Gas Trading > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Units (BOLD for emphasis):** > - **1 MMBtu = 1,000,000 Btu (British Thermal Units)** ≈ 1.055 GJ > - **1 Mcf = 1,000 cubic feet** of gas (≈ 1.037 MMBtu on average) > - **1 therm = 100,000 Btu** (≈ 0.1 MMBtu) > - **1 boe (barrel of oil equivalent) ≈ 5.8 MMBtu** > - **1 tonne of LNG ≈ 52 MMBtu** of natural gas (post-regasification) --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Deregulation Process — A Brief Historical Note The U.S. led the global gas deregulation wave. Three landmark regulatory acts reshaped the industry: - **Natural Gas Policy Act (NGPA), 1978** — Began decontrolling wellhead prices. - **FERC Order 436 (1985)** — Allowed optional open-access transportation on interstate pipelines. - **FERC Order 636 (1992)** — The "Mega Rule": mandated **unbundling** of pipeline services (transport, storage, sales separated) and forced pipelines to become **common carriers**. > [!NOTE] > **Unbundling** = separating the merchant (sales) function of a pipeline from its transport function. The pipeline becomes a toll-road operator, not a gas merchant. ## 2.2 The Five Dominant Trading Models ### 2.2.1 Model 1 — Monopoly / Vertically Integrated (Pre-Deregulation) - Single company controls E&P → pipeline → distribution → retail. - Prices set by **Cost-of-Service (COS) Regulation** with a regulated rate-of-return. - No competition, no spot market. ### 2.2.2 Model 2 — Single-Buyer Model (Transitional) - A state-owned or state-designated entity is the **sole offtaker** of all domestic gas (e.g., India's pre-2004 GAIL monopoly). - Producer sells to single buyer at an **Administered Price Mechanism (APM)**. ### 2.2.3 Model 3 — Bilateral Long-Term Contracts (LTC) - Backbone of the **European gas market** historically (Gazprom–E.ON, Sonatrach–Enel, etc.). - Oil-indexed pricing: gas price is **linked to crude oil** (typically with a 6–9 month lag). - Formula historically: $P_{gas} = P_{oil-ref} \times \text{slope} \times 0.1615$ (per MMBtu equivalent, slope often 0.10–0.15). ### 2.2.4 Model 4 — Hub-Based Spot & Forward Market (Most Deregulated) - Multiple **virtual trading points (hubs)** where gas is bought/sold on standardized contracts. - Spot price discovered by **supply-demand intersection**. - Examples: **Henry Hub (USA)**, **TTF (Netherlands)**, **NBP (UK)**, **JKM (Japan/Korea LNG)**. - The **Henry Hub** trades ~50,000 MMBtu/day in physical + ~1.5 million contracts in paper futures monthly. ### 2.2.5 Model 5 — Wholesale + Retail Competition (Mature Deregulation) - Industrial/commercial users choose their gas **marketer/supplier**. - LDC becomes a "**delivery-only**" company (e.g., Con Edison in NY, BGE in Maryland). - Retail choice program: ~60% of U.S. residential consumers now have retail supplier choice in deregulated states. ## 2.3 The 'Why' Behind Each Step — The Logic of Market Design > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why hub-based trading evolved:** > 1. **Network bottlenecks** required a centralized balancing point. > 2. **Pipeline capacity** is finite and expensive — virtual hubs decouple trade from physical flow. > 3. **Standardization** (MMBtu, MWh-thermal) allowed fungibility — gas is gas is gas. > 4. **Information efficiency** — daily index publications give transparent price signals. > 5. **Risk management** — financial derivatives (futures, swaps, options) require a transparent reference price. ## 2.4 Netback Pricing — The Inverse of Oil-Indexation In a deregulated market, gas competes with other fuels. The **Netback Value** is the highest price a buyer can pay for gas at the wellhead, given the alternative fuel's price at the burner-tip minus transport costs. $$P_{gas, wellhead} = P_{alt-fuel, burner-tip} - C_{transport} - C_{distribution} - \text{Markup}$$ For a gas-fired power plant competing with coal: $$P_{gas, wellhead} = \left(\frac{\eta_{coal}}{\eta_{gas}}\right) \cdot P_{coal, Btu} - C_{transport, gas}$$ Where: - $\eta$ = thermal efficiency of conversion technology - $P_{coal, Btu}$ = coal price in \$/MMBtu equivalent - $C_{transport, gas}$ = gas transport cost in \$/MMBtu ## 2.5 Gas-on-Gas Competition vs. Oil-Indexation | Feature | Oil-Indexed LTC | Hub-Based (Gas-on-Gas) | |---|---|---| | Price signal | Crude oil prices (lagged) | Real-time supply/demand | | Liquidity | Low (one buyer, one seller) | High (many participants) | | Flexibility | Rigid (take-or-pay) | Flexible (spot, swing) | | Risk | Buyers exposed to oil shocks | Buyers exposed to weather spikes | | Examples | Historical Gazprom contracts | Henry Hub, TTF, JKM | ## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **MASTER FORMULA TABLE — Save this for the ESE (End Semester Exam):** | # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $1 \text{ Mcf} \approx 1.037 \text{ MMBtu}$ | Volume → energy | MMBtu | | 2 | $1 \text{ MMBtu} = 1.055 \text{ GJ}$ | Energy conversion | GJ | | 3 | $1 \text{ tonne LNG} \approx 52 \text{ MMBtu}$ | LNG → gas | MMBtu | | 4 | $1 \text{ boe} \approx 5.8 \text{ MMBtu}$ | Oil → gas equivalent | MMBtu | | 5 | $P_{gas,LTC} = P_{oil,ref} \cdot s \cdot 0.1615$ | Oil-indexed LTC price | \$/MMBtu | | 6 | $P_{netback} = P_{alt,Btu} - C_{trans} - C_{dist}$ | Netback value | \$/MMBtu | | 7 | $P_{delivered} = P_{hub} + B + T + S$ | City-gate price = hub + basis + transport + storage | \$/MMBtu | | 8 | $Q_{LNG,mt} = \frac{Q_{gas,MMBtu}}{52}$ | LNG cargo size in tonnes | mt (metric tonnes) | | 9 | $\text{Heat Rate} = \frac{\text{Input MMBtu}}{\text{Output MWh}}$ | Power plant efficiency inverse | MMBtu/MWh | | 10 | $\text{Spark Spread} = P_{elec} \times HR - P_{gas}$ | Gas-power margin | \$/MMBtu | > **Variable Legend:** > $s$ = slope factor (0.10–0.15) > $B$ = basis differential (hub-to-city-gate) > $T$ = transportation tariff > $S$ = storage cost > $HR$ = heat rate (MMBtu/MWh) > $Q$ = quantity > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL TABLE FORMATTING NOTE:** In KTU exam answer sheets, **always** include units next to numerical values (e.g., \$4.20/MMBtu, not just 4.20). Examiners deduct 0.5 marks for missing units. ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering & Economic Utility - **Risk Hedging**: Industrial gas buyers use NYMEX futures to lock in prices 12–24 months ahead, protecting against winter spikes (which historically have hit $30+/MMBtu at Henry Hub, e.g., Feb 2021 Texas freeze). - **LNG Arbitrage**: A trader in 2022 could buy LNG at Henry Hub (\$6/MMBtu) and sell at JKM Asia (\$40/MMBtu) — the **cross-regional price arbitrage** defines the modern global gas market. - **Power Sector Coupling**: The **spark spread** formula links gas markets to electricity markets, making gas the marginal price-setter in many U.S. states (PJM, ERCOT). - **Carbon Pricing Interaction**: Adding a **carbon price** of \$50/tCO₂ raises the effective cost of coal-fired power by ~\$1.20/MMBtu thermal, shifting netback calculations in favor of gas. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Heat Content Conversion (Volume to Energy) **Problem:** A pipeline delivers 50,000 Mcf of natural gas per day. Express this in MMBtu/day, GJ/day, and equivalent tonnes of LNG per year. **Given:** - Heating value of gas: $H = 1,037$ Btu/scf (standard cubic foot, average for U.S. pipeline gas) - $1 \text{ MMBtu} = 10^6 \text{ Btu}$ - $1 \text{ GJ} = 10^9 \text{ J} = 947.817$ MMBtu - $1 \text{ tonne LNG} \approx 52$ MMBtu - 1 Mcf = 1,000 scf **Step 1 — Convert Mcf to Btu:** $$\begin{aligned} Q_{Btu/day} &= 50{,}000 \text{ Mcf/day} \times 1{,}000 \text{ scf/Mcf} \times 1{,}037 \text{ Btu/scf} \\ &= 50{,}000 \times 1{,}000 \times 1{,}037 \\ &= 51{,}850{,}000{,}000 \text{ Btu/day} \\ &= 5.185 \times 10^{10} \text{ Btu/day} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 — Convert to MMBtu/day:** $$Q_{MMBtu/day} = \frac{5.185 \times 10^{10}}{10^6} = 51{,}850 \text{ MMBtu/day}$$ **Step 3 — Convert to GJ/day:** $$Q_{GJ/day} = 51{,}850 \text{ MMBtu/day} \times 1.055 \text{ GJ/MMBtu} = 54{,}701.75 \text{ GJ/day}$$ **Step 4 — Annual LNG equivalent:** $$Q_{MMBtu/year} = 51{,}850 \times 365 = 18{,}925{,}250 \text{ MMBtu/year}$$ $$M_{LNG} = \frac{18{,}925{,}250}{52} = 363{,}947 \text{ tonnes LNG/year}$$ **Sanity Check:** A typical large LNG cargo is ~140,000–160,000 tonnes, so this daily flow equals ~2.3 LNG cargoes per year. ✓ ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Oil-Indexed LTC Price Calculation **Problem:** A 20-year LTC is signed in 2015 between a Gulf producer and a Japanese utility. Reference crude is JCC (Japanese Crude Cocktail) at \$70/bbl. Slope is 0.13. Calculate the gas price in \$/MMBtu. **Given:** - $P_{oil,ref} = \$70/\text{bbl}$ - $s = 0.13$ - JCC-to-MMBtu factor for the formula: $0.1615$ (since 1 bbl oil ≈ 5.8 MMBtu, and $\frac{1}{5.8} \approx 0.1724$; the 0.1615 is a slightly rounded version used in Asian LTCs including a small efficiency adjustment) **Formula:** $$P_{gas} = P_{oil,ref} \times s \times 0.1615$$ **Step-by-step substitution:** $$\begin{aligned} P_{gas} &= 70 \times 0.13 \times 0.1615 \\ &= 70 \times 0.020995 \\ &= \$1.4696 \text{/MMBtu} \end{aligned}$$ **Interpretation:** At \$70/bbl JCC, the Asian LTC gas price is ~\$1.47/MMBtu — very low by post-2022 standards (when JKM spot hit \$80+/MMBtu). > [!NOTE] > **Historical Context:** Between 2010–2020, Asian LTC prices hovered at \$8–12/MMBtu due to JCC averaging \$80–110/bbl. Many contracts had "S-curve" pricing to dampen extreme oil prices. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Netback Pricing for Gas vs. Coal Power **Problem:** A combined-cycle gas turbine (CCGT) has $\eta_{gas} = 0.55$ (55% efficiency). A coal plant competing with it has $\eta_{coal} = 0.38$. Coal price is \$2.50/MMBtu (thermal). Gas transportation cost from wellhead to plant is \$1.20/MMBtu. Find the breakeven wellhead gas price. **Formula:** $$P_{gas, wellhead} = \left(\frac{\eta_{coal}}{\eta_{gas}}\right) \cdot P_{coal, Btu} - C_{transport, gas}$$ **Step-by-step:** $$\begin{aligned} P_{gas, wellhead} &= \left(\frac{0.38}{0.55}\right) \times 2.50 - 1.20 \\ &= 0.6909 \times 2.50 - 1.20 \\ &= 1.7273 - 1.20 \\ &= \$0.5273 \text{ /MMBtu} \end{aligned}$$ **Interpretation:** If the wellhead gas price falls below \$0.53/MMBtu, gas-fired power becomes cheaper than coal-fired power (even without carbon pricing). This is why **shale gas revolution** in the U.S. (\$2–4/MMBtu wellhead) wiped out coal generation in PJM and ERCOT by 2020. > [!NOTE] > **Carbon Adjustment:** If a \$30/tCO₂ carbon price is applied to coal, the effective coal price becomes: > $P_{coal,adj} = 2.50 + 30 \times 0.0000923 = 2.5028$ \$/MMBtu (since 1 MMBtu coal ≈ 0.0923 tCO₂). > So carbon pricing has only a marginal effect unless it exceeds \$50/tCO₂. ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Spark Spread Calculation **Problem:** A CCGT has heat rate $HR = 7.0$ MMBtu/MWh. Electricity price is \$55/MWh. Gas price is \$4.50/MMBtu. Compute the spark spread (margin). **Formula:** $$\text{Spark Spread} = P_{elec} - (HR \times P_{gas})$$ **Step-by-step:** $$\begin{aligned} \text{Spark Spread} &= 55 - (7.0 \times 4.50) \\ &= 55 - 31.50 \\ &= \$23.50 \text{ /MWh} \end{aligned}$$ **Operating Margin (per MMBtu of gas burned):** $$\text{Margin}_{MMBtu} = \frac{23.50}{7.0} = \$3.357 \text{ /MMBtu}$$ **Interpretation:** When spark spread > \$0, the plant is profitable. If gas price spikes to \$10/MMBtu, spark spread becomes $55 - 70 = -\$15/\text{MWh}$ — the plant **idles**, which is the basis for **gas-to-coal switching** in real-time power markets. ## 3.5 Derivation 5 — LNG Cargo Arbitrage **Problem:** Spot LNG at Henry Hub (U.S.) is \$5.00/MMBtu. JKM (Japan/Korea Marker) is \$18.00/MMBtu. Liquefaction toll is \$3.00/MMBtu. Shipping cost (U.S. Gulf → Tokyo) is \$2.50/MMBtu. Regasification \$0.50/MMBtu. Should an arbitrageur ship LNG from U.S. to Japan? **Step 1 — Total Cost Delivered to Tokyo:** $$\begin{aligned} P_{delivered} &= P_{HH} + C_{liq} + C_{ship} + C_{regas} \\ &= 5.00 + 3.00 + 2.50 + 0.50 \\ &= \$11.00 \text{ /MMBtu} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 — Arbitrage Profit:** $$\begin{aligned} \pi &= P_{JKM} - P_{delivered} \\ &= 18.00 - 11.00 \\ &= \$7.00 \text{ /MMBtu} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Profit on a Standard 3.5 mtbtu LNG Cargo (160,000 tonnes):** $$\begin{aligned} \pi_{cargo} &= 7.00 \times 160{,}000 \times 52 \\ &= 7.00 \times 8{,}320{,}000 \\ &= \$58{,}240{,}000 \text{ per cargo} \end{aligned}$$ > [!NOTE] > This is the **money chart** that drove the 2016–2020 U.S. LNG export boom. Once the spread exceeds ~\$3/MMBtu, shipping is profitable. ## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Comparing the Four Major Trading Hubs | Attribute | Henry Hub (USA) | TTF (Netherlands) | NBP (UK) | JKM (Platts) | |---|---|---|---|---| | Market type | Hub + futures | Virtual hub | Virtual hub | Index (assessment) | | Standard contract | NYMEX futures (10,000 MMBtu) | ICE Endex | ICE | JKM spot assessment | | Price formation | Gas-on-gas | Gas-on-gas | Gas-on-gas | Bid/offer survey | | Liquidity rank | #1 globally | #2 globally | #3 | #1 in Asia | | Typical volatility | High (weather-driven) | High | Medium-high | Extreme (LNG shock) | | 2022 peak price | \$9.50/MMBtu | €310/MWh (~€94/MMBtu) | £8.00/therm | \$70+/MMBtu | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Deregulated Gas Industry — Functional Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB producerA[Producer A
Upstream E and P] producerB[Producer B
Shale Gas] producerC[Producer C
Offshore Gas] gatherer[Gatherer and Processor
Wellhead to Plant] storage[Storage Operator
Salt Cavern or Depleted Field] hubMain[Virtual Trading Hub
Henry Hub or TTF] spot[Spot Market
Next Day Delivery] forward[Forward Market
1 to 24 Months] futures[Futures Market
NYMEX or ICE] pipeline[Pipeline Transmission
FERC Regulated Tariff] marketer[Gas Marketer
Aggregator and Trader] ldc[LDC
Local Distribution Co] industrial[Industrial User
Power or Fertilizer] commercial[Commercial User
Hotels and Offices] residential[Residential User
Households] producerA --> gatherer producerB --> gatherer producerC --> gatherer gatherer --> pipeline gatherer --> storage storage --> hubMain pipeline --> hubMain hubMain --> spot hubMain --> forward hubMain --> futures hubMain --> marketer marketer --> ldc marketer --> industrial marketer --> commercial ldc --> residential ldc --> commercial ``` ## 4.2 Trading Model Evolution Timeline — Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR M1[Model 1
Vertically Integrated
Monopoly
1950 to 1978] M2[Model 2
Single Buyer
State offtaker
1978 to 1992] M3[Model 3
Long Term Contracts
Oil Indexed
1992 to 2010] M4[Model 4
Hub Based Trading
Spot and Forward
2010 to 2020] M5[Model 5
Global LNG Market
Gas on Gas Pricing
2020 to Present] M1 ==> M2 M2 ==> M3 M3 ==> M4 M4 ==> M5 M1 -.- n1[FERC Order 436] M2 -.- n2[FERC Order 636
Unbundling] M3 -.- n3[Henry Hub Growth
Shale Boom] M4 -.- n4[US LNG Export
JKM Coupling] ``` ## 4.3 Price Formation Mechanism — Hub Trading Flow ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph supply[Supply Side] s1[Domestic Production] s2[LNG Imports] s3[Storage Withdrawal] end subgraph hub[Virtual Hub Trading Point] h1[Buy Bid Stack] h2[Sell Offer Stack] h3[Market Clearing Price
MCP Discovery] h1 --> h3 h2 --> h3 end subgraph demand[Demand Side] d1[Power Generators] d2[Industrial Boilers] d3[Residential Heating] d4[LNG Export Terminals] end s1 --> h2 s2 --> h2 s3 --> h2 h3 --> d1 h3 --> d2 h3 --> d3 h3 --> d4 d1 --> h1 d2 --> h1 d3 --> h1 d4 --> h1 ``` ## 4.4 Market Participant Interaction Matrix ```mermaid flowchart TB prod[Producers] pipe[Pipeline Owners] store[Storage Firms] mkt[Gas Marketers] util[Utilities and LDCs] endU[End Users] fin[Financial Players
Banks and Hedge Funds] prod <-->|"Physical Gas
Hub Index Price"| mkt prod <-->|"Long Term LTC"| util pipe <-->|"Firm Transport
FT Tariff"| mkt pipe <-->|"Interruptible Transport
IT Discount"| endU store <-->|"Storage Service
Inject Withdraw"| mkt store <-->|"Capacity Release"| mkt mkt <-->|"City Gate Supply"| util util -->|"Bundled Service"| endU fin -.->|"Futures and Swaps
No Physical Flow"| mkt fin -.->|"NYMEX HH"| prod ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** Solid lines = physical gas flow / service contracts. Dotted lines = financial/paper transactions only. The **gas marketer** is the central node — it is the only player that interacts with every other layer of the value chain. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style, Module 3]** ### Q1. Differentiate between oil-indexed and gas-on-gas pricing in natural gas trading. [3 Marks] **Model Answer (3 marks, 1.5 each):** - **Oil-Indexed Pricing (1.5 marks):** The gas price is determined by a formula that ties it to a reference crude oil price (e.g., Brent, JCC, WTI) with a defined slope factor and time lag (typically 3–9 months). Predominant in long-term European and Asian LTCs. Advantages: revenue stability, predictable cash flows. Disadvantages: gas price does not reflect gas-specific supply-demand shocks; inefficient allocation. - **Gas-on-Gas Pricing (1.5 marks):** The gas price is determined purely by short-term supply and demand at a virtual trading hub (e.g., Henry Hub, TTF). Reflects real-time market fundamentals such as weather, storage levels, and LNG cargo arrivals. Dominant in North American, UK, and increasingly Asian markets. Advantages: transparent price signal, market efficiency. Disadvantages: high volatility, exposure to weather shocks. --- ### Q2. Define "unbundling" in the natural gas industry. State any two of its benefits. [3 Marks] **Model Answer (1 + 1 + 1 marks):** - **Definition (1 mark):** Unbundling is the regulatory and operational separation of the various services (production, gathering, transmission, storage, distribution, and retail supply) that were historically provided by a single vertically integrated gas utility. Under FERC Order 636 (USA) and EU Third Energy Package, transmission pipelines must offer **open-access, non-discriminatory** transport services separately from gas sales. - **Benefit 1 (1 mark):** **Promotes competition in upstream and midstream markets** — independent producers and marketers can use the same pipeline on equal terms, ending the monopoly advantage of integrated utilities. - **Benefit 2 (1 mark):** **Enhances price transparency and efficiency** — separate accounting for transport vs. commodity costs allows regulators and consumers to identify monopolistic rents; the true cost of gas vs. transport becomes visible. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > **ESE Pattern: Each Part B carries 14 marks, divided into sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Internal choice between Question A and Question B.** --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style] #### (a) Explain the key features of the **Hub-Based Spot and Forward Trading Model** in a deregulated natural gas industry. With a neat block diagram, describe the price discovery mechanism at a virtual trading hub. [7 Marks] **Model Answer Outline:** 1. **Definition and characteristics of hub-based model (2 marks):** - Gas traded at centralized virtual trading points (e.g., Henry Hub, TTF). - Standardized contracts (MMBtu, MWh thermal). - Transparent, published index prices (Platts, ICE settlements). - Multiple buyers and sellers — competitive price formation. 2. **Types of contracts traded (1.5 marks):** - **Day-ahead (next-day) spot** — physical delivery next gas day. - **Month-ahead forwards** — fixed price for entire month delivery. - **Seasonal forwards** — winter/summer strips. - **Calendar/year strips** — fixed price for full year. - **Financial futures** (NYMEX/ICE) — no physical delivery for most traders (99%+ cash-settled). 3. **Price discovery mechanism via block diagram (2.5 marks):** - Producers submit **offer curves** (minimum price they accept). - Buyers (utilities, marketers, power generators) submit **bid curves** (maximum price they will pay). - Exchange matches highest bid with lowest offer until **Market Clearing Price (MCP)** is determined. - The MCP becomes the **daily index price** (e.g., "Henry Hub next-day fixed price"). - Storage and LNG cargo arrivals provide supply elasticity; weather forecasts provide demand signals. 4. **Real-world example (1 mark):** Henry Hub physical day-ahead traded ~50 Bcf/day in 2023; the NYMEX Henry Hub futures contract (NG) is the most liquid energy futures contract in the world. --- #### (b) A European utility has a long-term gas contract indexed to Brent crude. Brent is currently trading at **\$95/bbl**. The contract specifies a **slope factor of 0.14** and uses the **standard JCC-to-MMBtu conversion of 0.1615**. The utility also has the option to buy spot gas at TTF at **€45/MWh**. Assume **1 MWh = 3.412 MMBtu** and **1 EUR = 1.10 USD**. #### (i) Calculate the LTC gas price in USD/MMBtu. #### (ii) Calculate the equivalent TTF spot price in USD/MMBtu. #### (iii) Which option is cheaper, and by how much (in USD/MMBtu)? [7 Marks] **Given:** - $P_{Brent} = \$95/\text{bbl}$, $s = 0.14$, factor = 0.1615 - $P_{TTF} = €45/\text{MWh}$, $1$ MWh $= 3.412$ MMBtu, $1$ EUR $= 1.10$ USD **Solution:** **Part (i) — LTC price [2 marks]:** $$\begin{aligned} P_{LTC} &= 95 \times 0.14 \times 0.1615 \\ &= 95 \times 0.02261 \\ &= \$2.148 \text{ /MMBtu} \end{aligned}$$ **Part (ii) — TTF spot price [3 marks]:** $$\begin{aligned} P_{TTF, USD/MWh} &= 45 \times 1.10 = \$49.50 \text{ /MWh} \\ P_{TTF, USD/MMBtu} &= \frac{49.50}{3.412} = \$14.508 \text{ /MMBtu} \end{aligned}$$ **Part (iii) — Comparison [2 marks]:** $$\Delta P = P_{TTF} - P_{LTC} = 14.508 - 2.148 = \$12.36 \text{ /MMBtu}$$ **The LTC is cheaper by \$12.36/MMBtu.** (This explains why European utilities stubbornly held on to oil-indexed LTCs even during 2022 price spikes — the alternative was even worse!) --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE** — [KTU University Exam – June 2023 Style] #### (a) Discuss the **role of gas marketers** in a deregulated market. How do they differ from integrated utilities? Describe the four main trading models in the natural gas industry with at least one example of each. [7 Marks] **Model Answer Outline:** 1. **Role of gas marketers (2 marks):** - **Aggregation:** Pool demand from small consumers to buy in bulk at hub. - **Risk management:** Hedge price exposure via NYMEX/ICE futures and swaps. - **Logistics bundling:** Combine pipeline transport, storage, and balancing services into a single delivered product. - **Retail supply:** Sell to end-users (industrial, commercial, residential) under tailored contracts (fixed, variable, indexed, capped). - **No physical assets** (in pure form) — they are paper traders + portfolio managers. 2. **Difference from integrated utilities (1 mark):** - Integrated utility = owns E&P + pipeline + LDC + retail → **vertically integrated** monopoly. - Marketer = contracts for transport and storage but doesn't own physical assets → **virtual/contract-only** player. Marketers compete in wholesale and retail markets; utilities hold monopoly franchises in distribution. 3. **Four trading models (4 marks = 1 mark each, with example):** - **Model 1 — Monopoly:** State-owned integrated utility (e.g., pre-1992 U.S. local gas companies; ONGC-GAIL pre-2004 in India). - **Model 2 — Single-Buyer:** State-designated monopoly buyer (e.g., GAIL India Ltd. as sole LNG importer 2004–2018). - **Model 3 — Bilateral LTC:** Long-term oil-indexed contracts (e.g., Gazprom–Wintershall 20-yr LTCs at German borders). - **Model 4 — Hub-Based Spot & Forward:** Virtual hub trading (e.g., Henry Hub USA, TTF Netherlands, NBP UK). --- #### (b) An Indian fertilizer plant consumes **100,000 MMBtu/month** of natural gas. It can procure gas under three pricing options: - **Option 1:** Administered Price Mechanism (APM) at **\$3.50/MMBtu** with guaranteed supply. - **Option 2:** Long-term contract indexed to Brent at **\$85/bbl** with **slope = 0.135** and **factor = 0.1615**. - **Option 3:** Henry Hub spot at **\$5.20/MMBtu** plus **liquefaction + shipping + regasification** costs of **\$6.00/MMBtu** total. #### Calculate the annual cost of gas under each option and recommend the best choice. [7 Marks] **Solution:** **Option 1 — APM [2 marks]:** $$C_1 = 3.50 \times 100{,}000 \times 12 = \$4{,}200{,}000 \text{ /year}$$ **Option 2 — LTC [2.5 marks]:** $$\begin{aligned} P_2 &= 85 \times 0.135 \times 0.1615 \\ &= 85 \times 0.02180 \\ &= \$1.853 \text{ /MMBtu} \\ C_2 &= 1.853 \times 100{,}000 \times 12 = \$2{,}223{,}600 \text{ /year} \end{aligned}$$ **Option 3 — Henry Hub + LNG [2.5 marks]:** $$\begin{aligned} P_3 &= 5.20 + 6.00 = \$11.20 \text{ /MMBtu} \\ C_3 &= 11.20 \times 100{,}000 \times 12 = \$13{,}440{,}000 \text{ /year} \end{aligned}$$ **Recommendation:** **Option 2 (LTC) is the cheapest at \$2.22 million/year**, saving \$1.98 million/year vs. APM and \$11.22 million/year vs. LNG spot. The recommendation assumes the LTC has reliable delivery terms; if take-or-pay penalties are high, Option 1 may offer better risk-adjusted value. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in the ESE:** > 1. **Forgetting units (–0.5 to –1 mark):** Always write \$/MMBtu, €/MWh, or mt/year. A bare number is treated as incomplete. > 2. **Wrong slope-factor convention (–1 to –2 marks):** The formula is $P_{gas} = P_{oil} \times s \times 0.1615$. Do NOT write $P_{gas} = P_{oil} \times 0.1615 \times 0.135$ and then call 0.135 a "conversion" — examiners expect the slope to be stated separately and applied to a clean formula. > 3. **Confusing Mcf and MMBtu (–1 mark):** Mcf is **volume**; MMBtu is **energy**. Conversion requires the heating value (~1,037 Btu/scf). Don't assume 1 Mcf = 1 MMBtu unless the problem states it. > 4. **Ignoring the carbon price in netback questions (–1 mark):** If the question says "with a carbon price of \$X/tCO₂," you must adjust the coal price. Many students skip this entirely. > 5. **Not drawing a block diagram in 7-mark questions (–1.5 marks):** A labelled Mermaid/diagram earns at least 1.5 marks by itself in KTU 2024 scheme marking schemes. > 6. **Mixing up Henry Hub and TTF market structures (–1 mark):** Both are gas-on-gas hubs, but TTF is virtual (no physical location); Henry Hub is a physical hub tied to the Sabine Pipeline interconnection. Examiners notice this distinction. > 7. **Not stating the year of regulatory reference (–0.5 marks):** Always cite "FERC Order 636 (1992)" or "EU Third Energy Package (2009)" — not just "FERC order" or "EU directive." --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **HIGH-DENSITY RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST — Module 3, Topic 3.1:** > > ✅ **Deregulation = structural unbundling** of the gas value chain (production, transport, storage, distribution, retail). > > ✅ **Four (or five) trading models** to know: Monopoly, Single-Buyer, Bilateral LTC, Hub-Based Spot/Forward, Global LNG. > > ✅ **Three pillars of trading:** Spot, Forward/Futures, Bilateral LTCs. > > ✅ **Key U.S. regulatory milestones:** NGPA 1978, FERC Order 436 (1985), FERC Order 636 (1992) — the "Mega Rule." > > ✅ **Major hubs:** Henry Hub (USA), TTF (Netherlands), NBP (UK), JKM (Asia). > > ✅ **Unit conversions (must memorize):** 1 Mcf ≈ 1.037 MMBtu; 1 MMBtu ≈ 1.055 GJ; 1 MMBtu = 10⁶ Btu; 1 tonne LNG ≈ 52 MMBtu; 1 boe ≈ 5.8 MMBtu. > > ✅ **LTC Oil-Index Formula:** $P_{gas} = P_{oil} \times s \times 0.1615$ \$/MMBtu. > > ✅ **Netback formula:** $P_{gas, wellhead} = P_{alt,Btu} \times (\eta_{alt}/\eta_{gas}) - C_{trans}$. > > ✅ **City-gate decomposition:** $P_{delivered} = P_{hub} + B + T + S$. > > ✅ **Spark spread:** $SS = P_{elec} - (HR \times P_{gas})$. > > ✅ **LNG arbitrage trigger:** Profitable when $P_{JKM} - P_{HH} > C_{liq} + C_{ship} + C_{regas}$ (≈ \$3–6/MMBtu). > > ✅ **Role of gas marketer:** Virtual aggregator, risk manager, retail supplier — no physical assets in pure form. > > ✅ **Oil-indexed vs. gas-on-gas:** LTCs give stability, hubs give efficiency — modern trend is hybrid (LTCs with hub-indexed pricing). > > ✅ **Global shift to gas-on-gas:** Since 2020, the share of oil-indexed European gas has dropped from ~80% to <20% as contracts are renegotiated to TTF-hub-indexed terms.

topics.title: Under Development and Unregulated Markets: Reasons Why Unregulated Markets Reproduce Underdevelopment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET526 - ENERGY ECONOMICS AND POLICY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Under Development and Unregulated Markets: Reasons Why Unregulated Markets Reproduce Underdevelopment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Unregulated Market**: A market structure in which the government imposes minimal or no restrictions on the entry, exit, pricing, production levels, and trading conditions of firms. Prices are determined purely by the interplay of demand and supply (the "invisible hand" of Adam Smith), with the assumption that competitive forces will self-correct inefficiencies, allocate resources optimally, and drive economic growth.

**Underdevelopment**: A structural condition in which an economy exhibits persistent low per-capita income, low productivity, limited industrial diversification, weak institutions, technological stagnation, high unemployment/underemployment, and dependence on primary commodity exports. It is characterized by a **vicious circle of poverty** (Nurkse, 1953) where low income leads to low savings, low investment, and back to low income.

**Reproducing Underdevelopment (Path Dependence)**: The process by which unregulated market mechanisms, when imposed on structurally weak economies, reinforce existing inequalities and bottlenecks, trapping the economy in a low-equilibrium trajectory rather than allowing convergence with developed economies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — Core Thesis**
> The "Washington Consensus" (1989) and the **IMF/World Bank Structural Adjustment Programmes (SAPs)** of the 1980s–1990s prescribed deregulation, privatization, and trade liberalization as universal remedies for underdevelopment. The empirical failure of these policies (e.g., the Lost Decade of Latin America, the Sub-Saharan African stagnation) is the primary motivation for studying why unregulated markets *reproduce* underdevelopment.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **marathon race** where the starting line is staggered:

- **Developed economies** start 5 km ahead, with proper shoes, water stations, and trained coaches (capital, institutions, technology).
- **Underdeveloped economies** start at km 0, barefoot, in the rain, with no coach.

If you now declare **"No rules, no referee, run as you please"** (unregulated market), the faster runners will simply run away further. The barefoot runners will collapse from exhaustion, twisted ankles, and dehydration within the first 2 km. The race becomes *more* unequal, not less. The "unregulated" condition did not create fairness — it amplified the pre-existing gap.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Takeaway**
> Unregulated markets assume *symmetric* actors. Underdeveloped economies are *asymmetric* — they have weak firms, weak states, asymmetric information, and external shocks. Removing regulation under asymmetry does not produce efficiency; it produces **exploitation, extraction, and entrenchment of poverty**.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in the Literature

- **Gini Coefficient (0–1)**: Measures income inequality. **0** = perfect equality, **1** = perfect inequality. KTU students should remember the **Lorenz Curve** that underlies this metric.
- **HDI (Human Development Index)**: Composite of life expectancy, education, and per-capita income (**0 to 1** scale, UNDP).
- **Hirschman-Herfindahl Index (HHI)**: Measures market concentration, where $HHI = \sum_{i=1}^{N} s_i^2$ and $s_i$ is the market share of firm $i$.
- **Infant Mortality Rate**, **Literacy Rate**, **GDP per capita (PPP, current international \$)** — standard World Bank indicators.

> [!TIP]
> For numerical answer scripts in KTU ESE, always cite figures in **constant USD (2017 PPP)** rather than nominal USD, as this is the standard reporting basis for cross-country development comparisons.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" — Seven Structural Reasons Unregulated Markets Reproduce Underdevelopment

Drawing from the works of **Albert Hirschman** (*The Strategy of Economic Development*, 1958), **Ha-Joon Chang** (*Kicking Away the Ladder*, 2002), **Dani Rodrik** (*One Economics, Many Recipes*, 2007), and **Joseph Stiglitz** (*Globalization and Its Discontents*, 2002), we can identify seven canonical reasons.

### Reason 1: Information Asymmetries (Stiglitz, 1980s)
In underdeveloped economies, the **invisible hand requires perfect information** to function. In reality:
- Lenders cannot distinguish good borrowers from bad ones (**adverse selection**).
- Borrowers take excessive risks after receiving loans (**moral hazard**).
- Result: Credit markets collapse, or interest rates skyrocket to **usurious levels** that productive firms cannot afford.

> [!NOTE]
