## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All questions below strictly follow the **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern**: Part A (3 marks each, short answer) and Part B (14 marks each, with internal choice). Model answers are written in the **step-by-step format** that KTU examiners reward with full marks.

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define the “Panchamrit” framework announced at COP-26 and list its five commitments.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Panchamrit (literally “five nectars”) is the collective term for India’s five climate commitments announced by the Hon’ble Prime Minister at the COP-26 summit in Glasgow in November 2021. The five commitments are:
1. India will achieve **500 GW of non-fossil fuel energy capacity** by 2030.
2. India will meet **50% of its energy requirements from renewable sources** by 2030.
3. India will **reduce the carbon intensity of its economy by 45%** from 2005 levels by 2030.
4. India will create an additional **carbon sink of 1 billion tonnes of CO₂ equivalent** through forests and tree cover by 2030.
5. India will achieve the target of **Net-Zero emissions by 2070**.

These five commitments form the **anchor of all recent Indian energy policy**, including NSM, NGHS, ECBC, and the CCT scheme.

*(Examiner note: 0.5 marks for each correct commitment + 0.5 for definition.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**What is the Perform, Achieve and Trade (PAT) scheme? Mention any two tradable items under it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Perform, Achieve and Trade (PAT) scheme is a **market-based energy efficiency mechanism** introduced by the Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE) under the provisions of the **Energy Conservation Act, 2001 (as amended in 2022)**. It applies to energy-intensive industries (DCs — Designated Consumers) across **13 sectors**, currently covering 1077 industrial units (Cycle-VII notified in 2023).

The mechanism requires each DC to achieve a **specific SEC (Specific Energy Consumption) target** notified by the BEE. Units that **over-achieve** their target receive **Energy Savings Certificates (ESCerts)**, while under-achievers must **purchase ESCerts** or pay a penalty of **₹10,000 per toe of shortfall**.

**Two tradable items:**
1. **ESCerts (Energy Savings Certificates)** — 1 ESCert = 1 tonne of oil equivalent (toe) of energy saved.
2. *(Bonus 0.5 marks)*: PAT-Cycle VII tradable energy efficiency certificates (introduced 2023).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — With Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU 2024, every Module 4 question has **internal choice** (either Part A or Part B with sub-parts). Two completely independent alternatives are given below.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Module 4 Choice]**
**(a)** *Discuss in detail the key pillars of India’s recent energy policy shift from 2015 to 2024. Highlight at least four major schemes under each pillar.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Calculate the Levelised Cost of Electricity (LCOE) for a 50 MW solar PV plant with the following data:*
- *Capital Cost: ₹2,500 million*
- *Annual O&M Cost: ₹25 million, escalating at 1% per year*
- *Year-1 Energy Generation: 87.5 million kWh*
- *Annual degradation: 0.5%*
- *Discount rate: 8% per annum*
- *Project life: 25 years*

*Comment on the result with reference to recent SECI solar auction tariffs.* **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Key Pillars of India’s Recent Energy Policy Shift (2015–2024)** — 7 marks

India’s energy policy since 2015 has shifted from a **fossil-centric, subsidy-driven, regulated model** to a **renewable-led, market-driven, and decarbonisation-focused model**. The shift can be organised under four key pillars:

**Pillar 1 — Renewable Capacity Expansion (Supply Push)**
- **National Solar Mission (NSM)** — Phase-III target of 30 GW, current target of **300 GW solar by 2030**.
- **PM-KUSUM** — solarisation of agricultural pumps; target **34.8 GW** (component A: 10 GW, B: 14 GW, C: 10.5 GW).
- **PM-Surya Ghar: Muft Bijli Yojana (Feb 2024)** — rooftop solar for **1 crore households**, 300 units free/month.
- **Offshore Wind Policy** — target of **30 GW offshore wind by 2030**.

**Pillar 2 — Energy Efficiency (Demand-Side Management)**
- **PAT Scheme Cycle-VII** — covers **1077 DCs** across 13 sectors.
- **Standards & Labelling (S&L)** — mandatory star labelling for **38 appliances**.
- **ECBC 2023 update & Eco-Niwas Samhita (ENS)** for residential buildings.
- **UNNATEE (Unlocking National Energy Efficiency Potential)** programme.

**Pillar 3 — Green Fuels & Sectoral Decarbonisation**
- **National Green Hydrogen Mission (Jan 2023)** — ₹19,744 crore, target **5 MT by 2030** (revised; original 1 MT/yr).
- **SATAT scheme** — **5,000 CBG plants** by 2025–26.
- **Ethanol Blending Programme** — **E20 achieved in 2025** (5 years ahead of schedule).
- **National Bioenergy Programme** (2018) — biogas, biomass, and waste-to-energy.

**Pillar 4 — Electric Mobility & Storage**
- **PM E-Drive Scheme (Sep 2024)** — ₹10,900 crore outlay, replacing FAME-II.
- **National Mission on Advanced Battery Storage** — **₹18,100 crore** for 50 GWh cell manufacturing.
- **Vehicle Scrappage Policy (2021)** — deregisters 15/20-year-old vehicles.
- **EV Manufacturing PLI Scheme** — ₹25,938 crore total outlay (auto + auto components).

**Pillar 5 — Market & Regulatory Reforms (Bonus)**
- **Green Open Access Rules 2022**, **Electricity (Amendment) Rules 2024**, **Real-Time Market**, **CCT Scheme 2023**.

---

**(b) LCOE Calculation for 50 MW Solar PV Plant** — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Write the LCOE formula**

$$
LCOE = \dfrac{\sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{C_t}{(1+r)^t}}{\sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{E_t}{(1+r)^t}}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Present Value of Costs (PVC)**

$$
PVC = C_0 + \sum_{t=1}^{25} \dfrac{C_{O\&M} \times (1+g)^{t-1}}{(1+r)^t}
$$

$$
PVC = 2500 + 25 \times \dfrac{1 - \left(\dfrac{1.01}{1.08}\right)^{25}}{0.08 - 0.01}
$$

Compute the ratio term:

$$
\left(\dfrac{1.01}{1.08}\right)^{25} = (0.9352)^{25} \approx 0.1938
$$

$$
PVC = 2500 + 25 \times \dfrac{1 - 0.1938}{0.07} = 2500 + 25 \times \dfrac{0.8062}{0.07} = 2500 + 25 \times 11.517 = 2500 + 287.93 = 2787.93\ \text{million ₹}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Present Value of Energy (PVE)**

$$
PVE = 87.5 \times \dfrac{1 - \left(\dfrac{0.995}{1.08}\right)^{25}}{0.08 - (-0.005)}
$$

$$
\left(\dfrac{0.995}{1.08}\right)^{25} = (0.9213)^{25} \approx 0.1288
$$

$$
PVE = 87.5 \times \dfrac{1 - 0.1288}{0.085} = 87.5 \times \dfrac{0.8712}{0.085} = 87.5 \times 10.249 = 896.79\ \text{million kWh}
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate LCOE**

$$
LCOE = \dfrac{2787.93}{896.79} = 3.108\ \text{₹/kWh}
$$

**Step 5 — Comment**

The calculated LCOE of approximately **₹3.11/kWh** is **higher** than the SECI auction tariffs discovered in 2023–24 (typically ₹2.50–₹2.80/kWh). This discrepancy arises because:
1. The capital cost in this example (₹50 lakh/MW equivalent) is conservative; current CapEx is **₹4.0–4.5 crore/MW**.
2. SECI tariffs benefit from **waivers on interstate transmission system (ISTS) charges** and **accelerated depreciation** benefits that are not factored into this LCOE.
3. The CUF assumed (20%) is conservative; newer projects achieve 23–25% CUF.

In a **revised calculation** with current CapEx of ₹4.25 crore/MW and CUF of 23%, the LCOE would fall to approximately **₹2.60/kWh**, aligning with actual auction tariffs.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Module 4 Choice]**
**(a)** *Explain the National Green Hydrogen Mission in detail. Discuss its targets, financial outlay, and key sub-components.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *A DISCOM has total annual consumption of 8,000 MU. As per the RPO notification for FY 2023–24:*
- *Solar RPO = 10.81%*
- *Non-Solar RPO = 11.43%*
- *Actual Solar Procurement = 750 MU*
- *Actual Non-Solar Procurement = 980 MU*

*Compute (i) the compliance status for each category, (ii) the shortfall in MU, and (iii) the penalty payable under the prevailing rules (₹0.10/kWh for solar shortfall).* **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer:**

**(a) National Green Hydrogen Mission (NGHM)** — 7 marks

**Background & Launch:**
The **National Green Hydrogen Mission** was approved by the Union Cabinet on **4 January 2023**, with a total financial outlay of **₹19,744 crore**. The mission is implemented by the **Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (MNRE)** through the **Solar Energy Corporation of India (SECI)**.

**Objectives & Targets:**
- Production of **5 million tonnes (MT) of Green Hydrogen** by 2030.
- Development of **~125 GW of renewable energy capacity** dedicated to Green Hydrogen production.
- Creation of **~6,00,000 jobs** (direct and indirect).
- Reduction in fossil fuel imports worth **₹1 lakh crore** cumulatively.
- Avoidance of **50 million tonnes of CO₂ emissions** annually by 2030.

**Key Sub-Components (Schemes):**
1. **SIGHT (Strategic Interventions for GH₂ Transition)** — two component programmes:
   - **Component-I**: Electrolyser manufacturing (₹4,440 crore) — PLI for domestic manufacturing of electrolyser capacity.
   - **Component-II**: Green Hydrogen production (₹13,050 crore) — incentives for production cost reduction.
2. **Green Hydrogen pilot projects** in steel, transport, and shipping sectors.
3. **R&D** — establishment of Centres of Excellence in academic and national labs (₹400 crore).
4. **Skill development** — training of ~50,000 personnel.
5. **Regulatory framework** — Standards for Green Hydrogen and Green Ammonia (Bureau of Indian Standards, 2023).

**Significance:**
- India imports **85% of its crude oil and 50% of its natural gas**, making energy security a national priority.
- Green Hydrogen can replace **grey hydrogen** used in fertilisers (5 MT/yr) and refining (1.5 MT/yr), accounting for **6.5 MT/yr of import substitution potential**.

---

**(b) RPO Compliance Calculation** — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Compute Solar RPO Quota**

$$
Q_{solar,quota} = 10.81\% \times 8000 = 864.8\ \text{MU}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Solar Compliance**

$$
Compliance_{solar} = \dfrac{750}{864.8} \times 100 = 86.72\%
$$

**Step 3 — Solar Shortfall**

$$
Shortfall_{solar} = 864.8 - 750 = 114.8\ \text{MU}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Non-Solar RPO Quota**

$$
Q_{non\text{-}solar,quota} = 11.43\% \times 8000 = 914.4\ \text{MU}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Non-Solar Compliance**

$$
Compliance_{non\text{-}solar} = \dfrac{980}{914.4} \times 100 = 107.18\%
$$

**Step 6 — Non-Solar Surplus**

$$
Surplus_{non\text{-}solar} = 980 - 914.4 = 65.6\ \text{MU}
$$

**Step 7 — Penalty Computation**

$$
Penalty = Shortfall_{solar} \times \text{₹}0.10/\text{kWh}
$$

$$
Penalty = 114.8 \times 10^6\ \text{kWh} \times \text{₹}0.10/\text{kWh} = \text{₹}1.148\ \text{crore}
$$

**Step 8 — Summary Table**

| Category | Quota (MU) | Procured (MU) | Shortfall/Surplus | Compliance % | Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Solar | 864.8 | 750.0 | **−114.8 (shortfall)** | 86.72% | Penalty of ₹1.148 cr |
| Non-Solar | 914.4 | 980.0 | +65.6 (surplus) | 107.18% | Carry forward or bank |

**Interpretation:**
The DISCOM has a **13.28% shortfall in solar RPO** but is **7.18% in excess for non-solar RPO**. Under the current framework, surplus non-solar RECs **cannot** be used to offset the solar shortfall. The DISCOM must either:
- Purchase **114.8 MU of solar RECs** in the IEX market,
- Pay the penalty of **₹1.148 crore**, or
- Carry forward the deficit for up to **2 years** under the banking provision.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner’s Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Unit mistake** — converting MU to kWh is mandatory. *1 MU = 10⁶ kWh*. Many students forget this unit conversion and lose **1 mark**.
> 2. **REC inter-changeability** — students often write that surplus non-solar RECs can be used for solar shortfall. **This is incorrect as of 2024** under the prevailing RPO framework.
> 3. **RPO percentage values** — the FY 2023–24 figures (Solar 10.81%, Non-Solar 11.43%) are based on the **MoP order dated 22.07.2022**. Using outdated values (e.g., 8% solar) leads to **wrong quota calculation** and full-mark deduction.
> 4. **Penalty base** — the penalty is on the **shortfall in kWh**, not on the percentage shortfall. Always compute the absolute shortfall first.
> 5. **CUF convention** — for solar LCOE, the CUF for a 1-axis tracker plant in India is **23–25%**, not 18–20%. Use 25% if the question states “tracker”, and 20% if it says “fixed-tilt”.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density, exam-ready summary of *Recent Trends and Shift in Policies in India* — Module 4.**

- **Panchamrit (COP-26, 2021)** is the *anchor* — remember all 5 commitments: 500 GW RE, 50% RE share, 45% CI reduction, 1 Bt CO₂ sink, Net-Zero 2070.
- **Five policy pillars**: Renewable Capacity → Energy Efficiency → Green Fuels → E-Mobility & Storage → Market & Regulatory Reforms.
- **LCOE formula** $\dfrac{\sum C_t/(1+r)^t}{\sum E_t/(1+r)^t}$ — know it cold; practise at least one numerical for solar PV and one for wind.
- **PAT scheme** — cap-and-trade, **ESCerts = 1 toe**, penalty **₹10,000/toe**, BEE is the implementing agency.
- **RPO calculation** — Solar 10.81% + Non-Solar 11.43% (FY 2023–24); penalty **₹0.10/kWh** on solar shortfall; 1 MU = **10⁶ kWh**.
- **Green Hydrogen Mission** — ₹19,744 crore, 5 MT/yr by 2030, SIGHT programme (₹4,440 + ₹13,050 crore), MNRE implementing, BIS standards (2023).
- **CCT Scheme (June 2023)** — carbon credits = **1 tCO₂eq**, MoEFCC, complements PAT and is distinct from RPO.
- **Green Open Access Rules 2022** — minimum load reduced to **100 kW**, ₹0.50/kWh DISCOM surcharge, 12-month banking.
- **PM E-Drive (2024)** — ₹10,900 crore, **replaces FAME-II**; covers e-2W, e-3W, e-4W, e-buses, and ambulances.
- **Vehicle Scrappage Policy (2021)** — deregisters **15-year-old petrol** and **20-year-old diesel** vehicles.
- **Floating Solar Policy 2024** — encourages FSPV on reservoirs, dams, and water bodies.
- **Real-Time Market (RTM)** — operational since **June 2020**, half-hourly contracts, introduced to balance RE variability.
- **Net-Zero 2070** — long-term pathway requires **annual non-fossil addition of 50 GW**, peaking coal demand by 2030, complete phase-out by 2070.
- **Paris Agreement NDC** — 5 targets updated on 26 August 2022 — the **45% CI reduction** and **50% RE share** are the binding numbers.
- **Just Transition** — refers to socio-economic protection of coal-mining communities; Just Energy Transition Partnerships (JETP) with **G7 ($20 bn)** and **JETP Indonesia ($20 bn)**; India is in the early stages of JETP formulation.
- **LiFE (Lifestyle for Environment)** movement — launched by India at COP-26; demand-side behaviour change complement to supply-side decarbonisation.
- **MNRE** is the apex nodal ministry for renewables; **BEE** for efficiency; **CEA** for grid planning; **CERC** for central tariff regulations.
- **Energy Conservation (Amendment) Act 2022** — expanded the mandate of BEE, introduced **carbon trading**, mandated minimum RE use in certain industries.
- **Coal** is still ~50% of installed capacity (2024) but its share is **declining 1–2 percentage points per year** — the *trends* to memorise are the *direction* and the *rate* of change.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Studies of Different Countries and Sector-Wise Emission Trends in India
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET526 - ENERGY ECONOMICS AND POLICY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Case Studies of Different Countries and Sector-Wise Emission Trends in India

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of the Topic

**Energy Economics and Policy** examines how nations design, implement, and evaluate policies to balance the **trilemma** of energy security, economic affordability, and environmental sustainability. Within Module 4, the focus is twofold:

1. **Country-Specific Case Studies** – Comparative analysis of energy and climate policies adopted by leading economies (Germany, Denmark, USA, China, UK, Japan, Norway, Sweden) and the lessons they offer for developing nations.
2. **Sector-Wise Emission Trends in India** – Disaggregated assessment of greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions across the power, transport, industry, agriculture, buildings, and waste sectors, with policy implications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Greenhouse Gas (GHG) Emission** is the release of gases that trap heat in the atmosphere, primarily Carbon Dioxide (**CO₂**), Methane (**CH₄**), Nitrous Oxide (**N₂O**), and Fluorinated gases (**F-gases**). All emissions are reported in units of **million tonnes of CO₂-equivalent (MtCO₂e)** using the **Global Warming Potential (GWP-100)** metric.

> [!NOTE]
> **GWP-100** is a conversion factor that expresses the heat-trapping effect of a gas over a 100-year horizon relative to CO₂. The Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (**IPCC**) provides standardized GWP values used globally in national inventories.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a household kitchen where several burners are running simultaneously — the gas stove, the oven, the water heater. The **total household emissions** depend on each appliance's *activity* (how long it runs) multiplied by its *emission intensity* (how dirty the fuel is). Similarly, a country's GHG inventory is the sum of emissions from its **economic sectors**, where each sector has its own activity driver and emission factor.

The **case-study approach** in energy policy is like reading the *playbook* of experienced players. When India wants to know *"Should we adopt feed-in tariffs like Germany, or carbon taxation like Sweden?"*, the answer is found by studying who tried what, what worked, what failed, and **why**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Core Metrics** for any country analysis:
> 1. **Total Emissions (MtCO₂e)** – absolute scale
> 2. **Per Capita Emissions (tCO₂/capita)** – equity indicator
> 3. **Carbon Intensity (kgCO₂/USD of GDP)** – efficiency indicator

## 1.3 Key Terminology Used Throughout

| Term | Definition | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **NDC** | Nationally Determined Contribution under the Paris Agreement | Country-level emission reduction pledge |
| **ETS / Cap-and-Trade** | Emissions Trading System with a fixed total cap | Market-based price discovery for carbon |
| **Carbon Tax** | A Pigouvian tax on the carbon content of fuels | Direct price signal on emissions |
| **Feed-in Tariff (FiT)** | Guaranteed price paid to renewable generators | De-risks renewable investment |
| **RECs** | Renewable Energy Certificates | Tradable proof of renewable generation |
| **PAT** | Perform, Achieve and Trade (India's industrial scheme) | Energy efficiency trading |
| **LCOE** | Levelized Cost of Electricity (₹/MWh) | Compares cost across technologies |
| **Kaya Identity** | Decomposition of emissions into population, GDP, energy intensity, and carbon intensity | Identifies drivers of emission change |
| **Net-Zero** | Balancing emitted GHG with equivalent removals | Long-term climate target |
| **Just Transition** | Fair shift to low-carbon economy protecting workers/communities | Social dimension of climate action |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Kaya Identity — Foundation of Emission Analysis

The **Kaya Identity**, proposed by Japanese economist Yoichi Kaya in 1993, decomposes a country's total CO₂ emissions into four multiplicative factors:

$$E \;=\; P \;\times\; \frac{G}{P} \;\times\; \frac{E_{total}}{G} \;\times\; \frac{C}{E_{total}}$$

Where:
- $E$ = total CO₂ emissions (MtCO₂)
- $P$ = population (persons)
- $G/P$ = GDP per capita (USD/person)
- $E_{total}/G$ = energy intensity of the economy (toe/USD or MJ/USD)
- $C/E_{total}$ = carbon intensity of the energy mix (MtCO₂/toe)

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Use of Kaya Identity**:
> - If $P$ grows fast but carbon intensity falls even faster → emissions decline.
> - It is the **conceptual backbone** of carbon footprint decomposition in any country case study.

## 2.2 Sectoral Emission Accounting Equation

For each sector, the emission inventory follows the **IPCC Tier 1/2/3 methodology**:

$$E_{sector} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} AD_i \;\times\; EF_i \;\times\; GWP_i$$

Where:
- $AD_i$ = Activity Data (e.g., tonnes of fuel burnt, km travelled, livestock count)
- $EF_i$ = Emission Factor (e.g., tCO₂/toe of coal, kgCH₄/head of cattle)
- $GWP_i$ = Global Warming Potential of gas $i$
- $n$ = number of activities in the sector

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **complete formula reference** for any numerical/empirical question on this topic. Memorise every entry for Part-B level marks.

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Kaya Identity | $E = P \times (G/P) \times (E_{total}/G) \times (C/E_{total})$ | MtCO₂ | Decomposes aggregate emissions |
| 2 | Per Capita Emissions | $e_{pc} = E / P$ | tCO₂/person | Equity comparison |
| 3 | Carbon Intensity (of GDP) | $CI = E / G$ | kgCO₂/USD | Decoupling indicator |
| 4 | Sectoral Emissions | $E_{s} = \sum AD_i \times EF_i \times GWP_i$ | MtCO₂e | IPCC inventory |
| 5 | Emission Reduction % | $\Delta E \,\% = \dfrac{E_{base} - E_{current}}{E_{base}} \times 100$ | % | NDC progress tracking |
| 6 | Renewable Share | $R_{share} = \dfrac{G_{RE}}{G_{total}} \times 100$ | % | Energy mix transition |
| 7 | Carbon Tax Revenue | $R = t_c \times C_{taxed}$ | ₹/USD | Fiscal policy tool |
| 8 | Abatement Cost | $AC = \dfrac{C_{abatement}}{E_{avoided}}$ | ₹/tCO₂ | Policy cost-effectiveness |
| 9 | Carbon Leakage | $\beta = \dfrac{\Delta E_{partner}}{\Delta E_{domestic}}$ | ratio | Border adjustment logic |
| 10 | LCOE | $LCOE = \dfrac{\sum_{t} \dfrac{I_t + F_t}{(1+r)^t}}{\sum_{t} \dfrac{E_t}{(1+r)^t}}$ | ₹/MWh | Technology comparison |
| 11 | Avoided Emissions | $E_{avoid} = E_{baseline} - E_{project}$ | MtCO₂ | Project evaluation |
| 12 | Energy Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{Useful output}}{\text{Energy input}}$ | % or ratio | PAT scheme |
| 13 | Emission Multiplier | $EM = \dfrac{\Delta E}{\Delta G}$ | tCO₂/M₹ added | Growth-emission elasticity |
| 14 | Net-Zero Target Year | $E_{yr} \le E_{sinks,\,yr}$ | MtCO₂ | Long-term climate goal |

> **Reminder for KTU Valuation**: Always specify **units** and the **base year** when writing any emission formula. A common valuation error is writing a numerical answer without quoting the *base year* (e.g., "1990 = 100" vs "2005 = 100").

## 2.4 Why Country Case Studies Matter — The "Policy Diffusion" Logic

Policy diffusion across nations follows a **five-stage** analytical logic:

1. **Diagnostic** – Measure current emission trajectory using Kaya identity.
2. **Benchmarking** – Compare per-capita and carbon-intensity metrics with peers.
3. **Instrument Selection** – Choose between price (tax/cap), regulatory (standards), or informational (labelling) tools.
4. **Implementation** – Address political economy, distributional impacts, and fiscal viability.
5. **Evaluation** – Use avoided emissions and cost-benefit ratios to refine next steps.

This is the same structure used by the **IEA, World Bank, and IPCC** in their country-level policy reviews.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Numerical Illustrations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Country Case Studies — Detailed Analytical Framework

### 3.1.1 Germany — The *Energiewende* (Energy Transition)

**Background:**
- Launched formally in **2010**, with roots in the 1990s renewable energy laws.
- Three pillars: (i) phase-out of nuclear power (completed 2023); (ii) coal phase-out by **2038** (targeted 2030); (iii) **65% renewable electricity by 2030** and **80% by 2030** (electricity target).
- *Erneuerbare-Energien-Gesetz* (EEG) — Renewable Energy Sources Act — introduced **feed-in tariffs (FiT)** in 2000.

**Quantitative Snapshot (typical, recent years):**
- Total CO₂ emissions: **~675 MtCO₂**
- Per capita: **~8.0 tCO₂/capita**
- Renewable share of electricity: **>50%** (wind + solar dominant)
- EEG surcharge funding: shifted from consumer levy to federal budget post-2022.

**Analytical Derivation of the *EEG Effect* on Renewable Capacity:**

Let $C_t$ denote installed renewable capacity at time $t$. Under the German FiT logic, capacity addition follows:

$$C_{t+1} - C_{t} \;=\; f(FiT_t, \; LCOE_t, \; r_t)$$

Where:
- $FiT_t$ = feed-in tariff at time $t$ (€/MWh)
- $LCOE_t$ = levelized cost of renewables at time $t$
- $r_t$ = risk-adjusted discount rate
- $f$ is an increasing function: higher $FiT$ relative to $LCOE$ → greater capacity addition

The policy is *effective* when $FiT_t \ge (LCOE_t + margin)$, ensuring developer profitability.

**Energy-Substitution Equation:**

$$E_{RE,t} \;=\; \int_{0}^{t} \bigl( FiT_{\tau} - LCOE_{\tau} \bigr)^+ \cdot k_{\tau} \; d\tau$$

Where $(x)^+ = \max(x,0)$ and $k_{\tau}$ is a policy-sensitivity coefficient. Renewable deployment rises cumulatively with the **FiT–LCOE wedge**.

> **Key Lesson for India:** India adapted the FiT into **Generation-Based Incentive (GBI)** and **Vidyut Vyapar Nigam (VVN)** mechanisms. However, the global trend has shifted from FiT to **reverse-auction based competitive bidding** (e.g., India's SECI tenders), which yields lower tariffs.

---

### 3.1.2 Denmark — A Renewable Powerhouse

**Background:**
- 1973 oil crisis prompted Denmark to invest heavily in **wind energy** and **district heating**.
- Currently generates **>60% of electricity from wind** (some hourly records exceed 100%).
- **Energy Islands** project: artificial islands in the North Sea acting as offshore wind hubs.
- Uses a **Nordic-style carbon tax** (highest in the world) on top of EU ETS.

**Per Capita Emissions Trajectory:**

$$e_{pc}(t) = e_{pc,0} \times \prod_{i=1}^{t} (1 + g_i)$$

Where $g_i < 0$ for years of decarbonisation. Denmark's per-capita emissions have fallen from ~13 tCO₂/capita (1990) to ~5 tCO₂/capita (recent) — a **structural decoupling** from economic growth.

**Thermal Co-firing Equation** (CHP logic):

$$Q_{CHP} = \eta_{elec} \times Q_{fuel} + \eta_{th} \times (1 - \eta_{elec}) \times Q_{fuel}$$

Combined Heat and Power (CHP) plants achieve **>90% total efficiency** by capturing waste heat for district heating, halving primary fuel use.

---

### 3.1.3 China — The Dual Strategy

**Background:**
- World's largest current emitter (~**11,000 MtCO₂**, ~30% of global total).
- Simultaneously the largest installer of solar PV and wind capacity.
- **30·60 target**: Peak emissions before 2030; carbon neutrality before 2060.
- Operates a **national ETS** (launched 2021) covering power sector (~40% of national emissions).

**Kaya Decomposition for China:**

$$\frac{\Delta E}{E} = \frac{\Delta P}{P} + \frac{\Delta (G/P)}{G/P} + \frac{\Delta (E_{total}/G)}{E_{total}/G} + \frac{\Delta (C/E_{total})}{C/E_{total}}$$

In China, the **population factor** is now near zero growth, the **GDP/capita** factor is strongly positive, the **energy intensity** factor is negative (efficiency gains), and the **carbon intensity** factor is also negative (renewables + nuclear). The result: emissions continue to rise but the **rate is decelerating**.

**Implied Future Trajectory:**

If China reduces $E_{total}/G$ by 3%/year and $C/E_{total}$ by 2%/year while GDP/capita grows at 4%/year, then net emission growth = $+4 - 3 - 2 = -1\%$/year → **emission peak achievable**.

---

### 3.1.4 USA — A Federal-State Patchwork

**Background:**
- **Inflation Reduction Act (IRA), 2022** — largest climate investment in US history (~$369 billion).
- State-level **Renewable Portfolio Standards (RPS)** in 30+ states.
- **California Cap-and-Trade** linked with Québec; **Regional Greenhouse Gas Initiative (RGGI)** for north-eastern power sector.
- Coal-to-gas switching drove most of US emission reductions 2010-2020; renewables + EVs accelerating the next phase.

**Cost-Effectiveness of RPS:**

$$C_{RPS} = \sum_{i} \big( LCOE_i - MC_i \big) \times Q_i \times (1+r)^{-t_i}$$

Where $LCOE_i$ is the LCOE of technology $i$, $MC_i$ is the market-clearing price, and $Q_i$ is quantity procured.

---

### 3.1.5 United Kingdom — The Climate Change Act Pioneer

**Background:**
- **Climate Change Act, 2008** — first legally binding national framework.
- **Sixth Carbon Budget**: cut emissions by **78% by 2035** vs 1990 baseline.
- **Carbon Price Floor (CPF)** — top-up tax on top of EU ETS (now UK ETS).
- Coal generation: < 2% of electricity in recent years (from 40% in 2012).

**Carbon Price Floor Effect:**

$$P_{effective} = \max(P_{EU\,ETS}, \; P_{CPF})$$

This guarantees a minimum carbon price, reducing ETS price volatility.

---

### 3.1.6 Sweden — The Carbon Tax Success Story

**Background:**
- Carbon tax introduced in **1991** at SEK 250/tCO₂; now among the highest globally (~SEK 1,300/tCO₂ ≈ €110).
- Result: emissions down **~27%** since introduction despite **GDP growth of ~80%**.
- Exceptionally high **forestry/biomass share** in the energy mix.

**Decoupling Equation:**

$$\eta_{decoupling} = \dfrac{\Delta E / E}{\Delta G / G} < 1 \; \text{is weak decoupling}$$

For **strong decoupling** (absolute reduction), $\Delta E < 0$ even when $\Delta G > 0$. Sweden is a textbook case of strong decoupling.

---

### 3.1.7 Japan — Post-Fukushima Recalibration

**Background:**
- 2011 Fukushima disaster → all 54 nuclear reactors taken offline.
- Renewed emphasis on **energy efficiency**, **renewables**, and **LNG imports** (high cost).
- **Green Transformation (GX)** plan with **JPY 150 trillion** in green investment over a decade.
- 2030 target: **46% emission reduction** (vs 2013).

---

### 3.1.8 Norway — The EV Capital of the World

**Background:**
- ~**88% of new cars sold** in 2023 were electric (BEV/PHEV).
- Achieved via: (i) **VAT exemption** on EVs; (ii) toll/fee discounts; (iii) bus-lane access; (iv) extensive charging network.
- Result: per-capita transport emissions have **decoupled** from vehicle ownership.

---

## 3.2 Sector-Wise Emission Trends in India — Detailed Numerical Matrix

India's total GHG emissions (excluding LULUCF) are approximately **3,400 MtCO₂e**, ranking India as the **third-largest emitter globally** (after China and USA). However, **per-capita emissions are among the lowest** for major economies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **India's Climate Targets (NDC updated 2022):**
> - Reduce **GDP emission intensity by 45%** by 2030 (vs 2005 baseline).
> - Achieve **50% cumulative electric power from non-fossil sources** by 2030.
> - **Net-zero by 2070**.

### 3.2.1 Sector-Wise Emission Share Matrix (Approximate, recent years)

| Sector | Approx. Share of Total GHG | Dominant Gas | Key Driver | Key Policy |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Electricity / Power** | **~40–44%** | CO₂ | Coal-fired generation | Renewable expansion, PAT |
| **Industry** | **~20–22%** | CO₂, CH₄ | Iron & steel, cement, chemicals | Perform Achieve Trade (PAT) |
| **Transport** | **~13–14%** | CO₂ | Road freight, passenger cars | FAME-II, EV policy, BS-VI norms |
| **Agriculture** | **~14%** | CH₄, N₂O | Rice paddies, livestock, fertilisers | Neem-coated urea, climate-smart agri |
| **Buildings** | **~5–8%** | CO₂ | Space cooling, lighting | ECBC, ECO Niwas Samhita |
| **Waste** | **~2–3%** | CH₄ | Landfills, wastewater | SWM rules 2016, biogas support |
| **Other / Fugitive** | **~3–4%** | CH₄ | Coal mining, oil & gas | CMM policy |

### 3.2.2 Detailed Sectoral Analysis

#### A. Power / Electricity Sector (Largest Emitter)

- Installed capacity ~**440 GW** (recent); renewable share ~**33%**.
- Coal still accounts for ~**45% of installed capacity** but **~70% of generation**.
- Thermal Plant Emission Factor (India average):
$$EF_{thermal} = \dfrac{\text{CO}_2 \text{ emitted (Mt)}}{\text{Generation (TWh)}} \approx 0.85 \text{ tCO}_2/\text{MWh}$$

- **Emission Trajectory Equation:**

$$E_{power}(t) = G_{total}(t) \times (1 - R_{share}(t)) \times EF_{thermal}(t)$$

- $G_{total}(t) \uparrow$ (electricity demand grows)
- $R_{share}(t) \uparrow$ (renewables rise → renewable share of generation)
- $EF_{thermal}(t) \downarrow$ (efficiency of coal plants improves)

The interplay determines whether $E_{power}$ rises or falls.

> **Key Policies:** Renewable Purchase Obligation (RPO); Green Term-Ahead Market (GTAM); National Green Hydrogen Mission; PM-KUSUM (solar pumps); PM-Surya Ghar Yojana (rooftop solar).

#### B. Industrial Sector

- Process emissions from **cement** (calcination) and **steel** (coke) are hard-to-abate.
- **PAT Scheme (BEE):** Energy-intensive industries (e.g., aluminium, cement, fertiliser, paper, textiles) given Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) targets; tradable **Energy Saving Certificates (ESCerts)**.

**SEC Reduction Target Equation:**

$$SEC_{target} = SEC_{baseline} \times (1 - \alpha)$$

Where $\alpha$ is the mandatory reduction percentage set by BEE.

**Hydrogen and Green Steel Pathways:**

- Reduction from blast furnace: $Fe_2O_3 + 3CO \rightarrow 2Fe + 3CO_2$ (high CO₂)
- Green hydrogen route: $Fe_2O_3 + 3H_2 \rightarrow 2Fe + 3H_2O$ (water as by-product)

The CO₂ intensity of steel can drop from ~2.0 tCO₂/t-steel to ~0.05 tCO₂/t-steel with green H₂.

#### C. Transport Sector

- Road transport accounts for **>85%** of transport emissions.
- India has committed to **30% EV penetration in private cars by 2030** and **80% for 2-wheelers and 3-wheelers**.
- **FAME-II** (Faster Adoption and Manufacturing of Electric Vehicles) provides demand incentives; PLI scheme supports domestic manufacturing.

**Fleet Emission Projection:**

$$E_{fleet}(t) = \sum_{v=1}^{n} N_v(t) \times km_v(t) \times EF_v(t)$$

Where $N_v$ = number of vehicles of type $v$, $km_v$ = annual kilometres, $EF_v$ = emission factor per km. EV penetration directly reduces $EF_v$.

#### D. Agriculture Sector

- **Rice paddy cultivation** is the largest methane source (anaerobic decomposition).
- **Enteric fermentation** in cattle (CH₄ from digestion) — India's cattle population is among the world's largest.
- **Synthetic fertiliser** use generates N₂O.

**Mitigation Cost Curve Concept:**

The **Marginal Abatement Cost (MAC)** of measures like Alternate Wetting and Drying (AWD) in rice is often **negative** (cost-saving), making it attractive.

$$MAC = \dfrac{\Delta C_{mitigation} - \Delta C_{savings}}{\Delta E_{avoided}}$$

When $MAC < 0$, mitigation is **financially attractive** even before carbon pricing.

#### E. Buildings Sector

- **Cooling demand** is rising rapidly due to climate change and rising incomes.
- **ECBC** (Energy Conservation Building Code) and **ECO Niwas Samhita** set performance standards.
- **Super-efficient appliances** programme targets ACs, fans, refrigerators.

#### F. Forestry (LULUCF) — India's Carbon Sink

- India's forests and tree cover act as a net **sink** absorbing ~**150–200 MtCO₂e/year**.
- Offsetting effect: net emissions after LULUCF are lower than gross emissions.

**Net Emission Equation:**

$$E_{net} = E_{gross} - E_{sink,LULUCF}$$

### 3.2.3 Comparative Country Matrix (KTU High-Yield)

| Country | Total Emissions (MtCO₂) | Per-Capita (tCO₂) | Carbon Intensity | Dominant Decarbonisation Policy |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| China | ~11,000 | ~7.7 | ~0.55 | National ETS + coal cap |
| USA | ~5,000 | ~14.4 | ~0.25 | IRA + RPS + federal tax credits |
| India | ~3,400 | ~2.4 | ~0.50 | NDC + PLI + renewable auctions |
| Germany | ~675 | ~8.0 | ~0.20 | Energiewende + EU ETS |
| Japan | ~1,000 | ~8.0 | ~0.30 | GX + FIT (now auctioned) |
| UK | ~330 | ~4.8 | ~0.12 | Climate Change Act + Carbon Price Floor |
| Sweden | ~40 | ~3.8 | ~0.08 | Carbon tax (1991) + nuclear |
| Denmark | ~30 | ~5.0 | ~0.10 | Wind + district CHP + carbon tax |
| Norway | ~35 | ~6.3 | ~0.10 | EV incentives + hydropower |

> **Interpretation for India:** India's per-capita emissions are among the lowest in the table, but carbon intensity is the **highest** — indicating significant scope for *efficiency-led* decoupling.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Kaya Identity — Causal Decomposition Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[POPULATION P] --> M[MULTIPLY ALL FACTORS]
    G[GDP PER CAPITA G P] --> M
    EI[ENERGY INTENSITY E TOTAL G] --> M
    CI[CARBON INTENSITY C E TOTAL] --> M
    M --> E[TOTAL CO2 EMISSIONS E]

    style P fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1976d2,color:#000
    style G fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#f57c00,color:#000
    style EI fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#7b1fa2,color:#000
    style CI fill:#ffebee,stroke:#c62828,color:#000
    style M fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#424242,color:#000
    style E fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram:** Multiply the four coloured nodes (population, affluence, energy intensity, carbon intensity) to obtain total emissions. Policy interventions target *one or more* of these factors.

## 4.2 Country Case Study Comparison Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT[COUNTRY CASE STUDIES]
    ROOT --> A[PRICE-BASED INSTRUMENTS]
    ROOT --> B[REGULATORY INSTRUMENTS]
    ROOT --> C[HYBRID INSTRUMENTS]

    A --> A1[Sweden Carbon Tax]
    A --> A2[UK Carbon Price Floor]
    A --> A3[EU ETS Germany Denmark]

    B --> B1[Germany EEG Feed In Tariff]
    B --> B2[USA RPS Standards]
    B --> B3[Japan Top Runner Programme]

    C --> C1[China National ETS + Coal Cap]
    C --> C2[Norway EV Package]
    C --> C3[India NDC + PLI + RPO]

    style ROOT fill:#37474f,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style A fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#1565c0,color:#000
    style B fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000
    style C fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Sector-Wise Emission Map of India

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IND[TOTAL INDIA GHG 3400 MTCO2E]
    IND --> S1[POWER SECTOR 44 PERCENT]
    IND --> S2[INDUSTRY 22 PERCENT]
    IND --> S3[TRANSPORT 14 PERCENT]
    IND --> S4[AGRICULTURE 14 PERCENT]
    IND --> S5[BUILDINGS 6 PERCENT]
    IND --> S6[OTHERS WASTE FUGITIVE 3 PERCENT]

    S1 --> S1A[COAL THERMAL]
    S1 --> S1B[GAS AND DIESEL]

    S2 --> S2A[STEEL AND CEMENT]
    S2 --> S2B[CHEMICALS AND FERTILISER]
    S2 --> S2C[TEXTILES AND PULP]

    S3 --> S3A[ROAD FREIGHT]
    S3 --> S3B[ROAD PASSENGER]
    S3 --> S3C[AVIATION AND SHIPPING]

    S4 --> S4A[RICE METHANE]
    S4 --> S4B[ENTERIC CATTLE]
    S4 --> S4C[FERTILISER N2O]

    style IND fill:#0d47a1,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style S1 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000
    style S2 fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
    style S3 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
    style S4 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
```
